topics.title: RF Circuit Introduction - Importance of Radio Frequency Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RF Circuit Introduction - Importance of Radio Frequency Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radio Frequency (RF)** refers to the portion of the electromagnetic spectrum that occupies frequencies from approximately **3 kHz to 300 GHz**, corresponding to free-space wavelengths ranging from **100 km down to 1 mm**. The field of **RF Engineering** encompasses the design, analysis, and implementation of electronic circuits, systems, and antennas that operate within this band.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT862) Module 1**, RF Circuit Design is defined as the discipline concerned with the development of circuits in which the **physical dimensions of components become comparable to the wavelength of the operating signal**, making traditional low-frequency lumped-element analysis (using Kirchhoff's Voltage and Current Laws) insufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> RF design differs fundamentally from low-frequency analog design due to the appearance of **wave propagation effects, distributed parameters, parasitic reactances, and electromagnetic field coupling**. The importance of RF design arises from its central role in **wireless communications, radar systems, satellite networks, IoT, 5G/6G, medical imaging (MRI), and defence electronics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Frequency Band Nomenclature (IEEE / ITU-R):**
> - **MF / HF (3–30 MHz):** AM radio, shortwave broadcasting
> - **VHF (30–300 MHz):** FM radio, television
> - **UHF (300 MHz–3 GHz):** Mobile phones, Wi-Fi, GPS
> - **SHF / Microwave (3–30 GHz):** Radar, satellite, 5G
> - **EHF / mmWave (30–300 GHz):** 5G mmWave, automotive radar, 6G research

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a calm pond. If you dip your finger slowly in and out, the water surface barely reacts — you see localized ripples. This is analogous to **low-frequency (DC / audio) circuits** where the current and voltage are well-defined at every point, and components behave exactly as labeled.

Now imagine slapping the pond surface hard. The ripples spread outward, bounce off the edges, and interact with one another — creating complex interference patterns. This is analogous to **RF circuits**, where the signal wavelength becomes comparable to the wire length, and the circuit itself starts behaving like a tiny **antenna**, with signals radiating, reflecting, and coupling in ways the schematic symbol never warned you about.

In other words: **at RF, the wire IS the circuit.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

The following constants are foundational to all RF design calculations and are highlighted in **bold** for immediate identification:

- Speed of light in free space: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Characteristic impedance of free space: **η₀ = 376.73 Ω ≈ 377 Ω**
- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Boltzmann constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – dBm (Decibel-milliwatt):** RF engineers almost universally express power in dBm, defined as
> $$P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{1\text{ mW}}\right)$$
> This logarithmic scale is preferred because RF power levels can span many orders of magnitude (from picowatts in receivers to kilowatts in radar transmitters).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why RF Design is Different – Core Reasoning

At low frequencies, the design engineer assumes:
1. The **wire is a perfect short circuit** (zero impedance).
2. Component values are **exactly** the value on the label.
3. Voltage and current are **uniform** throughout the circuit.
4. The circuit does **not interact** with its electromagnetic surroundings.

At Radio Frequencies, **all four assumptions collapse simultaneously**, which is why RF design demands an entirely different mindset and toolkit.

### The Four Pillars of RF Difficulty

**Pillar 1 — Distributed vs. Lumped Behaviour**
- A transmission line of length *l* carrying a signal of wavelength *λ* is treated as a *distributed* element when **l ≥ λ/10**.
- The voltage and current vary in space *and* time, so they must be described by the **Telegrapher's equations**, not Ohm's law.

**Pillar 2 — Parasitic Effects Dominate**
- Every lead, pad, and trace has stray inductance (~1 nH/mm) and capacitance (~few pF).
- At 1 GHz, a 5 mm wire bond has reactance **ωL ≈ 31 Ω**, which is no longer negligible.

**Pillar 3 — Skin Effect and Conductor Losses**
- At high frequencies, current is forced to flow only within a thin outer "skin" of the conductor.
- This increases AC resistance and dramatically alters Q-factor of inductors.

**Pillar 4 — Electromagnetic Radiation & Crosstalk**
- A current-carrying wire of length λ/4 is an efficient radiator.
- Circuits couple energy into neighbouring traces even when not designed to, causing interference and EMI.

## 2.2 The Importance of Radio Frequency Design

### (A) Spectrum as a Finite Natural Resource
- The usable RF spectrum is **finite** and regulated globally by **ITU-R** and nationally by bodies like India's **WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing)**.
- With over **50 billion connected IoT devices projected by 2030**, efficient spectrum utilisation is an existential engineering challenge.

### (B) Explosion of Wireless Applications
- Mobile communications (4G LTE, 5G NR, future 6G)
- Wi-Fi (2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz), Bluetooth, NFC
- Satellite comms (Ku, Ka, V bands)
- Radar (defence, automotive 77 GHz, weather)
- Medical: MRI (128 MHz RF coil), RF ablation, hyperthermia
- Industrial: RFID, microwave heating, plasma generation

### (C) Economic and Strategic Significance
- The global RF components market exceeded **USD 50 billion** in 2024.
- RF design capability is a **strategic national asset** (5G, defence, space).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects the **essential equations** for Module 1 of PEECT862. Memorise units and boundary conditions — KTU valuation penalises missing units heavily.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Wavelength in free space | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s |
| 2 | Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | m | ω = 2πf |
| 3 | AC resistance with skin effect | $R_{ac} = \dfrac{l}{\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \delta}$ | Ω | For solid round wire |
| 4 | Power in dBm | $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$ | dBm | Standard RF power unit |
| 5 | Voltage in dBμV | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{1\,\mu V}\right)$ | dBμV | Used in EMC/EMI |
| 6 | Friis transmission equation | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | W | Free-space path loss included |
| 7 | Path loss in dB | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$ | dB | Free-space |
| 8 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | unitless | $\vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ for passive |
| 9 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | ratio | Always ≥ 1 |
| 10 | Return loss | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB | Positive quantity |
| 11 | Noise figure | $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$ | dB | $F = \dfrac{S_{in}/N_{in}}{S_{out}/N_{out}}$ |
| 12 | Thermal noise power | $N = kTB$ | W | B = bandwidth in Hz |
| 13 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ | Ω | Becomes small at RF |
| 14 | Inductive reactance | $X_L = \omega L$ | Ω | Becomes large at RF |
| 15 | Quality factor of RLC | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | unitless | Critical for filters |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** When expressing **absolute value** of a quantity (e.g., $\vert \Gamma \vert$), always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` — **never** the keyboard pipe symbol `|` — inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table column separator.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The formulas above are not academic exercises. Every modern smartphone contains:
- A **front-end module** with power amplifiers designed using Friis equation and load-pull techniques.
- **SAW / BAW filters** characterised by Q-factors > 1000.
- **Antenna tuners** that minimise $\vert \Gamma \vert$ to maximise power transfer.
- **LNA design** that optimises noise figure (NF) to detect signals as weak as −110 dBm.

The discipline of RF engineering is, quite literally, what makes **wireless civilisation** possible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wavelength from Wave Equation

**Objective:** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the wavelength–frequency relationship $\lambda = c/f$.

**Step 1 — Maxwell's Curl Equation in Free Space (no sources)**

For a plane wave propagating in +x direction in free space ($\rho = 0$, $\vec{J} = 0$):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu_0 \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Curl to the First Equation**

Taking the curl of Faraday's law:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\mu_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$ and noting $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ in source-free region:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu_0 \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the Wave Equation**

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Wave Speed**

Comparing with the standard 1-D wave equation $\dfrac{\partial^2 E}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{v^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 E}{\partial t^2}$:

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = c$$

In free space, this velocity equals the speed of light.

**Step 5 — Relate v, f, λ**

For a sinusoidal wave of frequency *f*, in one period *T* the wave travels exactly one wavelength $\lambda$:

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \lambda f$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{f\,(\text{Hz})}\text{ metres}}$$

**KTU Quick-Reference Result:** At 1 GHz, $\lambda = 30$ cm. At 10 GHz, $\lambda = 3$ cm. At 77 GHz (automotive radar), $\lambda \approx 3.9$ mm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skin Depth in a Good Conductor

**Objective:** Derive the depth $\delta$ at which the current density in a conductor falls to $1/e$ of its surface value.

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Maxwell's Equation Inside a Conductor**

Inside a good conductor with conductivity $\sigma$, the curl of $\vec{H}$ is:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

**Step 2 — Combine with Faraday's Law**

Taking curl of $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3 — Assume Time-Harmonic Fields $e^{j\omega t}$**

Substituting $\partial/\partial t \to j\omega$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j \omega \mu \sigma \, \vec{E}$$

For a wave penetrating the conductor along +z:

$$\frac{\partial^2 E_x}{\partial z^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma \, E_x = \gamma^2 E_x$$

where the complex propagation constant is:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j \omega \mu \sigma} = (1 + j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Decaying Exponential**

The solution is of the form:

$$E_x(z) = E_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \, e^{-j\beta z}$$

with $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$.

**Step 5 — Define Skin Depth**

The skin depth $\delta$ is the distance at which amplitude drops by factor $1/e$:

$$\alpha \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$

**Numerical Insight:** For copper at 1 GHz, $\delta \approx 2.1 \,\mu\text{m}$. At 10 GHz, $\delta \approx 0.66 \,\mu\text{m}$. This is why **plating thickness** is critical in microwave PCBs and why the interior of a thick conductor is "wasted" copper at RF.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)

**Step 1 — Power Density from an Isotropic Radiator**

Total transmitted power $P_t$ spreads uniformly over a sphere of radius *R*:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Area of a Receiving Antenna**

By reciprocity, a lossless antenna of gain $G_r$ has effective aperture:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Received Power**

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = P_t \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Path Loss in dB**

$$PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_r}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight**

Path loss increases by **6 dB** every time the distance *R* doubles, and by **6 dB** every time the frequency *f* doubles. This is the single most important rule of thumb in RF link-budget design.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi transmitter delivers $P_t = +20$ dBm into an isotropic antenna. The receiver uses an antenna of gain $G_r = 6$ dBi. Distance $R = 100$ m. Compute received power $P_r$ in dBm. Transmitter antenna gain $G_t = 0$ dBi.

**Step A — Wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Step B — Free-Space Path Loss (Numerical)**

$$PL = \left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 100}{0.125}\right)^2 = (10053.1)^2 \approx 1.0107 \times 10^8$$

**Step C — Convert to dB**

$$PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.0107 \times 10^8) \approx 80.05 \text{ dB}$$

**Step D — Apply Friis in dB**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL = 20 + 0 + 6 - 80.05$$

$$\boxed{P_r \approx -54.05 \text{ dBm}}$$

This is well above the Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity of about −85 dBm, so the link is viable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic RF Transceiver Chain

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **canonical block-level functional architecture** of a superheterodyne-style RF transceiver, illustrating the conceptual flow from baseband information to radiated electromagnetic wave and back. This is the foundational mental model every KTU student must internalise.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BB1[Baseband Information Source] --> CODEC1[ADC / DAC Codec]
    CODEC1 --> BBF1[Baseband Filter]
    BBF1 --> MIX1[Upconversion Mixer]
    LO1[Local Oscillator Synthesiser] --> MIX1
    MIX1 --> IF1[IF Amplifier and Filter]
    IF1 --> PA[Power Amplifier]
    PA --> TXFIL[Tx Bandpass Filter]
    TXFIL --> ANT1[Antenna Tx]
    ANT1 -. RF Propagation Channel .-> ANT2[Antenna Rx]
    ANT2 --> RXFIL[Rx Bandpass Filter]
    RXFIL --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA --> IF2[IF Amplifier and Filter]
    IF2 --> MIX2[Downconversion Mixer]
    LO2[Local Oscillator Synthesiser] --> MIX2
    MIX2 --> BBF2[Baseband Filter]
    BBF2 --> CODEC2[ADC / DAC Codec]
    CODEC2 --> BB2[Baseband Information Sink]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block chain proceeds from left (information source) to right (radiation into channel), with the receiver mirror image following the wireless link. The **upconversion mixer** translates baseband to RF; the **LNA** (first active stage in the receive chain) dominates the receiver noise figure; the **PA** determines transmitter efficiency and linearity.

## 4.2 Frequency Spectrum Allocation Map

The diagram below shows the principal commercial, scientific, and military allocations in the RF / microwave region, contextualising **why** RF design is indispensable.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Electromagnetic Spectrum 3 kHz to 300 GHz] --> SP1
    SP1[3 kHz to 30 kHz VLF Submarine Comm] --> SP2
    SP2[30 kHz to 3 MHz LF MF AM Radio Maritime] --> SP3
    SP3[3 MHz to 30 MHz HF Shortwave Broadcasting] --> SP4
    SP4[30 MHz to 300 MHz VHF FM TV Aircraft] --> SP5
    SP5[300 MHz to 3 GHz UHF Mobile WiFi GPS] --> SP6
    SP6[3 GHz to 30 GHz SHF Microwave Radar Satellite 5G] --> SP7
    SP7[30 GHz to 300 GHz EHF mmWave 5G mmW 6G Auto Radar]
```

## 4.3 Why Each Block Matters — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What does the LNA do] --> A1[Minimises Noise Figure NF]
    Q2[What does the PA do] --> A2[Maximises Output Power and Efficiency]
    Q3[What does the Mixer do] --> A4[Translates Frequency Up or Down]
    Q4[What does the Filter do] --> A3[Rejects Out of Band Interferers]
    Q5[What does the Antenna do] --> A5[Converts Electrical to EM Wave and back]
    Q6[What does the LO do] --> A6[Provides Coherent Frequency Reference]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Schematic Note:** When asked to "draw and explain the block diagram of a radio receiver" or "transmitter," the diagram above in its essential form (without the coding details) is the model answer expected. Be sure to label the function of *each* block in one sentence.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CH1[Skin Effect] --> RL1[Increased Conductor Loss]
    CH2[Parasitic Reactance] --> RL2[Resonance Shifts]
    CH3[Distributed Behaviour] --> RL3[Transmission Line Effects]
    CH4[Radiation] --> RL4[Unintended EMI Crosstalk]
    CH5[Component Tolerances] --> RL5[Frequency Drift]
    RL1 --> SOL[Solution Proper RF PCB Materials and Plating]
    RL2 --> SOL
    RL3 --> SOL
    RL4 --> SOL
    RL5 --> SOL
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Radio Frequency. Mention any three applications of RF engineering in modern wireless systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Radio Frequency (RF)** refers to the portion of the electromagnetic spectrum lying between **3 kHz and 300 GHz**, used for wireless transmission of information through electromagnetic waves.

**Three applications:**
1. **Mobile cellular communications** – 4G/5G handsets operate in the 700 MHz – 6 GHz band, with emerging 5G mmWave at 24–40 GHz.
2. **Satellite communication** – Ku band (12–18 GHz) for DTH TV and Ka band (26.5–40 GHz) for broadband internet.
3. **Radar systems** – Air-traffic control at 2.7–2.9 GHz, weather radar at 5.6–9.4 GHz, automotive radar at 76–81 GHz.

(3 marks awarded for: correct definition with numerical range + 3 well-chosen applications. Definition alone = 1 mark; 3 applications = 2 marks.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is skin effect? Write the expression for skin depth and explain its significance at microwave frequencies.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Skin effect** is the tendency of alternating current to concentrate near the surface of a conductor as frequency increases, leaving the interior largely unused.

**Expression:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Significance at microwave frequencies:**
- At 10 GHz, $\delta$ in copper is ≈ 0.66 µm — current flows in an extremely thin outer shell.
- This **increases the effective AC resistance** of conductors, raising ohmic loss.
- Mitigations: use **plated silver or gold** surfaces, **wide and thin** microstrip traces, or **hollow waveguides** instead of solid wires.

(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error in 14-mark answers is writing a long essay without **sub-part structure**. Always answer part (a) and part (b) **separately with clear headings**, and **underline / box the final numerical answers**. Showing intermediate steps earns partial credit even if the final value is wrong.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the free-space path loss expression starting from the concept of an isotropic radiator. Define the term EIRP and explain its role in link-budget design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5.8 GHz WLAN link has $P_t = +23$ dBm, $G_t = 18$ dBi (parabolic dish), $G_r = 12$ dBi, and $R = 5$ km. Calculate: (i) Wavelength, (ii) Free-space path loss in dB, (iii) Received power in dBm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Isotropic Radiator Definition:** A hypothetical antenna radiating equally in all directions with gain $G_t = 1$ (0 dBi).

**Step 2 — Power Density at Distance R:**

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Aperture of Receive Antenna:**

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 4 — Received Power:**

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**

$$FSPL = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 6 — EIRP Definition:**

$$EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_{feed} \quad \text{(in dBm or dBW)}$$

EIRP bundles transmit power and antenna gain into a single regulatory metric. It is the **effective isotropic radiated power** and is limited by spectrum regulators (e.g., WPC in India, FCC in USA) to prevent interference.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 5.172 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 5.17 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Free-Space Path Loss**

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \pi \times 5000}{0.0517}\right)$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}(1.215 \times 10^6) = 20 \times 6.0846 = \boxed{121.69 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) Received Power**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL = 23 + 18 + 12 - 121.69$$

$$\boxed{P_r = -68.69 \text{ dBm}}$$

This is well above the typical 5.8 GHz receiver threshold of −82 dBm for 54 Mbps OFDM, confirming a viable 5 km link.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** What is the significance of radio frequency design? Discuss any **five** challenges encountered while designing RF circuits as compared to low-frequency analog circuits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a superheterodyne RF receiver. Identify the function of **each** block in one line. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Significance of RF Design:**
- RF design underpins **all modern wireless systems**: mobile phones, Wi-Fi, satellite, radar, GPS, IoT, defence.
- Spectrum is a **finite, regulated resource**; efficient design enables more users in less bandwidth.
- Drives innovation in materials (LTCC, GaN, GaAs), packaging (SiP, AiP), and measurement (VNA, spectrum analyser).

**Five Challenges:**

| # | Challenge | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Skin effect** | AC resistance rises with $\sqrt{f}$, increasing loss in conductors. |
| 2 | **Parasitic reactances** | A 1 nH lead at 5 GHz has reactance ≈ 31 Ω, comparable to source impedance. |
| 3 | **Distributed effects** | When trace length ≥ λ/10, lumped model fails; transmission-line theory needed. |
| 4 | **Impedance matching** | Mismatch causes reflections; need matching networks (L, π, T, stub). |
| 5 | **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)** | Traces radiate and couple; needs shielding, grounding, controlled-impedance layout. |

(Each well-explained point: 1 mark. Total = 5 marks. Introduction + significance = 2 marks.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Block Diagram of Superheterodyne Receiver:**

```
Antenna → RF Bandpass Filter → LNA → Image-Reject Filter
   → First Mixer ← Local Oscillator (LO1)
   → IF Amplifier + IF Bandpass Filter
   → Second Mixer ← Local Oscillator (LO2)
   → Baseband Amplifier → Demodulator → Output
```

**Function of Each Block (1-line each, 7 lines for 7 marks):**

1. **Antenna:** Captures the modulated RF signal from free space.
2. **RF Bandpass Filter:** Selects the desired band; rejects out-of-band interferers.
3. **LNA (Low Noise Amplifier):** Amplifies weak signal with minimum added noise.
4. **Image-Reject Filter:** Suppresses the image frequency that would otherwise downconvert onto the IF.
5. **First Mixer + LO1:** Translates RF down to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF).
6. **IF Amplifier + Filter:** Provides bulk of gain and adjacent-channel selectivity at a fixed, low frequency.
7. **Second Mixer + LO2 + Demodulator:** Converts IF to baseband and recovers the original information.

(1 mark per labelled block + 1 mark for the neat overall diagram. Total = 7 marks.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – RF Circuit Introduction):**

- ☐ **RF band** is **3 kHz – 300 GHz**; corresponding **wavelength range** is 100 km – 1 mm.
- ☐ **Wavelength formula** $\lambda = c/f$ — at 1 GHz, $\lambda = 30$ cm. **Memorise** this.
- ☐ **Skin depth** $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ — for copper at 1 GHz, $\delta \approx 2.1 \,\mu$m.
- ☐ **dBm** is the standard RF power unit: $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(P/1\text{mW})$.
- ☐ **Friis equation** in dB: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$, where $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi R/\lambda)$.
- ☐ **Reflection coefficient** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$; magnitude $\le 1$ for passive loads.
- ☐ **VSWR** $\ge 1$; **Return Loss** $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ (in dB).
- ☐ **Noise figure** $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$; **Thermal noise** $N = kTB$.
- ☐ **Reactances:** $X_L = \omega L$ *increases* with frequency; $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ *decreases* with frequency.
- ☐ **Distributed vs. lumped** boundary: trace length $l \ge \lambda/10$ ⇒ use transmission line theory.
- ☐ **Five RF challenges:** skin effect, parasitic reactance, distributed behaviour, EMI/radiation, impedance matching.
- ☐ **Five key applications:** mobile (4G/5G/6G), Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, satellite (Ku/Ka), radar, MRI/medical.
- ☐ **Generic RF chain:** Antenna → Filter → LNA → Mixer → IF → Demod (and the mirror Tx chain ending at PA → Filter → Antenna).
- ☐ **Spectrum is finite** and regulated by **ITU-R / WPC** — always consider regulatory EIRP limits in design.
- ☐ **Path loss grows 6 dB per doubling of R and 6 dB per doubling of f** — essential link-budget rule of thumb.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Warning:**
> - Always quote **units** (dBm, GHz, m) — losing units typically costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.
> - In derivations, **show every algebraic step** — partial credit is awarded for the journey, not just the destination.
> - For block diagrams, **label every block** with its function in one sentence; an unlabelled block = 0 marks.
> - When asked to "explain the importance," tie the answer to **real applications** (5G, satellite, IoT) — generic "RF is used in communication" answers fetch 1 mark only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RF Behaviour of Resistors, Inductors and Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** RF Behaviour of Resistors, Inductors and Capacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

At **Radio Frequencies (RF)**, the physical construction of passive lumped components—**resistors (R)**, **inductors (L)**, and **capacitors (C)**—introduces unavoidable parasitic elements that fundamentally alter their ideal low-frequency behaviour. The **RF behaviour of R, L, C** refers to the frequency-dependent response of these components when their **parasitic reactances (lead inductance, stray/shunt capacitance, skin effect, proximity effect, and dielectric loss)** become comparable in magnitude to the intended component reactance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, every lumped element at RF is modeled as an **equivalent circuit** containing the *desired* element plus *parasitic* R, L, and C. The **operational region** of any component is bounded by its **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)**—above which, for instance, an inductor behaves as a capacitor and a capacitor behaves as an inductor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> *"RF behaviour of Resistors, Capacitors and Inductors – RF equivalent circuit, Q factor and losses, skin effect, proximity effect, self-resonant frequency and dielectric losses."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine three musicians asked to play only a **single pure note** (DC/low frequency). Each does so perfectly:
- The **Resistor** is a *drunk drummer* — at low frequency he hits the drum at a steady rate (dissipating energy uniformly), but at RF he also has an **invisible drumstick length** (lead inductance) and a **floppy drum surface** (shunt capacitance) that make his hits erratic.
- The **Capacitor** is a *springy trampoline* — it stores charge. As frequency rises, the wires holding the trampoline act like a **coiled spring** (Equivalent Series Inductance, **ESL**) that fights the bounce, and the trampoline material has **friction** (Equivalent Series Resistance, **ESR**).
- The **Inductor** is a *heavy swinging pendulum* — it stores magnetic energy. At high frequency, the turns of the coil act like a **hidden parallel capacitor** (self-capacitance, **C_p**) and the wire has **resistance** (R_s) plus an **uneven current crowding** (skin effect) that changes the resistance with frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *component value printed on the body* (e.g., "10 nF" or "10 μH") is only meaningful up to its **self-resonant frequency**. Beyond SRF, the parasitic element *dominates* and the component flips its electrical identity.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used

- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Velocity of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Conductivity of copper: **σ_Cu ≈ 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m**
- Reference frequency: **f₀ = 1 GHz = 10⁹ Hz** (commonly used for RF normalization in KTU problems)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RF Behaviour of a Resistor

A real resistor at RF exhibits three parasitic mechanisms:

1. **Lead Inductance (L_lead):** The metallic leads and terminations form a small series inductance (≈ 1 nH/mm for a straight wire). This is **non-negligible above ~100 MHz** for through-hole resistors.
2. **Stray/Shunt Capacitance (C_stray):** The resistive element and its terminations form a small parallel capacitance (≈ 0.1–0.5 pF).
3. **Skin Effect (δ):** At high frequency, current crowds near the conductor surface, reducing the effective cross-sectional area and **increasing the AC resistance (R_AC)** above the DC value R_DC.

### Logical Steps for Resistor RF Analysis
- **Why:** A real resistor is a physical body, not a pure mathematical entity. Its geometry inherently creates both magnetic (inductive) and electric (capacitive) field energy storage.
- **How:** At low frequency, the impedance is ≈ R_DC. As f increases:
  - The inductive reactance **jωL_lead** rises linearly with f.
  - The capacitive susceptance **ωC_stray** rises linearly with f.
  - The skin depth shrinks, raising R_AC.
- **Net Effect:** Z_R(f) = R_AC(f) + jωL_lead − j/(ωC_stray) → Resistor becomes **inductive** at very high f.

## 2.2 RF Behaviour of a Capacitor

A real capacitor at RF is modeled as the ideal C in series with:
- **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR):** models dielectric loss + lead/foil ohmic loss.
- **Equivalent Series Inductance (ESL):** models lead + foil + tab inductance.

### Logical Steps for Capacitor RF Analysis
- **Why:** The current path through the capacitor's leads/foils always has finite length, creating an unavoidable series inductance.
- **How:** Below the **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)**, the capacitor looks capacitive (X_C dominates). At SRF, jωESL cancels 1/(jωC), and the impedance is **purely ESR** (minimum Z). Above SRF, the inductive reactance dominates, and the device behaves as an **inductor**.

## 2.3 RF Behaviour of an Inductor

A real inductor at RF is modeled as the ideal L in series with R_s (wire + core loss) and in parallel with **self-capacitance C_p** (turn-to-turn + layer-to-layer capacitance).

### Logical Steps for Inductor RF Analysis
- **Why:** A coil is constructed from multiple turns separated by insulation; the voltage difference between turns creates a distributed capacitance. The wire has finite resistance, and at high f, the **skin effect** (and in multi-layer coils, the **proximity effect**) increases R_s.
- **How:** Below SRF, the inductor looks inductive. At SRF, the parallel LC resonates and impedance is maximum (ideally infinite). Above SRF, the device behaves as a **capacitor**.

## 2.4 Quality Factor (Q)

The **Q factor** is the figure of merit for RF components. Higher Q ⇒ lower loss.

- **Inductor Q:** Q_L = ωL / R_s = ratio of stored magnetic energy to dissipated energy per cycle.
- **Capacitor Q:** Q_C = 1 / (ωC · ESR) = X_C / ESR.
- **Resistor Q:** Q_R (often used in attenuator design) is typically very low (≪ 1).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Skin depth | δ = 1 / √(π f μ σ) | m | f = frequency, μ = permeability, σ = conductivity |
| 2 | AC resistance ratio | R_AC / R_DC ≈ (π d) / (4 δ) for round wire | dimensionless | Valid when d >> δ |
| 3 | Resistor RF impedance | Z_R = R_AC + jωL_lead − j/(ωC_stray) | Ω | Series-parallel combination |
| 4 | Capacitor RF impedance | Z_C = ESR + jωESL − j/(ωC) | Ω | Series model |
| 5 | Inductor RF impedance (below SRF) | Z_L = R_s + jωL | Ω | Dominant inductive region |
| 6 | Inductor SRF | f_SR,L = 1 / (2π √(L C_p)) | Hz | Above this → capacitor-like |
| 7 | Capacitor SRF | f_SR,C = 1 / (2π √(ESL · C)) | Hz | Above this → inductor-like |
| 8 | Inductor Q | Q_L = ωL / R_s | dimensionless | Series model |
| 9 | Capacitor Q | Q_C = 1 / (ωC · ESR) | dimensionless | Series model |
| 10 | Reactance cancellation at SRF | ωSRF · L = 1 / (ωSRF · C) | – | Defines resonance condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are critical in designing **matching networks, VCO tank circuits, RF filters, bias tees, and EMC/EMI suppression networks**. Accurate parasitic-aware modeling is what separates a working RF PCB from a non-functional prototype.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Skin Depth (δ) in a Good Conductor

Starting from Maxwell's equations inside a conductor, the time-harmonic fields decay exponentially from the surface. The decay constant α is:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

The **skin depth** is defined as the distance at which the field amplitude drops to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

Substituting ω = 2πf:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2 \pi f \, \mu \, \sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \, \mu \, \sigma}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor 2/ω = 2/(2πf) = 1/(πf) under the square root yields the simplified form. This is the *gold-standard* form used in KTU derivations.

### Worked Example: Skin Depth in Copper at 1 GHz
Using σ = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m, μ ≈ μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m, f = 10⁹ Hz:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^{9} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}}}$$

Compute the product inside the square root:

$$\pi \times 10^{9} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7} = 4\pi^{2} \times 5.8 \times 10^{9}$$

$$= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 5.8 \times 10^{9} \approx 2.290 \times 10^{11}$$

So:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.290 \times 10^{11}}} = \frac{1}{4.785 \times 10^{5}} \approx 2.09 \, \mu m$$

This shows that at 1 GHz, current in copper flows in a *shell only ~2 μm thick*, drastically increasing AC resistance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: AC-to-DC Resistance Ratio of a Round Wire

For a round wire of diameter d (radius a) carrying uniform current density, R_DC = l / (σ π a²). With skin effect, current flows only in an annular ring of thickness δ near the surface, so:

$$R_{AC} = \frac{l}{\sigma \, \pi a \cdot \delta} \quad \text{(valid when } \delta \ll a\text{)}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{R_{AC}}{R_{DC}} = \frac{l / (\sigma \pi a \delta)}{l / (\sigma \pi a^{2})} = \frac{a}{\delta} = \frac{d}{2\delta}$$

A more accurate textbook (and KTU-exam-accepted) form for a solid round wire is:

$$\frac{R_{AC}}{R_{DC}} = \frac{a}{2\delta} = \frac{d}{4\delta} \cdot 2 = \frac{\pi d}{4\delta} \quad \text{(approximate closed form)}$$

**Conversion logic:** This shows that R_AC grows as √f (since δ ∝ 1/√f), making wire loss a major concern in RF coil design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Self-Resonant Frequency of an Inductor

Real inductor equivalent: L in series with R_s, in parallel with C_p (self-capacitance). Input impedance:

$$Z_{L,RF} = \frac{(R_s + j\omega L) \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_p}}{R_s + j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_p}}$$

At resonance, Im{Z} = 0. Assuming Q is high, R_s is negligible, so:

$$Z_{L,RF} \approx \frac{j\omega L \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_p}}{j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_p}} = \frac{L / C_p}{j(\omega L - 1/(\omega C_p))}$$

Setting the denominator imaginary part to zero (resonance condition):

$$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C_p} \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{SR}^{2} = \frac{1}{L C_p} \;\Rightarrow\; f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_p}}$$

**Conversion logic:** At f_SR, the idealised input impedance → ∞ (open circuit), so the device *stops behaving as an inductor* and starts behaving as a capacitor above this frequency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Quality Factor of a Practical Inductor

Energy stored (peak magnetic) in inductor: W_stored = (1/2) L I_peak²
Average power dissipated in R_s: P_loss = (1/2) I_peak² R_s

By definition:

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi \cdot W_{stored}}{W_{loss,per \, cycle}} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} L I_{peak}^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2} I_{peak}^{2} R_s \cdot T} = \frac{2 \pi f L}{R_s} = \frac{\omega L}{R_s}$$

This is the **series-resistance Q**, the most common KTU-tested form.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Quality Factor of a Practical Capacitor

Capacitor stores: W_stored = (1/2) C V_peak²
Power dissipated in ESR: P_loss = (1/2) I_peak² · ESR, with I_peak = ωC V_peak.

So:

$$P_{loss} = \tfrac{1}{2} (\omega C V_{peak})^{2} \cdot ESR = \tfrac{1}{2} \omega^{2} C^{2} V_{peak}^{2} \cdot ESR$$

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi f \cdot W_{stored}}{P_{loss}} = \frac{2 \pi f \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{peak}^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \omega^{2} C^{2} V_{peak}^{2} \cdot ESR} = \frac{1}{\omega C \cdot ESR}$$

Thus **Q_C = 1 / (ωC · ESR)**. Higher Q_C ⇒ better capacitor for RF filtering and VCO tanks.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Resistor (R) | Capacitor (C) | Inductor (L) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|--------------|
| Ideal element value | R | C | L |
| Main RF parasitic | L_lead + C_stray | ESL + ESR | R_s + C_p |
| Equivalent model at RF | R ∥ C_stray, in series with L_lead | ESR + ESL in series with C | R_s + L in series, in parallel with C_p |
| Behaviour **below** SRF | Mostly resistive (R dominates) | Capacitive (X_C dominates) | Inductive (X_L dominates) |
| Behaviour **above** SRF | Inductive (L_lead dominates) | Inductive (ESL dominates) | Capacitive (C_p dominates) |
| Q factor formula | Q_R ≈ 0 (low) | Q_C = 1 / (ωC·ESR) | Q_L = ωL / R_s |
| Typical SRF range | Not a discrete SRF; 3 dB roll-off | 100 MHz – 10 GHz | 10 MHz – 1 GHz |
| Loss mechanism | Skin effect, lead loss | Dielectric loss, ESR | Wire (skin) + core loss |
| KTU design focus | Attenuators, terminations | Bypass, coupling, tank | Matching, chokes, tank |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: RF Equivalent Circuits of R, L, C

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Resistor_RF["RESISTOR RF EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
        R1["R_AC with skin effect"] --- L1["L_lead series inductance"]
        L1 --- node1["node A"]
        node1 --- C1["C_stray shunt capacitance"]
        C1 --- node2["node B"]
        node1 --- node2
    end

    subgraph Capacitor_RF["CAPACITOR RF EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
        C2["Ideal C"] --- ESL1["ESL"]
        ESL1 --- ESR1["ESR"]
        ESR1 --- capNodeA["Terminal A"]
        capNodeA --- capNodeB["Terminal B"]
        C2 --- capNodeA
    end

    subgraph Inductor_RF["INDUCTOR RF EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
        L2["Ideal L"] --- RS1["R_s series resistance"]
        RS1 --- indNodeA["Terminal A"]
        indNodeA --- indNodeB["Terminal B"]
        Cp1["C_p self-capacitance shunt"]
        Cp1 --- indNodeA
        Cp1 --- indNodeB
    end

    subgraph FrequencyResponse["FREQUENCY DOMAIN BEHAVIOUR"]
        fAxis["Frequency f increases left to right"]
        fAxis --- Rregion["R: Resistive then Inductive above f_knee"]
        fAxis --- Cregion["C: Capacitive below SRF_C then Inductive above"]
        fAxis --- Lregion["L: Inductive below SRF_L then Capacitive above"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Component Behaviour as a Function of Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowFreq["LOW FREQUENCY REGION DC TO 100 KHZ"]
        LFa["Resistor: Behaves as R_DC"]
        LFb["Capacitor: Ideal C X_C large"]
        LFc["Inductor: Ideal L X_L small"]
    end

    subgraph MidFreq["MID FREQUENCY REGION 1 MHZ TO 100 MHZ"]
        MFa["Resistor: R_AC increases with sqrt f"]
        MFb["Capacitor: C dominant ESL negligible"]
        MFc["Inductor: L dominant C_p negligible"]
    end

    subgraph HighFreq["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION ABOVE 100 MHZ"]
        HFa["Resistor: L_lead dominates Inductive"]
        HFb["Capacitor: Approaching SRF_C then Inductive above"]
        HFc["Inductor: Approaching SRF_L then Capacitive above"]
    end

    LowFreq --> MidFreq --> HighFreq
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Parasitic Sources in Real Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceParasitics["SOURCES OF PARASITICS IN REAL COMPONENTS"]
        SP1["Lead Geometry contributes L_lead and C_stray"]
        SP2["Wire Resistivity contributes R_DC"]
        SP3["Skin Effect increases R_AC with frequency"]
        SP4["Proximity Effect further increases R_AC in coils"]
        SP5["Dielectric Loss contributes to ESR and loss tangent"]
        SP6["Turn to Turn Voltage in coils contributes C_p"]
        SP1 --> spOut["Net RF Equivalent Circuit"]
        SP2 --> spOut
        SP3 --> spOut
        SP4 --> spOut
        SP5 --> spOut
        SP6 --> spOut
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**Define Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF). Why is the SRF critical when selecting an inductor for an RF matching network?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)** of a component is the frequency at which the *desired reactance* of the component is exactly cancelled by its *intrinsic parasitic reactance*, so that the net impedance becomes purely resistive (capacitor) or maximally resistive (inductor).

$$f_{SR,L} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_p}} \quad \text{(inductor)} \qquad f_{SR,C} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ESL \cdot C}} \quad \text{(capacitor)}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** An RF matching network requires the inductor to behave *purely inductively*. Operating an inductor well below its SRF (typically ≤ 0.3 f_SR) guarantees inductive behaviour and predictable impedance, ensuring correct matching and avoiding spurious resonances. **(1 mark)**, with formula and definition earning the remaining 1 mark each.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**State the formula for skin depth and explain why it is important at RF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Skin depth is the depth in a conductor at which the current density falls to **1/e** of its surface value.

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} \quad \text{(metres)}$$

It is important at RF because **(i)** δ shrinks as √f, so the *effective conducting cross-section* shrinks, raising AC resistance; **(ii)** this causes **higher copper loss** in inductors, transmission lines, and PCB traces; **(iii)** it forces designers to use **litz wire, wider traces, or plated-through vias** to mitigate losses. (3 marks — 1 each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern: Module-Level Internal Choice. Answer ANY ONE of the following.]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **skin depth (δ)** in a good conductor, starting from the time-harmonic form of Maxwell's equations. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A copper wire (σ = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m, μ = μ₀) of diameter 1 mm carries signal at 1 GHz. Compute: **(i)** skin depth, **(ii)** AC-to-DC resistance ratio, **(iii)** Q factor of a 100 nH coil wound with 1 m of this wire (assume wire is straight, R_DC = l/(σA)). **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
Inside a good conductor, **J = σE** and Faraday's law gives ∇×E = −jωμH. Combining with Ampere's law (displacement current neglected) ∇×H = σE:

$$\nabla \times \nabla \times H = -j\omega\mu\sigma H \;\Rightarrow\; \nabla^{2}H = j\omega\mu\sigma H$$

For a plane wave penetrating in the +z direction, only the y-component of H is non-trivial, giving:

$$\frac{d^{2}H_y}{dz^{2}} = j\omega\mu\sigma H_y = \gamma^{2} H_y$$

The propagation constant is $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ (since $\sqrt{j} = (1+j)/\sqrt{2}$). The field solution is:

$$H_y(z) = H_0 \, e^{-\gamma z}$$

The real part of γ gives the decay constant:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

By definition, $\delta = 1/\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**(i) Skin depth at f = 1 GHz, σ = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m, μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^{9} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.29 \times 10^{11}}} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 2.09 \, \mu m$$

**(ii) AC-to-DC resistance ratio for d = 1 mm:**

$$\frac{R_{AC}}{R_{DC}} = \frac{d}{4\delta} \cdot \text{(closed-form approx)} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 2.09 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{8.36 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 119.6$$

So **R_AC ≈ 120 × R_DC** at 1 GHz — losses are massive.

**(iii) Q factor of a 100 nH coil with l = 1 m of this wire:**
- Wire cross-section A = π(d/2)² = π(0.5 × 10⁻³)² = 7.854 × 10⁻⁷ m²
- R_DC = l/(σA) = 1 / (5.8 × 10⁷ × 7.854 × 10⁻⁷) = 1 / 45.55 ≈ 0.02196 Ω
- R_AC = 119.6 × R_DC ≈ 2.626 Ω
- Q = ωL/R_s = (2π × 10⁹ × 100 × 10⁻⁹) / 2.626 = (628.3) / 2.626 ≈ **239**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a **circuit diagram**, explain the **RF equivalent circuit of a practical capacitor**. Define ESR and ESL and obtain the expression for its **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)**. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A 47 pF NP0 ceramic capacitor has ESL = 2 nH and ESR = 0.05 Ω. Compute: **(i)** its SRF, **(ii)** its Q at 500 MHz, **(iii)** its effective impedance magnitude at 800 MHz. Comment on its suitability as a **bypass capacitor at 2.4 GHz**. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
**RF Equivalent Circuit:** Ideal C in series with **ESL** (lead/foil inductance) and **ESR** (loss resistance: dielectric + ohmic). The terminals are the physical leads of the package. Diagram (textual):

```
  o---[ESR]---[ESL]---+---[|| C ideal]---+---o
                                            |
                       (terminal nodes)
```

- **ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance):** Represents the lumped effect of dielectric loss (loss tangent tan δ) and lead/foil ohmic loss. **(1 mark)**
- **ESL (Equivalent Series Inductance):** Represents the parasitic inductance of leads, foils, and internal tabs. **(1 mark)**
- **SRF Derivation:** Impedance of series RLC:

$$Z_C = ESR + j\omega ESL + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = ESR + j\left(\omega ESL - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

At SRF, Im{Z_C} = 0:

$$\omega_{SR} \cdot ESL = \frac{1}{\omega_{SR} C} \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{SR}^{2} = \frac{1}{ESL \cdot C}$$

$$\boxed{f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ESL \cdot C}}}$$

At f_SR, |Z_C| = ESR (minimum). Above f_SR, the capacitor looks inductive. **(5 marks distributed across definition + derivation + diagram)**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**(i) SRF:**

$$f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-9} \times 47 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{9.4 \times 10^{-20}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.066 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1}{1.926 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.19 \times 10^{8} \, Hz \approx 519 \, MHz$$

**(ii) Q at 500 MHz:**

$$Q_C = \frac{1}{\omega C \cdot ESR} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{8} \times 47 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.05}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{8} \times 2.35 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{7.38 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 135.5$$

**(iii) |Z_C| at 800 MHz:**

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 8 \times 10^{8} = 5.027 \times 10^{9} \, rad/s$$

$$\omega ESL = 5.027 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times 10^{-9} = 10.05 \, \Omega$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{5.027 \times 10^{9} \times 47 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{0.2363} \approx 4.232 \, \Omega$$

$$Z_C = 0.05 + j(10.05 - 4.232) = 0.05 + j5.818 \, \Omega$$

$$|Z_C| = \sqrt{0.05^{2} + 5.818^{2}} \approx 5.82 \, \Omega$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since f_SR ≈ 519 MHz, this capacitor is **inductive at 2.4 GHz** and therefore **unsuitable as a 2.4 GHz bypass capacitor**. A *smaller* value capacitor (e.g., 10 pF or 1 pF) with much higher SRF must be used for bypass at 2.4 GHz.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up series and parallel models** for Q factor — the formula Q = ωL/R_s applies to the *series-R* inductor model, while for the *parallel-R* model the formula is Q = R_p / (ωL). Mark deduction of 1–2 marks for using the wrong one.
> 2. **Forgetting to use μ₀ for non-magnetic conductors** in skin depth problems. A common error is omitting μ.
> 3. **Stating SRF formula without the 2π factor** — students often write f = 1/√(LC), missing the 2π → 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Not commenting on inductive/capacitive transition** above/below SRF — this is a *favourite 2-mark KTU follow-up* in Part B.
> 5. **Wrong units in skin depth** (writing mm instead of m) — 1 mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF):** Operating frequency at which parasitic reactance cancels the intended reactance. Always operate **well below** the SRF for the desired behaviour.
- **Skin Effect:** Current crowds within depth δ = 1/√(πfμσ) of the conductor surface, raising R_AC as √f.
- **Resistor RF behaviour:** Impedance = R_AC + jωL_lead − j/(ωC_stray). Becomes **inductive** at very high frequency.
- **Capacitor RF behaviour:** Modeled as C in series with **ESL** and **ESR**. SRF = 1/(2π√(ESL·C)). Becomes **inductive** above SRF.
- **Inductor RF behaviour:** Modeled as L in series with R_s, in parallel with C_p. SRF = 1/(2π√(L·C_p)). Becomes **capacitive** above SRF.
- **Q factor formulas:** Q_L = ωL/R_s (inductor), Q_C = 1/(ωC·ESR) (capacitor). Higher Q = lower loss.
- **Proximity effect:** In multi-turn coils, neighbouring turns further crowd current, *increasing R_AC* beyond the skin-effect prediction.
- **Design rules:** Use air-core or low-loss ceramic-core inductors at VHF/UHF; use NP0/C0G ceramic or film capacitors for RF; keep leads short; use wide, thick, plated PCB traces to reduce skin-effect loss.
- **Rule of thumb (KTU favourite):** Operate inductors at f ≤ 0.3·f_SR and capacitors at f ≤ 0.3·f_SR for predictable behaviour.
- **Litz wire, plating, and broadside traces** are practical techniques to combat skin/proximity effect.
- **Dielectric loss tangent (tan δ)** is what contributes to ESR in capacitors — a high tan δ material (e.g., X7R) is unsuitable for narrowband RF tanks; NP0/C0G is preferred.
- **Practical SRF measurements** on the VNA (e.g., looking for the dip in |Z|) are a common RF lab exercise in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planar Transmission Lines - Microstrip Lines and Striplines - Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planar Transmission Lines - Microstrip Lines and Striplines - Constructional Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Planar Transmission Lines — The Big Picture

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Planar Transmission Line** is a class of electromagnetic wave-guiding structures in which the conductor geometry is fabricated as a thin, flat layer (or multiple layers) on a planar substrate using printed circuit board (PCB) or monolithic microwave integrated circuit (MMIC) technology. Unlike traditional waveguides, planar lines support **quasi-TEM** or **TEM wave propagation** along flat, layered geometries and are the foundational building blocks of modern RF and Microwave Integrated Circuits (MICs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Planar transmission lines are the *only* practical means of integrating passive and active RF components (filters, couplers, antennas, amplifiers) onto a single dielectric substrate. They replaced bulky waveguides above approximately **1 GHz**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" of RF Signals

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a flat, printed ribbon (the **microstrip**) sitting on a plastic board (the **dielectric substrate**), with a large metal ground plane below it. When you push water into a garden hose, the water flows inside the tube. Similarly, when an RF signal (an alternating electromagnetic wave at high frequency, typically **30 MHz to 100 GHz**) is applied between the top strip and the ground plane, the electromagnetic energy is confined and guided along the length of the structure — much like water confined inside a hose.

The two most widely used planar transmission lines in RF Engineering are **Microstrip Lines** and **Striplines**. Both are essentially "printed" circuits, but their geometry and field confinement differ significantly.

---

## 1.3 Microstrip Line — Formal Definition

A **Microstrip Line** is an open-boundary planar transmission line consisting of a single thin metallic strip of width **W** and thickness **t**, printed on the top surface of a grounded dielectric substrate of height **h** and relative permittivity **ε_r**.

**Constructional Features (KTU Board-Examiner Focus):**

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Top Conductor (Strip)** | Thin metallic (usually **copper**, thickness **t ≈ 17 µm – 70 µm**) strip of width **W** |
| **Substrate** | Dielectric board of height **h** (typically **0.1 mm – 3 mm**) with relative permittivity **ε_r** (commonly **2.2 to 10.8**) |
| **Ground Plane** | Continuous metal sheet on the bottom surface of the substrate |
| **Boundary** | Top boundary is **open to free air (ε_r = 1)**; bottom boundary is bounded by the ground plane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Micro" and "Strip"?**
> The word *micro* refers to the micrometre-scale thickness typical in microwave frequencies, and *strip* refers to the flat rectangular conducting strip. Together they form a **two-conductor** system supporting a **quasi-TEM mode** of propagation.

---

## 1.4 Stripline — Formal Definition

A **Stripline** (also called **triplate line**) is a shielded planar transmission line in which a flat metallic strip of width **W** and thickness **t** is embedded *symmetrically* between two parallel ground planes, with dielectric material of relative permittivity **ε_r** filling the entire space between the ground planes (total height **2b** or equivalently **b** above and below the strip).

**Constructional Features (KTU Board-Examiner Focus):**

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Central Conductor (Strip)** | Thin metallic strip of width **W**, thickness **t**, centered between the two ground planes |
| **Dielectric Filler** | Homogeneous dielectric material with relative permittivity **ε_r** completely fills the region |
| **Two Ground Planes** | Two parallel metal plates (top and bottom) enclose the strip |
| **Boundary** | **Fully shielded** — no open boundary; fields are completely contained |

---

## 1.5 Why These Two Lines Matter in RF Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Microstrip** is preferred when **accessibility** to the top conductor is needed (for mounting active devices, stubs, or lumped elements).
> - **Stripline** is preferred when **shielding, lower radiation loss, and pure TEM operation** are required (e.g., high-Q filters, sensitive RF front-ends).
> - Both are fabricated using **photolithographic processes**, making them cheap, lightweight, and reproducible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How Microstrip Conducts EM Waves

The microstrip line operates on the principle of a **two-conductor transmission system**. When an RF signal is launched at one end:

1. An **alternating electric field (E-field)** is established between the top strip and the bottom ground plane.
2. A **magnetic field (H-field)** circulates around the strip.
3. Because the dielectric is **inhomogeneous** (substrate below + air above), the wave propagates in a **quasi-TEM mode** (not pure TEM, because phase velocity would otherwise depend on the medium).
4. The effective phase velocity lies between the velocity in free space and that in the dielectric.

### 2.2 Microstrip — Field Distribution Logic

- **Electric Field Lines:** Originate on the top strip and terminate on the ground plane. Some lines pass through the **substrate** (high ε_r region) and some pass through the **air** (low ε_r = 1 region) → this **field splitting** is the *root cause* of the quasi-TEM nature.
- **Magnetic Field Lines:** Form closed loops around the strip, residing mostly under the strip.
- **Phase Velocity:** $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$, where **ε_re (effective dielectric constant)** is a weighted average between 1 and ε_r.

### 2.3 Stripline — Field Distribution Logic

- **Electric Field Lines:** Purely **vertical** between the strip and the two ground planes (no air region).
- **Magnetic Field Lines:** Purely **horizontal** loops around the strip.
- Because the dielectric is **completely homogeneous** (ε_r everywhere), the wave propagates in a **pure TEM mode** with phase velocity:
$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective dielectric constant (Microstrip) | $\varepsilon_{re} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}}}$ | Microstrip (all W/h) |
| 2 | Characteristic impedance (W/h ≤ 1) | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8h}{W} + \dfrac{W}{4h}\right)$ | Microstrip (narrow strip) |
| 3 | Characteristic impedance (W/h ≥ 1) | $Z_0 = \dfrac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}\left[\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.444\right)\right]}$ | Microstrip (wide strip) |
| 4 | Phase velocity (Microstrip) | $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$ | Microstrip |
| 5 | Guided wavelength (Microstrip) | $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$ | Microstrip |
| 6 | Characteristic impedance (Stripline, round conductor) | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4b}{\pi d}\right)$ | Stripline (round center conductor, diameter d) |
| 7 | Approximate Z₀ for Stripline (flat strip) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98 b}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | Stripline (thin flat strip) |
| 8 | Phase velocity (Stripline) | $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | Stripline |
| 9 | Guided wavelength (Stripline) | $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | Stripline |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the vertical bar `\vert` typed directly inside markdown tables) when writing absolute values. This is a common formatting mistake students make in answer sheets during digital submission.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microstrip** → Used in **patch antennas, RF filters, power dividers, microwave amplifiers** (most commercial PCBs at GHz frequencies).
- **Stripline** → Used in **high-speed digital backplanes, sensitive RF filters, military/aerospace PCBs** requiring EMI shielding.
- **Mixed use** → Many modern RF modules use *both* lines on the same board, transitioning between them using **via transitions**.

---

## 2.6 Comparison Matrix — Microstrip vs. Stripline (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Property | Microstrip | Stripline |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary condition | **Open** (top), grounded (bottom) | **Fully shielded** (top + bottom) |
| Propagation mode | **Quasi-TEM** | **Pure TEM** |
| Phase velocity | $c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$ | $c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ |
| Dielectric | **Inhomogeneous** | **Homogeneous** |
| Radiation loss | **Higher** | **Negligible** |
| Conductor loss | Higher (field partly in air) | Lower |
| Ease of fabrication | **Easier** (no via needed for top access) | **Harder** (need alignment + vias) |
| Mounting components | **Easy** (top side accessible) | **Difficult** (strip buried) |
| Power handling | Lower | Higher |
| Typical application | Patch antennas, MMICs, RF PCBs | Filters, high-density interconnects |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Dielectric Constant (ε_re) for Microstrip

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** The microstrip line supports a quasi-TEM mode. Because part of the electric field exists in the air region (ε_r = 1) and part in the dielectric substrate (ε_r = ε_r), the effective dielectric constant ε_re is a weighted combination of these two. The well-known empirical formula (Hammerstad–Jensen, widely used in KTU-board-approved texts) is derived from conformal mapping techniques.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We begin by defining ε_re as a function of the strip width-to-height ratio (W/h). The empirical relationship is:

$$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the two limiting cases:**

- When **W/h → ∞** (very wide strip): The electric field is almost entirely inside the substrate, so ε_re → ε_r.
- When **W/h → 0** (very narrow strip): Half the field is in air and half in the substrate, so ε_re → (ε_r + 1)/2.

**Step 2 — Construct the average:**
$$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{(\varepsilon_r + 1)}{2} \quad \text{(zero-order term)} + \quad \frac{(\varepsilon_r - 1)}{2} \cdot f(W/h)$$

where $f(W/h) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}}}$ is the **filling factor**.

**Step 3 — Verify the limits:**

- As W/h → ∞: $f(W/h) \to 1$, so $\varepsilon_{re} \to \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} = \varepsilon_r$ ✓
- As W/h → 0: $f(W/h) \to 0$, so $\varepsilon_{re} \to \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2}$ ✓

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
The function $f(W/h)$ represents the **fraction of the total electric field energy stored in the dielectric** (versus in air). As the strip gets wider, more field energy is concentrated in the substrate → ε_re approaches ε_r.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Characteristic Impedance for Narrow Microstrip (W/h ≤ 1)

For a narrow microstrip line, we use the **conformal mapping** approximation that reduces the geometry to a parallel-plate capacitor-like structure.

**Starting Point:** The capacitance per unit length of an open microstrip can be approximated as:

$$C_a = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{W}{h} \quad \text{(parallel-plate approximation, neglecting fringing)}$$

**Step 1 — Add the fringing-field correction:**
For narrow strips, fringing fields significantly contribute to capacitance. The corrected expression (with logarithmic correction) is:

$$C_a \approx \varepsilon_0 \cdot \left[\frac{W}{h} + 0.441 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\ln(8h/W)}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substituting W/h ≤ 1 limit:**
In the limit W/h ≤ 1, the dominant term becomes the fringing logarithmic term. After substitution:

$$Z_0 = \frac{1}{c \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}} \cdot C_a}$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} \ln\!\left(\frac{8h}{W} + \frac{W}{4h}\right) \quad \text{ohms}}$$

**Step 3 — Physical meaning:**
The factor 60 (≈ 60π/π) comes from the conversion between SI units; the logarithmic term accounts for the fringing-field geometry. As **W decreases**, Z₀ **increases** (narrower strip = higher impedance).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Characteristic Impedance for Wide Microstrip (W/h ≥ 1)

For a wide strip, the parallel-plate approximation dominates and fringing becomes less important.

**Starting Point:** Using the parallel-plate approximation with fringing correction:

$$Z_0 = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}}{c \cdot C_a}$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}} \left[\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.444\right)\right]}}$$

**Step 1 — Verify continuity at W/h = 1:**
Plug W/h = 1: numerator = $120\pi / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$; denominator = $(1 + 1.393 + 0.667 \cdot \ln(2.444)) = (1 + 1.393 + 0.667 \cdot 0.894) ≈ 2.989$.
So $Z_0 \approx 120\pi / (2.989 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}) \approx 126.1 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$.
For the narrow-strip formula at W/h = 1: $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}) \ln(8 + 0.25) = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}) \cdot 2.196 \approx 131.7 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$.
The two are continuous within a few percent — acceptable empirical accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stripline Characteristic Impedance (Round Conductor)

For a stripline with a **round inner conductor of diameter d**, centered between two ground planes separated by total distance **2b**, the field configuration is a true TEM mode.

**Step 1 — Conformal mapping** maps the geometry to a coaxial-cable-like structure, yielding:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{4b}{\pi d}\right) \quad \text{ohms}}$$

**Step 2 — Boundary condition check:**
- As d → 0 (thin wire): Z₀ → ∞ (open circuit) ✓
- As d → 2b (fills the gap): Z₀ → 0 (short circuit) ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A microstrip line is fabricated on a substrate with ε_r = 4.2, h = 1.6 mm, and W = 3.0 mm (assuming zero thickness t = 0). Compute (a) effective dielectric constant, (b) characteristic impedance, (c) phase velocity, (d) guided wavelength at 2.5 GHz.

**Step 1 — Check W/h:**
W/h = 3.0 / 1.6 = 1.875 ≥ 1 → **Use wide-strip formula.**

**Step 2 — Compute ε_re:**

$$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{4.2 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.2 - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12(1.6)/3.0}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{re} = 2.6 + 1.6 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 6.4}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{re} = 2.6 + 1.6 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.4}} = 2.6 + 1.6 \cdot \frac{1}{2.7203}$$

$$\varepsilon_{re} = 2.6 + 0.5882 = 3.188$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z₀ (using wide-strip formula):**

$$\text{Denominator} = \frac{W}{h} + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln\!\left(\frac{W}{h} + 1.444\right)$$

$$= 1.875 + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln(3.319)$$

$$= 1.875 + 1.393 + 0.667 \cdot 1.200 = 4.069$$

$$Z_0 = \frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{3.188} \cdot 4.069} = \frac{376.99}{1.785 \cdot 4.069} = \frac{376.99}{7.263} = 51.9 \; \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{3.188}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.785} = 1.681 \times 10^8 \; m/s$$

**Step 5 — Compute guided wavelength at 2.5 GHz:**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.12 \; m$$

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} = \frac{0.12}{1.785} = 0.0672 \; m = 67.2 \; mm$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) ε_re ≈ 3.19
- (b) Z₀ ≈ 51.9 Ω
- (c) v_p ≈ 1.68 × 10⁸ m/s
- (d) λ_g ≈ 67.2 mm
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Microstrip Line (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AIR["AIR REGION epsilon_r EQUALS 1"]
        node1["OPEN BOUNDARY TOP SIDE"]
    end

    subgraph SUBSTRATE["DIELECTRIC SUBSTRATE epsilon_r EQUALS 4.2 TYPICAL"]
        node2["TOP METALLIC STRIP WIDTH W THICKNESS t"]
        node3["DIELECTRIC HEIGHT h"]
    end

    subgraph GROUND["GROUND PLANE BOTTOM"]
        node4["CONTINUOUS METAL GROUND"]
    end

    subgraph FIELD["FIELD DISTRIBUTION QUASI TEM"]
        node5["E FIELD PARTLY IN AIR PARTLY IN SUBSTRATE"]
        node6["H FIELD LOOPS AROUND STRIP"]
        node7["ENERGY PROPAGATES ALONG Z DIRECTION"]
    end

    node1 --- node2
    node2 --- node3
    node3 --- node4
    node5 --- node2
    node6 --- node2
    node7 --- node5
    node7 --- node6

    style SUBSTRATE fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Stripline (Side View)

```mermaid
    end

        nodeC["METALLIC STRIP WIDTH W THICKNESS t CENTERED"]
    end

        nodeD["HEIGHT b epsilon_r FILLER"]
    end

        nodeE["METAL PLATE LOWER SHIELD"]
    end

    subgraph TFIELD["TEM FIELD STRUCTURE"]
        nodeF["E FIELD PURELY VERTICAL BETWEEN STRIP AND GROUNDS"]
        nodeG["H FIELD PURELY HORIZONTAL LOOPS"]

    end

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeF --- nodeC
    nodeG --- nodeC
    nodeH --- nodeF
    nodeH --- nodeG

    style BOTGND fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000
    style TFIELD fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Architecture — Microstrip vs Stripline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MS["MICROSTRIP LINE"]
        ms1["TOP STRIP W t"]
        ms2["SUBSTRATE h epsilon_r"]

        ms5["OPEN TOP BOUNDARY"]

        ms1 --- ms2
        ms2 --- ms3
        ms3 --- ms4
        ms4 --- ms5
    end

    subgraph SL["STRIPLINE"]
        sl1["TOP GROUND"]
        sl2["DIELECTRIC FILLER 2b"]
        sl3["CENTER STRIP W t"]
        sl4["BOTTOM GROUND"]

        sl1 --- sl2
        sl2 --- sl3
        sl3 --- sl4
        sl4 --- sl5
    end

```

## 4.4 Constructional-Feature Checklist Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    R1["1 STRIP CONDUCTOR WIDTH W THICKNESS t"]:::feature

    R3["3 GROUND PLANE CONDUCTOR BOTTOM OR BOTH SIDES"]:::feature
    R4["4 BOUNDARY CONDITIONS OPEN OR SHIELDED"]:::feature

    ROOT --> R3
    ROOT --> R4
    ROOT --> R5

    classDef root fill:#003366,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:4px

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above block diagrams abstract the physical 3D geometry into layered functional blocks. Each "layer" corresponds to a physical layer in the actual fabricated structure. This visualization technique is preferred by KTU board examiners for descriptive answers, as it conveys **clarity of concept** more than hand-drawn approximations.
