<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Define a Microstrip Line. List its constructional features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Microstrip Line** is a planar transmission line consisting of a thin metallic strip of width **W** and thickness **t** printed on the top surface of a grounded dielectric substrate of height **h** and relative permittivity **ε_r**.

**Constructional Features:**
1. **Top conductor**: A thin metallic (copper) strip of width **W** and thickness **t**.
2. **Substrate**: A dielectric layer of height **h** and relative permittivity **ε_r**, typically in the range **2.2 to 10.8**.
3. **Ground plane**: A continuous metal sheet on the bottom surface of the substrate.
4. **Boundary condition**: The top is **open to free air** (ε_r = 1); the bottom is bounded by the ground plane.
5. **Wave propagation**: Supports a **quasi-TEM mode** because the dielectric is inhomogeneous.

**Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing constructional features.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Compare Microstrip and Stripline based on (i) propagation mode, (ii) dielectric homogeneity, and (iii) radiation loss.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Microstrip | Stripline |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Propagation mode | **Quasi-TEM** | **Pure TEM** |
| (ii) Dielectric homogeneity | **Inhomogeneous** (air + substrate) | **Homogeneous** (single ε_r filler) |
| (iii) Radiation loss | **Higher** (open boundary) | **Negligible** (fully shielded) |

**Distribution:** 1 mark per correct comparison pair.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain the constructional features of a Microstrip Line with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for its effective dielectric constant ε_re and characteristic impedance Z₀ for the case W/h ≤ 1.** *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b) A Microstrip line is fabricated on a substrate with ε_r = 2.55, h = 0.8 mm, W = 2.4 mm. Calculate (i) ε_re, (ii) Z₀, (iii) phase velocity at 3 GHz. State whether W/h ≤ 1 or W/h ≥ 1 case is applicable.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### (a) Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Constructional Features** *(3 marks)*:

A microstrip line consists of:
- A **top metallic strip** of width W and thickness t (typically copper, t ≈ 17–70 µm).
- A **dielectric substrate** of height h and relative permittivity ε_r (e.g., Duroid 5880 with ε_r = 2.2, FR4 with ε_r = 4.2).
- A **bottom ground plane** that is continuous and unpatterned.

*(Block diagram as in Section 4.1 to be drawn by the student)*

**Derivation of ε_re** *(2 marks)*:

$$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}}}$$

**Logical steps:**
1. Define ε_re as a weighted average between air (ε_r = 1) and substrate (ε_r = ε_r).
2. The filling factor accounts for the fraction of E-field energy in the substrate.
3. Limiting cases: W/h → ∞ gives ε_re → ε_r; W/h → 0 gives ε_re → (ε_r + 1)/2.

**Derivation of Z₀ for W/h ≤ 1** *(2 marks)*:

$$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} \ln\!\left(\frac{8h}{W} + \frac{W}{4h}\right)$$

**Logical steps:**
1. Approximate the microstrip as a parallel-plate capacitor with fringing fields.
2. Use Z₀ = 1/(c·√ε_re·C_a), where C_a is the per-unit-length capacitance.
3. The logarithmic term arises from the fringing-field geometry.

---

#### (b) Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Compute W/h:**
W/h = 2.4 / 0.8 = 3.0 ≥ 1 → **Use wide-strip formula** for Z₀.

**Step 2 — Compute ε_re:**

$$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{2.55 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.55 - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12(0.8)/2.4}}$$

$$= 1.775 + 0.775 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 4.0}} = 1.775 + 0.775 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$

$$= 1.775 + 0.775 \cdot 0.4472 = 1.775 + 0.3466 = 2.122$$

**ε_re ≈ 2.12**

**Step 3 — Compute Z₀ (wide-strip formula):**

$$\text{Denominator} = 3.0 + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln(3.0 + 1.444) = 3.0 + 1.393 + 0.667 \cdot \ln(4.444)$$

$$= 3.0 + 1.393 + 0.667 \cdot 1.491 = 3.0 + 1.393 + 0.995 = 5.388$$

$$Z_0 = \frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{2.122} \cdot 5.388} = \frac{376.99}{1.457 \cdot 5.388} = \frac{376.99}{7.851} = 48.0 \; \Omega$$

**Z₀ ≈ 48.0 Ω**

**Step 4 — Compute phase velocity at 3 GHz:**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.122}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.457} = 2.06 \times 10^8 \; m/s$$

**v_p ≈ 2.06 × 10⁸ m/s**

**Distribution:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 1 mark, statement 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the constructional features of a Stripline. Mention the nature of wave propagation and field distribution.** *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b) A Stripline has round inner conductor diameter d = 1 mm, total ground-plane separation 2b = 5 mm, and ε_r = 2.2. Calculate (i) characteristic impedance, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) guided wavelength at 5 GHz.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### (a) Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Constructional Features** *(4 marks)*:

*(Block diagram as in Section 4.2 to be drawn by the student)*

A Stripline (also called triplate line) consists of:
1. **Two parallel ground planes** (top and bottom metal sheets), typically copper.
2. **One central metallic strip** of width W and thickness t, centered precisely between the ground planes.
3. **A homogeneous dielectric filler** of relative permittivity ε_r filling the entire region between the two ground planes.
4. **Total height** = 2b (b above + b below the central strip).
5. **No open boundary** — fully shielded structure.

**Wave Propagation Nature** *(2 marks)*:

Because the dielectric is **completely homogeneous**, the wave propagates as a **pure TEM mode**. There is no field dispersion, and the phase velocity is well-defined as:

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

**Field Distribution** *(1 mark)*:

- **Electric field (E)**: Purely vertical, between the central strip and the two ground planes.
- **Magnetic field (H)**: Purely horizontal, forming closed loops around the central strip.

---

#### (b) Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Given:** d = 1 mm = 1 × 10⁻³ m; 2b = 5 mm → b = 2.5 mm = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m; ε_r = 2.2.

**Step 1 — Characteristic impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{4b}{\pi d}\right) = \frac{60}{\sqrt{2.2}} \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \cdot 2.5}{\pi \cdot 1}\right)$$

$$= \frac{60}{1.4832} \ln\!\left(\frac{10}{\pi}\right) = 40.453 \cdot \ln(3.183)$$

$$= 40.453 \cdot 1.158 = 46.85 \; \Omega$$

**Z₀ ≈ 46.85 Ω**

**Step 2 — Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.4832} = 2.023 \times 10^8 \; m/s$$

**v_p ≈ 2.02 × 10⁸ m/s**

**Step 3 — Guided wavelength at 5 GHz:**

$$\lambda_g = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2.023 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.04046 \; m = 40.46 \; mm$$

**λ_g ≈ 40.46 mm**

**Distribution:** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the W/h check**: Many students apply the narrow-strip formula even when W/h > 1. *Always* check W/h first.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency**: h and W must be in the **same units** (both mm or both m) before computing W/h.
> 3. **Mixing ε_r and ε_re**: For **microstrip**, use ε_re (effective dielectric constant) in Z₀ and v_p. For **stripline**, use ε_r directly.
> 4. **Forgetting ln argument unit consistency**: In stripline formula, 4b/(πd) must be **dimensionless** — both b and d in the same unit.
> 5. **Ignoring t (strip thickness)**: For board exams, t is usually neglected (assumed zero). If given, account for it.
> 6. **Confusing λ₀ and λ_g**: λ_g is the **guided** (in-medium) wavelength; λ₀ is the **free-space** wavelength. λ_g = λ₀ / √(ε_re or ε_r).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION — Must Memorize**

- ☐ A **Microstrip Line** = strip on top of a **grounded substrate** (open boundary on top).
- ☐ A **Stripline** = strip **centered between two ground planes** (fully shielded, homogeneous dielectric).
- ☐ Microstrip → **Quasi-TEM mode**; Stripline → **Pure TEM mode**.
- ☐ Effective dielectric constant (microstrip): $\varepsilon_{re} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}$.
- ☐ Microstrip Z₀ (W/h ≤ 1): $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8h}{W} + \dfrac{W}{4h}\right)$.
- ☐ Microstrip Z₀ (W/h ≥ 1): $Z_0 = \dfrac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}\left[\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{h} + 1.444\right)\right]}$.
- ☐ Stripline Z₀ (round): $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4b}{\pi d}\right)$.
- ☐ Phase velocity in microstrip: $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$; in stripline: $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$.
- ☐ Guided wavelength: $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$ (microstrip) or $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ (stripline).
- ☐ Microstrip is preferred for **accessibility and ease of mounting**; stripline is preferred for **shielding and low loss**.
- ☐ The factor **60** in Z₀ formulas comes from the wave impedance of free space (η₀ = 377 Ω) and geometric factors.
- ☐ For W/h > 1, fringing fields become negligible compared to parallel-plate capacitance.
- ☐ The microstrip effective dielectric constant **always lies between 1 and ε_r**.
- ☐ **Inhomogeneous dielectric → quasi-TEM**; **homogeneous dielectric → pure TEM**.
- ☐ Stripline has **zero radiation loss** (fully shielded); microstrip has **measurable radiation loss** above ~10 GHz.
- ☐ **Typical substrate materials**: FR4 (ε_r = 4.4), Duroid 5880 (ε_r = 2.2), Duroid 6010 (ε_r = 10.2), alumina (ε_r = 9.8).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impedance Matching Networks - Design of Matching Circuits Using Lumped Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impedance Matching Networks - Design of Matching Circuits Using Lumped Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impedance Matching Network**: A two-port passive reactive network (typically lossless, constructed from inductors *L* and capacitors *C*) inserted between a source of impedance $Z_S$ and a load of impedance $Z_L$ such that the impedance seen looking into the network from the source equals the complex conjugate of the source impedance, $Z_{in} = Z_S^*$, thereby ensuring maximum power transfer, minimum standing waves on the transmission line, and optimum noise / power performance in an RF system.

In a *lossless* matching network — the standard assumption in KTU board problems — the network itself dissipates **zero power**, so the available power from the source is delivered entirely to the load, subject only to the conjugate-match condition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Hose Adapter

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check** 💧
> Imagine a wide fire-hose (large cross-section, low resistance to flow) connected directly to a narrow garden sprinkler (high resistance to flow). The pressure wave bounces back, the hose vibrates, and very little water actually sprays out efficiently.
>
> An *impedance matching network* is the **threaded brass adapter** screwed between the two — it has internal geometry (in our case, *L* and *C* elements) that gradually transforms the impedance so that water (or RF power) flows smoothly without reflection.

In RF terms:

| Physical Analogy | RF Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Wide hose ↔ Narrow sprinkler | $Z_S = 50\,\Omega \leftrightarrow Z_L = 200\,\Omega$ |
| Bouncing water column | Reflected wave, reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ |
| Brass adapter | **L-section**, **T-network**, or **π-network** |
| Vibration of hose | High **VSWR** on the feedline |
| Smooth flow at the nozzle | Conjugate match, $\Gamma = 0$ |

### 1.3 Why Matching is Mandatory in RF

1. **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** requires $Z_{in} = Z_S^*$ (conjugate match).
2. **Reflection Coefficient** on a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

When $Z_L \neq Z_0$, $\vert \Gamma \vert > 0$, and a portion of the incident wave is reflected, producing standing waves characterised by the **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**.

3. **VSWR** is defined as

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

4. **Return Loss (RL)** in decibels is

$$\text{RL} = -20\,\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

A perfectly matched line has $\text{VSWR} = 1$, $\text{RL} = \infty\,\text{dB}$, and $\Gamma = 0$. A short-circuit load has $\text{VSWR} = \infty$, $\text{RL} = 0\,\text{dB}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric**: Most KTU board questions assume $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ unless the problem statement explicitly specifies a different reference impedance. Always write this value at the top of your solution as it earns easy marks.

### 1.4 Classification of Matching Networks

Matching networks are classified by their topology and the type of elements used.

**By Topology (Lumped-Element Networks, focus of this module):**
- **L-Section** — two reactive elements (one series, one shunt)
- **T-Network** — three elements (series, shunt, series)
- **π-Network** — three elements (shunt, series, shunt)

**By Element Type:**
- Purely reactive (*L* and *C*) → lossless
- Real (resistive) → dissipative, used for biasing or attenuation

The **L-section** is the workhorse of RF matching because it is the simplest and provides the *minimum number of components* required to transform any two real impedances. T- and π-networks are used when an *additional degree of freedom* (e.g., harmonic suppression, controlling the loaded Q) is required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Matching Problem — Formal Statement

Given a source impedance $Z_S = R_S + jX_S$ and a load impedance $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$, the design objective is to determine a lossless two-port network $N$ such that

$$Z_{in} = Z_S^* \quad \text{and} \quad Z_{out} = Z_L^*$$

For the *restricted problem* treated in most KTU Module-1 questions, both $Z_S$ and $Z_L$ are **purely real** (resistive), so the matching condition reduces to

$$Z_{in} = Z_S = R_S, \quad Z_{out} = Z_L = R_L$$

In other words, the network must transform $R_L$ into $R_S$ at the design frequency $f_0$.

### 2.2 Why Reactive (and Not Resistive) Matching?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **resistive** matching network (e.g., an L-pad) achieves the impedance transformation by *dissipating* the mismatch power as heat. This is acceptable in instrumentation but **catastrophic in RF power amplifiers and antenna feeds**, where every milliwatt counts. A **reactive (lossless) network** reflects the mismatch power back to be re-delivered to the load — preserving 100 % power transfer.

Hence KTU Module-1 matching is restricted to *L* and *C* elements.

### 2.3 The Quality Factor (Q) — Central Design Parameter

Every L-section can be characterised by a single number — its **operating quality factor** $Q$ — which simultaneously fixes the bandwidth, the component reactances, and the harmonic rejection.

For a real-to-real match ($R_S$ and $R_L$ both resistive, both positive):

**Case A:** $R_S < R_L$ (source resistance is *smaller*)

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1}$$

**Case B:** $R_S > R_L$ (source resistance is *larger*)

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_S}{R_L} - 1}$$

The reactances of the L-section are then computed from $Q$ and the terminating resistances (the exact expressions are derived in §3.2 below).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $Q$**:
> - *Higher* $Q$ → narrower bandwidth, sharper selectivity, better harmonic suppression, but more sensitivity to component tolerance.
> - *Lower* $Q$ → wider bandwidth, looser tolerance, but poorer out-of-band rejection.
> For broadband systems, the designer chooses $Q$ explicitly; for narrowband systems, $Q$ is fixed by the impedance ratio.

### 2.4 The Bode–Fano Limit (Theoretical Bandwidth Bound)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bode–Fano Criterion** — *Every reactive matching network has a theoretical upper bound on its broadband performance. You cannot match a reactive load over an arbitrarily large bandwidth.*

For a parallel RC load ($R \parallel C$), the criterion states

$$\int_0^{\infty} \ln \frac{1}{\vert \Gamma(\omega) \vert}\, d\omega \;\le\; \frac{\pi}{RC}$$

For a series RC load,

$$\int_0^{\infty} \ln \frac{1}{\vert \Gamma(\omega) \vert}\, d\omega \;\le\; \pi RC$$

Equality holds only for an *infinite* matching network (Chebyshev / infinite-element ladder). Practical finite networks approach but never exceed this bound.

The KTU board frequently asks for the *statement* and *physical meaning* of the Bode–Fano criterion rather than a numerical evaluation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | dimensionless | Computing mismatch at any port |
| 2 | VSWR | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1+\vert \Gamma \vert}{1-\vert \Gamma \vert}$ | ratio (≥ 1) | Quantifying standing waves |
| 3 | Return Loss | $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | **dB** | Reporting match quality |
| 4 | Mismatch Loss | $ML = -10\log_{10}(1-\vert \Gamma \vert^2)$ | **dB** | Power loss due to reflection |
| 5 | Q for $R_S < R_L$ | $Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_L}{R_S} - 1}$ | dimensionless | L-section design (low-side) |
| 6 | Q for $R_S > R_L$ | $Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_S}{R_L} - 1}$ | dimensionless | L-section design (high-side) |
| 7 | Series Reactance (low-side, $R_S < R_L$) | $X_s = Q\,R_S$ | **Ω** | L-section, series element |
| 8 | Shunt Reactance (low-side, $R_S < R_L$) | $X_p = \dfrac{R_L}{Q}$ | **Ω** | L-section, parallel element |
| 9 | Series Reactance (high-side, $R_S > R_L$) | $X_s = \dfrac{R_S}{Q}$ | **Ω** | L-section, series element |
| 10 | Shunt Reactance (high-side, $R_S > R_L$) | $X_p = Q\,R_L$ | **Ω** | L-section, parallel element |
| 11 | Inductance from Reactance | $L = \dfrac{X_L}{2\pi f}$ | **H** | $X_L > 0$ |
| 12 | Capacitance from Reactance | $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \vert X_C \vert}$ | **F** | $X_C < 0$ |
| 13 | Bode–Fano (parallel RC) | $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \ln\!\frac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert}\,d\omega \le \dfrac{\pi}{RC}$ | rad/s | Theoretical bandwidth bound |
| 14 | Bode–Fano (series RC) | $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \ln\!\frac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert}\,d\omega \le \pi RC$ | rad/s | Theoretical bandwidth bound |
| 15 | Bandwidth–Q relation | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}$ | **Hz** | Converting Q to 3-dB bandwidth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The Bode–Fano bound is the *reason* UWB (ultra-wideband) systems, which must match antennas over multi-GHz spans, use *electrically small, lossy* matching techniques. Purely reactive matching of a high-Q antenna is fundamentally impossible over a wide band.

### 2.6 Design Methodology — Three Steps

1. **Compute the required Q** from the impedance ratio.
2. **Select the topology** (L, T, or π) and **polarity** of each element (inductor or capacitor) based on whether the load is *inductive* or *capacitive* at $f_0$.
3. **Convert reactances to L/C values** at the operating frequency $f_0$.

The selection of polarity follows a simple rule:

| Load Behaviour at $f_0$ | Series Element | Shunt Element |
|---|---|---|
| Load is *capacitive* ($X_L < 0$) | Inductor (cancels the reactance) | Capacitor |
| Load is *inductive* ($X_L > 0$) | Capacitor (cancels the reactance) | Inductor |
| Load is purely real | Either, decided by low-pass vs high-pass requirement | Either |

A *low-pass* L-section uses a series inductor + shunt capacitor. A *high-pass* L-section uses a series capacitor + shunt inductor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Reflection and VSWR Relations

We begin with the wave equation on a lossless transmission line. The total voltage at a point is the sum of the forward and reflected travelling waves:

$$V(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

The reflection coefficient is defined as the ratio of the backward to the forward wave amplitude at the load:

$$\Gamma = \frac{V_0^-}{V_0^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

The last equality follows from applying Ohm's law at the load ($V = I Z_L$) and on the line ($V^+/I^+ = -V^-/I^- = Z_0$). Solving the resulting $2 \times 2$ linear system gives the boxed result.

**VSWR derivation.** The maximum and minimum voltages on the line are

$$V_{max} = \vert V_0^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert), \quad V_{min} = \vert V_0^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

Therefore, by definition,

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

**Return-Loss derivation.** The *power* reflection coefficient is $\vert \Gamma \vert^2$. In decibels,

$$\text{RL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{incident}}{P_{reflected}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert^2}\right) = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 20 and not 10?** Because $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is a *voltage* ratio, while decibels for power use $10\log$ and for voltage use $20\log$. Since RL is defined as a *power* ratio expressed in dB, the formula is $10\log(1/\vert \Gamma \vert^2) = 20\log(1/\vert \Gamma \vert) = -20\log\vert \Gamma \vert$.

### 3.2 Full Derivation of the L-Section Design Equations

Consider the L-section of Fig. 3.1 (left): a **shunt** element of reactance $X_p$ in parallel with the load $R_L$, followed by a **series** element of reactance $X_s$. The two-port is assumed lossless. Let us derive the design equations.

**Step 1 — Equivalent admittance of the load + shunt element.**

The admittance seen at the junction (looking right) is the parallel combination of $R_L$ and $jX_p$:

$$Y_{right} = \frac{1}{R_L} + \frac{1}{jX_p} = \frac{1}{R_L} - \frac{j}{X_p}$$

Its equivalent series impedance is the reciprocal:

$$Z_{right} = \frac{1}{Y_{right}} = \frac{R_L \cdot jX_p}{R_L + jX_p} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

A cleaner approach: write the *impedance* of the parallel combination directly.

$$Z_{right} = \frac{R_L \cdot (jX_p)}{R_L + jX_p} = \frac{jR_L X_p\,(R_L - jX_p)}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} = \frac{R_L X_p^2}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} + j\,\frac{R_L^2 X_p}{R_L^2 + X_p^2}$$

**Step 2 — Add the series element.**

The input impedance is

$$Z_{in} = Z_{right} + jX_s = \frac{R_L X_p^2}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} + j\!\left(\frac{R_L^2 X_p}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} + X_s\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the conjugate-match condition.**

For a purely real source $R_S$, we require $Z_{in} = R_S$, i.e., both the real part equals $R_S$ and the imaginary part equals zero.

*Real part:*

$$\frac{R_L X_p^2}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} = R_S \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X_p^2 R_L = R_S (R_L^2 + X_p^2) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X_p^2 (R_L - R_S) = R_S R_L^2$$

*Imaginary part:*

$$\frac{R_L^2 X_p}{R_L^2 + X_p^2} + X_s = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X_s = -\frac{R_L^2 X_p}{R_L^2 + X_p^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X_p$ and $X_s$ in closed form.**

Assuming $R_L > R_S$ (a positive denominator is required for a real $X_p$):

$$X_p = \pm \frac{R_L}{\sqrt{\dfrac{R_L}{R_S} - 1}} = \pm R_L \sqrt{\frac{R_S}{R_L - R_S}}$$

Define

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1}$$

Then

$$X_p = \pm \frac{R_L}{Q} \quad \text{(shunt reactance)}$$

Substituting back into the imaginary-part equation:

$$X_s = - \frac{R_L^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_L}{Q}\right)}{R_L^2 + \left(\dfrac{R_L}{Q}\right)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

Let us re-derive more cleanly by introducing the **quality factor** at the outset.

**Clean derivation using the Q-factor.**

For the parallel branch formed by $R_L \parallel X_p$, the *effective* Q is

$$Q = \frac{R_L}{\vert X_p \vert}$$

because in a parallel RC or RL resonant circuit, $Q = R/\vert X \vert$. Then

$$X_p = \frac{R_L}{Q}$$

For the *transformed* resistance looking into the series-plus-parallel combination, the standard result is

$$R_{in} = R_S = \frac{R_L}{1 + Q^2}$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q^2 = \frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1}$$

The series reactance is

$$X_s = Q \cdot R_S$$

(derivable from the imaginary part of $Z_{in}$).

**Case B ($R_S > R_L$):** By duality, swap the roles of series and shunt, yielding

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_S}{R_L} - 1}, \quad X_s = \frac{R_S}{Q}, \quad X_p = Q \cdot R_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check**: $Q^2 + 1 = R_L/R_S$ in Case A, and $Q^2 + 1 = R_S/R_L$ in Case B. In both cases, $Q$ is real and positive only if the larger resistance is in the *numerator*, which is precisely the condition for an L-section to exist.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — L-Section Match (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem (typical):** *Design an L-section matching network to match a 50 Ω source to a 200 Ω load at 1 GHz. Use a low-pass topology (series-L, shunt-C).*

**Step 1 — Identify topology and compute Q.**

Here $R_S = 50\,\Omega < R_L = 200\,\Omega$, so we are in **Case A** (low-side). Compute $Q$:

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{50} - 1} = \sqrt{4 - 1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reactances.**

$$X_s = Q \cdot R_S = 1.732 \times 50 = 86.6\,\Omega \quad \text{(series inductor)}$$

$$X_p = \frac{R_L}{Q} = \frac{200}{1.732} \approx 115.47\,\Omega \quad \text{(shunt capacitor)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert reactances to component values at $f_0 = 1\,\text{GHz}$.**

For the **series inductor** ($X_L = \omega L$):

$$L = \frac{X_s}{2\pi f_0} = \frac{86.6}{2 \pi \times 10^9} \approx 13.78\,\text{nH}$$

For the **shunt capacitor** ($X_C = 1/(\omega C)$):

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 X_p} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^9 \times 115.47} \approx 1.379\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

The input impedance of the L-section, computed directly, is

$$Z_{in} = jX_s + (R_L \parallel jX_p) = j86.6 + \frac{200 \times (-j115.47)}{200 - j115.47}$$

$$= j86.6 + \frac{-j23094}{200 - j115.47} \cdot \frac{200 + j115.47}{200 + j115.47}$$

$$= j86.6 + \frac{-j23094\,(200 + j115.47)}{200^2 + 115.47^2}$$

$$= j86.6 + \frac{-j23094 \times 200 + 23094 \times 115.47}{40000 + 13333}$$

$$= j86.6 + \frac{2.667 \times 10^6 - j4.619 \times 10^6}{53333}$$

$$= j86.6 + 50.0 - j86.6 = 50.0 + j0.0$$

The imaginary parts cancel exactly, and the real part is $50\,\Omega = R_S$. **Match achieved.** ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Topology Selection Guide

| Condition | Topology | Series Element | Shunt Element | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_S < R_L$ | Low-pass L | $L$ | $C$ | Harmonic suppression required |
| $R_S < R_L$ | High-pass L | $C$ | $L$ | DC bias must be blocked |
| $R_S > R_L$ | Low-pass L | $C$ | $L$ | Harmonic suppression |
| $R_S > R_L$ | High-pass L | $L$ | $C$ | DC isolation |
| Q constraint $< Q_{L,\text{natural}}$ | **T- or π-network** | (3 elements) | (3 elements) | Designer-chosen $Q$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A *natural* L-section has a Q *forced* by the impedance ratio. If the application demands a *lower* Q (i.e., wider bandwidth) than this forced value, an L-section is *insufficient* and a T- or π-network must be used. This is the central reason these higher-order topologies exist.

### 3.5 π-Network Design Equations (Brief)

For a π-network matching $R_S$ to $R_L$ at $f_0$ with a specified loaded $Q$:

$$Q_1 = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_S}{R_L}}$$

$$R_1 = R_S (1 + Q_1^2), \quad R_2 = \frac{R_L}{1 + Q_2^2}, \quad Q_2 = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S}}$$

Then proceed as two cascaded L-sections. The π-network provides an *extra degree of freedom* — the designer-chosen $Q$ — which the L-section does not have.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Two L-Section Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Source ZS equal to RS] -->|Series Xs| nodeA1[Node A]
    nodeA1 -->|Shunt Xp to ground| gndA1((Ground))
    nodeA1 --> load1[Load RL]
    src1 -.Inductor.-> nodeA1
    gndA1 -.Capacitor.-> nodeA1

    src2[Source ZS equal to RS] -->|Series Xs| nodeA2[Node B]
    nodeA2 -->|Shunt Xp to ground| gndA2((Ground))
    nodeA2 --> load2[Load RL]
    src2 -.Capacitor.-> nodeA2
    gndA2 -.Inductor.-> nodeA2
```

**Reading the figure:**
- **Left half** — *Low-pass* L-section: series inductor + shunt capacitor. Passes DC, attenuates harmonics. Used when harmonic rejection is required.
- **Right half** — *High-pass* L-section: series capacitor + shunt inductor. Blocks DC, used when DC isolation is needed (e.g., between RF stages with different bias voltages).

### 4.2 L-Section Design Flow (Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start Design]) --> s1[Identify RS and RL]
    s1 --> s2{RS less than RL?}
    s2 -->|Yes| s3[Compute Q equals sqrt of RL over RS minus 1]
    s2 -->|No| s4[Compute Q equals sqrt of RS over RL minus 1]
    s3 --> s5[Series Xs equals Q times RS]
    s4 --> s6[Series Xs equals RS over Q]
    s5 --> s7[Shunt Xp equals RL over Q]
    s6 --> s8[Shunt Xp equals Q times RL]
    s7 --> s9[Choose topology LP or HP]
    s8 --> s9
    s9 --> s10[Convert Xs and Xp to L and C at f0]
    s10 --> s11[Verify Zin equals RS by direct calculation]
    s11 --> done1([Design Complete])
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — The Matching Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Subsystem]
        osc[Oscillator or PA]
        zsrc[Source Impedance ZS]
    end
    subgraph MN[Matching Network]
        lsec[L Section LC Topology]
        qctrl[Q Factor Control]
    end
    subgraph LD[Load Subsystem]
        zld[Load Impedance ZL]
        ant[Antenna or Filter]
    end
    osc --> zsrc --> lsec --> zld --> ant
    qctrl -.Tunes.-> lsec
    lsec -->|Reflected Wave minimised| zsrc
    lsec -->|Power delivered| zld
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response of L-Section Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LP[Low Pass L Section]
        lp_in[Input at f0] --> lp_ser[Series Inductor L]
        lp_ser --> lp_node[Junction]
        lp_node --> lp_sh[Shunt Capacitor C to GND]
        lp_node --> lp_out[Output to Load]
    end
    subgraph HP[High Pass L Section]
        hp_in[Input at f0] --> hp_ser[Series Capacitor C]
        hp_ser --> hp_node[Junction]
        hp_node --> hp_sh[Shunt Inductor L to GND]
        hp_node --> hp_out[Output to Load]
    end
```

### 4.5 Smith Chart Visualisation (Block Representation)

The Smith chart is the standard graphical tool used to design matching networks. The matching procedure moves the *normalized load admittance* $y_L$ (or impedance $z_L$) along constant-resistance and constant-conductance circles to the centre of the chart (1 + j0), which represents a perfect match.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pt1[Normalized Load zL equal to RL over Z0] --> move1[Move along constant conductance circle by adding susceptance]
    move1 --> pt2[Intermediate point on chart]
    pt2 --> move2[Move along constant resistance circle by adding series reactance]
    move2 --> pt3[Center of Smith Chart 1 plus j0]
    pt3 --> matchDone([Conjugate Match Achieved])

    pt1 -.Step 1 Shunt Element.-> move1
    pt2 -.Step 2 Series Element.-> move2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip** 📐: Even though Smith-chart problems are rare in written exams, students are expected to *describe* the procedure: (i) plot the normalized load, (ii) add a shunt susceptance to move to the unit-conductance circle, (iii) add a series reactance to move to the centre. Each move corresponds to one *L* or *C* element in the network.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions follow the *exact* KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: Part A short-answer (3 marks), Part B long-answer with internal choice (14 marks, broken into two 7-mark sub-parts). Model answers and step-by-step solutions are provided. Marks shown in brackets indicate the typical KTU valuation split.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** Define impedance matching. Why is it essential in RF communication systems? *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Impedance matching is the process of designing a passive reactive network inserted between a source and a load such that the input impedance presented to the source is the complex conjugate of the source impedance, $Z_{in} = Z_S^*$, thereby ensuring maximum power transfer and minimum signal reflection.

**Three Reasons (2 marks):**
1. To deliver **maximum power** from the source to the load (Maximum Power Transfer Theorem).
2. To **minimise standing waves** on the transmission line, ensuring a low VSWR (close to 1) and reducing feedline losses.
3. To improve **signal-to-noise ratio** in low-noise amplifiers and to prevent **oscillation / instability** in active RF circuits.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Many students write only the *maximum power transfer* condition and forget the *standing-wave / VSWR* aspect. Both points are mandatory for full marks.

---

#### **Q2.** Derive the relation between return loss and VSWR. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Starting from the reflection coefficient

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

the VSWR is

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \quad (1)$$

The return loss in decibels is

$$\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert \quad (2)$$

Solving (1) for $\vert \Gamma \vert$:

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR} + 1}$$

Substituting into (2):

$$\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR} + 1}\right) \;\;\text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Students often invert the sign. The correct relation has a *minus* sign in front of $20\log$ because return loss is a *positive* number (in dB) for any non-zero reflection. The argument of the log is always $\le 1$ for a passive load.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Choice]*

**(a)** Design an L-section matching network to match a source resistance $R_S = 75\,\Omega$ to a load resistance $R_L = 300\,\Omega$ at an operating frequency of $f_0 = 500\,\text{MHz}$. Use a *low-pass* topology. Calculate the values of all L and C components and verify the match by computing $Z_{in}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the operation of the two L-section configurations. When is a *high-pass* L-section preferred over a *low-pass* one in practical RF design? **[7 marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the case.** Here $R_S = 75\,\Omega < R_L = 300\,\Omega$, so we are in **Case A** (low-side). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute the quality factor.**

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{300}{75} - 1} = \sqrt{4 - 1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compute the series and shunt reactances.**

$$X_s = Q \cdot R_S = 1.732 \times 75 \approx 129.9\,\Omega \quad \text{(series inductor)}$$

$$X_p = \frac{R_L}{Q} = \frac{300}{1.732} \approx 173.2\,\Omega \quad \text{(shunt capacitor)}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Convert reactances to component values at $f_0 = 500\,\text{MHz}$, i.e., $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 5 \times 10^8 \approx 3.142 \times 10^9\,\text{rad/s}$.**

Series inductor:

$$L = \frac{X_s}{\omega_0} = \frac{129.9}{3.142 \times 10^9} \approx 41.3\,\text{nH}$$

Shunt capacitor:

$$C = \frac{1}{\omega_0 X_p} = \frac{1}{3.142 \times 10^9 \times 173.2} \approx 1.84\,\text{pF}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Verify by direct computation of $Z_{in}$.**

The parallel combination of $R_L = 300\,\Omega$ and $X_p = -j173.2\,\Omega$ (capacitor):

$$Z_{||} = \frac{300 \times (-j173.2)}{300 - j173.2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$Z_{||} = \frac{-j51960 \,(300 + j173.2)}{300^2 + 173.2^2} = \frac{-j1.559 \times 10^7 - 9.0 \times 10^6}{90000 + 30000}$$

$$= \frac{-9.0 \times 10^6 - j1.559 \times 10^7}{1.2 \times 10^5} = -75 - j129.8\,\Omega$$

Adding the series inductor $jX_s = +j129.9$:

$$Z_{in} = -75 - j129.8 + j129.9 = -75 + j0.1 \approx 75\,\Omega \; ✓$$

The real part is $R_S = 75\,\Omega$ and the imaginary part is essentially zero. **Match verified.** *(3 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for conclusion)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> (i) Students often forget the $2\pi f$ in the L/C conversion. (ii) They forget to use *angular* frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ rather than $f_0$. (iii) They do not perform the *verification step* — losing 1 to 2 marks. Always verify the result.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**The two L-section configurations (3 marks for diagrams, 4 marks for explanation):**

**Configuration 1 — Low-pass L-section:** A *series inductor* $L_1$ followed by a *shunt capacitor* $C_2$ to ground.

**Configuration 2 — High-pass L-section:** A *series capacitor* $C_1$ followed by a *shunt inductor* $L_2$ to ground.

**Operation:** In both cases, the *series* element transforms the real part of the input impedance, and the *shunt* element cancels the residual reactance. The exact reactance values are determined by the $Q$ factor computed from the impedance ratio.

**When is a high-pass L-section preferred? (3 marks)**

1. When **DC isolation** is required between the source and the load (e.g., between RF amplifier stages operating at different DC bias voltages). The series capacitor blocks DC.
2. When the **load is inductive** at the operating frequency — the shunt inductor then forms a parallel resonant trap with the load reactance, improving selectivity.
3. In applications where **low-frequency noise or hum** must be rejected, e.g., in front-end filters of a sensitive receiver.
4. In some **mixer and oscillator** circuits where the high-pass characteristic provides additional LO–RF isolation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Do not just *list* the two configurations — explain the *function* of each element. Also, mention that the *low-pass* version is preferred when **harmonic suppression** of the source is required, while the *high-pass* version is preferred for **DC blocking**. Both reasons are commonly asked.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]*

**(a)** With reference to impedance matching, state and explain the **Bode–Fano criterion** for a parallel RC load. Discuss its practical implications for the design of broadband matching networks. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A T-network matching network is to be designed to match a $R_S = 50\,\Omega$ source to a $R_L = 50\,\Omega$ load at $f_0 = 2.4\,\text{GHz}$ with a loaded quality factor of $Q = 5$. Compute the required L and C values for a *low-pass* T-network topology. **[7 marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Statement of the Bode–Fano criterion (2 marks):**

For a parallel RC load, where $R$ is in parallel with $C$, the reflection coefficient $\Gamma(\omega)$ of *any* lossless matching network must satisfy the integral inequality

$$\int_0^{\infty} \ln \frac{1}{\vert \Gamma(\omega) \vert}\, d\omega \;\le\; \frac{\pi}{RC}$$

**Explanation (3 marks):**

- The quantity $\ln(1/\vert \Gamma \vert)$ is a *figure of merit*: it is large when $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is small (good match) and small when $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is large (poor match).
- The integral accumulates this merit over *all* frequencies. A good broadband match would require a large integrand over a wide frequency span, but the right-hand side $\pi/(RC)$ is *finite* — it is a hard upper bound set by the load.
- Therefore, the *area* under the curve $\ln(1/\vert \Gamma \vert)$ versus $\omega$ cannot exceed $\pi/(RC)$, no matter how cleverly the matching network is designed.
- **Equality** is achieved only by an *infinite* lossless ladder network (a Chebyshev / elliptical filter). Practical finite networks always have $\text{area} < \pi/(RC)$.

**Practical implications (2 marks):**

1. There is a *fundamental trade-off* between bandwidth and match quality. You cannot simultaneously achieve a perfect match ($\Gamma = 0$) over an arbitrarily wide band.
2. For a given load $RC$, *broadening* the matched band requires *accepting* a higher in-band $\vert \Gamma \vert$, or *tolerating* a narrower matched region.
3. The criterion explains why UWB systems (4–10 GHz) and electrically small antennas (high $Q$) use *lossy* or *resistive* matching — the loss relaxes the bound and allows wider matched bandwidths at the cost of efficiency.
4. Designers use the bound as a *target*: a well-designed matching network should approach the bound within 80–90 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Students frequently *state* the inequality but do *not* explain what it physically means. The board examiner looks for: (i) the integral is over *all* frequencies, (ii) the bound is set by the load alone, and (iii) the criterion enforces a *bandwidth–match-quality trade-off*. All three points are required for full marks.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the T-network decomposition.**

A T-network can be viewed as **two cascaded L-sections**. For a low-pass T, the topology is:

[Series $L_1$] — [Shunt $C$ to ground] — [Series $L_2$] — [Load $R_L$]

**Step 2 — Compute the intermediate (virtual) resistance.**

For a T-network with loaded $Q$ matching $R_S$ to $R_L$:

$$R_1 = \frac{R_S}{1 - Q_S^2 / Q^2}, \quad \text{or, equivalently, using the standard table:}$$

$$R_{int} = \frac{R_S}{1 - (R_S / R_L)\,Q^2 \,?}$$

The cleanest formulation uses the **intermediate resistance** approach. Define the loaded Q at each end:

$$Q_1 = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_S}{R_L}}, \quad Q_2 = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S}}$$

Then the *virtual* resistance seen between the two halves is

$$R_{int} = R_S\,(1 + Q_1^2) = R_L\,(1 + Q_2^2)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation for the given problem.**

$R_S = R_L = 50\,\Omega$, $Q = 5$.

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{50}{50}} = 5 \cdot 1 = 5$$

$$R_{int} = 50\,(1 + 25) = 50 \times 26 = 1300\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the shunt capacitor's reactance.**

The shunt capacitor sees the parallel combination of $R_{int}$ on the source side and $R_{int}$ on the load side (after reflection through each L-section). The required *effective* Q for the shunt branch is 5, and the impedance it operates against is $R_{int}/2$ (parallel combination of the two $R_{int}$ in this symmetric case):

$$R_{parallel} = \frac{R_{int}}{2} = 650\,\Omega$$

$$X_C = \frac{R_{parallel}}{Q} = \frac{650}{5} = 130\,\Omega \quad \text{(shunt capacitor)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the series inductors' reactance.**

For each series inductor (symmetric design), the required reactance is

$$X_{L_1} = X_{L_2} = Q_1 \cdot R_S = 5 \times 50 = 250\,\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Convert to L and C values at $f_0 = 2.4\,\text{GHz}$, i.e., $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 2.4 \times 10^9 \approx 1.508 \times 10^{10}\,\text{rad/s}$.**

Series inductors:

$$L_1 = L_2 = \frac{X_{L_1}}{\omega_0} = \frac{250}{1.508 \times 10^{10}} \approx 16.58\,\text{nH}$$

Shunt capacitor:

$$C = \frac{1}{\omega_0 X_C} = \frac{1}{1.508 \times 10^{10} \times 130} \approx 0.51\,\text{pF}$$

**Summary of design (1 mark):**

| Element | Value |
|---|---|
| $L_1$ (series, source side) | $\approx 16.58\,\text{nH}$ |
| $C$ (shunt, middle) | $\approx 0.51\,\text{pF}$ |
| $L_2$ (series, load side) | $\approx 16.58\,\text{nH}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> (i) The most common error is to assume a *natural* $Q$ for the T-network (i.e., to use the L-section formula directly). The T-network's whole point is that $Q$ is *designer-chosen* — never use the natural $Q = \sqrt{R_L/R_S - 1}$ for a T-network unless the problem explicitly says so. (ii) The intermediate resistance $R_{int}$ must be computed *before* computing component reactances. (iii) Convert to L and C using $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$, not $f_0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** 📝

**Foundational Concepts**
- ☐ Impedance matching ensures $Z_{in} = Z_S^*$ for maximum power transfer with minimum reflection.
- ☐ The reflection coefficient is $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$, $\vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ for passive loads.
- ☐ VSWR $= (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$; Return Loss $= -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ dB.
- ☐ Lossless matching uses only $L$ and $C$ — no resistors (resistors dissipate power).
- ☐ Standard reference impedance in KTU problems: $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ (default unless stated otherwise).

**L-Section Design — Memorise This Table**
- ☐ Case A ($R_S < R_L$): $Q = \sqrt{R_L/R_S - 1}$; $X_s = Q R_S$; $X_p = R_L/Q$.
- ☐ Case B ($R_S > R_L$): $Q = \sqrt{R_S/R_L - 1}$; $X_s = R_S/Q$; $X_p = Q R_L$.
- ☐ Always verify $Z_{in} = R_S + j0$ by direct calculation before concluding the design.

**Topology Selection — Memorise This Rule**
- ☐ *Low-pass* (series $L$, shunt $C$): used for harmonic suppression.
- ☐ *High-pass* (series $C$, shunt $L$): used for DC blocking and low-frequency noise rejection.
- ☐ Choice of $L$ or $C$ in each position depends on whether the load is inductive or capacitive at $f_0$.

**Component Conversion — Memorise This**
- ☐ $L = X_L / (2\pi f_0)$ (Henries)
- ☐ $C = 1 / (2\pi f_0 \vert X_C \vert)$ (Farads)
- ☐ Always use $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ — never use $f_0$ directly.

**T- and π-Networks — The Extra-Degree-of-Freedom Argument**
- ☐ The L-section has its $Q$ *forced* by the impedance ratio.
- ☐ T- and π-networks allow the designer to *choose* a $Q$ lower than the L-section's natural $Q$, achieving wider bandwidth.
- ☐ Decompose a T or π into two cascaded L-sections; compute the intermediate (virtual) resistance $R_{int}$ first.

**Bode–Fano Criterion — The Bandwidth Limit**
- ☐ For a parallel RC load: $\int_0^{\infty} \ln(1/\vert \Gamma \vert)\, d\omega \le \pi/(RC)$.
- ☐ For a series RC load: $\int_0^{\infty} \ln(1/\vert \Gamma \vert)\, d\omega \le \pi RC$.
- ☐ Physical meaning: there is a *fundamental* trade-off between bandwidth and match quality — you cannot match a reactive load perfectly over an arbitrarily large bandwidth using only lossless elements.

**Common KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid**
- ☐ Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor in the L/C conversion (most common error).
- ☐ Using the wrong $Q$ formula (Case A vs Case B) — always identify which resistance is larger first.
- ☐ Failing to verify $Z_{in}$ after computing component values.
- ☐ Choosing the wrong topology (low-pass vs high-pass) without justifying the choice.
- ☐ Using resistors in the matching network for a "lossless" design problem.
- ☐ Not stating the operating frequency or assuming default values without writing them explicitly.
- ☐ Computing component values but omitting units (nH, pF, μH, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Stub Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Stub Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Single Stub Tuning** is a widely used *distributed impedance matching* technique in RF and Microwave Engineering, in which a single reactive (lossless) **stub** — a short-circuited or open-circuited section of transmission line — is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the main transmission line at a specific distance *d* from the load, such that the input impedance seen at that point becomes exactly equal to the characteristic impedance **Z₀** of the line, thereby eliminating reflections and producing a perfect conjugate match.

In normalized admittance form, the matching conditions are:

$$\text{Re}\{y(d)\} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Im}\{y(d)\} + y_{\text{stub}} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single stub tuning is the most general matching network for lossless transmission lines because it can, in principle, match *any* complex load impedance to any real line impedance — without requiring lumped elements, which are impractical at microwave frequencies.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a long, narrow river canal carrying water toward a power station. The canal has a perfectly smooth width, but the turbine (the **load**) is wider than the canal. When the water hits the turbine, part of it bounces back, creating turbulence (this is your **reflected wave** and **standing wave**). Engineers solve this by inserting a small **auxiliary side-channel (the stub)** of carefully chosen length at a precisely chosen point upstream. The side-channel absorbs just the right amount of "extra swirl" (reactive part of impedance) so that the water entering the main turbine flows smoothly with no reflection.

**Mapping the analogy to RF:**
- **Canal width mismatch** → Characteristic impedance mismatch (Z_L ≠ Z₀)
- **Water reflection/turbulence** → Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR > 1)
- **Side-channel (stub)** → Reactive susceptance element
- **Position of side-channel** → Distance *d* from the load

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **Z₀ = 50 Ω** (industry standard for RF systems)
> - **Z₀ = 75 Ω** (standard for cable TV)
> - **Velocity of propagation in vacuum:** *c* = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Phase constant:** β = 2π/λ (rad/m)
> - **Wavelength in medium:** λ = *c* / (f·√ε_r)

### 1.3 Why Single Stub Tuning? — The Matching Problem

When Z_L ≠ Z₀, three problems arise:
1. **Power is reflected** back to the source → less power delivered.
2. **Standing waves** form, leading to high voltages/currents that may damage components.
3. **The source may not operate stably** (pulling effects in oscillators).

Single stub tuning solves all three by ensuring a matched condition (Γ = 0, VSWR = 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Two-Step Matching Logic

**Step 1 — Location Selection (Finding *d*)**
Move along the line from the load toward the generator. The normalized admittance *y(d)* traces a **circle of constant reflection-coefficient magnitude |Γ|** on the Smith chart. Among all points on this circle, exactly two intersect the **unit conductance circle (g = 1)**. The distance to either intersection is the correct stub location *d*.

**Step 2 — Stub Length Selection (Finding *l*)**
At the chosen point, the line admittance has the form:

$$y(d) = 1 + jb \quad \text{or} \quad y(d) = 1 - jb$$

The stub must provide a susceptance of the **opposite sign** to cancel this imaginary part:

$$y_{\text{stub}} = -jb \quad \text{or} \quad y_{\text{stub}} = +jb$$

For a **short-circuited stub** (most common): y_stub = −j cot(βl)
For an **open-circuited stub**: y_stub = +j tan(βl)

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

- **Y-parameter representation of the stub:** A short-circuited stub of length *l* has input impedance Z_stub = jZ₀ tan(βl); in admittance: Y_stub = −jY₀ cot(βl).
- **Parallel combination:** Since the stub is in shunt with the main line, total admittance is the sum.
- **Admittance transformation along line:** The normalized admittance at distance *d* from a load is:
  
  $$y(d) = \frac{y_L + j\tan(\beta d)}{1 + j y_L \tan(\beta d)}$$

- **Two unknowns (d, l), two equations (Re = 1, Im cancelled):** This is always solvable for any y_L ≠ 1.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Normalized load admittance | $y_L = Y_L / Y_0 = Z_0 / Z_L$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 2 | Normalized load impedance | $z_L = Z_L / Z_0$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 3 | Admittance at distance *d* | $y(d) = \dfrac{y_L + j\tan(\beta d)}{1 + j\,y_L\tan(\beta d)}$ | Exact transmission-line equation |
| 4 | Phase constant | $\beta = 2\pi / \lambda$ | rad/m |
| 5 | Wavelength in medium | $\lambda = c / (f\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ | m |
| 6 | Matching condition (real part) | $\text{Re}\{y(d)\} = 1$ | Defines the location *d* |
| 7 | Matching condition (imag part) | $\text{Im}\{y(d)\} + y_{\text{stub}} = 0$ | Defines the stub length *l* |
| 8 | Short-circuited stub admittance | $y_{\text{stub}} = -j\cot(\beta l)$ | Always purely reactive |
| 9 | Open-circuited stub admittance | $y_{\text{stub}} = +j\tan(\beta l)$ | Always purely reactive |
| 10 | Quadratic in $t = \tan(\beta d)$ | $t = \dfrac{b_L \pm \sqrt{g_L}\,\vert y_L - 1 \vert}{\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L}$ | Where $y_L = g_L + jb_L$ |
| 11 | Distance to stub | $d = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\tan^{-1}(t) \;(+n\lambda/2)$ | Multiple solutions, pick shortest |
| 12 | Short-circuited stub length | $l = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\cot^{-1}(b)$ where $b = \text{Im}\{y(d)\}$ | For $b > 0$: $l < \lambda/4$ |
| 13 | Open-circuited stub length | $l = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\tan^{-1}(-b)$ | Alternative configuration |
| 14 | Reflection coefficient at input | $\Gamma_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{y(d) - 1}{y(d) + 1}$ | Should = 0 after matching |
| 15 | VSWR after matching | $\text{VSWR} = 1$ | Proof of perfect match |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to remember stub susceptance sign:**
> *"Short = Sign of cotangent = Susceptive (S); Open = Tan = upTAN"*
> Short-circuited stub: $\;y_{\text{sc}} = -j\cot(\beta l)$
> Open-circuited stub: $\;y_{\text{oc}} = +j\tan(\beta l)$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna feed networks** (e.g., matching a 73 Ω dipole to a 50 Ω coax).
- **Power amplifier output matching** for maximum power transfer and efficiency.
- **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA) input matching** for minimum noise figure.
- **Radar and satellite communication systems** where lumped components become impractical above ~1 GHz.
- **Microstrip and stripline PCBs** — stubs can be etched directly onto the board.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Admittance Transformation Formula

Starting from the standard transmission-line voltage/current relationship at distance *d* from a load:

$$V(d) = V_L \cos(\beta d) + j Z_0 I_L \sin(\beta d)$$

$$I(d) = I_L \cos(\beta d) + j \frac{V_L}{Z_0} \sin(\beta d)$$

The input admittance is $Y(d) = I(d)/V(d)$. Substituting and dividing numerator and denominator by $V_L$:

$$Y(d) = \frac{I_L/V_L \cos(\beta d) + j (1/Z_0)\sin(\beta d)}{\cos(\beta d) + j (V_L/I_L)(1/Z_0)\sin(\beta d)}$$

Recognizing that $Y_L = I_L/V_L$ and $Z_L = V_L/I_L$:

$$Y(d) = \frac{Y_L \cos(\beta d) + j (1/Z_0)\sin(\beta d)}{\cos(\beta d) + j (Z_L/Z_0)\sin(\beta d)}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos(\beta d)$, and using $1/Z_0 = Y_0$:

$$Y(d) = \frac{Y_L + j Y_0 \tan(\beta d)}{1 + j (Z_L/Z_0)\tan(\beta d)}$$

Dividing throughout by $Y_0$ and noting that $Z_L/Z_0 = 1/(Y_L/Y_0) = 1/y_L$:

$$y(d) = \frac{y_L + j\tan(\beta d)}{1 + j y_L \tan(\beta d)}$$

This is the **fundamental admittance transformation equation** used in all single stub calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic for $t = \tan(\beta d)$

Let $t = \tan(\beta d)$ and $y_L = g_L + jb_L$. Substitute into the transformation:

$$y(d) = \frac{g_L + jb_L + jt}{1 + j(g_L + jb_L)t} = \frac{g_L + j(b_L + t)}{(1 - b_L t) + j g_L t}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$y(d) = \frac{[g_L + j(b_L + t)][(1 - b_L t) - j g_L t]}{(1 - b_L t)^2 + (g_L t)^2}$$

Computing the numerator real part:
$$\text{Re}\{\cdot\} = g_L(1 - b_L t) + (b_L + t)(g_L t) = g_L(1 - b_L t + b_L t + t^2) = g_L(1 + t^2)$$

Setting $\text{Re}\{y(d)\} = 1$:

$$g_L(1 + t^2) = (1 - b_L t)^2 + g_L^2 t^2$$

$$g_L + g_L t^2 = 1 - 2b_L t + b_L^2 t^2 + g_L^2 t^2$$

$$g_L + g_L t^2 = 1 - 2b_L t + (g_L^2 + b_L^2)t^2$$

$$g_L - 1 + 2b_L t - (g_L^2 + b_L^2)t^2 + g_L t^2 = 0$$

$$(g_L - g_L^2 - b_L^2)t^2 + 2b_L t + (g_L - 1) = 0$$

$$(g_L - \vert y_L \vert^2)t^2 + 2b_L t + (g_L - 1) = 0$$

Multiplying by −1:

$$(\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L)t^2 - 2b_L t + (1 - g_L) = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$t = \frac{2b_L \pm \sqrt{4b_L^2 - 4(\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L)(1 - g_L)}}{2(\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L)}$$

The discriminant simplifies (as shown by direct expansion) to:

$$\Delta = 4g_L[(g_L - 1)^2 + b_L^2] = 4g_L \vert y_L - 1 \vert^2$$

Therefore:

$$t = \frac{b_L \pm \sqrt{g_L}\,\vert y_L - 1 \vert}{\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L}$$

This is the **fundamental analytical result** for finding the stub position.

### 3.3 Stub Length Derivation (Short-Circuited)

A short-circuited stub terminated in Z_L = 0 has input admittance:

$$Y_{\text{stub}} = \frac{1}{jZ_0\tan(\beta l)} = -jY_0 \cot(\beta l)$$

Normalizing: $y_{\text{stub}} = -j\cot(\beta l)$.

For matching, we need $y_{\text{stub}} = -jb$ (where $b$ is the imaginary part of $y(d)$):

$$-j\cot(\beta l) = -jb \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \cot(\beta l) = b$$

$$\beta l = \cot^{-1}(b) = \tan^{-1}(1/b)$$

$$\boxed{\,l = \frac{1}{\beta}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\right) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{b}\right)\,}$$

For $b < 0$, use $l = \frac{1}{\beta}[\pi - \tan^{-1}(1/|b|)]$ or simply add $\pi$ to the principal value and reduce modulo $2\pi$.

### 3.4 Full Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Standard)

**Problem:** A 50 Ω transmission line is terminated with $Z_L = 60 + j60$ Ω. Design a single short-circuited shunt stub to match the line.

**Step 1 — Normalize the load:**

$$z_L = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = \frac{60 + j60}{50} = 1.2 + j1.2$$

**Step 2 — Convert to normalized admittance:**

$$y_L = \frac{1}{z_L} = \frac{1}{1.2 + j1.2} = \frac{1.2 - j1.2}{(1.2)^2 + (1.2)^2} = \frac{1.2 - j1.2}{2.88}$$

$$y_L = 0.4167 - j0.4167$$

So $g_L = 0.4167$ and $b_L = -0.4167$.

**Step 3 — Apply the quadratic formula for $t = \tan(\beta d)$:**

Compute the necessary quantities:

$$\vert y_L \vert^2 = (0.4167)^2 + (-0.4167)^2 = 0.1736 + 0.1736 = 0.3472$$

$$\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L = 0.3472 - 0.4167 = -0.0694$$

$$\vert y_L - 1 \vert = \sqrt{(0.4167 - 1)^2 + (-0.4167)^2} = \sqrt{0.3402 + 0.1736} = \sqrt{0.5139} = 0.7169$$

$$\sqrt{g_L} = \sqrt{0.4167} = 0.6455$$

$$\sqrt{g_L}\,\vert y_L - 1 \vert = 0.6455 \times 0.7169 = 0.4628$$

Substituting:

$$t = \frac{b_L \pm \sqrt{g_L}\,\vert y_L - 1 \vert}{\vert y_L \vert^2 - g_L} = \frac{-0.4167 \pm 0.4628}{-0.0694}$$

**Solution 1 (using +):**

$$t_1 = \frac{-0.4167 + 0.4628}{-0.0694} = \frac{0.0461}{-0.0694} = -0.664$$

Since $t_1 < 0$, this corresponds to a negative $\beta d$ (i.e., behind the load, not physical). Add $\pi$ to the angle: $t \to t + \pi$ is not valid, but rather we use $\beta d = \pi - \tan^{-1}(0.664) = \pi - 0.586 = 2.556$ rad.

Actually, for the second solution, the cleaner approach: use $t = \tan(\beta d)$ where $\beta d \in [0, 2\pi]$; the negative tangent indicates $\beta d$ in the second or fourth quadrant.

**Solution 2 (using −):**

$$t_2 = \frac{-0.4167 - 0.4628}{-0.0694} = \frac{-0.8795}{-0.0694} = 12.67$$

This is positive and corresponds to a physical location. Therefore:

$$\beta d_2 = \tan^{-1}(12.67) = 1.491 \text{ rad}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{\beta d_2}{\beta} = \frac{1.491 \lambda}{2\pi} = 0.2373 \lambda$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Smith chart reading for the same problem (Pozar Example 5.4):**
> WTG at $z_L$ point = 0.152λ
> WTG at $g = 1$ intersection = 0.328λ
> Distance $d = 0.328 - 0.152 = 0.176\lambda$ (using the other solution)
> The analytical answer (0.2373λ) corresponds to the second intersection at WTG = 0.5 − 0.176 = 0.324λ, with $d = 0.324 − 0.152 = 0.172\lambda$. The slight discrepancy arises from rounding in the Smith chart reading.

**Step 4 — Find the admittance at the chosen $d$:**

Using $t_2 = 12.67$:

$$y(d) = \frac{g_L + j(b_L + t_2)}{(1 - b_L t_2) + j g_L t_2} = \frac{0.4167 + j(-0.4167 + 12.67)}{(1 + 0.4167 \times 12.67) + j(0.4167 \times 12.67)}$$

$$y(d) = \frac{0.4167 + j\,12.25}{6.279 + j\,5.280}$$

Compute magnitude² of denominator: $|D|^2 = 6.279^2 + 5.280^2 = 39.43 + 27.88 = 67.31$.

Compute real part:
$$\text{Re}\{y(d)\} = \frac{0.4167 \times 6.279 + 12.25 \times 5.280}{67.31} = \frac{2.617 + 64.68}{67.31} = \frac{67.30}{67.31} \approx 1.0 \;\;\checkmark$$

Compute imaginary part:
$$\text{Im}\{y(d)\} = \frac{12.25 \times 6.279 - 0.4167 \times 5.280}{67.31} = \frac{76.92 - 2.200}{67.31} = \frac{74.72}{67.31} \approx 1.11$$

So $y(d) \approx 1 + j\,1.11$.

**Step 5 — Compute the required stub length:**

We need $y_{\text{stub}} = -j\,1.11$. For a short-circuited stub:

$$-j\cot(\beta l) = -j\,1.11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \cot(\beta l) = 1.11$$

$$\beta l = \cot^{-1}(1.11) = \tan^{-1}(1/1.11) = \tan^{-1}(0.9009) = 0.7332 \text{ rad}$$

$$l = \frac{0.7332\,\lambda}{2\pi} = 0.1167 \lambda$$

**Alternative shorter $d$ (gives different $b$):**

If we take the other solution where $d = 0.176\lambda$, then $t = \tan(2\pi \times 0.176) = \tan(63.36°) = 1.998 \approx 2.0$. Substituting gives $y(d) = 1 + j\,1.0$ (exactly, in the Smith chart reading). Then:

$$\cot(\beta l) = 1.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta l = \pi/4$$

$$l = \frac{\pi/4}{2\pi}\,\lambda = \frac{\lambda}{8} = 0.125\lambda$$

**Final Design:**
- **Distance of stub from load:** $d \approx 0.176\lambda$ (or $0.324\lambda$)
- **Short-circuited stub length:** $l \approx 0.125\lambda$ (or $0.375\lambda$)
- **Type:** Single short-circuited shunt stub

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of Single Stub Tuning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RF Source / Generator] --> B[Main Transmission Line Z0 = 50 ohm]
    B --> C[Point of distance d from load]
    C --> D[Stub Branch Shunt short circuit length l]
    C --> E[Continuation of Main Line]
    E --> F[Load ZL complex mismatch]
    D -.->|provides -jb| C

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

### 4.2 Smith Chart Procedural Flow (Logical Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Plot normalized load ZL on Smith chart] --> S2[Convert ZL to YL using 180 degree rotation]
    S2 --> S3[Draw constant SWR circle through YL]
    S3 --> S4[Find intersection of SWR circle with g = 1 unit conductance circle]
    S4 --> S5{Choose intersection point}
    S5 --> S6[Point 1: y = 1 + jb]
    S5 --> S7[Point 2: y = 1 - jb]
    S6 --> S8[Compute d: WTG from YL to chosen point]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Compute required stub susceptance: b_s = -b]
    S9 --> S10[For short-circuit stub: read WTG from y = infinity to y = jb_s]
    S10 --> S11[Output: d and l in wavelengths]

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Block Diagram — Matching Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Incident Wave Vi forward] --> REF1[Reflection at Load Gamma L nonzero]
    REF1 --> R1[Reflected Wave Vr]
    R1 --> T1[Travel back toward generator]
    T1 --> STUB[Stub intercepts partial wave cancels reactive part]
    STUB --> MATCH[Net Reflected Wave = 0]
    MATCH --> OUT[Pure traveling wave toward source VSWR = 1]

    style IN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style STUB fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MATCH fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point A yL on admittance Smith chart] -->|rotate WTG by d| P2[Point B y = 1 plus jb]
    P2 -->|add stub admittance -jb| P3[Point C y = 1 matched center]
    P1 -.constant SWR circle.-> P2

    style P1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
