## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Butterworth Element Values

**Starting Point:** The squared magnitude of the characteristic function for Butterworth is:

$$
\vert K(j\omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; \varepsilon^{2}\,\omega^{2N}
$$

To find the ladder elements, we factor the characteristic polynomial into **left-half-plane roots** $s_k$ in the complex $s$-plane:

$$
K(s)K(-s) \;=\; \varepsilon^{2}\,(-s^{2})^{N} \;=\; \varepsilon^{2}\,s^{2N} \cdot (-1)^{N}
$$

The roots lie on the **unit circle** in the $s$-plane, located at angles $\theta_k = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + \dfrac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N$.

**Step-by-step extraction of $g_k$ (Foster's theorem applied recursively):**

$$
g_1 \;=\; 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right)
$$

$$
g_k \;=\; 2\sin\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}\right] \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

$$
g_{N+1} \;=\; 1 \quad \text{(for all N with equal terminations)}
$$

**Worked Example — N = 3 Butterworth:**

$$
g_1 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0000
$$

$$
g_2 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{6}\right) = 2 \times 1.0 = 2.0000
$$

$$
g_3 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0000
$$

$$
g_4 = 1.0000
$$

**Resulting prototype ladder:**

$$
g_0 = 1 \;\;-\;\; L_1 = g_1 = 1 \;\;-\;\; C_2 = g_2 = 2 \;\;-\;\; L_3 = g_3 = 1 \;\;-\;\; g_4 = 1
$$

---

### 3.2 Full Derivation of Chebyshev Element Values

**Step 1: Compute $\beta$ from the prescribed ripple $L_{Ar}$ (dB):**

$$
\beta \;=\; \ln\!\left[\coth\!\left(\frac{L_{Ar}}{17.37}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\gamma$ from order $N$ and $\beta$:**

$$
\gamma \;=\; \sinh\!\left(\frac{\beta}{2N}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the auxiliary $a_k$ values:**

$$
a_k \;=\; \sin\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}\right] \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 4: Compute the auxiliary $b_k$ values:**

$$
b_k \;=\; \gamma^{2} \;+\; \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{N}\right) \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 5: Compute $g_1$:**

$$
g_1 \;=\; \frac{2\,a_1}{\gamma}
$$

**Step 6: Recursive computation for $k = 2, 3, \ldots, N$:**

$$
g_k \;=\; \frac{4\,a_{k-1}\,a_k}{b_{k-1}\,g_{k-1}}
$$

**Step 7: Determine $g_{N+1}$:**

$$
g_{N+1} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } N \text{ is odd} \\[4pt] \coth^{2}(\beta/4) & \text{if } N \text{ is even} \end{cases}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — N = 3 Chebyshev with $L_{Ar} = 0.5$ dB

**Given:** $N = 3$, $L_{Ar} = 0.5$ dB.

**Step 1:** Compute $\beta$.

$$
\beta \;=\; \ln\!\left[\coth\!\left(\frac{0.5}{17.37}\right)\right] \;=\; \ln\!\left[\coth(0.0288)\right]
$$

Since $\coth(x) \approx \dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{x}{3}$ for small $x$:

$$
\coth(0.0288) \approx \frac{1}{0.0288} + \frac{0.0288}{3} \approx 34.722 + 0.0096 \approx 34.7316
$$

$$
\beta \approx \ln(34.7316) \approx 3.5477
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\gamma$.

$$
\gamma \;=\; \sinh\!\left(\frac{3.5477}{6}\right) \;=\; \sinh(0.5913) \;=\; \frac{e^{0.5913} - e^{-0.5913}}{2}
$$

$$
e^{0.5913} \approx 1.8063, \quad e^{-0.5913} \approx 0.5536
$$

$$
\gamma \approx \frac{1.8063 - 0.5536}{2} \approx 0.6264
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $a_1, a_2, a_3$.

$$
a_1 = \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = 0.5000
$$

$$
a_2 = \sin\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{6}\right) = 1.0000
$$

$$
a_3 = \sin\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) = 0.5000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $b_1, b_2, b_3$.

$$
\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2} = 0.7500
$$

$$
b_1 = \gamma^{2} + 0.7500 = 0.3924 + 0.7500 = 1.1424
$$

$$
\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = 0.7500
$$

$$
b_2 = \gamma^{2} + 0.7500 = 1.1424
$$

$$
\sin^{2}(\pi) = 0.0000
$$

$$
b_3 = \gamma^{2} + 0 = 0.3924
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $g_1$.

$$
g_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.5000}{0.6264} \approx 1.5963
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $g_2$.

$$
g_2 = \frac{4 \times a_1 \times a_2}{b_1 \times g_1} = \frac{4 \times 0.5000 \times 1.0000}{1.1424 \times 1.5963} = \frac{2.0000}{1.8237} \approx 1.0967
$$

**Step 7:** Compute $g_3$.

$$
g_3 = \frac{4 \times a_2 \times a_3}{b_2 \times g_2} = \frac{4 \times 1.0000 \times 0.5000}{1.1424 \times 1.0967} = \frac{2.0000}{1.2528} \approx 1.5963
$$

**Step 8:** Compute $g_4$ (since $N = 3$ is odd).

$$
g_4 = 1.0000
$$

**Resulting Chebyshev prototype ladder:**

$$
g_1 \approx 1.5963, \quad g_2 \approx 1.0967, \quad g_3 \approx 1.5963, \quad g_4 = 1.0000
$$

---

### 3.4 Frequency & Element Transformation: LP → Bandpass

To convert the prototype lowpass filter into a bandpass filter with **center frequency $\omega_0$** and **fractional bandwidth $FBW$**:

**Frequency mapping:**

$$
\omega \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{FBW}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_0} - \frac{\omega_0}{\omega}\right)
$$

**Element transformation rules:**

| Prototype Element | Bandpass Realization |
|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Series $L_s = g_k$ | Series $L_s^{'} = \dfrac{g_k}{FBW \cdot \omega_0}$, Parallel $C_p^{'} = \dfrac{FBW}{g_k \cdot \omega_0}$ |
| Shunt $C_p = g_k$ | Parallel $C_p^{'} = \dfrac{g_k}{FBW \cdot \omega_0}$, Series $L_s^{'} = \dfrac{FBW}{g_k \cdot \omega_0}$ |

**Resonance check:** Each transformed branch is **series-resonant** (or parallel-resonant) at $\omega_0$:

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_s^{'}\,C_p^{'}}}
$$

This ensures the **midband of the bandpass** coincides with the **cutoff of the prototype lowpass**, preserving the prescribed insertion-loss shape around $\omega_0$.

### 3.5 Impedance Scaling (Level Normalization)

To denormalize the **1 Ω / 1 rad/s** prototype to a practical source/load impedance $Z_0$:

$$
L^{'} \;=\; Z_0 \cdot L, \quad C^{'} \;=\; \frac{C}{Z_0}, \quad R^{'} \;=\; Z_0 \cdot R
$$

### 3.6 End-to-End Numerical Pipeline (Summary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical KTU workflow for a bandpass filter design problem:**
>
> 1. Specify $N$, passband ripple $L_{Ar}$ (dB), cutoff frequencies.
> 2. Compute $\beta, \gamma, a_k, b_k, g_k$ (or use Butterworth closed form).
> 3. Identify which $g_k$ are series $L$ and which are shunt $C$ based on ladder parity.
> 4. Apply frequency transformation to bandpass.
> 5. Compute $FBW$ and $\omega_0$.
> 6. Apply element transformations to obtain bandpass $L^{'}, C^{'}$ values.
> 7. Apply impedance scaling for the desired $Z_0$ (typically **50 Ω**).
> 8. Verify with simulation (optional in KTU exam).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **Insertion Loss Method design procedure** as a block-level functional flow — illustrating how each mathematical specification step feeds the next physical realization step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spec Input: N, Ripple dB, Cutoff omega_c, Impedance Z0] --> B[Choose Approximation: Butterworth or Chebyshev]
    B --> C1[Butterworth: closed-form gk = 2 sin((2k-1)pi/2N)]
    B --> C2[Chebyshev: compute beta, gamma, ak, bk, then gk recursively]
    C1 --> D[Build Lowpass Prototype Ladder: g0-g1-g2-...-gN-gN+1]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E{Desired Filter Type?}
    E -->|Lowpass| F1[Apply Impedance and Frequency Scaling Directly]
    E -->|Highpass| F2[Apply LP to HP Transform: omega to 1/omega]
    E -->|Bandpass| F3[Apply LP to BP Transform: omega to (1/FBW)(omega/omega0 - omega0/omega)]
    E -->|Bandstop| F4[Apply LP to BS Transform: omega to FBW(omega0/omega - omega/omega0) inverse]
    F1 --> G[Final L and C Values]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Verify PLR meets Spec at Sample Frequencies]
    H --> I[Output: Realizable Filter Schematic]
```

### 4.2 Prototype Ladder Structure (Doubly Terminated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source g0 equals 1 ohm] --> L1[Series L equals g1]
    L1 --> C2[Shunt C equals g2]
    C2 --> L3[Series L equals g3]
    L3 --> CN[Shunt C equals gN for even N]
    CN --> Llast[Series L equals gN for odd N]
    Llast --> load[Load gN+1 equals 1 ohm]
    style src fill:#e8f4ff
    style load fill:#e8f4ff
    style L1 fill:#fff4e8
    style C2 fill:#fff4e8
    style L3 fill:#fff4e8
    style CN fill:#fff4e8
    style Llast fill:#fff4e8
```

### 4.3 Element Transformation Block (LP → BP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prototype Series Ls equals gk] --> B[Becomes Series Ls prime equals gk divided by FBW times omega0]
    A --> C[In Parallel with Cp prime equals FBW divided by gk times omega0]
    D[Prototype Shunt Cp equals gk] --> E[Becomes Shunt Cp prime equals gk divided by FBW times omega0]
    D --> F[In Series with Ls prime equals FBW divided by gk times omega0]
    B --> G[Resonant at omega0]
    C --> G
    E --> H[Resonant at omega0]
    F --> H
    style G fill:#e8ffe8
    style H fill:#e8ffe8
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the Insertion Loss Method of filter design. List its two main advantages over the image parameter method.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Insertion Loss Method** is a network synthesis procedure in which the **power loss ratio (PLR)** of a filter is mathematically prescribed as a function of frequency (e.g., Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic) and the corresponding lossless two-port ladder network is realized to match this prescribed response.

**Advantages over image parameter method:**

1. **Optimum design** — yields the lowest-order network for a given specification, whereas the image method produces an overdesigned network.
2. **Predictable response** — the synthesized filter matches the mathematically defined $P_{LR}(\omega)$ exactly at all frequencies (not just at cutoff).
3. **Systematic frequency and element transformations** — LP, HP, BP, and BS filters can be derived from a single lowpass prototype.

*(Any 2 advantages – 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Compare Butterworth and Chebyshev lowpass prototype responses with respect to passband shape, selectivity, and group delay.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Butterworth | Chebyshev |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Passband shape | Maximally flat (no ripple) | Equal ripple of amplitude $L_{Ar}$ |
| Stopband shape | Monotonic | Monotonic |
| Selectivity (roll-off) | Moderate | **Higher** for same N |
| Group delay | More linear (better transient) | More distorted (poorer transient) |
| Pole locations | On a circle | On an ellipse |
| Element value symmetry | Symmetric for equal terminations | Asymmetric |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> Design a 3rd-order **Butterworth lowpass filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 2\pi \times 10^{6}$ rad/s and source/load impedance $Z_0 = 50$ Ω using the Insertion Loss Method.
>
> **(a)** Compute the prototype element values $g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Apply frequency and impedance scaling to obtain the final filter component values. Sketch the circuit. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a):** Compute prototype element values.

For a 3rd-order Butterworth filter with normalized cutoff $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s:

$$
g_1 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2 \times 3}\right) = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0000
$$

$$
g_2 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{6}\right) = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 2 \times 1.0 = 2.0000
$$

$$
g_3 = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0000
$$

$$
g_4 = 1.0000 \quad \text{(equal termination)}
$$

**Prototype Ladder:** Source $g_0 = 1$ — Series $L = g_1 = 1$ — Shunt $C = g_2 = 2$ — Series $L = g_3 = 1$ — Load $g_4 = 1$.

*(7 marks — awarded for correct formula application and numerical evaluation)*

---

**Part (b):** Frequency and impedance scaling.

**Frequency scaling factor:** The prototype cutoff is $\omega_c^{'} = 1$ rad/s. The desired cutoff is $\omega_c = 2\pi \times 10^6$ rad/s. Therefore:

$$
\text{Scaling factor} = \omega_c = 2\pi \times 10^{6} \approx 6.283 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Impedance scaling factor:** $Z_0 = 50$ Ω.

After **frequency scaling** ($\omega_c = 2\pi \times 10^6$ rad/s) and **impedance scaling** ($Z_0 = 50$ Ω):

For a **series inductor** $L_k$ in the prototype (where $L_k = g_k$ for odd $k$):

$$
L_k^{\text{final}} = \frac{g_k \cdot Z_0}{\omega_c} = \frac{g_k \times 50}{2\pi \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{H}
$$

For a **shunt capacitor** $C_k$ in the prototype (where $C_k = g_k$ for even $k$):

$$
C_k^{\text{final}} = \frac{g_k}{Z_0 \cdot \omega_c} = \frac{g_k}{50 \times 2\pi \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{F}
$$

**Numerical values:**

$$
L_1^{\text{final}} = \frac{1 \times 50}{2\pi \times 10^{6}} = \frac{50}{6.2832 \times 10^{6}} \approx 7.9577 \;\mu\text{H}
$$

$$
C_2^{\text{final}} = \frac{2}{50 \times 2\pi \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2}{3.1416 \times 10^{8}} \approx 6.3662 \;\text{nF}
$$

$$
L_3^{\text{final}} = \frac{1 \times 50}{2\pi \times 10^{6}} \approx 7.9577 \;\mu\text{H}
$$

$$
R_S^{\text{final}} = R_L^{\text{final}} = Z_0 = 50 \;\Omega
$$

**Final Circuit:**

```
   50 Ω ──[L1 = 7.96 µH]──┬──[L3 = 7.96 µH]──┬── 50 Ω
                          │                  │
                         ─┴─                 ─┴─
                         ─┬─ C2 = 6.37 nF    ─┬─
                          │                  │
                          └──────────────────┘
```

*(7 marks — awarded for correct scaling formulas, correct substitution, correct numerical evaluation, and labeled circuit diagram)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> A Chebyshev lowpass filter is required with order $N = 3$ and passband ripple $L_{Ar} = 0.5$ dB.
>
> **(a)** Derive step-by-step the prototype element values $g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4$. (8 marks)
>
> **(b)** State and justify the **frequency transformation** required to convert this lowpass prototype to a **bandpass filter** with lower cutoff $f_L = 900$ MHz and upper cutoff $f_U = 1100$ MHz. Compute the center frequency $\omega_0$ and fractional bandwidth $FBW$. (6 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a):** Derivation of Chebyshev element values.

**Step 1:** Compute $\beta$ from $L_{Ar} = 0.5$ dB.

$$
\beta = \ln\!\left[\coth\!\left(\frac{0.5}{17.37}\right)\right] = \ln[\coth(0.0288)] \approx 3.5477
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\gamma$.

$$
\gamma = \sinh\!\left(\frac{\beta}{2N}\right) = \sinh\!\left(\frac{3.5477}{6}\right) = \sinh(0.5913) \approx 0.6264
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $a_k$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$.

$$
a_1 = \sin(\pi/6) = 0.5000, \quad a_2 = \sin(3\pi/6) = 1.0000, \quad a_3 = \sin(5\pi/6) = 0.5000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $b_k$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$.

$$
b_1 = \gamma^{2} + \sin^{2}(\pi/3) = 0.3924 + 0.7500 = 1.1424
$$

$$
b_2 = \gamma^{2} + \sin^{2}(2\pi/3) = 0.3924 + 0.7500 = 1.1424
$$

$$
b_3 = \gamma^{2} + \sin^{2}(\pi) = 0.3924 + 0.0000 = 0.3924
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $g_1$.

$$
g_1 = \frac{2 a_1}{\gamma} = \frac{2 \times 0.5000}{0.6264} \approx 1.5963
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $g_2$ recursively.

$$
g_2 = \frac{4 a_1 a_2}{b_1 g_1} = \frac{4 \times 0.5000 \times 1.0000}{1.1424 \times 1.5963} = \frac{2.0000}{1.8237} \approx 1.0967
$$

**Step 7:** Compute $g_3$ recursively.

$$
g_3 = \frac{4 a_2 a_3}{b_2 g_2} = \frac{4 \times 1.0000 \times 0.5000}{1.1424 \times 1.0967} = \frac{2.0000}{1.2528} \approx 1.5963
$$

**Step 8:** Compute $g_4$ (since $N = 3$ is odd).

$$
g_4 = 1.0000
$$

*(8 marks — full marks for showing each step with correct numerical evaluation)*

---

**Part (b):** Frequency transformation to bandpass.

**Frequency transformation formula:**

$$
\omega \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{FBW}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_0} - \frac{\omega_0}{\omega}\right)
$$

**Justification:** This substitution maps the prototype's $\omega = 0$ (DC) to $\omega = \omega_0$ (bandpass center), $\omega = +1$ to $\omega = \omega_U$ (upper cutoff), and $\omega = -1$ to $\omega = \omega_L$ (lower cutoff). Hence the lowpass passband $[-1, +1]$ becomes the bandpass passband $[\omega_L, \omega_U]$.

**Compute $\omega_0$:**

$$
\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \cdot \omega_U} = \sqrt{(2\pi \times 900 \times 10^{6})(2\pi \times 1100 \times 10^{6})}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi \times \sqrt{900 \times 10^{6} \times 1100 \times 10^{6}} = 2\pi \times \sqrt{9.9 \times 10^{17}}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 9.9499 \times 10^{8} \approx 6.251 \times 10^{9} \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Equivalent:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 10^{9}$ rad/s = $2\pi$ GHz rad/s (approximately).

**Compute $FBW$:**

$$
FBW = \frac{\omega_U - \omega_L}{\omega_0} = \frac{2\pi \times 200 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 10^{9}} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.200
$$

*(6 marks — 3 marks for stating the transformation with justification, 3 marks for correct numerical computation of $\omega_0$ and $FBW$)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\varepsilon$ in Butterworth $P_{LR}$** — Many students write $P_{LR} = 1 + \omega^{2N}$, omitting $\varepsilon^2$. When $L_{Ar}$ is specified, $\varepsilon$ must be computed first.
> 2. **Confusing series/shunt in ladder parity** — For N = 3 (odd), the prototype is: series $L$ — shunt $C$ — series $L$. For N = 4: series $L$ — shunt $C$ — series $L$ — shunt $C$. Getting the alternation wrong loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up $g_k$ indices** — $g_0$ and $g_{N+1}$ are **terminations**, not reactive elements. Many students incorrectly treat $g_{N+1}$ as a capacitor.
> 4. **Not converting $L_{Ar}$/17.37 inside $\coth$** — The 17.37 is **not** a divisor of dB value; it's the conversion factor from dB to nepers multiplied by 2. Mis-using it changes $\beta$ drastically.
> 5. **Skipping the $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \omega_U}$ check** — Examiners often give $\omega_L$ and $\omega_U$ directly; you must confirm the geometric mean equals the desired center frequency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Insertion Loss Method**

- **Definition:** Specify $P_{LR}(\omega)$ mathematically → synthesize ladder to match. This is the **Insertion Loss Method**.
- **Two canonical responses** for KTU exams: **Butterworth** (maximally flat) and **Chebyshev** (equal ripple).
- **Butterworth $g_k$:** Closed-form $g_k = 2\sin\!\big[(2k-1)\pi/(2N)\big]$ for $k = 1, \ldots, N$, and $g_{N+1} = 1$.
- **Chebyshev $g_k$:** Multi-step process — compute $\beta$, $\gamma$, $a_k$, $b_k$, then $g_1 = 2a_1/\gamma$, and recurse $g_k = 4 a_{k-1} a_k/(b_{k-1} g_{k-1})$.
- **Ladder alternation:** $g_0$ (R-source), $g_1$ (series L), $g_2$ (shunt C), $g_3$ (series L), ..., $g_{N+1}$ (R-load).
- **Three transformation layers required in any practical problem:**
  1. **Approximation layer** — choose Butterworth/Chebyshev/Elliptic.
  2. **Frequency transformation layer** — LP → HP/BP/BS via $\omega$-substitution.
  3. **Impedance scaling layer** — $L \to Z_0 L$, $C \to C/Z_0$, $R \to Z_0 R$.
- **Center frequency** of a bandpass is the **geometric mean**: $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \omega_U}$.
- **FBW definition:** $(\omega_U - \omega_L)/\omega_0$.
- **Element transformation rule for LP → BP:** A series $L_s$ becomes a series $L_s^{'}$ paralleled by $C_p^{'}$, where $L_s^{'} C_p^{'} = 1/\omega_0^{2}$. Likewise, a shunt $C_p$ becomes a shunt $C_p^{'}$ in series with $L_s^{'}$.
- **Final result must be verified** by computing $P_{LR}$ at a few sample frequencies (KTU bonus marks for this).
- **The 17.37 in the $\coth$ formula** is the conversion: $17.37 = 20/\ln(10) \times (1/2) \times 2$ — memorize it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active RF Components - Bipolar Junction Transistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Active RF Components - Bipolar Junction Transistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) in RF Context:** The Bipolar Junction Transistor is a three-terminal, current-controlled active semiconductor device (Emitter, Base, Collector) whose high-frequency behavior is characterized by the **hybrid-π small-signal equivalent circuit**, where parasitic capacitances (Cπ, Cμ) and the **base spreading resistance** (r<sub>bb'</sub>) fundamentally limit the transistor's RF performance. The key figures of merit are the **transition frequency** f<sub>T</sub> and the **maximum oscillation frequency** f<sub>max</sub>.

In the KTU 2024 RF Engineering syllabus (Module 2), the BJT is studied as a foundational **active two-port component** for low-noise amplifiers (LNAs), mixers, oscillators, and RF power amplifiers operating from HF up to S-band frequencies. The BJT remains competitive in RFICs and BiCMOS processes because of its **high transconductance-to-current ratio**, excellent **1/f noise corner**, and inherent linearity for low-distortion RF front-ends.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water pipeline with a controllable valve**:
- The **base terminal** is the small hand-wheel controlling the valve's position.
- A tiny rotation of the wheel (small base current I<sub>B</sub>) allows a **large flow** of water between the main inlet (collector) and outlet (emitter).
- The **mechanical inertia** of the wheel represents the **charge-storage time** — you cannot turn the valve infinitely fast.
- The **friction** in the wheel's axle corresponds to the **base spreading resistance** r<sub>bb'</sub>, which dissipates precious RF signal power before it ever reaches the active valve mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, you must master the high-frequency **hybrid-π model**, the derivation of f<sub>T</sub>, the f<sub>max</sub> expression, and the reason why **S-parameters** (not h-parameters) are used to characterize RF transistors above ~100 MHz.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Notes |
|----------|--------|--------------|-------|
| Thermal Voltage | V<sub>T</sub> | **kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** | Sets the scale of transconductance |
| Boltzmann Constant | k | **1.38064852 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Fundamental |
| Electronic Charge | q | **1.602176634 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | Fundamental |
| Reference Frequency | f<sub>0</sub> | **1 GHz (typical RF bench)** | Used for s-parameter extraction |
| Characteristic Impedance | Z<sub>0</sub> | **50 Ω** | Standard RF measurement reference |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model — Step-by-Step Logic

At low frequencies (audio range), the BJT is adequately represented by a simple current source g<sub>m</sub>·V<sub>be</sub> with input resistance r<sub>π</sub>. As the **operating frequency rises into the RF regime**, the following physical phenomena become non-negligible:

1. **Base-Emitter depletion capacitance** (part of C<sub>π</sub>) — junction capacitance that must be charged/discharged every cycle.
2. **Base-Collector depletion capacitance** (C<sub>μ</sub>) — the infamous *Miller capacitance* that gets multiplied by voltage gain, choking input impedance.
3. **Base spreading resistance** (r<sub>bb'</sub>) — the ohmic resistance of the neutral base region between the external base contact and the active emitter periphery. It acts as a *series lossy element* at the input.
4. **Transit time effects** — minority carriers take a finite time (τ<sub>F</sub>) to cross the base, introducing excess phase and limiting high-frequency gain.

The complete **hybrid-π model** is therefore constructed as follows:

```
Input node (b') → connects via rbb' → external base (b)
                 ↓
              rπ ∥ Cπ  (parallel combination)
                 ↓
              Emitter (e, grounded in CE)
                 
Between b' and collector (c): Capacitor Cμ (Miller cap)
From c to e: Current source gm·vb'e (in parallel with ro)
```

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table** — it contains every equation required for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Voltage-to-current gain of BJT | 40 mA/V per mA of I<sub>C</sub> |
| 2 | Input Resistance | $r_{\pi} = \dfrac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T \beta_0}{I_C}$ | Small-signal base-emitter resistance | 1 – 10 kΩ |
| 3 | Unity-Gain Frequency | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | Frequency where \vert h<sub>fe</sub> \vert = 1 | 5 – 50 GHz (SiGe) |
| 4 | β-cutoff Frequency | $f_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{f_T}{\beta_0}$ | Frequency where \vert β \vert drops by 3 dB | 50 – 500 MHz |
| 5 | α-cutoff Frequency | $f_{\alpha} \approx f_T \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}$ | Common-base cutoff | ≈ f<sub>T</sub> for high β |
| 6 | Max Oscillation Freq. | $f_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_T}{8\pi r_{bb'} C_{\mu}}}$ | Absolute RF upper limit (Mason's gain = 1) | 10 – 100 GHz |
| 7 | Short-Circuit C-E Gain | $h_{fe}(j\omega) = \dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{\beta}}$ | Low-frequency current gain roll-off | — |
| 8 | Noise Voltage of r<sub>bb'</sub> | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k T r_{bb'} \Delta f$ | Thermal noise of base resistance | — |
| 9 | Base Current Shot Noise | $\overline{i_{nb}^2} = 2 q I_B \Delta f$ | Fundamental shot noise | — |
| 10 | Transit Time Relation | $\tau_F = \dfrac{C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}}{g_m}$ | Forward transit time | 5 – 50 ps |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic curiosities — they directly govern RF design decisions:

- **Telecom Front-Ends:** The LNA in a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi receiver needs an RF BJT (or HBT) with f<sub>T</sub> ≫ 5 GHz to provide sufficient gain and low noise figure (NF < 2 dB).
- **Radar T/R Modules:** S-band pulsed radar uses SiGe HBT power transistors with f<sub>max</sub> > 50 GHz for high-PAE power amplifiers.
- **Software-Defined Radio:** Wide-bandwidth RF BJTs (e.g., BFP840) with flat S<sub>21</sub> from 100 MHz to 6 GHz enable multi-standard receivers.
- **Oscillator Design:** f<sub>max</sub> sets the absolute upper bound on the realizable oscillation frequency of a feedback oscillator — no transistor can oscillate above ≈ f<sub>max</sub>/2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Short-Circuit Current Gain h<sub>fe</sub>(jω) from the Hybrid-π Model

**Step 0: Setup.** We excite the common-emitter configuration with the output **short-circuited** (V<sub>CE</sub> = 0 AC), inject an AC base current i<sub>b</sub>, and measure the short-circuit collector current i<sub>c</sub>.

**Step 1: Node Equation at the Internal Base Node b'.**
With the output shorted, the voltage across C<sub>μ</sub> equals V<sub>b'e</sub> (since V<sub>c</sub> = 0). Therefore, the base current splits into three parallel paths:

$$
i_b = \frac{v_{b'e}}{r_{\pi}} + v_{b'e} \cdot s C_{\pi} + v_{b'e} \cdot s C_{\mu}
$$

**Explanation:** The current entering the base must supply the resistive path through r<sub>π</sub>, the diffusion + depletion capacitance C<sub>π</sub>, **and** charge/discharge the Miller capacitance C<sub>μ</sub> (which now has V<sub>b'e</sub> across it because the collector is AC-grounded).

**Step 2: Output Current Expression.**
The collector node sees two contributions flowing *out* into the short:
- The dependent current source: g<sub>m</sub> · v<sub>b'e</sub>
- The current through C<sub>μ</sub>: −v<sub>b'e</sub> · s C<sub>μ</sub> (negative because it flows *into* the collector, reducing the external short-circuit current)

$$
i_c = g_m \cdot v_{b'e} - v_{b'e} \cdot s C_{\mu} = v_{b'e}\,(g_m - s C_{\mu})
$$

**Step 3: Form the Transfer Function.**

$$
h_{fe}(s) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{v_{b'e}(g_m - s C_{\mu})}{v_{b'e}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\right)}
$$

Cancelling v<sub>b'e</sub>:

$$
h_{fe}(s) = \frac{g_m - s C_{\mu}}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}
$$

**Step 4: Introduce β<sub>0</sub> = g<sub>m</sub>·r<sub>π</sub> and Factor.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by r<sub>π</sub>:

$$
h_{fe}(s) = \frac{\beta_0 - s r_{\pi} C_{\mu}}{1 + s r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the Pole and Zero.**
The denominator defines a single pole at:

$$
\omega_{\beta} = \frac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_{\beta} = \frac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}
$$

The numerator has a right-half-plane (RHP) zero at:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{g_m}{C_{\mu}}
$$

This RHP zero is critical — at very high frequencies it boosts gain and introduces excess phase, affecting amplifier stability.

**Step 6: Final Compact Form.**

$$
\boxed{\;h_{fe}(j\omega) = \dfrac{\beta_0 \left(1 - j\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_z}\right)}{1 + j\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{\beta}}}\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unity-Gain Frequency f<sub>T</sub>

**Step 1: Magnitude of h<sub>fe</sub> at ω ≫ ω<sub>β</sub> (but ω ≪ ω<sub>z</sub>).**
For typical RF operating points, g<sub>m</sub> ≫ ω C<sub>μ</sub> in the band of interest, so the RHP zero is negligible:

$$
\vert h_{fe}(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_{\beta})^2}}
$$

**Step 2: For ω ≫ ω<sub>β</sub>.**

$$
\vert h_{fe}(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{\beta_0 \omega_{\beta}}{\omega} = \frac{\beta_0}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\omega}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute β<sub>0</sub> = g<sub>m</sub>·r<sub>π</sub>.**

$$
\vert h_{fe}(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{g_m \cdot r_{\pi}}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\omega} = \frac{g_m}{(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\omega}
$$

**Step 4: Set \vert h<sub>fe</sub> \vert = 1 to Define ω<sub>T</sub>.**

$$
1 = \frac{g_m}{(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\omega_T}
$$

Solving:

$$
\omega_T = \frac{g_m}{C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}\;}
$$

**Step 5: Critical Identity f<sub>T</sub> = β<sub>0</sub>·f<sub>β</sub>.**

$$
f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = \beta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})} = \frac{g_m r_{\pi}}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}
$$

This identity is the single most tested relation in KTU RF exams.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Oscillation Frequency f<sub>max</sub>

**Step 1: Mason's Unilateral Power Gain (U).**
For a BJT with unilateralization (i.e., C<sub>μ</sub> neutralized), the maximum available power gain at frequency ω is:

$$
U(\omega) \approx \frac{\omega_T}{8\pi \omega^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}}
$$

**Step 2: Define f<sub>max</sub> as the Frequency where U = 1.**

$$
1 = \frac{\omega_T}{8\pi (2\pi f_{max})^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}} = \frac{\omega_T}{32\pi^3 f_{max}^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute ω<sub>T</sub> = 2π f<sub>T</sub> and Solve.**

$$
1 = \frac{2\pi f_T}{32\pi^3 f_{max}^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}} = \frac{f_T}{16\pi^2 f_{max}^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}}
$$

$$
f_{max}^2 = \frac{f_T}{16\pi^2 r_{bb'} C_{\mu}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_{max} = \frac{1}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{f_T}{r_{bb'} C_{\mu}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{f_T}{8\pi \, r_{bb'} \, C_{\mu}}}\;}
$$

**Physical Insight:** This is the single most important formula for *practical* RF BJT design — f<sub>max</sub> (not f<sub>T</sub>) limits oscillators and power-added efficiency. To maximize f<sub>max</sub>, you must simultaneously:
- Increase f<sub>T</sub> (use HBT, reduce C<sub>π</sub>)
- **Minimize r<sub>bb'</sub>** (heavy base doping, self-aligned emitter)
- **Minimize C<sub>μ</sub>** (small collector area, thick epi)

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Model Problem)

**Given:** I<sub>C</sub> = 5 mA, V<sub>CE</sub> = 10 V, V<sub>T</sub> = 25 mV, β<sub>0</sub> = 100, r<sub>bb'</sub> = 50 Ω, C<sub>μ</sub> = 1 pF, C<sub>π</sub> = 5 pF.

**Step 1 — g<sub>m</sub>:**

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2 \text{ S} = 200 \text{ mA/V}
$$

**Step 2 — r<sub>π</sub>:**

$$
r_{\pi} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.2} = 500 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — f<sub>T</sub>:**

$$
f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \frac{0.2}{2\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{0.2}{3.77 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
f_T \approx 5.305 \text{ GHz}
$$

**Step 4 — f<sub>β</sub>:**

$$
f_{\beta} = \frac{f_T}{\beta_0} = \frac{5.305 \text{ GHz}}{100} = 53.05 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 5 — f<sub>max</sub>:**

$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{5.305 \times 10^9}{8\pi \times 50 \times 1 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.305 \times 10^9}{1.2566 \times 10^{-9}}}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{4.222 \times 10^{18}} \approx 20.55 \text{ GHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** f<sub>max</sub> (20.55 GHz) is roughly 4× larger than f<sub>T</sub> (5.3 GHz). This is typical for well-designed RF BJTs — a healthy ratio indicating low r<sub>bb'</sub>·C<sub>μ</sub> product.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hybrid-π Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (Block Architecture)

The diagram below is a **functional block-architecture** representation showing signal flow and parasitic paths of the BJT hybrid-π model. It maps the electrical interconnections faithfully without attempting a physical transistor cross-section.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PORT B-B PRIME"]
        bNode["BASE TERMINAL B"]
    end
    subgraph BIAS["BIAS AND PARASITIC NETWORK"]
        rbb["RBB PRIME 50 OHM"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["INTERNAL NODE B PRIME"]
        nodeBP["NODE B PRIME"]
        rpi["R PI 500 OHM"]
        cpi["C PI 5 PF"]
        cmu["C MU 1 PF"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT PORT COLLECTOR C"]
        gmSrc["DEPENDENT SOURCE GM VBE"]
        roOut["RO OUTPUT RES"]
        cNode["COLLECTOR NODE C"]
    end
    eNode["EMITTER E GROUNDED"]
    bNode --> rbb --> nodeBP
    nodeBP --- rpi
    nodeBP --- cpi
    nodeBP --- cmu
    cmu --- cNode
    cNode --- gmSrc
    cNode --- roOut
    rpi --- eNode
    cpi --- eNode
    gmSrc --- eNode
    roOut --- eNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **base terminal B** connects through r<sub>bb'</sub> to the internal node **B'**.
- At B', three parallel paths lead to emitter: r<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>π</sub>, and C<sub>μ</sub> (which also reaches the collector).
- The current source g<sub>m</sub>·V<sub>b'e</sub> models the transistor's amplifying action from collector to emitter.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Frequency Response of \vert h<sub>fe</sub> \vert (Bode Plot Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BODE["BJT CURRENT GAIN BODE PLOT"]
        lowGain["LOW FREQ GAIN BETA0 EQUALS 100 DECIBELS AT 40 DB"]
        rollOff["SLOPE MINUS 20 DB PER DECADE FROM F BETA 53 MHZ"]
        unityGain["UNITY GAIN AT F T EQUALS 5.305 GHZ"]
        rhpZero["RHP ZERO AT FZ EQUALS GM BY 2 PI C MU APPROX 31.8 GHZ"]
    end
    lowGain --> rollOff
    rollOff --> unityGain
    unityGain -.-> rhpZero
```

**Interpretation:** Above f<sub>β</sub>, the gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** (single pole). It crosses unity at f<sub>T</sub>. The RHP zero eventually flattens or even boosts gain at ultra-high frequencies.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — RF BJT as a Two-Port S-Parameter Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S2P["S PARAMETER TWO PORT NETWORK"]
        port1["PORT 1 INPUT A1 B1 S11 S12"]
        port2["PORT 2 OUTPUT A2 B2 S21 S22"]
        block["BJT CHIP BLACK BOX"]
    end
    port1 --> block
    block --> port2
    subgraph DEFINITIONS["S PARAMETER PHYSICAL MEANINGS"]
        s11["S11 INPUT REFLECTION COEFFICIENT"]
        s21["S21 FORWARD TRANSMISSION CURRENT GAIN AT RF"]
        s12["S12 REVERSE ISOLATION FEEDBACK"]
        s22["S22 OUTPUT REFLECTION COEFFICIENT"]
    end
    s11 --- port1
    s21 --- port2
    s12 --- port1
    s22 --- port2
```

**Engineering meaning:**
- **S<sub>21</sub>** is the RF equivalent of h<sub>fe</sub>. Above f<sub>β</sub>, \vert S<sub>21</sub> \vert rolls off at −20 dB/decade and reaches 0 dB at f<sub>T</sub>.
- **S<sub>12</sub>** quantifies the *internal feedback* through C<sub>μ</sub>. Reducing C<sub>μ</sub> reduces S<sub>12</sub> and improves stability.
- **S<sub>11</sub>** and **S<sub>22</sub>** are matched against 50 Ω to minimize mismatch loss.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — f<sub>T</sub> and f<sub>max</sub> Design Trade-Off Space

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TRADEOFF["RF BJT DESIGN TRADEOFFS"]
        gmBoost["INCREASE GM RAISE IC OR LOWER TEMP"]
        cpiReduce["REDUCE C PI USE THIN BASE HBT"]
        cmuReduce["REDUCE C MU SMALLER COLLECTOR AREA"]
        rbbReduce["REDUCE R BB PRIME HEAVY BASE DOPING SELF ALIGNED EMITTER"]
    end
    gmBoost --> fTup["INCREASE F T"]
    cpiReduce --> fTup
    cmuReduce --> fTup
    cmuReduce --> fmaxup["INCREASE F MAX"]
    rbbReduce --> fmaxup
    fTup --> fmaxup
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define the transition frequency f<sub>T</sub> of a BJT. Why is it considered the most important figure of merit for RF applications?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** The transition frequency f<sub>T</sub> is the frequency at which the common-emitter short-circuit current gain \vert h<sub>fe</sub> \vert of the BJT drops to **unity (0 dB)**. Mathematically: $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$.
> - **Significance (2 marks):** It represents the intrinsic bandwidth of the transistor — no useful current gain is available beyond f<sub>T</sub>. For RF design, f<sub>T</sub> must be at least **3 – 5 times** the operating frequency to ensure adequate gain and phase margin. It also determines f<sub>max</sub> via $\sqrt{f_T / (8\pi r_{bb'}C_{\mu})}$ and bounds the maximum realizable oscillation frequency.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Explain the role of the base-spreading resistance r<sub>bb'</sub> in limiting the high-frequency performance of a BJT.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** r<sub>bb'</sub> is the ohmic resistance of the neutral base region between the external base contact and the active emitter periphery (typical values: 20 – 200 Ω).
> - **Two key effects (2 marks):** (i) It dissipates RF input power as heat, increasing the **noise figure** by contributing thermal noise $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTr_{bb'}\Delta f$. (ii) It appears in the denominator of f<sub>max</sub>, making it the **single most critical design parameter** for maximizing oscillation frequency. Reducing r<sub>bb'</sub> via self-aligned emitter geometry and heavy base doping can raise f<sub>max</sub> by 2 – 5× without changing f<sub>T</sub>.

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks Total]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Set B]** Starting from the high-frequency hybrid-π model of a BJT, derive the expression for the unity-gain frequency f<sub>T</sub>. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon BJT biased at I<sub>C</sub> = 10 mA, V<sub>CE</sub> = 8 V, V<sub>T</sub> = 25 mV, has the following small-signal parameters: β<sub>0</sub> = 120, C<sub>π</sub> = 8 pF, C<sub>μ</sub> = 1.5 pF, r<sub>bb'</sub> = 80 Ω. Calculate (i) g<sub>m</sub>, (ii) r<sub>π</sub>, (iii) f<sub>T</sub>, (iv) f<sub>β</sub>, and (v) f<sub>max</sub>.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of f<sub>T</sub> [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Hybrid-π model statement (1 mark):* Draw the model with r<sub>bb'</sub>, r<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>μ</sub>, g<sub>m</sub>V<sub>b'e</sub> source, and output resistance r<sub>o</sub>.

*Step 2 — Short-circuit the output (1 mark):* Set V<sub>CE</sub> = 0 (AC), so V<sub>c</sub> = 0. Voltage across C<sub>μ</sub> equals V<sub>b'e</sub>.

*Step 3 — Node equations (2 marks):* 
$i_b = V_{b'e}\left[\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})\right]$ and $i_c = V_{b'e}(g_m - sC_{\mu})$.

*Step 4 — Transfer function (1 mark):* 
$h_{fe}(s) = \dfrac{g_m - sC_{\mu}}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{\beta_0\left(1 - s\dfrac{C_{\mu}}{g_m}\right)}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$.

*Step 5 — Pole frequency (1 mark):* Identify $\omega_{\beta} = 1/[r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})]$.

*Step 6 — Apply \vert h<sub>fe</sub> \vert = 1 in high-frequency limit ω ≫ ω<sub>β</sub> but ω ≪ ω<sub>z</sub> = g<sub>m</sub>/C<sub>μ</sub> (1 mark):* Yield $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$.

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks = 1.4 marks each]:**

*Step (i):* 
$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.4 \text{ S} = 400 \text{ mA/V}
$$

*Step (ii):* 
$$
r_{\pi} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.4} = 300 \text{ }\Omega
$$

*Step (iii):* 
$$
f_T = \frac{0.4}{2\pi \times (8 + 1.5) \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{0.4}{5.969 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 6.70 \text{ GHz}
$$

*Step (iv):* 
$$
f_{\beta} = \frac{f_T}{\beta_0} = \frac{6.70 \text{ GHz}}{120} \approx 55.86 \text{ MHz}
$$

*Step (v):* 
$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{6.70 \times 10^9}{8\pi \times 80 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.70 \times 10^9}{3.016 \times 10^{-9}}}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{2.222 \times 10^{18}} \approx 47.13 \text{ GHz}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks Total] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** With the help of a neatly labelled diagram, explain the **high-frequency hybrid-π equivalent circuit of a BJT**. Define each component and state the typical magnitude of each parameter for a modern RF BJT.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain why **S-parameters** are preferred over **h-parameters** for characterizing RF transistors above 100 MHz. List the four S-parameters of a two-port network and state the physical meaning of each.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model [7 marks]:**

*Diagram (3 marks):* Draw the equivalent circuit (reference SECTION 4 Diagram 1):
- External base (B) → internal base (B') via **r<sub>bb'</sub>** (base spreading resistance).
- Between B' and emitter (E): **r<sub>π</sub>** (input resistance) parallel with **C<sub>π</sub>** (sum of base-emitter depletion and diffusion capacitances).
- Between B' and collector (C): **C<sub>μ</sub>** (base-collector depletion capacitance — the Miller capacitor).
- From C to E: dependent current source **g<sub>m</sub>V<sub>b'e</sub>** (transistor's intrinsic amplification) parallel with **r<sub>o</sub>** (output resistance, due to Early effect).

*Definitions and typical values (4 marks = 0.5 each):*

| Parameter | Meaning | Typical Range |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| r<sub>bb'</sub> | Base spreading resistance | 10 – 100 Ω |
| r<sub>π</sub> | Input resistance β/g<sub>m</sub> | 0.5 – 5 kΩ |
| C<sub>π</sub> | B-E capacitance (depletion + diffusion) | 2 – 20 pF |
| C<sub>μ</sub> | B-C depletion capacitance | 0.2 – 2 pF |
| g<sub>m</sub> | Transconductance | 50 – 500 mA/V |
| r<sub>o</sub> | Output resistance (Early) | 10 – 100 kΩ |

**(b) S-Parameters vs h-Parameters [7 marks]:**

*Why S-parameters are preferred (4 marks):*
1. **Open/short-circuit instability (1 mark):** Active RF transistors can oscillate or self-destruct when measured with open-circuited (for h<sub>22</sub>) or short-circuited (for h<sub>11</sub>) terminations at high frequencies. S-parameters use matched 50 Ω terminations, ensuring **stable, repeatable measurements**.
2. **Difficulty of realizing true opens/shorts (1 mark):** At GHz frequencies, parasitic inductance of a "short" and parasitic capacitance of an "open" invalidate the assumption.
3. **Direct measurement with VNA (1 mark):** Vector Network Analyzers natively measure incident/reflected traveling waves (a, b parameters), so S-parameters require no further conversion.
4. **Cascadability (1 mark):** S-parameters of cascaded networks multiply via T-parameters, easing design of multi-stage amplifiers.

*Four S-parameters (3 marks = 0.75 each):*

| S-Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|------------|------------------|
| S<sub>11</sub> | b<sub>1</sub>/a<sub>1</sub> \vert <sub>a2=0</sub> | Input reflection coefficient (input match) |
| S<sub>21</sub> | b<sub>2</sub>/a<sub>1</sub> \vert <sub>a2=0</sub> | Forward transmission (gain, ≈ h<sub>fe</sub> at RF) |
| S<sub>12</sub> | b<sub>1</sub>/a<sub>2</sub> \vert <sub>a1=0</sub> | Reverse isolation (≈ 0, sets stability) |
| S<sub>22</sub> | b<sub>2</sub>/a<sub>2</sub> \vert <sub>a1=0</sub> | Output reflection coefficient (output match) |

Here a<sub>n</sub> and b<sub>n</sub> are normalized incident and reflected waves at port n.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose 2 – 3 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to AC-short the output** during f<sub>T</sub> derivation — the short-circuit condition is essential; otherwise, the C<sub>μ</sub> branch becomes v<sub>π</sub>·(1 − A<sub>v</sub>), producing the Miller effect.
> 2. **Using h-parameters in numerical problems above 100 MHz** — examiners award zero for physically invalid measurements.
> 3. **Mixing up f<sub>T</sub> and f<sub>max</sub>** — f<sub>T</sub> is current-gain bandwidth; f<sub>max</sub> is the power-gain upper limit. f<sub>max</sub> < f<sub>T</sub> always.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final numerical answer — KTU mandates SI units (Hz, Ω, F, S).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets:**

- [ ] **Hybrid-π model** has six elements: r<sub>bb'</sub>, r<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>μ</sub>, g<sub>m</sub>V<sub>b'e</sub>, r<sub>o</sub>.
- [ ] **g<sub>m</sub> = I<sub>C</sub>/V<sub>T</sub>** (transconductance scales linearly with collector current).
- [ ] **r<sub>π</sub> = β<sub>0</sub>/g<sub>m</sub>** (input resistance is set by bias current and DC gain).
- [ ] **C<sub>μ</sub> is the Miller capacitance** — the dominant bandwidth-limiting factor because it gets multiplied by voltage gain.
- [ ] **f<sub>T</sub> = g<sub>m</sub> / [2π(C<sub>π</sub> + C<sub>μ</sub>)]** — unity-current-gain frequency (current-gain bandwidth product).
- [ ] **f<sub>β</sub> = f<sub>T</sub>/β<sub>0</sub>** — β-cutoff frequency; equals 53 MHz in our worked example.
- [ ] **f<sub>α</sub> ≈ f<sub>T</sub>** — α-cutoff in common-base (slightly higher).
- [ ] **f<sub>max</sub> = √[f<sub>T</sub> / (8π r<sub>bb'</sub> C<sub>μ</sub>)]** — Mason's unilateral power gain = 1.
- [ ] **S-parameters use 50 Ω matched terminations**, hence are stable and measurable at GHz frequencies.
- [ ] **S<sub>21</sub> ≈ h<sub>fe</sub> at RF**; **S<sub>12</sub> ≈ 0** for unilateral design (sets stability via Rollett's condition K > 1).
- [ ] **r<sub>bb'</sub>** is the *single most critical* design lever — reducing it boosts f<sub>max</sub> without sacrificing f<sub>T</sub>.
- [ ] **HBT (Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor)** uses SiGe or AlGaAs emitter to enhance f<sub>T</sub> and lower 1/f noise, extending BJT utility to mm-wave (e.g., 77 GHz automotive radar).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** Solve at least one derivation of f<sub>T</sub> and one numerical on f<sub>max</sub> weekly. Always draw the hybrid-π model before any calculation — KTU examiners award 1 – 2 marks for a correct, fully-labelled diagram even in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction, Functionality, Power Frequency Limitations of High Frequency Transistors, GaAs Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction, Functionality, Power Frequency Limitations of High Frequency Transistors, GaAs Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High-Frequency Transistors (HFTs)** are semiconductor devices specifically engineered to provide useful power gain and signal amplification at radio frequencies (RF, **3 kHz – 300 GHz**) and microwave frequencies (**300 MHz – 300 GHz**). Unlike general-purpose transistors designed for audio or digital logic, HFTs must overcome fundamental physical constraints including carrier **transit time**, **parasitic capacitances**, **base resistance**, and **breakdown phenomena** that degrade performance as frequency increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** "High-frequency transistors are active semiconductor devices whose internal geometry, doping profiles, and material properties (often III-V compounds like GaAs, InP, or SiGe) are optimized to minimize transit time, reduce parasitic reactances, and provide power gain at microwave and millimeter-wave frequencies."

### 1.2 Classification of High-Frequency Transistors

High-frequency transistors are broadly classified into three major families:

| Family | Examples | Typical fT Range | Material System |
|--------|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Bipolar Devices** | BJT, SiGe HBT, InGaP/GaAs HBT | **25 – 500 GHz** | Si, SiGe, GaAs, InP |
| **Field-Effect Devices** | MESFET, MOSFET, HEMT, pHEMT, mHEMT | **50 – 800 GHz** | GaAs, InP, GaN, SiC |
| **Bulk-Effect Devices** | Gunn diode, Tunnel diode, IMPATT | **30 – 300 GHz** | GaAs, InP |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Highway Traffic Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway:**
>
> Imagine electrons inside a transistor as cars travelling on a highway:
>
> - **Low-frequency transistor** = A short, slow truck moving crates (charges) across a small bridge. Even if slow, the bridge is short, so the trip is fast enough.
>
> - **High-frequency transistor** = A Formula 1 race car designed to cross a much longer bridge (the active region) at extremely high speeds. To make the crossing fast, we:
>   - **Shorten the bridge** (reduce base length → thinner epitaxial layers)
>   - **Use a faster car** (higher electron mobility → GaAs or InP)
>   - **Reduce toll booths** (minimize parasitic capacitance and base resistance)
>
> When the **signal frequency exceeds the inverse of the transit time**, the car cannot complete its journey before the next signal cycle arrives, and the transistor loses its ability to amplify. This is the essence of the **frequency limitation**.

### 1.4 Why GaAs for RF? — The Material Advantage

**Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** is a direct-bandgap III-V compound semiconductor with critical physical constants:

- **Electron Mobility (μn): 8500 cm²/V·s** (vs. Si: 1400 cm²/V·s)
- **Electron Saturation Velocity (vsat): 1.0 × 10⁷ cm/s** (vs. Si: 1.0 × 10⁷ cm/s, but reached at lower field)
- **Bandgap Energy (Eg): 1.42 eV** (vs. Si: 1.12 eV)
- **Peak Drift Velocity:** Higher peak velocity than Si at low electric fields
- **Semi-insulating substrate** (ρ ≈ 10⁷–10⁹ Ω·cm) → naturally isolates devices → eliminates parasitic substrate coupling

> [!NOTE]
> The semi-insulating substrate of GaAs is its **single greatest advantage** for RFIC design because it inherently suppresses the **substrate parasitic losses** that plague silicon RFICs, which require expensive SOI or trap-rich substrates to mimic this property.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details of High-Frequency Transistors

#### 2.1.1 Construction of a Microwave BJT (Silicon)

A modern high-frequency BJT uses an **epitaxial planar structure** with critical dimensional features:

- **Emitter:** Heavily doped n⁺ (≈ 10¹⁹ cm⁻³), very shallow depth ≈ 0.1 – 0.3 μm
- **Base:** Thin p-type layer (≈ 50 – 200 nm), doping ≈ 10¹⁸ cm⁻³
- **Collector:** n-type epitaxial layer on n⁺ buried layer for low collector resistance
- **Polysilicon emitter contact** to reduce emitter resistance
- **Multiple emitter/base fingers (interdigitated layout)** to reduce RC delays
- **Substrate:** n⁺ silicon with buried n⁺ layer for low collector series resistance

**Why interdigitated geometry?** Because at high frequencies, the emitter-base junction capacitance Cbe must be charged/discharged through base resistance Rb. The figure-of-merit is the **RC time constant τb = Rb·Cbe**, which must be minimized by:
- **Short, wide emitter fingers** (lower Rb)
- **Thin base region** (lower Cbe)
- **Multiple parallel fingers** (reduce current crowding)

#### 2.1.2 Construction of a GaAs MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor FET)

The **MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is the workhorse of GaAs RF design:

- **Substrate:** Semi-insulating (SI) GaAs (resistivity > 10⁷ Ω·cm)
- **Active channel:** n-type GaAs layer (≈ 0.1 – 0.5 μm thick, doping ≈ 10¹⁷ cm⁻³)
- **Gate:** Schottky metal contact (Ti/Pt/Au) — forms a rectifying junction controlling channel depletion
- **Source/Drain:** Ohmic contacts (AuGe/Ni/Au alloy) alloyed at ≈ 420 °C
- **Channel isolation:** Mesa etching or ion implantation damage to confine current

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MESFET vs. MOSFET:** A MESFET has **no gate oxide**. The gate is a direct metal-semiconductor (Schottky) contact. This eliminates gate-oxide leakage and breakdown issues, but introduces higher gate leakage current. MESFETs are also **normally-on (depletion mode)** devices by default.

#### 2.1.3 Construction of a HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)

The HEMT (also called **MODFET** or **2DEG FET**) is structurally more sophisticated:

- **Heterostructure junction** between two materials with different bandgaps:
  - Example: AlGaAs (wide bandgap, Eg ≈ 2.0 eV) / GaAs (Eg = 1.42 eV)
  - Example: AlGaN / GaN (for power HEMTs)
  - Example: InAlAs / InGaAs on InP (for millimeter-wave HEMTs)
- **2DEG (Two-Dimensional Electron Gas)** forms at the heterointerface due to band bending
- The 2DEG is **physically separated from donor impurities** → dramatically reduced ionized-impurity scattering
- **Electron mobility in 2DEG: ≈ 9000 cm²/V·s at 300 K** (vs. 8500 for bulk GaAs)
- **Low-Temperature Mobility: ≈ 10⁶ cm²/V·s** at 77 K (LN2) → very low-noise amplifiers

#### 2.1.4 Construction of a GaAs HBT (Heterojunction Bipolar Transistor)

- **Emitter:** Wide-bandgap material (e.g., AlGaAs, InGaP) → emitter injection efficiency is high even with low emitter doping → can heavily dope the base
- **Base:** GaAs, very heavily doped (≈ 10¹⁹ – 10²⁰ cm⁻³) → extremely low base resistance
- **Collector:** GaAs
- **Bandgap engineering** allows independent optimization of emitter, base, and collector doping → overcomes the classic Si-BJT doping trade-off

### 2.2 Functionality at High Frequencies — Operational Principles

#### 2.2.1 Transit Time and Cutoff Frequency

The **most fundamental limit** on transistor high-frequency performance is the finite time required for charge carriers to traverse the active region.

For an electron moving through the base of width Wb with saturation velocity vsat:

$$\tau_b = \frac{W_b}{v_{sat}}$$

The **current gain cutoff frequency fT** is defined as the frequency at which the short-circuit common-emitter current gain ∣hfe∣ falls to unity:

$$f_T = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{ec}}$$

where τec is the **total emitter-to-collector transit time**, which includes:

$$\tau_{ec} = \tau_e + \tau_b + \tau_{cs} + \tau_c$$

- **τe** = emitter charging time = re·Cbe (re = emitter dynamic resistance = kT/qIe)
- **τb** = base transit time = Wb²/(2Dn) for diffusion, or Wb/vsat for drift
- **τcs** = base-collector space-charge transit time
- **τc** = collector charging time = rc·Cbc

#### 2.2.2 Maximum Frequency of Oscillation (fmax)

The frequency at which the **power gain** falls to unity (unilateral power gain = 1):

$$f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{f_T}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests this distinction:**
> - **fT** = frequency where current gain = 1 (only)
> - **fmax** = frequency where power gain = 1 (the true figure of merit for RF power amplifiers and oscillators)

#### 2.2.3 Power-Frequency Limitations — The Fundamental Trade-off

There is a **fundamental trade-off between power handling capability and frequency** in any transistor. This is expressed by the **Johnson Figure of Merit (JFOM)** or **Baliga's figure of merit**:

$$(P \cdot f^2)_{max} = \frac{E_{BR}^2 \cdot v_{sat}^2}{4\pi X_c}$$

Where:
- **EBR** = breakdown electric field (V/cm)
- **vsat** = carrier saturation velocity (cm/s)
- **Xc** = parasitic reactance per unit area (Ω·cm²)

For **GaN**, the Johnson FOM is **≈ 27× higher** than for **Si**, and **≈ 10× higher** than for **GaAs** — explaining why GaN HEMTs dominate modern high-power RF applications (radar, base stations, EW).

#### 2.2.4 Noise Considerations

Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise voltage across a resistor R:

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$

Noise figure (Friis cascade for n stages):

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \ldots$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers > 80% of KTU numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Base Transit Time** | $\tau_b = W_b \big/ v_{sat}$ | seconds | Time for electron to cross base |
| 2 | **Cutoff Frequency** | $f_T = 1 \big/ (2\pi \tau_{ec})$ | Hz | Frequency where $\vert h_{fe}\vert = 1$ |
| 3 | **Max Oscillation Freq** | $f_{max} = \sqrt{f_T \big/ (8\pi R_b C_{bc})}$ | Hz | Frequency where power gain = 1 |
| 4 | **Emitter Charging Time** | $\tau_e = r_e C_{be} = (kT \big/ qI_E) C_{be}$ | seconds | Time to charge BE junction |
| 5 | **Total Transit Time** | $\tau_{ec} = \tau_e + \tau_b + \tau_{cs} + \tau_c$ | seconds | Sum of all delay contributions |
| 6 | **Johnson-Nyquist Noise** | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V | Thermal noise voltage |
| 7 | **Johnson FOM** | $(P f^2)_{max} = E_{BR}^2 v_{sat}^2 \big/ (4\pi X_c)$ | W·Hz² | Power-frequency trade-off |
| 8 | **Friis Cascade NF** | $F_{tot} = F_1 + (F_2-1)\big/G_1 + \ldots$ | dB | Total noise figure of cascade |
| 9 | **2DEG Sheet Density** | $n_s = C_g (V_{gs} - V_{th}) \big/ q$ | cm⁻² | Channel charge per area |
| 10 | **Transit Angle** | $\theta = \omega \tau_{ec} = 2\pi f \tau_{ec}$ | radians | Phase lag of carrier transit |

> [!WARNING]
> **Don't confuse transit angle θ with time delay τ.** They are related by $\theta = \omega \tau$ but represent different things — θ is a phase angle (dimensionless), τ is a time (in seconds). KTU questions often test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Device | Reason |
|-------------|------------------|--------|
| **Cellular base-station PAs** | GaN HEMT | Highest power + frequency |
| **LNA in 5G mm-wave** | InP HEMT or GaAs pHEMT | Lowest noise figure |
| **Wi-Fi front-end (2.4/5 GHz)** | GaAs HBT + pHEMT | Cost-effective integration |
| **Satellite down-converter** | GaAs pHEMT | Low noise + high gain |
| **Radar T/R modules (X-band)** | GaN HEMT | High power density |
| **Automotive radar (77 GHz)** | SiGe HBT or InP HEMT | mm-wave capability + integration |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutoff Frequency fT from Total Transit Time

**Starting point:** The small-signal common-emitter current gain hfe(s) for a transistor has one dominant pole:

$$h_{fe}(s) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + s\tau_{ec}}$$

where:
- **hfe0** = low-frequency common-emitter current gain (≈ βDC)
- **τec** = total emitter-to-collector transit time

**Step 1 — Magnitude condition for fT:**

By definition, ∣hfe(jω)∣ = 1 at ω = ωT.

$$\left\vert h_{fe}(j\omega_T) \right\vert = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2}} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Solve for ωT:**

$$\sqrt{1 + (\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2} = h_{fe0}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$1 + (\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2 = h_{fe0}^2$$

$$(\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2 = h_{fe0}^2 - 1$$

For hfe0 ≫ 1 (which is always true in active region):

$$(\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2 \approx h_{fe0}^2$$

$$\omega_T \tau_{ec} = h_{fe0}$$

$$\omega_T = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\tau_{ec}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply definition fT = ωT / (2π):**

$$f_T = \frac{h_{fe0}}{2\pi \tau_{ec}} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{ec}} \quad (\text{for } h_{fe0} \gg 1)$$

> This is the canonical KTU formula and the foundation for all higher-frequency derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Frequency of Oscillation fmax

**Starting point:** Mason's **Unilateral Power Gain (U)** of a transistor in unilateral approximation:

$$U \approx \frac{(f_T / f)^2}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}$$

**Step 1 — Set U = 1 to find fmax:**

$$1 = \frac{(f_T / f_{max})^2}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}$$

$$(f_T / f_{max})^2 = 8\pi R_b C_{bc}$$

$$\frac{f_T^2}{f_{max}^2} = 8\pi R_b C_{bc}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for fmax:**

$$f_{max}^2 = \frac{f_T^2}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{f_T}{\sqrt{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}}$$

This is the textbook KTU result:

$$\boxed{f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{f_T}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}}}$$

**Numerical Example (typical GaAs MESFET):**
- fT = 50 GHz
- Rb = 5 Ω
- Cbc = 0.05 pF = 5 × 10⁻¹⁴ F

Substitute:

$$f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^9}{8\pi \times 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-14}}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$8\pi \times 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-14} = 200\pi \times 10^{-14} \approx 6.283 \times 10^{-11}$$

Now:

$$\frac{f_T}{\text{denominator}} = \frac{50 \times 10^9}{6.283 \times 10^{-11}} = 7.958 \times 10^{20}$$

$$f_{max} = \sqrt{7.958 \times 10^{20}} \approx 8.92 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz} \approx 89.2 \text{ GHz}$$

> **Result: fmax ≈ 89 GHz**, which is consistent with a high-performance GaAs MESFET.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Johnson Figure of Merit (Power-Frequency Limit)

**Starting point:** For a transistor, the maximum breakdown voltage VBR scales linearly with the active layer thickness:

$$V_{BR} = E_{BR} \cdot L$$

The maximum current density Jmax scales with the charge storage and velocity:

$$J_{max} = q \cdot n_s \cdot v_{sat} = \frac{\varepsilon E_{BR}}{\tau_{tr}}$$

where the transit time τtr = L / vsat.

**Step 1 — Maximum RF power into a matched load:**

For a Class-A amplifier, the maximum RF power is proportional to VBR × Jmax:

$$P_{max} \propto V_{BR} \cdot J_{max} = E_{BR} L \cdot \frac{\varepsilon E_{BR}}{L / v_{sat}} = \varepsilon E_{BR}^2 v_{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency limit from transit time:**

The maximum operating frequency fmax,device is proportional to 1/τtr:

$$f_{max,device} = \frac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L}$$

Therefore:

$$f_{max,device}^2 = \frac{v_{sat}^2}{4\pi^2 L^2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the product Pmax · fmax,device²:**

$$P_{max} \cdot f_{max,device}^2 = \varepsilon E_{BR}^2 v_{sat} \cdot \frac{v_{sat}^2}{4\pi^2 L^2}$$

Eliminating L² using the impedance constraint (Xc ∝ L/ε):

$$(P \cdot f^2)_{max} = \frac{E_{BR}^2 \cdot v_{sat}^2}{4\pi X_c}$$

> **This is the Johnson Limit.** It is a *material property* — independent of device geometry. It defines the absolute ceiling of RF power-frequency performance for any semiconductor.

**Numerical Comparison (material ranking):**

| Material | EBR (MV/cm) | vsat (10⁷ cm/s) | Relative JFOM |
|----------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Si** | 0.3 | 1.0 | 1.0 (baseline) |
| **GaAs** | 0.4 | 1.0 | 1.8 |
| **InP** | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.8 |
| **SiC** | 3.0 | 2.0 | 410 |
| **GaN** | 3.3 | 2.5 | 760 |
| **Diamond** | 10.0 | 2.7 | 8100 |

> This explains the modern shift to **GaN** for high-power microwave applications.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Base Transit Time for Drift Transistor

For a uniformly doped base (diffusion only):

$$\tau_b = \frac{W_b^2}{2 D_n}$$

For a **drift transistor** with built-in field due to doping gradient, electrons drift as well as diffuse. The built-in field is:

$$E_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \frac{1}{N(x)} \cdot \frac{dN(x)}{dx}$$

For an exponential doping profile $N(x) = N(0) \exp(-x/W_b)$:

$$E_{bi} = -\frac{kT}{q} \cdot \frac{\eta}{W_b}$$

where $\eta = \ln[N(0)/N(W_b)]$.

The transit time becomes:

$$\tau_{b,drift} = \frac{W_b^2}{2 D_n} \cdot \frac{2}{\eta}\left(1 - \frac{1 - e^{-\eta}}{\eta}\right)$$

For η ≫ 1 (heavy doping gradient), this reduces to:

$$\tau_{b,drift} \approx \frac{W_b}{\mu_n E_{bi}} = \frac{W_b}{v_{drift}}$$

**Example:** Wb = 100 nm, μn = 1400 cm²/V·s, Ebi = 10⁴ V/cm.

$$v_{drift} = \mu_n E_{bi} = 1400 \times 10^4 = 1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$\tau_{b,drift} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}}{1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}} = 7.14 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} = 7.14 \text{ ps}$$

This yields fT ≈ 1/(2π × 7.14 ps) ≈ **22 GHz**, typical for a SiGe HBT.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing GaAs Devices

| Parameter | GaAs MESFET | GaAs HEMT (pHEMT) | GaAs HBT |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Operating Principle** | Field effect via Schottky gate | Field effect via 2DEG | Bipolar injection |
| **Typical fT** | 30 – 50 GHz | 100 – 200 GHz | 50 – 300 GHz |
| **Typical fmax** | 60 – 100 GHz | 200 – 500 GHz | 100 – 500 GHz |
| **Noise Figure (10 GHz)** | 1.5 – 2.5 dB | 0.5 – 1.0 dB | 1.5 – 3.0 dB |
| **Breakdown Voltage** | Low (≈ 10 V) | Low (≈ 8 V) | Medium (≈ 15 V) |
| **Power Density** | 0.5 W/mm | 0.8 W/mm | 1.5 W/mm |
| **Threshold Control** | Difficult (normally-on) | Difficult (normally-on) | Excellent (NPN) |
| **Integration with Digital** | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| **Key Use** | MMIC power | LNA, mm-wave | Mixed-signal RFIC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

graph TD
    node1[Semi Insulating GaAs Substrate]
    node2[N plus Buried Layer Optional]

    node3 --> node7
    node7 --> node6
    node6 --> node8
