```mermaid
    node9 --> node6
    node6 --> node10
    node10 --> node5
```

### 4.3 Energy Band Diagram: AlGaAs / GaAs HEMT (Heterostructure)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Metal Schottky Gate]
    nodeB[AlGaAs Wide Bandgap Donor Layer]
    nodeC[Spacer Layer Undoped]
    nodeD[GaAs Channel 2DEG Here]
    nodeE[Buffer Layer]
    nodeF[Semi Insulating Substrate]

    nodeA -- Conduction Band Step Down --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Conduction Band Discontinuity --> nodeC
    nodeC -- Quantum Well 2DEG Formation --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

### 4.4 Power-Frequency Limitation Trade-Off (Johnson Limit)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Increase Frequency f]
    node2[Reduce Active Layer Thickness L]
    node3[Transit Time Decreases]
    node4[fT and fmax Increase]
    node5[Breakdown Voltage VBR Decreases]
    node6[Maximum Power Pmax Decreases]
    node7[Power Frequency Product Constant Johnson Limit]
    node8[Material Choice GaN best]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
    node1 --> node5 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
```

### 4.5 Functional Block Diagram: fT and fmax Extraction

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Small Signal hfe S Parameter Measurement]
    nodeB[Extrapolate Current Gain at 20 dB decade]
    nodeC[Find Frequency where hfe equals 1]
    nodeD[That Frequency equals fT]
    nodeE[Measure S21 and S12 parameters]
    nodeF[Compute Mason Unilateral Gain U]
    nodeG[Find Frequency where U equals 1]
    nodeH[That Frequency equals fmax]
    nodeI[Apply formula fmax equals sqrt of fT divided by 8 pi Rb Cbc]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two different parameters?** fT tells you the **current-handling speed** (good for logic, transimpedance amplifiers). fmax tells you the **power-gain capability** (good for power amplifiers and oscillators). A high fT does **NOT** automatically mean a high fmax — the ratio fmax/fT is determined by the base-resistance-collector-capacitance product Rb·Cbc, which is purely a layout and process engineering challenge.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "cutoff frequency" (fT) of a high-frequency transistor. Why is it important for RF design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The cutoff frequency fT is the frequency at which the small-signal common-emitter current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ of a transistor falls to unity. It is mathematically defined as:

$$f_T = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{ec}}$$

where $\tau_{ec}$ is the total emitter-to-collector transit time.

**Importance for RF design (1.5 marks):**

1. fT sets the **upper frequency limit** for useful current amplification — above fT, the device cannot provide current gain.
2. fT is the primary **figure of merit** used to compare high-frequency transistors from different technologies (Si BJT vs. SiGe HBT vs. GaAs HEMT).
3. fT is a direct measure of the **device intrinsic speed** and is heavily influenced by base width, carrier velocity, and parasitic capacitances.

> **Valuation tip:** KTU awards 1 mark for the correct formula, 1 mark for stating the physical meaning, and 1 mark for any one design implication. Do not write more than ½ page.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any THREE advantages of GaAs over silicon for RF applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher electron mobility** — GaAs has electron mobility of 8500 cm²/V·s compared to silicon's 1400 cm²/V·s (≈ 6× higher). This enables **faster transit times** and **lower series resistance**, both of which directly increase fT and fmax.

2. **Semi-insulating substrate** — GaAs wafers have resistivity greater than 10⁷ Ω·cm in their semi-insulating form. This provides **natural device isolation** with extremely low parasitic substrate capacitance, eliminating the need for junction isolation or expensive SOI substrates.

3. **Higher peak electron velocity** — At typical operating electric fields, GaAs exhibits **higher electron drift velocity** than silicon, reducing the base transit time τb. Additionally, GaAs has a **negative differential mobility region** that enables bulk-effect microwave devices like **Gunn diodes** — impossible in silicon.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of a **GaAs MESFET**. Discuss why GaAs is preferred over silicon at microwave frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit, derive the expression for the **cutoff frequency fT** of a high-frequency transistor in terms of transit time. State clearly the contributions of each delay component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Construction of GaAs MESFET (3 marks):**

A GaAs MESFET is built on a **semi-insulating (SI) GaAs substrate** (resistivity > 10⁷ Ω·cm). The following layers/regions are present:

- **Active channel:** An n-type GaAs epitaxial layer, typically 0.1 – 0.5 μm thick with doping concentration ≈ 10¹⁷ cm⁻³, grown on the SI substrate by MBE or MOCVD.
- **Source and Drain:** Ohmic contacts made of AuGe/Ni/Au alloy, annealed at ≈ 420 °C to form low-resistance non-rectifying contacts.
- **Gate:** A **Schottky metal contact** (typically Ti/Pt/Au) deposited directly on the n-GaAs channel. The Schottky barrier creates a rectifying junction whose depletion width modulates the channel conductance.
- **Channel isolation:** Mesa etching or ion implantation damage regions are used to confine current flow between source and drain.
- **Geometry:** The gate length Lg is the critical dimension. For microwave operation, Lg ≤ 0.5 μm is required; for mm-wave, Lg ≤ 0.1 μm.

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**

When a negative voltage Vgs is applied to the Schottky gate (gate reverse-biased), the depletion region beneath the gate widens, **pinching off** the conducting channel. This modulates the drain current Id as:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

for $V_{GS} \geq V_P$, where VP is the pinch-off voltage (typically −1 to −5 V). When the drain is biased (VDS > 0), electrons flow from source to drain through the un-depleted portion of the channel, and the gate voltage controls this flow.

**Why GaAs preferred over Si at microwave frequencies (2 marks):**

1. **Higher electron mobility** (8500 vs. 1400 cm²/V·s) → lower parasitic resistance and faster transit time → higher fT and fmax.
2. **Semi-insulating substrate** → eliminates parasitic capacitance to substrate, critical for high-frequency operation.
3. **Higher breakdown field** and ability to form Schottky barriers directly on the semiconductor (no native oxide like SiO₂ in silicon) → enabling MESFETs that are impossible to fabricate in standard silicon technology.
4. **Ability to form heterostructures** (with AlGaAs, InGaP) → enabling HEMTs and HBTs not possible in pure silicon.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Small-signal model of transistor (2 marks):**

A high-frequency transistor in common-emitter configuration has a hybrid-π small-signal equivalent circuit. The dominant signal path involves the emitter current ie flowing through:
- The emitter-base capacitance Cbe
- The base region (transit time τb)
- The base-collector capacitance Cbc
- The collector depletion region transit time τcs

**Total transit time (2 marks):**

The total emitter-to-collector transit time is the sum of four delays:

$$\tau_{ec} = \tau_e + \tau_b + \tau_{cs} + \tau_c$$

- **τe** = emitter RC delay = re · Cbe = (kT/qIe) · Cbe
- **τb** = base transit time = Wb/(2Dn) for diffusion; Wb/vsat for drift
- **τcs** = collector space-charge transit time = Xc/(2vsat)
- **τc** = collector RC delay = rc · Cbc

**Derivation of fT (3 marks):**

The small-signal current gain hfe(s) has one dominant pole:

$$h_{fe}(s) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + s\tau_{ec}}$$

At ω = ωT, the magnitude is unity:

$$\left\vert h_{fe}(j\omega_T)\right\vert = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_T \tau_{ec})^2}} = 1$$

Since $h_{fe0} \gg 1$:

$$\omega_T \tau_{ec} \approx h_{fe0}$$

$$f_T = \frac{h_{fe0}}{2\pi \tau_{ec}} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{ec}}$$

> **Final boxed answer:** $\boxed{f_T = 1/(2\pi \tau_{ec})}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and operation of a **High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)**. How is the 2DEG formed? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **maximum frequency of oscillation (fmax)** of a transistor in terms of fT, Rb and Cbc. Explain why fmax is more relevant than fT for RF power amplifier design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Construction of HEMT (3 marks):**

The HEMT is a heterostructure field-effect transistor. Key layers:

- **Wide-bandgap material:** AlGaAs (Eg ≈ 2.0 eV) grown on GaAs substrate.
- **Donor layer:** Heavily doped n⁺ AlGaAs (Si-doped, ≈ 10¹⁸ cm⁻³).
- **Spacer layer:** Undoped AlGaAs, ≈ 2 – 5 nm thick, to physically separate ionized donors from the channel.
- **Channel:** Undoped GaAs, where the 2DEG forms at the heterointerface.
- **Buffer layer:** Undoped GaAs to isolate channel from substrate defects.
- **Schottky gate:** Ti/Pt/Au, controls 2DEG density.
- **Ohmic source/drain:** AuGe/Ni/Au alloy.

**2DEG Formation (2 marks):**

At the AlGaAs/GaAs heterointerface, the **conduction band has a discontinuity** $\Delta E_c \approx 0.3$ eV. Free electrons from the n⁺ AlGaAs donors fall into the lower-energy GaAs side and become **trapped in the triangular quantum well** formed at the interface. Because the spacer layer is undoped, the 2DEG electrons are **physically separated** from the ionized donor impurities → dramatically reduced ionized-impurity scattering → **mobility rises to ≈ 9000 cm²/V·s at 300 K** and even higher at cryogenic temperatures.

**Operation (2 marks):**

A negative gate bias Vgs depletes the 2DEG by raising the conduction band edge above the Fermi level in the well. When $V_{gs} < V_{th}$, the 2DEG is fully depleted and no current flows. When $V_{gs} > V_{th}$ (less negative), the 2DEG density increases linearly:

$$n_s = \frac{C_g}{q}(V_{gs} - V_{th})$$

A drain bias causes electrons to drift through the 2DEG channel from source to drain with extremely high velocity → high transconductance gm → high fT and fmax.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Mason's Unilateral Power Gain (3 marks):**

For a transistor in unilateral approximation (neutralizing the feedback via Cbc), the maximum available power gain is Mason's U:

$$U = \frac{1}{4f^2 \cdot 8\pi R_b C_{bc}} \cdot \left(\frac{f_T}{f}\right)^2$$

Simplified:

$$U = \frac{(f_T / f)^2}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}$$

**Deriving fmax (3 marks):**

Setting U = 1 to find the frequency at which power gain = 1:

$$1 = \frac{(f_T / f_{max})^2}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}$$

$$(f_T)^2 = f_{max}^2 \cdot 8\pi R_b C_{bc}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{f_T}{8\pi R_b C_{bc}}}}$$

**Why fmax > fT for RF PA design (1 mark):**

A power amplifier must deliver **power** to a load, not just current. fmax is the frequency at which the **power gain** drops to unity — beyond fmax the device cannot deliver useful RF power. Although fT is higher than fmax (because current gain = 1 at a higher frequency than power gain = 1), an RF PA designer must size the device so that fmax is comfortably above the operating frequency, otherwise the PA will have insufficient gain to drive the output stage.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing fT and fmax:** Students often interchange these or use them interchangeably. fT = current gain = 1; fmax = power gain = 1. Examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks if you fail to distinguish.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the semi-insulating substrate** as GaAs's key advantage — examiners allocate 1 – 2 marks specifically for this point.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of inequality:** When asked "why is fmax > fT?" — answer correctly: fmax < fT always. The question is poorly worded if asked this way; check the formula.
>
> 4. **Mixing up τe and τb:** Emitter charging time τe = re·Cbe involves the dynamic emitter resistance re = kT/qIe. Base transit time τb involves diffusion or drift velocity. Examiners check this carefully.
>
> 5. **Missing the Johnson FOM:** Many students forget to mention the **P·f² product** when discussing power-frequency limitations. KTU consistently tests this in 14-mark questions.
>
> 6. **Wrong doping notation:** Always write doping as n⁺ (heavily doped n-type), p (lightly doped p-type), etc. — examiners are strict about semiconductor notation.
>
> 7. **Drawing wrong Mermin limit:** Some students cite the 2DEG incorrectly — write it as "Two-Dimensional Electron Gas", not "2-dimensional electron layer" or similar informal terms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **fT definition and formula:** $f_T = 1/(2\pi \tau_{ec})$ — frequency where current gain $\vert h_{fe}\vert = 1$.
- [ ] **fmax formula:** $f_{max} = \sqrt{f_T / (8\pi R_b C_{bc})}$ — frequency where unilateral power gain U = 1.
- [ ] **Total transit time:** $\tau_{ec} = \tau_e + \tau_b + \tau_{cs} + \tau_c$ — four distinct contributions, each must be named.
- [ ] **GaAs advantages:** High mobility (8500 cm²/V·s), semi-insulating substrate, ability to form heterostructures, direct bandgap.
- [ ] **Johnson Figure of Merit:** $(Pf²)_{max} = E_{BR}^2 v_{sat}^2 / (4\pi X_c)$ — fundamental P-f trade-off, material-dependent.
- [ ] **2DEG:** Two-Dimensional Electron Gas, formed at AlGaAs/GaAs heterointerface; physically separated from donors → high mobility.
- [ ] **MESFET vs HEMT vs HBT:** Three different device families on GaAs — memorize the comparison matrix in Section 3.5.
- [ ] **Johnson-Nyquist noise:** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — fundamental thermal noise limit.
- [ ] **Friis cascade:** First stage dominates noise; design LNA first.
- [ ] **Material ranking by Johnson FOM:** Si < GaAs < InP < SiC < GaN < Diamond. GaN is the modern king of high-power microwave.
- [ ] **Drift transistor base transit time:** Heavy base doping gradient creates built-in field → drift-assisted transport → much shorter τb than pure diffusion.
- [ ] **Why normally-on:** Both MESFETs and HEMTs are normally-on (depletion-mode) devices because the Schottky gate is reverse-biased; threshold control is a circuit design challenge.
- [ ] **HBT advantage over BJT:** Wide-bandgap emitter allows low emitter doping + high base doping → low base resistance → high fmax.
- [ ] **Frequency ranges:** RF = 3 kHz – 300 GHz; Microwave = 300 MHz – 300 GHz; mm-wave = 30 – 300 GHz.
- [ ] **Remember kT/q = 25.85 mV** at room temperature — used in emitter resistance calculation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** If you only have time to memorize 5 things from this topic, memorize:
> 1. **fT formula** + its derivation
> 2. **fmax formula** + its derivation
> 3. **Johnson FOM** + the material ranking table
> 4. **GaAs vs Si advantages** (3 specific points)
> 5. **HEMT 2DEG concept** (why it's high-mobility)
>
> These five topics account for **> 60% of marks** in any typical KTU question on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of RF Field Effect Transistors and High Electron Mobility Transistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of RF Field Effect Transistors and High Electron Mobility Transistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 RF Field Effect Transistors (RF FETs)

An **RF Field Effect Transistor (RF FET)** is a specialized class of FET whose physical structure, doping profile, and gate geometry are engineered to deliver high gain, low noise, and efficient operation at microwave and millimeter-wave frequencies (typically **300 MHz to 300 GHz**). The most common variants encountered in RF engineering are the **MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor FET)** and the **HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)**, both predominantly fabricated on compound semiconductors such as **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)**, **Indium Phosphide (InP)**, **Gallium Nitride (GaN)**, and **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** RF FETs are voltage-controlled devices in which the transconductance and transit-time of charge carriers are optimized through hetero-epitaxial growth and sub-micron gate lithography to minimize parasitic capacitances and maximize the unity-current-gain cutoff frequency ($f_T$) and maximum oscillation frequency ($f_{max}$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap & Tunnel" Picture

Imagine a water pipeline with a control valve. In a conventional silicon MOSFET, the water (electrons) flows through a "rough" channel where it bumps into impurities (dopant atoms), losing energy as heat. The valve controls the flow, but the water arrives at the other end a little muddy and slow.

Now imagine an **RF FET** as the same pipeline, but:
- The pipe is made of a very smooth ceramic (compound semiconductor).
- The valve is so thin (**sub-micron gate**, sometimes 0.1 µm) that it can snap open and shut billions of times per second.
- The water travels in a frictionless "sheet" on top of the pipe (the **2-Dimensional Electron Gas** in HEMTs) instead of through the rough interior.

The result: cleaner, faster, and more controllable flow — exactly what we need for **high-frequency RF signals**.

### 1.3 High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)

A **High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)** — also called a **Modulation-Doped FET (MODFET)** or **Heterojunction FET (HFET)** — is an RF FET that exploits the bandgap discontinuity at a **heterojunction** (interface between two different semiconductor materials) to confine electrons in a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)**. Because the 2DEG is physically separated from the donor impurity atoms, ionized-impurity scattering is dramatically reduced, yielding very high electron mobilities and exceptionally high transit velocities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant — Standard Metrics:**
> - **Electron mobility in undoped GaAs:** ≈ **8500 cm²/V·s**
> - **Electron mobility in 2DEG (AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT @ 77 K):** ≈ **1,000,000 cm²/V·s** (≈ 100× enhancement)
> - **Typical $f_T$ of GaAs HEMT:** **50–200 GHz**
> - **Typical $f_T$ of InP HEMT:** **> 500 GHz**

### 1.4 Why RF FETs are NOT Conventional MOSFETs at High Frequencies

| Parameter | Silicon MOSFET | RF FET (MESFET/HEMT) |
|---|---|---|
| Semiconductor | Si (E_g = 1.12 eV) | GaAs, InP, GaN (E_g > 1.4 eV) |
| Electron mobility | ~1400 cm²/V·s | 8500–12000 cm²/V·s |
| Gate dielectric | SiO₂ (native) | Schottky metal (MESFET) or heterojunction (HEMT) |
| Parasitic capacitance | High | Ultra-low |
| Noise figure @ 10 GHz | Poor | Excellent (NF < 1 dB possible) |
| Cutoff frequency $f_T$ | < 50 GHz | Up to **> 500 GHz** |

### 1.5 Historical Context and Evolution

The progression of RF transistors follows a clear evolutionary chain designed to push the **frequency frontier** ever higher:

1. **Silicon BJT (1950s)** → limited by minority carrier storage.
2. **Silicon MOSFET (1960s)** → gate oxide capacitance limits $f_T$.
3. **GaAs MESFET (1970s)** → Schottky gate removes oxide; higher mobility.
4. **HEMT / MODFET (1980 — Mimura, 1980; Delagebeaudeuf, 1980)** → 2DEG enables record low noise.
5. **InP HEMT, mHEMT, GaN HEMT (1990s–Present)** → mm-wave, high-power, and 5G/6G applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor FET) — Operating Principle

The MESFET replaces the oxide-semiconductor interface of a MOSFET with a **metal-semiconductor (Schottky) barrier**. A typical GaAs MESFET consists of:

- A **semi-insulating (SI) GaAs substrate** (resistivity ≈ **10⁷–10⁹ Ω·cm**) that minimizes parasitic substrate capacitance and leakage.
- An **n-type active channel** grown epitaxially on top, with doping $N_D$ and thickness $a$ (typically 0.1–0.3 µm).
- **Source and Drain ohmic contacts** (alloyed AuGe/Ni/Au) at the two ends of the channel.
- A **Schottky gate contact** (Ti/Pt/Au) directly on top of the channel.

#### Operating Logic — Step by Step

1. A **negative gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$** reverse-biases the Schottky junction, creating a **depletion region** of width $W(x)$ underneath the gate.
2. As $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative, $W$ widens and **pinches off** the conducting channel. The pinch-off voltage is:

$$V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

where $q$ is the electron charge, $N_D$ is the channel doping, $a$ is the channel thickness, and $\varepsilon_s$ is the semiconductor permittivity.

3. When $V_{DS} > 0$ is applied, electrons drift from source to drain, producing $I_{DS}$.
4. The **velocity-saturation** phenomenon in GaAs (peak velocity ≈ **1.0–1.5 × 10⁷ cm/s** at $E \approx 3.5$ kV/cm) is the key to high-frequency operation — carriers reach their saturation velocity in a very short distance under the gate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Saturation in GaAs:** Unlike silicon, GaAs exhibits a **negative differential mobility** region. Electrons transfer from the high-mobility Γ valley to low-mobility L and X valleys as the electric field increases, leading to a sharp velocity peak and saturation. This is what gives GaAs MESFETs their superior high-frequency response over silicon.

### 2.2 The HEMT — Operating Principle

The HEMT is built around a **heterojunction**, most commonly the **AlGaAs / GaAs** interface (or AlGaN / GaN for power applications).

#### Layer Structure (from bottom to top)

1. **SI GaAs substrate**
2. **Undoped GaAs buffer layer** (~1 µm) — provides a clean crystal template.
3. **Undoped spacer layer** (~3–5 nm of AlGaAs) — physically separates donors from 2DEG.
4. **n-type AlGaAs donor layer** (Si-doped, $N_D \approx 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, ~30 nm)
5. **Undoped AlGaAs Schottky layer** (~30 nm) — prevents gate leakage.
6. **Schottky gate metal** (Ti/Pt/Au)

#### The 2DEG Formation

At the AlGaAs / GaAs interface, the **conduction band discontinuity** ($\Delta E_C \approx 0.3$ eV for Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As/GaAs with $x \approx 0.3$) creates a **quantum well** — a triangular potential well about **10 nm wide**. Electrons donated by the Si donors in the AlGaAs fall into this well and become **quantized into discrete sub-bands** in the direction perpendicular to the interface, but are free to move in the plane of the interface. This is the **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2DEG is so fast:** Because the GaAs buffer is undoped, the 2DEG electrons experience **no ionized-impurity scattering** at room temperature. Scattering is dominated only by **lattice phonons**, so mobility is extraordinarily high. At cryogenic temperatures (77 K, 4 K), mobility skyrockets to **> 10⁶ cm²/V·s**, making HEMTs the lowest-noise transistors ever built for radio astronomy and deep-space receivers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinch-off voltage (MESFET) | $V_P = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$ | Threshold for channel pinch-off; **V** |
| 2 | Depletion width under gate | $W = a\sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}}$ | where $V_{bi}$ = built-in potential; **m** |
| 3 | Transconductance (saturation) | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_{DS}}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg|_{V_{DS}}$ | **Siemens (S)** or **mS/mm** (per unit gate width) |
| 4 | Saturation drain current (ideal) | $I_{DSS} = \dfrac{\mu_n \varepsilon_s W_d Z}{2 a L}\,(V_{GS} - V_P)^2$ | where $Z$ = gate width, $L$ = gate length, $W_d$ = channel depth |
| 5 | Transit time under gate | $\tau_t = \dfrac{L}{v_{sat}}$ | $v_{sat}$ ≈ 1×10⁷ cm/s for GaAs; **s** |
| 6 | Unity-current-gain cutoff freq. | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$ | **Hz**; typical 50–200 GHz for HEMTs |
| 7 | Maximum oscillation freq. | $f_{max} = \dfrac{f_T}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{r_{DS}}{R_g + R_s + R_i}}$ | depends on parasitic resistances |
| 8 | 2DEG sheet carrier density | $n_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s \cdot \Delta V_{th}}{q\, d}$ | **cm⁻²**; $\Delta V_{th}$ = threshold shift |
| 9 | Fukui Noise Equation | $NF_{min} = 1 + K_f \dfrac{f}{f_T}\sqrt{g_m (R_g + R_s)}$ | Used to estimate minimum NF |
| 10 | Power gain (Mason's) | $U = \dfrac{(f_{max}/f)^2 + 1}{\text{loss factors}}$ | **dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $f_T$ and $f_{max}$ formulas are the **two most-asked metrics** in KTU board exams. A transistor is considered "useful" at frequency $f$ only if $f < f_T/2$ for switching, or $f < f_{max}$ for power-amplifier applications. Designing RF amplifiers is essentially the art of maximizing $g_m / (C_{gs}+C_{gd})$ while minimizing gate resistance $R_g$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Device | Primary Application | Why it is chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **GaAs MESFET** | Low-noise amplifier (LNA) up to X-band (12 GHz), microwave switches | Mature, low cost, adequate performance |
| **AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT** | LNAs in satellite receivers, DBS LNBs, radio astronomy | Lowest NF at room & cryogenic temperatures |
| **InP HEMT / mHEMT** | mm-wave (60–300 GHz) amplifiers, 5G/6G front-ends | Highest $f_T$ and $f_{max}$ of any 3-terminal device |
| **AlGaN/GaN HEMT** | RF power amplifiers (cell towers, radar), high-voltage switches | High breakdown field (3 MV/cm), high current density |
| **GaN-on-SiC HEMT** | X- to W-band radar T/R modules | Combines power density with thermal conductivity of SiC |

### 2.5 Why the HEMT has Higher $f_T$ than MESFET

The MESFET's channel contains ionized donors throughout. Electrons scatter off these donors, reducing mobility. In the HEMT, donors are **spatially separated** from the 2DEG by the spacer layer, so electrons see a "clean" channel. This gives:

$$\mu_{2DEG} \gg \mu_{n,\text{MESFET channel}} \Rightarrow v_{sat} \text{ reached faster} \Rightarrow \tau_t \text{ smaller} \Rightarrow f_T \text{ higher}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MESFET Pinch-Off Voltage

We start from the Poisson equation in the depletion region under the Schottky gate, assuming a uniformly doped n-type channel of doping $N_D$ (cm⁻³) and thickness $a$ (m).

**Step 1 — One-dimensional Poisson equation**

$$
\frac{d^2 V(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}
$$

where $x$ is the depth from the metal-semiconductor interface into the semiconductor, $q$ is the electron charge (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C), and $\varepsilon_s$ is the permittivity of the semiconductor (for GaAs, $\varepsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m).

**Step 2 — Integrate once with boundary condition $E(a) = 0$ at the edge of the channel**

$$
E(x) = -\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(a - x)
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again to get potential**

$$
V(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}\left(ax - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_2
$$

Setting $V(0) = 0$ at the metal-semiconductor interface (reference), $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $x = W$, where depletion region ends**

The potential at $x = W$ equals the sum of the built-in potential $V_{bi}$ and the externally applied reverse bias $(V_{bi} - V_{GS})$:

$$
V(W) = V_{bi} - V_{GS} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}\left(aW - \frac{W^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Pinch-off occurs when $W = a$ (depletion spans the full channel)**

Substituting $W = a$:

$$
V_{bi} - V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{\varepsilon_s}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the pinch-off voltage**

$$
\boxed{V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}}
$$

For typical GaAs MESFET values: $N_D = 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $a = 0.2$ µm, $\varepsilon_s = 1.16 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $V_{bi} \approx 0.7$ V.

### 3.2 Derivation of $f_T$ from First Principles

**Step 1 — Charge under the gate**

The total charge $Q$ stored in the channel beneath the gate of width $Z$ and length $L$ at the pinch-off condition is:

$$
Q = q N_D \cdot a \cdot Z L
$$

**Step 2 — Gate-source capacitance**

$$
C_{gs} = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{\varepsilon_s Z L}{a}
$$

(using the parallel-plate approximation, valid for $V_{GS} \ll V_P$).

**Step 3 — Transconductance**

In saturation, an incremental change in $V_{GS}$ modulates the drain current:

$$
g_m = \frac{\partial I_{DS}}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{Z \mu_n \varepsilon_s (V_{GS} - V_P)}{a L}
$$

**Step 4 — Transit time**

Electrons traverse the gate length at saturation velocity $v_{sat}$:

$$
\tau_t = \frac{L}{v_{sat}}
$$

**Step 5 — Definition of $f_T$**

By definition, $f_T$ is the frequency at which the short-circuit current gain $\vert h_{21} \vert = 1$. For a simple FET model:

$$
f_T = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute**

Using $C_{gs} \gg C_{gd}$ for a well-designed device and the expressions above:

$$
\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi C_{gs}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mu_n (V_{GS} - V_P)}{L^2} \approx \frac{v_{sat}}{2 \pi L}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** $f_T$ is **inversely proportional to gate length $L$**. To double $f_T$, halve the gate length. This is why modern mm-wave HEMTs use **25 nm** or even **15 nm** gate lengths. Once $L$ becomes comparable to the mean-free-path of electrons (~10 nm in GaAs), non-stationary transport (ballistic, velocity-overshoot) further boosts $f_T$ above this simple estimate.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A GaAs MESFET has the following parameters: $N_D = 1.5 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $a = 0.2$ µm, $L = 0.5$ µm, $Z = 100$ µm, $\mu_n = 6000$ cm²/V·s, $v_{sat} = 1.2 \times 10^7$ cm/s, $V_{bi} = 0.75$ V, $V_{GS} = -1.0$ V. Find: (a) pinch-off voltage, (b) $f_T$, (c) $\tau_t$.

**Solution:**

(a) Using $V_P = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$:

Convert to SI: $N_D = 1.5 \times 10^{23}$ m⁻³, $a = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ m, $\varepsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.16 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m.

$$
\frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1.5 \times 10^{23})(4 \times 10^{-14})}{2 (1.16 \times 10^{-10})}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.96}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.138 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_P = 0.75 - 4.138 = -3.39 \text{ V}
$$

(b) $\tau_t = L / v_{sat} = (0.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}) / (1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}) = 4.17 \text{ ps}$.

$$
f_T = \frac{1}{2 \pi \tau_t} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (4.17 \times 10^{-12})} \approx 38.2 \text{ GHz}
$$

(c) Computed in (b) above: $\tau_t \approx 4.17$ ps.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: MESFET vs. HEMT

| Feature | GaAs MESFET | AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT |
|---|---|---|
| Channel type | Doped n-GaAs bulk channel | **Undoped** 2DEG at heterointerface |
| Electron mobility @ 300 K | 4000–6000 cm²/V·s | **8000–9000** cm²/V·s |
| Electron mobility @ 77 K | ~15000 cm²/V·s | **> 100,000** cm²/V·s |
| Dominant scattering | Ionized impurity | Phonon only |
| Noise figure @ 12 GHz | ~1.5 dB | **~0.4 dB** |
| $f_T$ typical | 20–40 GHz | **60–100 GHz** |
| Fabrication complexity | Moderate | High (MBE/MOCVD) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Threshold voltage control | Good | **Excellent** (via doping + gate recess) |
| Power handling | Moderate | Moderate (GaN HEMT: very high) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a MESFET. Why is GaAs preferred over Si for fabricating RF MESFETs?

**Model Answer:**
A **MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor)** is a field-effect transistor in which the gate electrode forms a rectifying Schottky contact directly with the semiconductor channel (no gate oxide).

GaAs is preferred over Si for RF MESFETs because:
1. **Higher electron mobility** (~8500 cm²/V·s vs ~1400 cm²/V·s for Si) → faster transit time.
2. **Higher electron saturation velocity** (~1 × 10⁷ cm/s) → higher $f_T$.
3. **Larger bandgap** (1.42 eV vs 1.12 eV) → higher breakdown voltage and better high-temperature operation.
4. **Availability of semi-insulating substrate** (resistivity > 10⁷ Ω·cm) → minimizes parasitic capacitance and substrate losses.
5. **Negative differential mobility** in GaAs supports velocity saturation, beneficial for low-noise design.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for reasons)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is a 2DEG? Why is it essential for HEMT operation?

**Model Answer:**
A **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** is a thin layer of electrons (~10 nm thick) confined at the interface of a heterojunction (e.g., AlGaAs/GaAs), where motion is restricted in one direction (perpendicular to the interface) but free in the other two.

It is essential for HEMT operation because:
1. The 2DEG is **physically separated from the ionized donors** in the wider-bandgap material, eliminating ionized-impurity scattering.
2. This results in **very high electron mobility** (up to 10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K), enabling **very high $f_T$ and very low noise figures**.
3. The high sheet-carrier density provides **high transconductance**, essential for low-noise RF amplification.
4. The quantum confinement produces **discrete sub-bands**, making transport quasi-ballistic and ultra-fast.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for importance)**

---

### PART B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — MESFET Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

(a) **(7 marks)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a GaAs MESFET. Derive the expression for the pinch-off voltage.

(b) **(7 marks)** A GaAs MESFET has the following parameters: $N_D = 1 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, channel thickness $a = 0.25$ µm, gate length $L = 0.6$ µm, gate width $Z = 200$ µm, electron saturation velocity $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s, $\varepsilon_s = 1.16 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m, $V_{bi} = 0.8$ V. Calculate: (i) pinch-off voltage, (ii) transit time, (iii) unity-current-gain cutoff frequency $f_T$.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The MESFET cross-section (as shown in Section 4.1) consists of:
- Semi-insulating GaAs substrate.
- n-type GaAs active channel of thickness $a$ and doping $N_D$.
- Source and drain ohmic contacts at the two ends.
- A Schottky gate contact in the middle, reverse-biased by $V_{GS}$.

**Working Principle:**
- Application of a negative $V_{GS}$ widens the depletion region under the gate, reducing the effective channel cross-section and modulating $I_{DS}$.
- When the depletion region spans the full channel thickness ($W = a$), pinch-off occurs and $I_{DS} \to 0$.
- For $V_{DS} > 0$, electrons drift from source to drain at velocity $v_{sat}$ in the high-field region beneath the gate, producing a saturated drain current.

**Derivation of $V_P$:** (see Section 3.1 for full derivation)

$$\boxed{V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

(i) Convert to SI: $N_D = 10^{23}$ m⁻³, $a = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m.

$$
V_P = 0.8 - \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{23})(6.25 \times 10^{-14})}{2 (1.16 \times 10^{-10})}
$$

$$
V_P = 0.8 - \frac{1.0}{2.32 \times 10^{-10}} = 0.8 - 4.31 = -3.51 \text{ V}
$$

(ii) $\tau_t = L / v_{sat} = (0.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}) / (1.0 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}) = 6.0$ ps.

(iii) $f_T = 1 / (2 \pi \tau_t) = 1 / (2 \pi \times 6.0 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 26.5$ GHz.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — HEMT Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

(a) **(7 marks)** Explain the construction of an AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT with a neat diagram. Describe the formation of 2DEG and its significance in achieving high mobility.

(b) **(7 marks)** Compare the MESFET and HEMT in terms of (i) channel structure, (ii) noise figure, (iii) cutoff frequency, (iv) fabrication complexity, and (v) typical applications. List any four advantages of HEMT over MESFET.

**Model Solution to (a):**

(See Section 4.1 and 4.2 for diagrams.)

**Construction:** The HEMT consists of a layered structure grown by MBE/MOCVD: SI GaAs substrate → undoped GaAs buffer → undoped AlGaAs spacer (3–5 nm) → n-AlGaAs donor layer (Si-doped, ~30 nm) → undoped AlGaAs Schottky layer → Schottky gate metal.

**2DEG Formation:** The wider bandgap of AlGaAs (1.8 eV for $x = 0.3$) versus GaAs (1.42 eV) produces a conduction band discontinuity $\Delta E_C \approx 0.3$ eV at the AlGaAs/GaAs interface. Free electrons from the ionized Si donors in the AlGaAs fall into the resulting triangular quantum well and become **quantized** in the perpendicular direction but free in the plane. This confined sheet of electrons is the **2DEG**.

**Significance:**
- The 2DEG is in the **undoped** GaAs, so it suffers no ionized-impurity scattering.
- Mobility rises dramatically (~8500 cm²/V·s at 300 K, ~10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K).
- High mobility and saturation velocity give **higher $f_T$** and **lower NF**.

**Model Solution to (b):** (see the comparison matrix in Section 3.4 for the table)

**Four advantages of HEMT over MESFET:**
1. Lower noise figure (NF as low as 0.3 dB at X-band).
2. Higher $f_T$ and $f_{max}$ (up to 200+ GHz for GaAs HEMT).
3. Higher transconductance for the same gate length.
4. Sharper pinch-off and better threshold-voltage control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $V_P$:** Students often forget that $V_{bi}$ is **subtracted**, not added. $V_P$ is always **negative** for an n-channel depletion-mode MESFET/HEMT.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** $N_D$ must be in **m⁻³** (not cm⁻³) when using SI units. Mixed-unit mistakes cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $f_T$ with $f_{max}$:** $f_T$ is the current-gain cutoff; $f_{max}$ is the power-gain cutoff and is **always smaller** than $f_T$ for the same device. Examiner will deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 4. **Forgetting $V_{bi}$:** Many students write $V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$ and skip the built-in potential term. The correct formula is $V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$.
> 5. **Calling 2DEG a "gas":** It is a **quasi-2D sheet of electrons** at cryogenic/lattice temperature, not a literal gas. Use the precise term "**Two-Dimensional Electron Gas**" in answers.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] An **RF FET** is a field-effect transistor optimized for high-frequency operation, typically built on **GaAs, InP, or GaN**.
- [x] The **MESFET** uses a **Schottky metal gate** on a doped semiconductor channel.
- [x] The **HEMT (MODFET/HFET)** uses a **heterojunction** to confine electrons in an undoped **2DEG**.
- [x] **2DEG** = Two-Dimensional Electron Gas, a quantized electron layer at a hetero-interface with mobility **> 10⁶ cm²/V·s** at 77 K.
- [x] **Pinch-off voltage** for MESFET: $V_P = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$.
- [x] **Cutoff frequency** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi C_{gs}} \approx \dfrac{v_{sat}}{2 \pi L}$ — **inversely proportional to gate length**.
- [x] **Maximum oscillation frequency** $f_{max}$ depends on $f_T$, output resistance, and parasitic resistances.
- [x] **HEMT > MESFET** in mobility, NF, $f_T$, and $f_{max}$, but is more expensive to fabricate.
- [x] **AlGaAs/GaAs HEMT**: standard for low-noise microwave LNAs.
- [x] **AlGaN/GaN HEMT**: standard for high-power RF amplifiers (5G base stations, radar).
- [x] **InP HEMT**: standard for mm-wave and sub-mm-wave applications (terahertz, satellite links).
- [x] Heterojunction is formed by **MBE** (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) or **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition).
- [x] Velocity saturation in GaAs (negative differential mobility) is the key physical mechanism behind high-frequency performance.
- [x] Gate length is the single most important design parameter: **smaller L → higher $f_T$**.
- [x] MESFET and HEMT are both **depletion-mode** by default; enhancement-mode versions require special recess engineering.
- [x] Applications: LNAs, mixers, multipliers, power amplifiers, oscillators, switches in microwave and mm-wave systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — RF CIRCUIT MEASUREMENTS AND CHARACTERIZATION USING VECTOR NETWORK ANALYSER (VNA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Vector Network Analyser (VNA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Network Analyser (VNA):** A precision electronic test and measurement instrument used in Radio Frequency (RF) and microwave engineering to measure the **network parameters** — specifically the **Scattering (S) parameters** — of an electrical network or component. Unlike scalar analysers, the VNA measures both the **magnitude** and the **phase** of the signals, hence the term *vector*.

The VNA stimulates the Device Under Test (DUT) with a known continuous wave (CW) RF signal and measures how the DUT modifies this signal — by reflecting some portion of it and transmitting the rest. The instrument works in the frequency domain, sweeping a synthesised source across a user-defined frequency range (typically **300 kHz to 110 GHz** in modern commercial units) and computing the **complex ratio** of the incident, reflected, and transmitted waves.

**Key Performance Metric:** Measurement accuracy is governed by the calibration standards and the **effective directivity, source match, and load match** of the test set, which are quantified in **dB** (decibels).

### 1.2 Concept of S-Parameters (Scattering Parameters)

> [!NOTE]
> **S-Parameters:** A complete mathematical framework used to characterise high-frequency networks by relating the **incident and reflected voltage waves** at each port. At microwave frequencies, voltage and current become difficult to measure directly; S-parameters use travelling power waves instead, making them the de-facto standard for RF characterisation.

The one-port S-parameter is the **reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$, and the two-port S-matrix is a 2×2 complex matrix:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where each $S_{ij}$ is a **complex number** (magnitude + phase).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The RF Multi-Meter with X-Ray Vision"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: VNA as an Echocardiogram for Circuits**
> Imagine your heart (the RF circuit) and a doctor using an ultrasound probe (the VNA). The probe sends a harmless ping (the RF stimulus) into the body and listens to the echoes that bounce back. From the timing, intensity, and phase of these echoes, the doctor constructs an image of the heart's structure and its pumping health. The **VNA** does precisely this for electronic circuits:
> - **The RF stimulus** = the "ping" sent into the circuit
> - **The reflected wave** = the echo from impedance mismatches
> - **The transmitted wave** = the signal that passed through
> - **The S-parameter** = the analysed "image" of the circuit's RF behaviour

For a low-frequency student, think of a **digital multi-meter (DMM)**: it measures DC voltage, current, and resistance at a single fixed frequency (DC = 0 Hz). The VNA, on the other hand, is a "frequency-swept, phase-aware, multi-port multi-meter" for the RF world, capable of plotting how a circuit behaves across millions of frequency points in a single sweep.

### 1.4 Why VNAs Are Indispensable in RF Engineering

| RF Phenomenon | Low-Frequency Counterpart | Why VNA is Needed |
|----------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| Skin effect, parasitics | Negligible | Trace losses dominate above 100 MHz |
| Standing waves (VSWR) | Non-existent | Caused by mismatched impedances |
| Radiation | Not an issue | Trace lengths approach $\lambda$/4 |
| Phase coherence | Less critical | Circuits become *distributed*, not *lumped* |

Because lumped-element models fail at RF, the VNA provides the only practical way to **directly observe** the true high-frequency behaviour of antennas, filters, amplifiers, mixers, cables, connectors, and PCB traces.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for Scattering Parameters

At low frequencies, we use **Z-parameters (impedance)**, **Y-parameters (admittance)**, or **h-parameters (hybrid)** to describe a two-port network. These require the measurement of **open-circuit voltages** and **short-circuit currents**. At RF/microwave frequencies (typically above **100 MHz**):

1. It is practically impossible to realise a true open or short circuit (parasitics dominate).
2. Active devices may oscillate or self-destruct under open/short conditions.
3. Absolute voltage/current measurements require defined references that are not physically available at GHz speeds.

**S-parameters solve this** by using *normalised travelling power waves* at the ports, which are easy to measure with directional couplers.

### 2.2 The Scattering Matrix — Conceptual Logic

For a generic N-port network, define at each port $i$ the **incident wave** $a_i$ and the **reflected wave** $b_i$. The linear relationship is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_N \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & \cdots & S_{1N} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} & \cdots & S_{2N} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ S_{N1} & S_{N2} & \cdots & S_{NN} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_N \end{bmatrix}
$$

The general element is defined as:

$$
S_{ij} = \left. \frac{b_i}{a_j} \right|_{a_k = 0 \text{ for } k \neq j}
$$

The condition $a_k = 0$ means **all other ports are terminated in the system reference impedance $Z_0$** (typically **50 Ω**), which is physically realisable at RF.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a student must memorise for Module 2 VNA-related problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Definition / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless complex number |
| 2 | Return Loss | $RL \;(\text{dB}) = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | Expressed in **dB** (always positive) |
| 3 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Ratio, $\geq 1$ |
| 4 | S-parameter element | $S_{ij} = \left. \dfrac{b_i}{a_j} \right\|_{a_k=0,\;k\neq j}$ | Complex, dimensionless |
| 5 | Reflection coefficient at port 1 | $S_{11} = \left. \dfrac{b_1}{a_1} \right\|_{a_2=0}$ | Input reflection coefficient |
| 6 | Forward transmission | $S_{21} = \left. \dfrac{b_2}{a_1} \right\|_{a_2=0}$ | Forward gain/loss (complex) |
| 7 | Reverse transmission | $S_{12} = \left. \dfrac{b_1}{a_2} \right\|_{a_1=0}$ | Reverse isolation (complex) |
| 8 | Reflection at port 2 | $S_{22} = \left. \dfrac{b_2}{a_2} \right\|_{a_1=0}$ | Output reflection coefficient |
| 9 | Insertion Loss (magnitude) | $IL \;(\text{dB}) = -20 \log_{10}\vert S_{21} \vert$ | Positive dB for lossy DUT |
| 10 | Power delivered to load | $P_{del} = \dfrac{\vert V_S \vert^2}{4 R_S}$ | Maximum power transfer theorem |
| 11 | Group Delay | $\tau_g = -\dfrac{d \phi}{d \omega}$ | Measured in **seconds** |
| 12 | Conversion of $\Gamma$ to impedance | $Z = Z_0 \dfrac{1 + \Gamma}{1 - \Gamma}$ | Used on Smith Chart |
| 13 | Return Loss in terms of VSWR | $RL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{VSWR + 1}{VSWR - 1}\right)$ | Positive dB |
| 14 | Mismatch Loss | $ML \;(\text{dB}) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2\right)$ | Loss due to mismatch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reference impedance $Z_0$ in **all** S-parameter and VNA measurements is **50 Ω** unless explicitly stated otherwise (some specialised systems use **75 Ω** for video/cable TV).

## 2.4 Two-Port Network Interpretation (Most Common DUT)

For a **two-port network**, four S-parameters fully characterise the linear behaviour:

- **$S_{11}$** — Input reflection coefficient (port 2 matched). Indicates how well the input is matched to $Z_0$.
- **$S_{22}$** — Output reflection coefficient (port 1 matched). Indicates how well the output is matched.
- **$S_{21}$** — Forward transmission coefficient. Represents the **gain** (if $\vert S_{21} \vert > 1$ in dB) or **insertion loss** of the DUT.
- **$S_{12}$** — Reverse transmission coefficient. Represents **reverse isolation** or **feedback**.

For a **reciprocal network** (e.g., passive filters, cables, antennas): $S_{12} = S_{21}$.
For a **lossless, reciprocal network**: $\vert S_{11} \vert^2 + \vert S_{21} \vert^2 = 1$ and $S_{11} S_{12}^* + S_{21} S_{22}^* = 0$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of S-Parameter Measurements

| Application | S-Parameter Used | Engineering Insight |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Antenna design | $S_{11}$ | Impedance matching & bandwidth |
| Filter design | $S_{21}, S_{11}$ | Passband insertion loss, stopband rejection |
| Amplifier design | $S_{11}, S_{21}, S_{12}, S_{22}$ | Gain, stability, matching |
| Cable quality check | $S_{21}, S_{11}$ | Continuity, losses, reflections |
| Mixer characterisation | $S$-parameters at RF, LO, IF ports | Conversion gain/loss |
| PCB trace tuning | $S_{11}$ | Controlled-impedance design |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Transmission Line Theory to Reflection Coefficient

Consider a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated by an arbitrary load $Z_L$. A forward-travelling voltage wave $V^+ e^{-\gamma z}$ is incident on the load. The voltage and current at the load are:

$$
V_L = V^+ + V^- \quad \text{and} \quad I_L = \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}
$$

By Ohm's law at the load, $V_L = Z_L \, I_L$. Substituting:

$$
V^+ + V^- = Z_L \cdot \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $Z_0$:

$$
Z_0(V^+ + V^-) = Z_L(V^+ - V^-)
$$

Rearranging to group $V^+$ and $V^-$ terms:

$$
Z_0 V^+ + Z_0 V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_L V^-
$$

$$
V^+(Z_0 - Z_L) = -V^-(Z_0 + Z_L)
$$

$$
\frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

**Defining the reflection coefficient** as the ratio of reflected to incident voltage wave:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

This is the **fundamental definition** of the reflection coefficient at a single port, valid for any frequency where transmission line theory applies.

## 3.2 Derivation: The S-Parameter Matrix for a Two-Port Network

For a two-port, define normalised power waves at each port:

$$
a_i = \frac{V_i^+}{\sqrt{Z_0}}, \qquad b_i = \frac{V_i^-}{\sqrt{Z_0}}
$$

The factor $\sqrt{Z_0}$ normalises the waves so that $\vert a_i \vert^2$ represents the power incident at port $i$ and $\vert b_i \vert^2$ the reflected power. By definition:

$$
b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2
$$

$$
b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Deriving individual S-parameters from boundary conditions:**

- **For $S_{11}$:** Set $a_2 = 0$ (port 2 terminated in $Z_0$ so no wave reflects back into port 1). With $a_2 = 0$, the first equation reduces to $b_1 = S_{11} a_1$, hence:

$$
S_{11} = \left. \frac{b_1}{a_1} \right|_{a_2 = 0}
$$

This is the **reflection coefficient at port 1** when port 2 is matched.

- **For $S_{21}$:** Again with $a_2 = 0$, the second equation gives $b_2 = S_{21} a_1$, hence:

$$
S_{21} = \left. \frac{b_2}{a_1} \right|_{a_2 = 0}
$$

This is the **forward transmission coefficient** (signal flowing from port 1 to port 2).

- **For $S_{12}$:** Set $a_1 = 0$ (port 1 matched). The first equation yields $b_1 = S_{12} a_2$, giving:

$$
S_{12} = \left. \frac{b_1}{a_2} \right|_{a_1 = 0}
$$

- **For $S_{22}$:** With $a_1 = 0$, the second equation yields $b_2 = S_{22} a_2$, giving:

$$
S_{22} = \left. \frac{b_2}{a_2} \right|_{a_1 = 0}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Reciprocity Condition for Lossless Two-Port

For a **reciprocal network** (one made of passive, linear, bilateral components), the scattering matrix is symmetric:

$$
S_{12} = S_{21}
$$

The proof follows from the symmetry of the impedance/admittance matrices. The S-matrix is related to the Z-matrix by:

$$
S = (Z - Z_0 I)(Z + Z_0 I)^{-1}
$$

For a reciprocal network, $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, and direct matrix algebra shows that this implies $S_{12} = S_{21}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Unitarity for a Lossless Two-Port

For a **lossless** network, total incident power must equal total reflected/transmitted power (no power dissipated):

$$
\vert S_{11} \vert^2 + \vert S_{21} \vert^2 = 1 \quad \text{(input side)}
$$

$$
\vert S_{22} \vert^2 + \vert S_{12} \vert^2 = 1 \quad \text{(output side)}
$$

These come from power conservation $\sum \vert a_i \vert^2 = \sum \vert b_i \vert^2$ applied to each port independently.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Reflection Coefficient to Impedance Conversion

> **Problem:** A VNA measures $S_{11} = 0.333 \angle 0°$ at $f = 1$ GHz. Calculate the equivalent load impedance and the return loss. Assume $Z_0 = 50 \; \Omega$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the value:

$$
\Gamma = S_{11} = 0.333 + j 0
$$

Step 2 — Compute the load impedance:

$$
Z_L = Z_0 \frac{1 + \Gamma}{1 - \Gamma} = 50 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.333}{1 - 0.333} = 50 \cdot \frac{1.333}{0.667}
$$

$$
Z_L = 50 \cdot 2.0 = 100 \; \Omega
$$

Step 3 — Compute the Return Loss:

$$
RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert = -20 \log_{10}(0.333) = -20 \cdot (-0.477) \approx 9.54 \; \text{dB}
$$

> **Result:** $Z_L = 100 \; \Omega$, $RL = 9.54 \; \text{dB}$.

### Example 2: VSWR to Return Loss

> **Problem:** A cable has VSWR = 2.0. Find the return loss and the corresponding $\vert \Gamma \vert$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\vert \Gamma \vert$ from VSWR:

$$
\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1} = \frac{2.0 - 1}{2.0 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333
$$

Step 2 — Return Loss:

$$
RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.333) = 9.54 \; \text{dB}
$$

### Example 3: Insertion Loss of a Filter

> **Problem:** A low-pass filter is measured by a VNA. At 2.4 GHz, $S_{21} = 0.707 \angle -90°$. Find (a) the insertion loss in dB, (b) the group delay variation around this frequency, assuming the phase changes by $0.5°$ per MHz.

**Solution (a):**

$$
IL = -20 \log_{10} \vert 0.707 \vert = -20 \log_{10}(0.707) = -20 \cdot (-0.1505) = 3.01 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
\frac{d\phi}{df} = \frac{0.5° / \text{MHz}}{1} = 0.5 \; \text{°/MHz} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} \; \text{rad/MHz} = 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad/MHz}
$$

$$
\tau_g = -\frac{d\phi}{d\omega} = -\frac{d\phi}{2\pi \, df} = -\frac{8.727 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \cdot 10^6} = -1.389 \; \text{ns}
$$

The negative sign indicates a delay (convention: $\tau_g > 0$ for a causal system), so $\tau_g \approx 1.39 \; \text{ns}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define S-parameters. Why are S-parameters preferred over Z and Y parameters at microwave frequencies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

S-parameters (Scattering Parameters) are a set of four complex numbers — $S_{11}, S_{12}, S_{21}, S_{22}$ — that describe the input-output relationship between incident and reflected travelling waves at the ports of a two-port network at high frequencies.

> Z and Y parameters require open-circuit or short-circuit conditions at ports, which is **impractical at microwave frequencies** because:
> 1. Stray capacitances and inductances make true opens and shorts impossible to realise.
> 2. Active devices may oscillate or get damaged under open/short terminations.
> 3. S-parameters are measured with the ports terminated in the **matched reference impedance $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$**, which is physically realisable.
> Hence, S-parameters are the universal standard for RF characterisation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the parameter $S_{11}$? Explain the term "return loss" with its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$S_{11}$ is the **input reflection coefficient** of a two-port network, measured when port 2 is terminated in the system reference impedance $Z_0$. It indicates how well the input of the DUT is matched to the source impedance. A value of $S_{11} = 0$ indicates a perfect match.

**Return Loss (RL)** is the logarithmic measure (in dB) of the power reflected back from the DUT, given by:

$$
RL \; (\text{dB}) = -20 \, \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert
$$

A higher return loss (larger positive dB value) indicates a better impedance match.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS — INTERNAL CHOICE (Answer ANY ONE)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Vector Network Analyser (VNA). Discuss the role of directional couplers in separating incident and reflected waves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VNA measures the following S-parameters of a two-port amplifier at 2 GHz:

$$
S_{11} = 0.4 \angle -60°, \quad S_{21} = 5.0 \angle 120°, \quad S_{12} = 0.1 \angle 30°, \quad S_{22} = 0.3 \angle -45°
$$

Calculate the **input return loss in dB**, the **forward transducer power gain in dB**, and the **reverse isolation in dB**. Comment on the stability based on the Rollett condition. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — VNA Construction and Working

**Block Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

The VNA consists of the following major functional blocks:

1. **Synthesised Signal Source:** Generates a stable, low-phase-noise CW RF signal whose frequency is digitally swept across the user-defined range. A YIG-tuned oscillator or DDS-PLL combination is used.
2. **Power Splitter / Test Set:** Divides the source signal into two paths — the **reference channel** and the **test channel**.
3. **Reference Channel Receiver (R):** Samples the incident wave $a_1$ for phase and amplitude reference.
4. **Directional Couplers (DC1 and DC2):** Separate the incident wave ($a_1$), the reflected wave ($b_1$), and the transmitted wave ($b_2$) at the DUT ports with high directivity (typically **> 30 dB**).
5. **Test Channel Receivers (A and B):** Convert the high-frequency RF signals to baseband or IF using mixers and ADCs, preserving both magnitude and phase.
6. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Computes the complex ratios $b_1/a_1$ and $b_2/a_1$ to yield $S_{11}$ and $S_{21}$ in real time, and stores calibration coefficients.
7. **Display Unit:** Plots the magnitude, phase, group delay, Smith chart, or polar format versus frequency.

**Role of Directional Couplers:**
Directional couplers are **four-port passive devices** that sample the forward and reverse travelling waves on a transmission line independently. The **directivity** (typically 25–40 dB) determines how well the coupler can separate the reflected wave from the incident wave. Without them, simultaneous measurement of $S_{11}$ and $S_{21}$ at port 1 would be impossible.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:**

$$
S_{11} = 0.4 \angle -60°, \quad S_{21} = 5.0 \angle 120°, \quad S_{12} = 0.1 \angle 30°, \quad S_{22} = 0.3 \angle -45°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A magnitude of $S_{21} = 5.0$ implies the amplifier provides voltage gain. The dB computations are identical in form.

**Step 1 — Input Return Loss:**

$$
RL_{in} = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert = -20 \log_{10}(0.4) = -20 \cdot (-0.398) = 7.96 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Forward Transducer Power Gain (assuming both source and load are matched to $Z_0$):**

$$
G_T = \vert S_{21} \vert^2 = (5.0)^2 = 25 \quad \text{(linear scale)}
$$

Converting to dB:

$$
G_T \; (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(25) = 10 \cdot 1.398 = 13.98 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Reverse Isolation:**

$$
\text{Isolation} = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{12} \vert = -20 \log_{10}(0.1) = -20 \cdot (-1) = 20 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Rollett's Stability Condition:**

A two-port network is **unconditionally stable** if:

$$
K = \frac{1 - \vert S_{11} \vert^2 - \vert S_{22} \vert^2 + \vert \Delta \vert^2}{2 \vert S_{12} S_{21} \vert} > 1
$$

where $\Delta = S_{11} S_{22} - S_{12} S_{21}$.

Compute $\vert S_{11} \vert^2 = 0.16$, $\vert S_{22} \vert^2 = 0.09$, $\vert S_{12} S_{21} \vert = 0.1 \times 5.0 = 0.5$.

Compute $\Delta$ (in complex form):
- $S_{11} S_{22} = 0.4 \cdot 0.3 \angle (-60 - 45) = 0.12 \angle -105°$
- $S_{12} S_{21} = 0.1 \cdot 5.0 \angle (30 + 120) = 0.5 \angle 150°$
- $\Delta = 0.12 \angle -105° - 0.5 \angle 150°$

Converting to rectangular:
- $S_{11} S_{22} = 0.12 \cos(-105°) + j \, 0.12 \sin(-105°) = -0.0310 - j \, 0.1159$
- $S_{12} S_{21} = 0.5 \cos(150°) + j \, 0.5 \sin(150°) = -0.4330 + j \, 0.2500$

So $\Delta = -0.0310 - j \, 0.1159 - (-0.4330 + j \, 0.2500) = 0.4020 - j \, 0.3659$

$$
\vert \Delta \vert = \sqrt{0.4020^2 + 0.3659^2} = \sqrt{0.1616 + 0.1339} = \sqrt{0.2955} = 0.5436
$$

Now compute K:

$$
K = \frac{1 - 0.16 - 0.09 + (0.5436)^2}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{1 - 0.16 - 0.09 + 0.2955}{1.0} = \frac{1.0455}{1.0} = 1.0455
$$

Since $K > 1$ and $\vert \Delta \vert < 1$, the amplifier is **unconditionally stable**.

> **Final Answers:** $RL_{in} = 7.96 \; \text{dB}$, $G_T = 13.98 \; \text{dB}$, Isolation $= 20 \; \text{dB}$, Stable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the S-parameter matrix of a two-port network from fundamental definitions. Define each parameter with the appropriate boundary condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **SOLT (Short-Open-Load-Thru) calibration** procedure of a VNA in detail. Why is calibration necessary before performing any RF measurement? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of S-Matrix

Define normalised power waves at ports 1 and 2:

$$
a_1 = \frac{V_1^+}{\sqrt{Z_0}}, \quad b_1 = \frac{V_1^-}{\sqrt{Z_0}}, \quad a_2 = \frac{V_2^+}{\sqrt{Z_0}}, \quad b_2 = \frac{V_2^-}{\sqrt{Z_0}}
$$

By the **principle of linearity and superposition**, the reflected waves at both ports are linear combinations of the incident waves:

$$
b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2, \quad b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Boundary conditions for each parameter:**

| Parameter | Condition | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| $S_{11} = b_1 / a_1 \,\vert_{a_2=0}$ | Port 2 matched in $Z_0$ | Input reflection coefficient |
| $S_{21} = b_2 / a_1 \,\vert_{a_2=0}$ | Port 2 matched in $Z_0$ | Forward transmission coefficient |
| $S_{12} = b_1 / a_2 \,\vert_{a_1=0}$ | Port 1 matched in $Z_0$ | Reverse transmission coefficient |
| $S_{22} = b_2 / a_2 \,\vert_{a_1=0}$ | Port 1 matched in $Z_0$ | Output reflection coefficient |

Each $S_{ij}$ is a complex number that can be displayed as magnitude and phase, or as a real-imaginary pair on a Smith chart.

#### Part (b) — SOLT Calibration Procedure

**Why Calibration is Necessary:**
A real VNA has **systematic errors** introduced by the test cables, connectors, couplers, and receiver imperfections. The dominant errors are:
- **Directivity error** (leakage in couplers)
- **Source match error** (mismatch at the source)
- **Load match error** (mismatch at the load)
- **Frequency response / tracking errors** of the receivers

Calibration mathematically **removes** these errors by measuring known precision standards and computing correction coefficients (the **12-term error model**).

**The SOLT Procedure:**

| Step | Standard Connected | Purpose |
|------|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | **SHORT** | Defines a known reflection ($\Gamma = -1$) at the test port |
| 2 | **OPEN** | Defines a known reflection ($\Gamma = +1$) at the test port |
| 3 | **LOAD (50 Ω)** | Defines a perfect match ($\Gamma = 0$) |
| 4 | **THRU** | Connects port 1 to port 2 with zero-length, zero-loss transmission for $S_{21}$ and $S_{12}$ normalisation |

After measuring all four standards, the VNA's internal processor solves the 12-term error model equations to extract the systematic error terms. All subsequent DUT measurements are mathematically corrected using these error coefficients.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Calibration:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **omitting the directivity** discussion in calibration. Examiners specifically look for the mention of "directivity" and "source match" as separate error terms. Always state that the **12-term error model** corrects for **directivity, source match, load match, reflection tracking, transmission tracking, and isolation** in both forward and reverse directions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — VNA:**

- ✅ **VNA** measures both **magnitude and phase** of S-parameters across a swept RF frequency range.
- ✅ The **reference impedance is 50 Ω** (75 Ω in some video systems) for all S-parameter definitions.
- ✅ **S-parameters use normalised power waves** — measurable even when true open/short circuits are impossible.
- ✅ **$S_{11}$** = input reflection coefficient (port 2 matched); **$S_{22}$** = output reflection coefficient (port 1 matched).
- ✅ **$S_{21}$** = forward gain/insertion loss; **$S_{12}$** = reverse isolation/feedback.
- ✅ For a **reciprocal** network: $S_{12} = S_{21}$. For a **lossless** network: $\vert S_{11} \vert^2 + \vert S_{21} \vert^2 = 1$.
- ✅ **Return Loss** $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert$ in dB — higher is better.
- ✅ **Insertion Loss** $IL = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{21} \vert$ in dB — lower is better.
- ✅ **VSWR** $= (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert) / (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$, always $\geq 1$.
- ✅ **Group delay** $\tau_g = -d\phi / d\omega$ measures signal distortion in non-linear-phase systems.
- ✅ **SOLT calibration** (Short-Open-Load-Thru) uses four known standards to extract 12 systematic error terms.
- ✅ **Rollett stability factor** $K > 1$ AND $\vert \Delta \vert < 1$ is required for **unconditional stability** of a two-port.
- ✅ **Directional couplers** in the test set are responsible for separating $a_1$ and $b_1$ — directivity > 30 dB is desirable.
- ✅ VNA displays: **Log Mag, Phase, Smith Chart, Group Delay, Polar, SWR** — all derived from the same complex $S_{ij}$ data.
- ✅ Always include **units (dB, degrees, seconds)** in numerical answers — failing to do so costs 0.5–1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: S-Parameter, Reflection Coefficient and Insertion Loss Measurement, Modelling and Simulation of RF Circuits Using
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** S-Parameter, Reflection Coefficient and Insertion Loss Measurement, Modelling and Simulation of RF Circuits Using # 📘 Module 2: S-Parameters, Reflection Coefficient, Insertion Loss & RF Circuit Simulation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome Mapping (COs):** > - **CO2:** Apply S-parameter formalism to characterize high-frequency two-port networks and quantify reflection, transmission, and insertion loss in RF subsystems. > - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate). ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1.1 S-Parameters (Scattering Parameters) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** S-parameters are a mathematical framework that characterizes the electrical behavior of linear RF/microwave networks by relating the **incident and reflected traveling waves** at each port. For an **N-port network**, they form an **N × N scattering matrix `[S]`** whose elements $S_{ij}$ represent the wave amplitude leaving port *i* due to a wave incident at port *j*. For a **2-port network**, the defining equations are: $$ b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2 $$ $$ b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2 $$ In matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ where $a_i$ is the **incident (incoming) wave** at port *i* and $b_i$ is the **reflected (outgoing) wave** at port *i*. **The Four Standard 2-Port S-Parameters:** - **$S_{11}$** → Input Reflection Coefficient (port 2 matched) - **$S_{21}$** → Forward Transmission / Gain (port 2 matched) - **$S_{12}$** → Reverse Transmission / Isolation (port 1 matched) - **$S_{22}$** → Output Reflection Coefficient (port 1 matched) > [!NOTE] > **Why S-Parameters instead of Z, Y, or ABCD?** > At RF/microwave frequencies (typically **> 500 MHz**), defining open-circuit and short-circuit conditions is impractical because parasitic inductances and capacitances dominate. Furthermore, **Voltage and Current are difficult to measure directly** because they are not uniquely defined for traveling waves on a transmission line. S-parameters use **measurable incident/reflected power waves**, making them the de-facto standard characterization method up to ~**110 GHz** and beyond. --- ### 1.1.2 Reflection Coefficient (Γ — Gamma) **Formal Definition:** The reflection coefficient at a port is the **complex ratio of the reflected wave amplitude to the incident wave amplitude** at that port. $$ \Gamma = \frac{V^{-}}{V^{+}} = \frac{b}{a} $$ For a 2-port with all other ports terminated in matched loads ($Z_0$): $$ \Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \frac{S_{12}\,S_{21}\,\Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\,\Gamma_L} $$ When the load is matched ($\Gamma_L = 0$), this simplifies to $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$. **Return Loss** (in dB) is related to $\Gamma$ as: $$ RL \;(\text{dB}) = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert $$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Echo in a Valley" 🏔️** > Imagine shouting into a long valley (your RF signal traveling down a transmission line). If the valley walls are perfectly absorbing (matched load, $Z_L = Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$), your voice disappears — no echo. If the valley closes abruptly into a stone wall (open or short circuit), you get a strong echo. The **reflection coefficient Γ** is essentially the *loudness* of that echo. $S_{11}$ is the echo heard when port 2 is perfectly absorbed. --- ### 1.1.3 Insertion Loss (IL) **Formal Definition:** Insertion Loss is the **loss of signal power** resulting from the insertion of a device (filter, cable, attenuator) into a transmission line, typically expressed as a positive value in **decibels (dB)**. $$ IL \;(\text{dB}) = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21} \vert = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out,\;delivered\;to\;load}}\right) $$ For a **passive matched 2-port**, $\vert S_{21} \vert \le 1$, so IL is **positive**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insertion Loss vs. Attenuation — The Examiner's Trap!** > Many students confuse these. The KTU board specifically tests the difference: > - **Attenuation** is the *intrinsic* loss of a component (e.g., resistive loss in a cable). > - **Insertion Loss** = Attenuation + Mismatch Loss + Reflection Loss at both ports. --- ### 1.1.4 Modelling and Simulation of RF Circuits **Formal Definition:** RF circuit modelling and simulation refers to the use of **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tools** that employ numerical electromagnetics (EM), harmonic balance, and S-parameter-based methods to predict the high-frequency behavior of circuits *before fabrication*. > [!NOTE] > **Industry-Standard RF CAD Tools (KTU Favourite Topics):** > - **Keysight ADS (Advanced Design System)** — Gold standard for RF/microwave circuit design. > - **AWR Microwave Office / Cadence AWR** — Strong in planar EM. > - **ANSYS HFSS** — 3D full-wave FEM EM solver. > - **CST Studio Suite** — Time-domain 3D EM. > - **Cadence Spectre RF / Virtuoso** — For RF-IC design. > - **Keysight EMPro / Momentum** — Planar EM solvers. > > Simulation methods commonly used: **S-parameter (linear)**, **Harmonic Balance (nonlinear)**, **Transient/Convolution**, **EM Co-simulation**. # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics Behind S-Parameters At every port of a microwave network, the total voltage and current can be decomposed into **two traveling waves**: 1. **Incident wave** $a_i$ — traveling *into* the port. 2. **Reflected wave** $b_i$ — traveling *out of* the port. These are **power-normalized waves** (unlike voltage waves), defined as: $$ a_i = \frac{V_i^{+}}{\sqrt{Z_0}} \quad ; \quad b_i = \frac{V_i^{-}}{\sqrt{Z_0}} $$ where $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ is the **reference impedance** (industry standard). The **power** carried by each wave is: $$ P_i^{+} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert a_i \vert^2 \quad ; \quad P_i^{-} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert b_i \vert^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Scattering" Analogy** > The term *scattering* is borrowed from quantum mechanics. Just as a particle scatters off a potential well, an incident EM wave "scatters" off an RF network, producing both transmitted (through) and reflected components. The S-matrix is the "scattering matrix" that quantifies this phenomenon. --- ## 2.2 Why S-Parameters are the Industry Standard - ✅ **Directly measurable** with a **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)**. - ✅ **Cascade-able** — S-parameters of cascaded networks combine via **T-matrix conversion**. - ✅ **Reference-impedance agnostic** — defined at a known $Z_0$. - ✅ **Valid at any frequency** where wave behavior holds. - ✅ **Suitable for active and passive** networks alike. --- ## 2.3 Properties of the S-Matrix | Property | Mathematical Condition | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Reciprocity** | $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$ | For a reciprocal network (no ferrites, no active devices) | | **Losslessness** | $[S]^{\dagger}[S] = [I]$ | All power entering exits — no absorption | | **Symmetry** | $S_{ii} = S_{jj}$ for symmetric networks | Geometric/mirror symmetry | | **Magnitude bounds (passive)** | $\vert S_{ij} \vert \le 1$ | No power amplification | | **Unitary (lossless)** | $\sum_k \vert S_{ik} \vert^2 = 1$ | Conservation of energy | --- ## 2.4 Reflection Coefficient — Extended Theory The reflection coefficient at any load $Z_L$ attached to a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is: $$ \Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} $$ **Standing Wave Ratio (SWR / VSWR)** is derived from $\vert\Gamma\vert$: $$ VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert} $$ > [!NOTE] > **VSWR = 1** → perfect match ($\Gamma = 0$) > **VSWR = ∞** → total reflection (open or short) > **VSWR < 2** → generally considered acceptable for most RF designs. --- ## 2.5 Insertion Loss Measurement — Theory Insertion Loss is measured using the **three-step substitution method** (gold standard): 1. **Step 1 — Reference Measurement:** Connect the **signal source directly to the load** through a thru-calibration line. Measure $P_{ref}$. 2. **Step 2 — Device Insertion:** Insert the **Device Under Test (DUT)** between source and load. Measure $P_{DUT}$. 3. **Step 3 — Compute IL:** $$ IL \;(\text{dB}) = P_{ref}\;(\text{dBm}) - P_{DUT}\;(\text{dBm}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Instrument Required:** A **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)** is the instrument of choice for S-parameter measurements. It contains an internal signal generator, a test set with directional couplers, and coherent receivers to capture both magnitude and phase of $a_i$ and $b_i$ waves. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Unitless, complex | | 2 | Return Loss | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | dB, always $\ge 0$ | | 3 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1+\vert\Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$ | $\ge 1$ | | 4 | Mismatch Loss | $ML = -10\log_{10}\!\left(1-\vert\Gamma\vert^2\right)$ | dB | | 5 | Insertion Loss | $IL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert$ | dB, positive for passive | | 6 | Transducer Gain | $G_T = \dfrac{P_L}{P_{avs}}$ | dB | | 7 | Power Wave Definitions | $a_i = \dfrac{V_i^{+}}{\sqrt{Z_0}}, \; b_i = \dfrac{V_i^{-}}{\sqrt{Z_0}}$ | $\sqrt{W}$ | | 8 | Port Power | $P_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\vert a_i\vert^2 - \vert b_i\vert^2\right)$ | W (delivered) | | 9 | 2-port Network Eqs. | $b_1 = S_{11}a_1 + S_{12}a_2;\; b_2 = S_{21}a_1 + S_{22}a_2$ | Linear | | 10 | Input Reflection w/ Load | $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L}$ | Complex | | 11 | Output Reflection w/ Source | $\Gamma_{out} = S_{22} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S}{1 - S_{11}\Gamma_S}$ | Complex | | 12 | Unilateral Power Gain | $G_{TU} = \dfrac{\vert S_{21}\vert^2}{\left(1-\vert S_{11}\vert^2\right)\left(1-\vert S_{22}\vert^2\right)}$ | Unitless ratio | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in **wireless base-station design (5G NR), radar front-ends, satellite transponders, RFIC design, and EMI/EMC compliance testing**. Every commercial wireless product you use — from smartphones to Wi-Fi routers — relies on S-parameter simulation to ensure signal integrity. # 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Reflection Coefficient from Transmission Line Theory **Starting Point:** A transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated with a load $Z_L$. The total voltage and current at the load are: $$ V(z) = V^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V^- e^{+j\beta z} $$ $$ I(z) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z} $$ At the load ($z = 0$): $$ V_L = V^+ + V^- $$ $$ I_L = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} $$ **Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law at the load:** $$ V_L = Z_L I_L \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V^+ + V^- = Z_L \cdot \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $Z_0$:** $$ Z_0 (V^+ + V^-) = Z_L (V^+ - V^-) $$ **Step 3 — Expand:** $$ Z_0 V^+ + Z_0 V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_L V^- $$ **Step 4 — Collect $V^-$ terms on one side, $V^+$ on the other:** $$ Z_0 V^- + Z_L V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_0 V^+ $$ $$ V^-(Z_0 + Z_L) = V^+(Z_L - Z_0) $$ **Step 5 — Take the ratio $\dfrac{V^-}{V^+} = \Gamma$:** $$ \boxed{\;\Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}\;} $$ > This is the **fundamental reflection coefficient equation** — derivable in any RF course. The KTU board expects you to derive it from first principles. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Input Reflection Coefficient of a 2-Port **Goal:** Express $\Gamma_{in} = b_1/a_1$ in terms of the S-parameters and the load reflection $\Gamma_L$. **Step 1 — From the 2-port S-parameter equations:** $$ b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2 $$ $$ b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2 $$ **Step 2 — Define the load reflection coefficient:** At port 2, the reflected wave $a_2$ (incoming to the network from the load) relates to the outgoing wave $b_2$ via the load: $$ a_2 = \Gamma_L\, b_2 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $a_2$ into the second equation:** $$ b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22}(\Gamma_L b_2) = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22}\Gamma_L b_2 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $b_2$:** $$ b_2 - S_{22}\Gamma_L b_2 = S_{21} a_1 $$ $$ b_2 (1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L) = S_{21} a_1 $$ $$ b_2 = \frac{S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 5 — Express $a_2$:** $$ a_2 = \Gamma_L b_2 = \frac{\Gamma_L S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute into $b_1$ equation:** $$ b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} \cdot \frac{\Gamma_L S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 7 — Factor $a_1$:** $$ b_1 = a_1 \left[ S_{11} + \frac{S_{12} S_{21} \Gamma_L}{1 -
