S_{22}\Gamma_L} \right] $$ **Step 8 — Therefore $\Gamma_{in} = b_1/a_1$:** $$ \boxed{\;\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \frac{S_{12} S_{21}\, \Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\, \Gamma_L}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Case:** When port 2 is matched ($\Gamma_L = 0$), then $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$ — this is exactly the definition of $S_{11}$ measured by a VNA with the other port terminated in $Z_0$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Insertion Loss from S-Parameters **Starting Point:** A 2-port network is inserted between a source with available power $P_{avs}$ and a load $Z_L$. **Step 1 — Power delivered to the load** is the magnitude-squared of the wave emerging from port 2 reaching the load: $$ P_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert b_2 \vert^2 (1 - \vert\Gamma_L\vert^2) $$ **Step 2 — Source available power** is delivered when the source is conjugate-matched: $$ P_{avs} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert a_S \vert^2 $$ where $a_S$ is the source wave amplitude. **Step 3 — Transducer Power Gain** is defined as: $$ G_T = \frac{P_L}{P_{avs}} = \frac{\vert S_{21}\vert^2 (1 - \vert\Gamma_S\vert^2)(1 - \vert\Gamma_L\vert^2)}{\vert (1 - S_{11}\Gamma_S)(1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L) - S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S\Gamma_L \vert^2} $$ **Step 4 — Special Case (matched system):** $\Gamma_S = \Gamma_L = 0$: $$ G_T = \vert S_{21}\vert^2 $$ **Step 5 — Insertion Loss definition** (IL in dB, positive for loss): $$ \boxed{\;IL \;(\text{dB}) = -10\log_{10} G_T = -10\log_{10}(\vert S_{21}\vert^2) = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Board Expectation):** > - **Passive device** (filter, cable): $\vert S_{21}\vert \le 1$ → IL is **positive** dB. > - **Active device** (LNA, PA): $\vert S_{21}\vert > 1$ → IL becomes **negative**, and we call it **Gain**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A microwave amplifier has measured S-parameters at 2 GHz as: $$ S_{11} = 0.4\,\angle -60^\circ, \quad S_{21} = 2.5\,\angle 80^\circ $$ $$ S_{12} = 0.05\,\angle 30^\circ, \quad S_{22} = 0.5\,\angle -45^\circ $$ The source and load are matched ($Z_S = Z_L = 50\,\Omega$). **Find:** (a) Return Loss at the input, (b) Insertion Loss / Gain, (c) VSWR at the input. **Solution (a) — Return Loss:** Since $\Gamma_S = \Gamma_L = 0$, we have $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$. $$ RL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{11}\vert = -20\log_{10}(0.4) = -20 \times (-0.3979) $$ $$ \boxed{RL = 7.96 \text{ dB}} $$ **Solution (b) — Insertion Loss:** $$ IL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert = -20\log_{10}(2.5) = -20 \times 0.3979 $$ $$ \boxed{IL = -7.96 \text{ dB}} $$ The **negative** value indicates **gain**, so: $$ \text{Gain} = 7.96 \text{ dB} $$ **Solution (c) — Input VSWR:** $$ VSWR_{in} = \frac{1 + \vert S_{11}\vert}{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert} = \frac{1 + 0.4}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{1.4}{0.6} $$ $$ \boxed{VSWR_{in} = 2.33} $$ # 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 S-Parameter Conceptual Flow (2-Port Network) ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Signal Source
a1]:::srcstyle p1[Port 1
Z0 = 50 ohm]:::portstyle nw[Two-Port RF Network
DUT]:::netstyle p2[Port 2
Z0 = 50 ohm]:::portstyle ld[Matched Load
a2]:::ldstyle src -- "Incident wave a1" --> p1 p1 -- "Forward into DUT" --> nw nw -- "Transmitted to load" --> p2 p2 -- "Delivered Power" --> ld p1 -. "Reflected wave b1
S11 component" .-> src nw -. "Reverse leakage b1
S12 component" .-> src p2 -. "Reflected wave b2
S22 component" .-> nw nw -. "Forward transmission b2
S21 component" .-> p2 classDef srcstyle fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b1c1c,color:#000 classDef portstyle fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 classDef netstyle fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000 classDef ldstyle fill:#fcd5ce,stroke:#7b2d26,color:#000 ``` > **Figure 1:** Two-port network showing the four wave quantities $a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2$ and the corresponding S-parameters linking them. --- ## 4.2 VNA Measurement Architecture for S-Parameters ```mermaid flowchart TB synth[Frequency Synthesizer
1 to 110 GHz]:::synstyle split[Power Splitter]:::compstyle ref[Reference Receiver R]:::recstyle test[Test Receiver A]:::recstyle testB[Test Receiver B]:::recstyle sw[RF Switch
S1 S2]:::compstyle port1[Port 1
DUT input]:::portstyle port2[Port 2
DUT output]:::portstyle dsp[DSP Processor
Compute Sij]:::dspstyle synth --> split split --> ref split --> sw sw --> port1 sw --> port2 port1 -. "Incident a1" .-> test port1 -. "Reflected b1" .-> test port2 -. "Transmitted b2" .-> testB port2 -. "Reflected b2" .-> testB ref --> dsp test --> dsp testB --> dsp classDef synstyle fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000 classDef compstyle fill:#e2ece9,stroke:#344e41,color:#000 classDef recstyle fill:#f8edeb,stroke:#6d2c4d,color:#000 classDef portstyle fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 classDef dspstyle fill:#d8e2dc,stroke:#2b2d42,color:#000 ``` > **Figure 2:** Block diagram of a **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)** — the instrument used to measure S-parameters. The test set directs signals, samples incident and reflected waves, and the DSP computes the complex ratio $b_i/a_j$. --- ## 4.3 RF Circuit Simulation Workflow (Modelling & Simulation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Define Specifications
Frequency, Gain, NF, P1dB]:::stepstyle B[Step 2: Schematic Capture
Lumped + Distributed Elements]:::stepstyle C[Step 3: Choose Simulation Type
S-parameter Linear
or Harmonic Balance Nonlinear]:::stepstyle D[Step 4: Component Models
Vendor Models or EM Extracted]:::stepstyle E[Step 5: Run Simulation
Sweep Frequency or Power]:::stepstyle F[Step 6: EM Co-simulation
if layout dependent]:::stepstyle G[Step 7: Post-Processing
Smith Chart, Eye Diagram, Spectra]:::stepstyle H[Step 8: Design Verification
Meets Specs or Iterate]:::stepstyle A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H H -. "Iterate if needed" .-> B classDef stepstyle fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003566,color:#000 ``` > **Figure 3:** A typical RF design flow inside ADS, AWR, or HFSS. Every KTU question on simulation expects you to identify these stages. --- ## 4.4 Smith Chart — Mapping Reflection Coefficient ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Z_L on Smith Chart]:::smithstyle B[Compute Gamma
Z_L minus Z0 over Z_L plus Z0]:::calstyle C[Plot Gamma on Complex Plane]:::plotstyle D[Read Return Loss and VSWR]:::resultstyle A --> B --> C --> D classDef smithstyle fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b1c1c,color:#000 classDef calstyle fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000 classDef plotstyle fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000 classDef resultstyle fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000 ``` > **Figure 4:** Conceptual flow showing how a load impedance $Z_L$ is mapped to a reflection coefficient point on the complex Smith Chart — essential for impedance matching problems. # 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define S-parameters. Why are they preferred over Z, Y, and ABCD parameters at microwave frequencies? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** S-parameters are a set of network parameters that relate the incident and reflected traveling waves at each port of an RF network, forming the scattering matrix $[S]$. For a 2-port, $b_1 = S_{11}a_1 + S_{12}a_2$ and $b_2 = S_{21}a_1 + S_{22}a_2$. S-parameters are preferred at microwave frequencies because: 1. **Open and short circuit conditions are impractical** at high frequencies due to parasitic reactances and radiation. 2. **Voltage and current are not uniquely defined** for traveling waves — only incident/reflected power waves are reliably measurable. 3. They are **directly measurable using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)**. 4. They enable **direct cascade of networks** via T-matrix conversion. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for reasons)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Insertion Loss. How is it measured? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Insertion Loss (IL) is the ratio (expressed in dB) of the power delivered to a load before and after inserting a Device Under Test (DUT) into the transmission path. $$ IL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert $$ **Measurement Procedure:** IL is measured using the **substitution method** with a VNA: - Step 1: Calibrate the VNA (SOLT) at both ports. - Step 2: Measure $S_{21}$ with the DUT inserted. - Step 3: Read $\vert S_{21}\vert$ in dB directly from the VNA — this is the Insertion Loss. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for measurement)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — Choose ONE) ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### **Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Derive the expression for reflection coefficient at the input of a two-port network in terms of S-parameters and load reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L$.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write 2-port S-parameter equations** (1 mark): $$ b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2 \qquad (1) $$ $$ b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2 \qquad (2) $$ **Step 2 — Relate the wave reflected from the load** (1 mark): The wave $a_2$ entering port 2 from the load is the load-reflected version of $b_2$: $$ a_2 = \Gamma_L\, b_2 \qquad (3) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute (3) into (2)** (1 mark): $$ b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} \Gamma_L b_2 $$ $$ b_2(1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L) = S_{21} a_1 $$ $$ b_2 = \frac{S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 4 — Find $a_2$ using (3)** (1 mark): $$ a_2 = \frac{\Gamma_L S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $a_2$ into equation (1)** (1 mark): $$ b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} \cdot \frac{\Gamma_L S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ **Step 6 — Factor $a_1$ and take ratio $b_1/a_1$** (2 marks): $$ \frac{b_1}{a_1} = S_{11} + \frac{S_{12} S_{21} \Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L} $$ $$ \boxed{\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \frac{S_{12} S_{21}\, \Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\, \Gamma_L}} $$ --- #### **Q.A(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A microwave transistor has the following S-parameters at 4 GHz (with $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$):** $$ S_{11} = 0.6\,\angle -150^\circ, \quad S_{21} = 2.0\,\angle 60^\circ $$ $$ S_{12} = 0.05\,\angle 30^\circ, \quad S_{22} = 0.5\,\angle -80^\circ $$ **Compute: (i) Return Loss at input, (ii) Insertion Loss / Gain, (iii) VSWR at the input.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Part (i) — Return Loss:** (2 marks) Since the test setup is matched, $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$. $$ RL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{11}\vert = -20\log_{10}(0.6) $$ $$ RL = -20 \times (-0.2218) = 4.44 \text{ dB} $$ **Part (ii) — Insertion Loss / Gain:** (2 marks) $$ IL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert = -20\log_{10}(2.0) $$ $$ IL = -20 \times 0.301 = -6.02 \text{ dB} $$ Since IL is negative, this represents a **Gain of 6.02 dB**. **Part (iii) — Input VSWR:** (3 marks) $$ VSWR_{in} = \frac{1 + \vert S_{11}\vert}{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert} = \frac{1 + 0.6}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{1.6}{0.4} $$ $$ \boxed{VSWR_{in} = 4.0} $$ --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### **Q.B(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Explain in detail the measurement setup for S-parameters using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA). Include a block diagram and the calibration procedure.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction to VNA** (1 mark): A Vector Network Analyzer is the instrument used to measure the complex S-parameters (magnitude **and** phase) of a Device Under Test (DUT) over a swept frequency range. It uses coherent detection to capture both amplitude and phase of incident and reflected waves. **Step 2 — Major Internal Blocks** (2 marks): - **Frequency Synthesizer** — Generates a stable, sweepable RF signal. - **Test Set / Power Splitter** — Splits the signal into a reference and a test path. - **Directional Couplers** — Separate forward (incident) and reverse (reflected) waves at each port. - **Coherent Receivers** — Detect both magnitude and phase relative to the reference. - **DSP / Display** — Computes complex ratios $S_{ij} = b_i/a_j$. **Step 3 — Calibration (SOLT Method)** (2 marks): The VNA must be calibrated before measurement using the **SOLT** (Short-Open-Load-Thru) procedure: - Connect **Short**, **Open**, **Load** (matched $Z_0$), and **Thru** standards at each port. - The VNA measures known reflection coefficients of each standard and stores error coefficients (directivity, source match, reflection tracking). - These error coefficients are then mathematically removed from subsequent DUT measurements. **Step 4 — Measurement Procedure** (2 marks): 1. After calibration, connect the DUT between Port 1 and Port 2. 2. VNA sweeps the chosen frequency range. 3. At each frequency point, the VNA computes $S_{11}, S_{21}, S_{12}, S_{22}$ from the wave measurements. 4. Results are displayed on a Smith Chart or in rectangular (dB vs. frequency) format. --- #### **Q.B(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **Discuss in detail the modelling and simulation of RF circuits using ADS / AWR. What are the typical simulation types used?** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Need for Simulation** (1 mark): At RF/microwave frequencies, building multiple physical prototypes is costly. **CAD tools** like **Keysight ADS**, **AWR Microwave Office**, and **ANSYS HFSS** allow engineers to predict circuit behavior using mathematical models. **Step 2 — Schematic Capture** (1 mark): The first step is drawing the circuit using **lumped elements** (R, L, C), **distributed elements** (microstrip lines, couplers), and **vendor device models** (transistor S-parameter or non-linear models). **Step 3 — Simulation Types** (3 marks): - **S-Parameter (Linear) Simulation:** Small-signal AC analysis producing $S_{ij}$ vs. frequency. Used for filters, matching networks, amplifiers at linear operating points. - **Harmonic Balance (HB):** Nonlinear, steady-state analysis used for mixers, PAs, and frequency multipliers. Solves the circuit in the frequency domain using Fourier techniques. - **Transient / Convolution Simulation:** Time-domain analysis for digitally modulated signals and impulse responses. - **Electromagnetic (EM) Co-simulation:** The layout is exported to a 2.5D or 3D EM solver (Momentum, FEM, FDTD) to capture distributed effects like coupling, radiation, and discontinuities. **Step 4 — Post-Processing** (1 mark): Results are visualized using **Smith Charts**, **rectangular plots (dB vs. frequency)**, **polar plots**, **eye diagrams**, and **spectra**. **Step 5 — Iteration & Design Verification** (1 mark): Engineers compare simulated results to specifications and iterate the design until requirements (gain, NF, P1dB, return loss, group delay) are met — only then is fabrication initiated. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks!** > > 1. **Sign Error in Insertion Loss:** Many students write $IL = +20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert$ for **all** cases. Remember: this gives positive IL for passive devices only. For amplifiers (gain), $IL$ becomes negative and you must explicitly say "**Gain** = $+$ IL magnitude". > 2. **Forgetting the Phase of S-Parameters:** KTU questions often involve phase angles. Computing only magnitudes loses 2–3 marks. Always retain the angle in intermediate steps. > 3. **Confusing $S_{11}$ with $\Gamma$:** $S_{11} = \Gamma_{in}$ **only when port 2 is matched**. Otherwise, use the full expression with $\Gamma_L$. > 4. **VNA Calibration Ignored:** If the question asks about S-parameter measurement, **never skip the calibration step** (SOLT or TRL). Examiners specifically test this. > 5. **Simulation Tools — Be Specific:** Don't just say "software is used." Always mention the **specific tool (ADS, AWR, HFSS)** and the **specific simulation type (S-parameter, Harmonic Balance, EM)**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** ### ✅ S-Parameter Fundamentals - S-parameters relate **incident ($a$) and reflected ($b$) traveling waves**. - Standard 2-port S-parameters: $S_{11}, S_{21}, S_{12}, S_{22}$. - Preferred at RF because V/I are hard to measure; open/short circuits impractical. - $S_{11}$ = Input reflection; $S_{22}$ = Output reflection; $S_{21}$ = Forward gain/loss; $S_{12}$ = Reverse isolation. ### ✅ Reflection Coefficient - $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ - $0 \le \vert\Gamma\vert \le 1$ for passive loads. - $\Gamma = 0$ → matched; $\vert\Gamma\vert = 1$ → total reflection. - $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ in dB. - $VSWR = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$. ### ✅ Insertion Loss - $IL = -20\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert$. - Measured via **substitution method** or directly via VNA $S_{21}$. - Active devices (LNAs, PAs) yield **negative IL = Gain**. ### ✅ Input Reflection with Load - $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + (S_{12} S_{21} \Gamma_L)/(1 - S_{22} \Gamma_L)$. - Reduces to $S_{11}$ when port 2 matched ($\Gamma_L = 0$). ### ✅ S-Matrix Properties - **Reciprocal network** (no ferrites, no active): $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$. - **Lossless network**: $[S]^{\dagger}[S] = [I]$ (unitary). - **Passive**: $\vert S_{ij}\vert \le 1$. ### ✅ VNA Measurement - Measures complex (magnitude **and** phase) S-parameters. - Requires **SOLT or TRL calibration** before measurement. - Reference impedance is **50 Ω** (industry standard; 75 Ω in cable TV). ### ✅ RF Circuit Simulation - Tools: **ADS, AWR, HFSS, CST**. - Linear: **S-parameter** simulation. - Nonlinear: **Harmonic Balance (HB)**. - EM effects: **Momentum, FEM (HFSS), FDTD (CST)**. - Simulation must precede fabrication to save cost and time. ### ✅ Numerical Conversions to Practice - $\vert S_{21}\vert = 0.707$ → $IL = 3$ dB. - $\vert S_{11}\vert = 0.316$ → $RL = 10$ dB → $VSWR = 1.926$. - $VSWR = 2$ → $\vert\Gamma\vert = 1/3$ → $RL = 9.54$ dB.

topics.title: Open Source or Commercial EM Simulation Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Open Source or Commercial EM Simulation Software ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electromagnetic (EM) Simulation Software** refers to a class of specialized Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) tools that numerically solve **Maxwell's Equations** under user-defined boundary conditions to predict the behavior of electromagnetic fields, waves, and currents in arbitrary RF/microwave structures. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT862 - RF Engineering)**, EM simulators are indispensable for modeling passive structures such as **microstrip patch antennas**, **waveguides**, **filters**, **couplers**, and **interconnects** before physical fabrication, thereby reducing prototyping cost and time-to-market. The two principal paradigms of EM simulation tools available to a B.Tech RF engineer are: 1. **Open-Source EM Simulators** — Free, publicly available tools (e.g., **openEMS**, **Qucs**, **4nec2**, **EMPy**, **gprMax**). 2. **Commercial EM Simulators** — Licensed, industry-grade tools (e.g., **ANSYS HFSS**, **CST Studio Suite**, **Keysight ADS / EMPro**, **Cadence AWR Microwave Office**, **Sonnet Suites**, **FEKO**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEECT862, Module 2):** The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to *list*, *compare*, and *justify* the choice between open-source and commercial EM tools for RF passive structure simulation. Examiner value is given to naming specific software, the underlying numerical method, and the application domain. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an EM simulator as a **virtual "wind-tunnel for electromagnetic waves."** Just as an aerospace engineer cannot see air, but can place a scaled aircraft model inside a wind tunnel with smoke to visualize turbulence, an RF engineer cannot see microwaves. Instead, they "feed" the geometry, material properties, and excitation into the simulator, and the software returns field plots (E-field, H-field), S-parameters (S₁₁, S₂₁), far-field radiation patterns, and current distributions. - **Open-Source tools** are like a *homemade wind tunnel* — built by hobbyists and academics, freely shared, transparent in numerical method, but requiring more user expertise to operate. - **Commercial tools** are like a *certified Boeing wind tunnel* — professionally maintained, packed with auto-meshing, optimization routines, and customer support, but expensive and proprietary. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** No single simulator is "the best" — the choice depends on the structure being analyzed (e.g., open radiating antenna vs. closed waveguide cavity), the required accuracy, the available computational budget, and licensing constraints. This trade-off is a recurring KTU exam question. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The fundamental constants that every EM simulator internally normalizes are: $$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854187817 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{F/m} \quad \text{(vacuum permittivity)}$$ $$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{H/m} \quad \text{(vacuum permeability)}$$ $$c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0}} = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{m/s} \quad \text{(speed of light in vacuum)}$$ $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \approx 377 \;\; \Omega \quad \text{(intrinsic impedance of free space)}$$ A correct EM simulator **must reproduce** these values when the boundary condition is set to a vacuum box with PEC walls — this is the *canonical sanity check* every KTU lab viva begins with. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind EM Simulation Maxwell's curl equations in the time domain are: $$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}, \quad \nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$ For all but the simplest geometries (parallel plates, rectangular waveguides), these coupled Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) have **no closed-form analytical solution**. Therefore, we resort to **numerical discretization**. Every EM simulator implements one (or a hybrid of) the following numerical kernels: ### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Numerical EM Methods | **Numerical Method** | **Full Form** | **Best Suited For** | **Examples of Software Using It** | |---|---|---|---| | **FEM** | Finite Element Method | Volumetric, arbitrary-shape, closed RF structures (waveguides, cavities) | **ANSYS HFSS**, **COMSOL Multiphysics** | | **FDTD** | Finite-Difference Time-Domain | Broadband transient, transient EMC/EMI, photonic structures | **CST Microwave Studio (Time Domain Solver)**, **Lumerical FDTD**, **gprMax**, **openEMS** | | **MoM** | Method of Moments (Integral Eq.) | Open radiating structures (wire antennas, scattering from PEC surfaces) | **FEKO**, **4nec2**, **NEC**, **Sonnet** (planar MoM) | | **FIT** | Finite Integration Technique | Generalized 3D broadband electromagnetics (hybrid of FDTD/FEM) | **CST Studio Suite** (proprietary kernel) | ### 2.3 Open-Source vs Commercial: Side-by-Side Trade-Off | **Criterion** | **Open-Source EM Tools** | **Commercial EM Tools** | |---|---|---| | **Cost** | **Free** (GPL / BSD licenses) | **High** (₹ lakhs per seat/year) | | **Numerical Methods Available** | Mostly MoM (4nec2), FDTD (openEMS), SPICE-like (Qucs) | FEM, FDTD, FIT, MoM, hybrid (HFSS, CST, ADS) | | **User Interface (GUI)** | Minimal or CLI-based; steep learning curve | Polished GUI with mesh preview, parametric sweeps | | **Solver Speed** | Slower (relies on local CPU/RAM, no GPU acceleration in many cases) | Highly optimized, GPU-accelerated, MPI cluster support | | **Documentation & Tutorials** | Sparse, community-driven | Vendor manuals, application notes, training courses | | **Accuracy** | Adequate for academic / pedagogical work | **Industry-grade**, validated against measurements | | **Use Case in KTU Labs** | Suitable for student projects, MSc/PhD research | Required for industry-grade product design | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Where It Appears in EM Simulators** | |---|---|---| | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$ | Faraday's Law (time-domain) | Solved explicitly in **FDTD** leapfrog update | | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} + \sigma \vec{E}$ | Ampere-Maxwell Law | Solved explicitly in **FDTD** leapfrog update | | $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \le \frac{\lambda_{\min}}{10}$ | Spatial discretization rule | Mesh-resolution guideline in **FEM / FDTD** | | $\Delta t \le \frac{1}{c \sqrt{\frac{1}{\Delta x^2} + \frac{1}{\Delta y^2} + \frac{1}{\Delta z^2}}}$ | Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) stability limit | **FDTD** solver time-step ceiling | | $N_{\text{cells}} \approx k \cdot \left(\frac{L}{\Delta x}\right)^3$ | Memory scaling law | FEM/FDTD RAM estimation | | $Z_{in}(f) = \frac{V(f)}{I(f)}$ | Input impedance computed from port | All simulators compute this for antenna feed | | $S_{11}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | Reflection coefficient | Return-loss plot in any S-parameter viewer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Pipes:** In LaTeX table cells above, the symbol `\vert` is used instead of `|` to denote absolute value. This prevents Markdown table-parsing errors. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Antenna Design (4G/5G base stations):** MoM-based tools (FEKO, 4nec2) predict radiation patterns, gain, and Specific Absorption Rate (SAR). - **Microwave Filter Tuning (Cavity / Microstrip):** FEM-based tools (HFSS, CST) predict insertion loss and out-of-band rejection of a 4-pole BPF before any metal is machined. - **Signal Integrity (PCB Traces):** 2.5D planar MoM solvers (ADS Momentum, Sonnet) compute crosstalk between coupled microstrips on FR4. - **EMC Compliance (Automotive/Aerospace):** FDTD (CST, openEMS) models the electromagnetic susceptibility of an aircraft fuselage to lightning strikes. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why the FDTD Time-Step is Bounded (Courant Limit) The **FDTD method** discretizes space and time using Yee's staggered grid. To guarantee numerical stability, the time step $\Delta t$ must satisfy the **Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL)** condition. We start with the 1-D wave equation: $$\frac{\partial^2 E_x}{\partial z^2} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 E_x}{\partial t^2}$$ Apply central finite differences (forward in time, centered in space): $$\frac{E_x^{n+1}_k - 2 E_x^{n}_k + E_x^{n-1}_k}{(\Delta t)^2} = \frac{c^2}{(\Delta z)^2} \left[ E_x^{n}_{k+1} - 2 E_x^{n}_k + E_x^{n}_{k-1} \right]$$ Rearrange to express the next time-step explicitly: $$E_x^{n+1}_k = 2E_x^{n}_k - E_x^{n-1}_k + \left(\frac{c \, \Delta t}{\Delta z}\right)^2 \left[ E_x^{n}_{k+1} - 2E_x^{n}_k + E_x^{n}_{k-1} \right]$$ Let the **Courant number** be defined as: $$S = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{\Delta z}$$ Substitute back: $$E_x^{n+1}_k = \left(2 - 2S^2\right) E_x^{n}_k + S^2 \left[ E_x^{n}_{k+1} + E_x^{n}_{k-1} \right] - E_x^{n-1}_k$$ For numerical stability, **von Neumann stability analysis** requires: $$\vert S \vert \le 1 \implies \Delta t \le \frac{\Delta z}{c}$$ Extending to 3-D, the bound becomes: $$\Delta t \le \frac{1}{c \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\Delta x)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta y)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta z)^2}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering takeaway:** This is exactly why a coarse mesh (large $\Delta x$) makes the simulation *run faster*, and why a finer mesh requires *more time-steps* per simulation duration. KTU practicals frequently ask students to compute the required $\Delta t$ for a chosen mesh. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Choosing Mesh Resolution for a Microstrip Patch Antenna **Problem:** A rectangular microstrip patch antenna operates at **2.4 GHz** on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, height $h = 1.6$ mm. Find the recommended mesh cell size $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ and the maximum stable time-step $\Delta t$ for FDTD. **Given:** $$f = 2.4 \;\; \text{GHz}, \quad \varepsilon_r = 4.4, \quad h = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}$$ **Step 1 — Compute the wavelength inside the substrate:** $$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \;\; \text{m}$$ $$\lambda_{\text{sub}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{0.125}{\sqrt{4.4}} = 0.0596 \;\; \text{m} \;\; \text{(i.e., 59.6 mm)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the "λ/10" rule for FDTD mesh resolution:** $$\Delta x = \Delta y = \frac{\lambda_{\text{sub}}}{10} = \frac{0.0596}{10} = 5.96 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}$$ $$\Delta z = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}$$ (We use a finer z-resolution to capture the substrate vertical field variation.) **Step 3 — Compute the speed of wave inside the substrate:** $$c_{\text{sub}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{4.4}} = 1.431 \times 10^8 \;\; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Apply CFL stability bound:** $$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{1}{c_{\text{sub}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\Delta x)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta y)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta z)^2}}}$$ Substituting numerical values: $$\frac{1}{(\Delta x)^2} = \frac{1}{(5.96 \times 10^{-3})^2} = 2.816 \times 10^{4}$$ $$\frac{1}{(\Delta y)^2} = 2.816 \times 10^{4}$$ $$\frac{1}{(\Delta z)^2} = \frac{1}{(0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2} = 1.5625 \times 10^{6}$$ Summing inside the square root: $$\sqrt{2(2.816 \times 10^{4}) + 1.5625 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{1.6186 \times 10^{6}} = 1272.3$$ Therefore: $$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{1}{(1.431 \times 10^8)(1272.3)} = \frac{1}{1.821 \times 10^{11}} \approx 5.49 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{s}$$ **Result:** $\Delta t_{\max} \approx 5.49$ ps. OpenEMS will automatically select a slightly smaller value (~98% of $\Delta t_{\max}$) for safety. CST/HFSS hide this detail from the user but perform the same internal calculation. ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Tool-Selection Decision Table | **If Your Design Is...** | **Recommended Numerical Method** | **Open-Source Choice** | **Commercial Choice** | |---|---|---|---| | Wire dipole / Yagi–Uda antenna | MoM | **4nec2**, **NEC-2** | **FEKO**, **HFSS** | | Microstrip patch antenna | FEM or FDTD | **openEMS** | **HFSS**, **CST** | | Rectangular waveguide filter | FEM | (none robust) | **HFSS**, **CST** | | Planar microwave filter (microstrip) | 2.5D MoM | **Qucs-S + Qucsator** | **ADS Momentum**, **Sonnet** | | EMI/EMC on a PCB enclosure | FDTD or FIT | **gprMax** | **CST Studio Suite** | | Optical / photonic structure | FDTD / eigenmode | **MEEP** (MIT) | **Lumerical FDTD** | | Phased array / signal integrity | Hybrid FEM/MoM | (limited) | **HFSS + IE Solver**, **Cadence Clarity** | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 EM Simulation Workflow — Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts the canonical workflow followed inside *any* EM simulator (open-source or commercial): ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Geometry Definition
CAD Input STEP or SAT] nodeB[Material Assignment
er mur sigma] nodeC[Boundary Conditions
PEC PMC Radiation PML] nodeD[Excitation / Port Setup
Waveguide Lumped Plane Wave] nodeE[Meshing Strategy
Tetrahedral Hexahedral Conformal] nodeF[Numerical Solver
FEM FDTD MoM FIT] nodeG[Post-Processing
S Parameters Field Plots] nodeH[Convergence Check
Delta S less than 0.02 dB] nodeI[Optimization Loop
Parametric Sweep] nodeJ[Final Result
Validated Design] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH -->|Not Converged| nodeI nodeI --> nodeE nodeH -->|Converged| nodeJ ``` ### 4.2 Comparison Architecture: Open-Source vs Commercial EM Software ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OpenSource [Open Source EM Tools] os1[4nec2 and NEC2
Wire Antenna MoM] os2[openEMS
FDTD 3D Solver] os3[Qucs and QucsS
Circuit plus Planar] os4[gprMax
Ground Penetrating Radar FDTD] os5[MEEP
Photonic FDTD MIT] end subgraph Commercial [Commercial EM Tools] cm1[ANSYS HFSS
Gold Standard FEM] cm2[CST Studio Suite
FIT and FDTD Time Domain] cm3[Keysight ADS Momentum
Planar 2.5D MoM] cm4[FEKO
Hybrid MoM plus FEM] cm5[Sonnet Suites
Planar MoM High Accuracy] end RFStudent((KTU B.Tech
RF Engineer)) --> Decision{Decision Driver} Decision -->|Budget equals zero and Research| OpenSource Decision -->|Industry Project and High Accuracy| Commercial OpenSource --> Application1[Suitable for Academic and MSc Research] Commercial --> Application2[Suitable for Industry and Product Design] ``` ### 4.3 Numerical Method Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start New EM Simulation] --> q1{Is structure closed cavity?} q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is broadband transient required?} q1 -->|No| q3{Is structure wire-like?} q3 -->|Yes| MoM[Use Method of Moments MoM
FEKO or 4nec2] q3 -->|No| q4{Is dielectric volumetric?} q4 -->|Yes| FEM[Use Finite Element Method FEM
HFSS or COMSOL] q4 -->|No| Planar[Use 2.5D Planar MoM
ADS Momentum or Sonnet] q2 -->|Yes| FDTD[Use FDTD
CST Time Domain or openEMS] q2 -->|No| FEM2[Use FEM Eigenmode or Driven Modal
HFSS] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is an open-source EM simulation software? Give two examples. **Model Answer:** An open-source EM simulator is a freely available electromagnetic field solver distributed under a permissive license (GPL/BSD/MIT) whose source code is publicly inspectable and modifiable. Examples: **4nec2** (MoM-based wire-antenna solver) and **openEMS** (3-D FDTD solver based on MATLAB/Octave scripting). *(Mark split: 1 mark definition + ½ mark per example + ½ mark listing the numerical method.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Mention any two commercial EM simulation software tools used in RF engineering. **Model Answer:** Two commercial tools widely used in RF engineering are: 1. **ANSYS HFSS** — industry-standard 3-D FEM-based solver for antennas, waveguides, and microwave components. 2. **Keysight ADS (Advanced Design System)** with the **Momentum** planar EM solver — used for microstrip/RFIC layout-level simulation. *(Mark split: ½ mark per tool name + ½ mark identifying the numerical method/utility of each.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module-Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]* **(a)** Compare open-source and commercial EM simulation software based on any **seven** criteria. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the general workflow of an FDTD-based EM simulator. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per criterion):** | **S.No.** | **Criterion** | **Open-Source** | **Commercial** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cost | Free, no license fee | Expensive, license-based | | 2 | GUI | Minimal or CLI-based | Polished, parametric GUI | | 3 | Numerical Methods | Limited (mostly MoM, FDTD) | Wide (FEM, FDTD, FIT, MoM, hybrid) | | 4 | Solver Speed | CPU-only, slower | GPU/MPI-accelerated, faster | | 5 | Documentation | Community, sparse | Vendor manuals + application notes | | 6 | Accuracy | Academic accuracy | Industry-grade, measurement-validated | | 7 | Technical Support | Forum-based only | Vendor help-desk + training | **(b) FDTD Workflow Block Diagram (7 marks):** The FDTD simulation cycle proceeds as follows: 1. **Geometry & Material Assignment:** Define the 3-D structure (dielectrics, metals, substrates) and assign $\varepsilon_r, \mu_r, \sigma$. 2. **Boundary Condition Setup:** Use PEC (Perfect Electric Conductor), PMC (Perfect Magnetic Conductor), and **PML (Perfectly Matched Layer)** to truncate the computational domain without reflections. 3. **Mesh Generation:** Discretize space using Yee's staggered grid with $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \le \lambda_{\min}/10$. 4. **Excitation:** Apply a Gaussian pulse, sinusoidal source, or plane wave. 5. **Time Marching Loop:** Update E and H fields using leapfrog integration. Time step limited by CFL: $$\Delta t \le \frac{1}{c \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\Delta x)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta y)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta z)^2}}}$$ 6. **Convergence Check:** Continue until fields decay below a residual threshold or S-parameters stabilize. 7. **Post-Processing:** Extract $S_{11}, S_{21}$, far-field patterns via FFT of time-domain fields. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative Choice]* **(a)** Explain the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** as used in commercial EM simulators like ANSYS HFSS. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A microstrip antenna operates at **5.8 GHz** on a substrate of $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ and height $h = 0.8$ mm. Compute (i) the wavelength inside the substrate, (ii) the recommended FDTD mesh cell size $\Delta x$, and (iii) the maximum stable time-step $\Delta t$ assuming a cubic mesh $\Delta x = \Delta y = \Delta z$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) FEM in HFSS (7 marks):** The Finite Element Method breaks the computational domain into thousands of small **tetrahedral elements** (curved surfaces) or hexahedral bricks. Within each element, the field is approximated by a polynomial basis function (typically first- or second-order vector elements). The governing wave equation in HFSS (driven-modal formulation): $$\nabla \times \left(\frac{1}{\mu_r} \nabla \times \vec{E}\right) - k_0^2 \varepsilon_r \vec{E} = 0$$ is converted via the **Galerkin weighted-residual method** into a sparse matrix equation: $$[A]\{\vec{E}\} = \{b\}$$ which is solved for the unknown electric field $\vec{E}$ at every node. **Advantages:** - Handles arbitrary geometries and inhomogeneous materials naturally. - Adaptive mesh refinement gives high accuracy with controlled memory. - Excellent for closed metallic cavities (waveguide filters, cavity resonators). **Limitations:** - Produces a dense matrix equation — large RAM consumption. - Slower per frequency point compared to MoM. - Less efficient for purely open-region radiation problems unless complemented with an Integral Equation (IE) boundary. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **Given:** $f = 5.8 \times 10^9$ Hz, $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $h = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, cubic mesh. **Step 1 — Wavelength in substrate:** $$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 0.0517 \;\; \text{m}$$ $$\lambda_{\text{sub}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{0.0517}{\sqrt{2.2}} = 0.0349 \;\; \text{m} = 34.9 \;\; \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Recommended mesh cell size (λ/10 rule):** $$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda_{\text{sub}}}{10} = \frac{0.0349}{10} = 3.49 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m} \approx 3.5 \;\; \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum stable time-step (cubic mesh CFL):** Speed in substrate: $$c_{\text{sub}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.2}} = 2.022 \times 10^8 \;\; \text{m/s}$$ For a cubic mesh $\Delta x = \Delta y = \Delta z = \Delta$: $$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{c_{\text{sub}} \sqrt{3}}$$ $$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{3.49 \times 10^{-3}}{(2.022 \times 10^8) \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{3.49 \times 10^{-3}}{3.502 \times 10^8} = 9.97 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta t_{\max} \approx 9.97 \;\; \text{ps}}$$ --- ### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in EM-Software Questions:** > 1. **Naming without method = ½ mark only.** Students often write "ANSYS HFSS" but forget to mention it uses the **FEM** — losing the method-mapping marks. > 2. **Confusing MoM with FDTD.** Wire antennas → MoM (FEKO, NEC, 4nec2). Volumetric dielectric → FEM (HFSS) or FDTD (CST, openEMS). Mixing these up is a recurrent KTU valuation error. > 3. **Forgetting the CFL condition.** When asked about FDTD stability, students often quote $\Delta t = \Delta x/c$ but omit the $\sqrt{3}$ factor for a 3-D cubic mesh. > 4. **No "λ/10" justification.** Just stating $\Delta x = 1$ mm is not enough — show that $\Delta x = \lambda_{\text{sub}}/10$. > 5. **Open-source ≠ "no accuracy."** Open-source tools *are* accurate if the user understands the numerical method. Many students lose marks by claiming open-source tools are "useless for industry." --- ### 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **EM Simulation Software** numerically solves Maxwell's equations for arbitrary RF structures. - ✅ Two categories: **Open-Source** (free, e.g., 4nec2, openEMS, MEEP) vs **Commercial** (paid, e.g., HFSS, CST, ADS). - ✅ **FEM** → HFSS, COMSOL → best for closed cavities & complex dielectrics. - ✅ **FDTD** → CST Time-Domain, openEMS, MEEP → best for broadband transient EMC. - ✅ **MoM** → FEKO, 4nec2, ADS Momentum → best for wire antennas & planar circuits. - ✅ **FIT** → CST proprietary kernel → generalized 3-D broadband. - ✅ **Mesh rule:** $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z \le \lambda_{\min}/10$ for accurate field sampling. - ✅ **CFL Stability (FDTD):** $\Delta t \le \dfrac{1}{c \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\Delta x)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta y)^2} + \dfrac{1}{(\Delta z)^2}}}$. - ✅ **PML boundary** is the modern standard for open-domain truncation in FDTD/FEM. - ✅ For a **5.8 GHz patch** on $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$: $\lambda_{\text{sub}} \approx 34.9$ mm, $\Delta x \approx 3.5$ mm, $\Delta t_{\max} \approx 10$ ps. - ✅ KTU expects: name the tool + state the numerical method + cite one realistic application.

topics.title: Amplifier Design Using S-Parameters - Characteristics of Amplifier Power Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amplifier Design Using S-Parameters - Characteristics of Amplifier Power Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Amplifier Design Using S-Parameters

In RF and microwave engineering, **S-parameters (Scattering Parameters)** are the fundamental network characterization quantities used to describe the behavior of linear two-port networks under matched-impedance conditions. For amplifier design, S-parameters provide a complete mathematical framework to characterize the small-signal behavior of a transistor (or any active two-port) at high frequencies, where traditional h-, y-, or z-parameters become practically impossible to measure due to the difficulty of achieving open/short circuit conditions.

The general two-port S-parameter representation is given by the matrix equation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $a_i$ and $b_i$ represent the **incident** and **reflected** normalized power waves at port $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Amplifier design using S-parameters requires mastery of three foundational pillars — (1) **Stability Analysis**, (2) **Gain Definitions**, and (3) **Power Relations**. Each of these determines whether an amplifier can physically oscillate, how much gain it produces, and how efficiently it delivers power.

The **characteristics of amplifier power relations** describe the quantitative linkage between the source power, the available gain of the transistor, the matching network losses, and the actual power delivered to the load. The three principal power relations in amplifier theory are:

1. **Transducer Power Gain ($G_T$)** — Ratio of power delivered to the load to the power available from the source.
2. **Available Power Gain ($G_A$)** — Ratio of power available from the network output to the power available from the source.
3. **Operating Power Gain ($G_P$)** — Ratio of power delivered to the load to the power input to the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Distinction**: $G_T$ depends on both source ($\Gamma_S$) and load ($\Gamma_L$) matching. $G_A$ depends only on $\Gamma_S$ (assuming conjugate-matched load). $G_P$ depends only on $\Gamma_L$ (assuming conjugate-matched source). Mastering these distinctions is critical for KTU board questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine an RF amplifier as a **water distribution system** for a city:

- **The Source ($\Gamma_S$)** is the **high-pressure water reservoir** supplying water. Its internal impedance determines how much flow can be drawn.
- **The Matching Network at the Input** is a **valve and pipe transition** that converts the reservoir's pressure to match what the central processing unit (the **transistor**) needs.
- **The Transistor** is the **central pump/motor** — it boosts the water pressure but has its own internal "preferences" (reflections) about what pressure it accepts best.
- **The Matching Network at the Output** is another **valve** that converts the boosted pressure to what the city pipes (load) need.
- **The Load ($\Gamma_L$)** is the **city** that consumes the water.

The **S-parameters** are like **calibration cards** that tell you exactly how this entire system behaves at each junction — how much water is reflected back, how much is transferred forward, and how much leaks backward. **Power relations** are the equations that tell you, given this configuration, how efficiently water reaches the city.

If the pump is poorly matched to the valves, water sloshes back and forth (instability/oscillation). If perfectly matched (conjugate matching), maximum water flow is achieved. If mismatched deliberately, less water reaches the city, but the system might be safer or more linear (design trade-off).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid**: The three gains correspond to **three engineering perspectives**:
> - $G_T$ → "What the **engineer** delivers to the load" (full system)
> - $G_A$ → "What the **transistor** can output if perfectly matched at the load" (transistor capability)
> - $G_P$ → "What the **transistor** delivers to a specific load with a perfect source" (load-specific design)

---

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Notation | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference Impedance** | $Z_0$ = **50 Ω** | Standard RF | Ohms |
| **Stability Factor** | $K$ | $>1$ (unconditional) | Dimensionless |
| **Rollett's Stability Factor** | $\Delta = S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21}$ | $\|\Delta\| < 1$ for stability | Dimensionless |
| **Maximum Available Gain** | $G_{A,max}$ or $MAG$ | dB | dB |
| **Maximum Stable Gain** | $G_{MSG}$ | dB | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **reference impedance of 50 Ω** is the IEEE standard for RF measurement, although **75 Ω** is used in cable TV systems. KTU questions almost universally assume **$Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Amplifier Design Flow

Designing an RF amplifier using S-parameters follows a systematic **four-stage decision flow**:

**Stage 1 — Stability Analysis**
The first mandatory step is to determine whether the transistor, when terminated in arbitrary source and load impedances, will be **unconditionally stable** (stable for all passive source/load terminations) or **potentially unstable**.

**Stage 2 — Gain Computation**
Once stability is confirmed, the **maximum achievable gain** is computed using:
- **Maximum Available Gain (MAG)** if $K > 1$ and $|\Delta| < 1$ (unconditional stability).
- **Maximum Stable Gain (MSG)** if $K < 1$ but $|\Delta| < 1$ (only stable for matched source/load).

**Stage 3 — Matching Network Design**
For simultaneous conjugate matching, the source and load reflection coefficients are:
$$
\Gamma_{SM} = \frac{B_1 \pm \sqrt{B_1^2 - 4 \vert C_1 \vert^2}}{2 C_1}
$$
$$
\Gamma_{LM} = \frac{B_2 \pm \sqrt{B_2^2 - 4 \vert C_2 \vert^2}}{2 C_2}
$$

where $B_1, B_2, C_1, C_2$ are computed from the S-parameters.

**Stage 4 — Power Relation Analysis**
Once matching is designed, the **three power gains** ($G_T$, $G_A$, $G_P$) are calculated to assess the actual delivered power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence matters**: A transistor might be able to provide 20 dB of MAG but oscillate if you ignore stability. A proper design always checks stability FIRST.

---

### 2.2 Stability Conditions — Rollett's Two Conditions

For a two-port network to be **unconditionally stable**, the **Rollett's Conditions** must be satisfied:

$$
K = \frac{1 - \vert S_{11} \vert^2 - \vert S_{22} \vert^2 + \vert \Delta \vert^2}{2 \vert S_{12} S_{21} \vert} > 1
$$

$$
\vert \Delta \vert = \vert S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21} \vert < 1
$$

**Geometric interpretation of $\Delta$**:
- $\Delta$ is the **determinant of the S-matrix** and represents the degree of mismatch between the input and output ports.
- When $|\Delta| \to 0$, the network is near **perfect bilateral isolation** (e.g., $S_{12} \to 0$ or $S_{21} \to 0$).

**Alternative stability test** — the $\mu$-test (Edwards-Sinsky):
$$
\mu = \frac{1 - \vert S_{11} \vert^2}{\vert S_{22} - \Delta \cdot S_{11}^* \vert + \vert S_{12} S_{21} \vert} > 1
$$

> [!TIP]
> The $\mu$-test is **single-criterion** (only $\mu > 1$ needed) and is preferred in modern CAD tools like ADS and AWR. However, KTU board examinations typically use Rollett's conditions.

---

### 2.3 Stability Circles

When the transistor is **not unconditionally stable**, there exist regions in the Smith Chart where the input/output reflection coefficients can cause oscillation. These regions are bounded by **stability circles** defined by:

$$
C_S = \frac{(S_{11} - \Delta S_{22}^*)^*}{\vert S_{11} \vert^2 - \vert \Delta \vert^2}
$$

$$
R_S = \left\vert \frac{S_{12} S_{21}}{\vert S_{11} \vert^2 - \vert \Delta \vert^2} \right\vert
$$

- $C_S$ = **Center of source stability circle**
- $R_S$ = **Radius of source stability circle**

Similar expressions exist for the **load stability circle** ($C_L$, $R_L$) by swapping indices 1 and 2.

**Interpretation**:
- If $|\Gamma_S| = 1$ (matched source) lies inside the stability circle, the **unstable region is the interior**.
- If it lies outside, the **unstable region is the exterior**.

---

### 2.4 The Three Power Gains — Detailed Definitions

#### 2.4.1 Transducer Power Gain $G_T$

The most general and useful gain definition in amplifier design:

$$
G_T = \frac{P_L}{P_{avs}} = \frac{1 - \vert \Gamma_S \vert^2}{\vert 1 - \Gamma_{in} \Gamma_S \vert^2} \cdot \vert S_{21} \vert^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert^2}{\vert 1 - S_{22} \Gamma_L \vert^2}
$$

where:
- $P_L$ = power delivered to the load
- $P_{avs}$ = power available from the source
- $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L}$ = input reflection coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus**: $G_T$ is the **most commonly asked** gain in board examinations because it accounts for both source and load matching simultaneously.

#### 2.4.2 Operating Power Gain $G_P$

$$
G_P = \frac{P_L}{P_{in}} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - \Gamma_{in} \Gamma_S \vert^2} \cdot \vert S_{21} \vert^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert^2}{\vert 1 - S_{22} \Gamma_L \vert^2}
$$

Note that $G_P$ is **independent of $\Gamma_S$** because $P_{in}$ already accounts for the input mismatch loss.

#### 2.4.3 Available Power Gain $G_A$

$$
G_A = \frac{P_{avn}}{P_{avs}} = \frac{1 - \vert \Gamma_S \vert^2}{\vert 1 - S_{11} \Gamma_S \vert^2} \cdot \vert S_{21} \vert^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\vert 1 - \Gamma_{out} \Gamma_L \vert^2}
$$

where $\Gamma_{out} = S_{22} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S}{1 - S_{11}\Gamma_S}$ is the output reflection coefficient with arbitrary source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Conjugate Matching**: When $\Gamma_S = S_{11}^*$ and $\Gamma_L = S_{22}^*$, then $G_T = G_A = G_P = G_{TU,max}$, the maximum unilateral transducer gain.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Conditions / Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rollett's $K$ | $K = \dfrac{1 - \vert S_{11} \vert^2 - \vert S_{22} \vert^2 + \vert \Delta \vert^2}{2 \vert S_{12} S_{21} \vert}$ | $K > 1$ required | Dimensionless |
| Determinant $\Delta$ | $\Delta = S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21}$ | $\vert \Delta \vert < 1$ required | Dimensionless |
| Maximum Available Gain | $MAG = \dfrac{\vert S_{21} \vert}{\vert S_{12} \vert}\left(K - \sqrt{K^2 - 1}\right)$ | Only if $K > 1$ | Linear |
| Maximum Stable Gain | $MSG = \dfrac{\vert S_{21} \vert}{\vert S_{12} \vert}$ | $K \leq 1$, but $\vert \Delta \vert < 1$ | Linear |
| Transducer Power Gain | $G_T = \dfrac{(1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2)\vert S_{21}\vert^2(1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2)}{\vert 1-\Gamma_S\Gamma_{in}\vert^2 \vert 1-S_{22}\Gamma_L\vert^2}$ | General case | Linear |
| Operating Power Gain | $G_P = \dfrac{\vert S_{21}\vert^2 (1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2)}{(1-\vert\Gamma_{in}\vert^2)\vert 1-S_{22}\Gamma_L\vert^2}$ | Load-dependent only | Linear |
| Available Power Gain | $G_A = \dfrac{(1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2)\vert S_{21}\vert^2}{(1-\vert\Gamma_{out}\vert^2)\vert 1-S_{11}\Gamma_S\vert^2}$ | Source-dependent only | Linear |
| Input Reflection Coeff. | $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_L}{1-S_{22}\Gamma_L}$ | Bounded by Smith Chart | Dimensionless |
| Output Reflection Coeff. | $\Gamma_{out} = S_{22} + \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S}{1-S_{11}\Gamma_S}$ | Bounded by Smith Chart | Dimensionless |
| Stability Circle Center | $C_S = \dfrac{(S_{11}-\Delta S_{22}^*)^*}{\vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert\Delta\vert^2}$ | Source side | Dimensionless |
| Stability Circle Radius | $R_S = \left\vert \dfrac{S_{12}S_{21}}{\vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert\Delta\vert^2}\right\vert$ | Source side | Dimensionless |
| MSG (dB) | $MSG_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\vert S_{21}\vert^2}{\vert S_{12}\vert^2}\right)$ | In decibels | dB |
| Conjugate Match $\Gamma_S$ | $\Gamma_{SM} = \dfrac{B_1 \pm \sqrt{B_1^2 - 4\vert C_1\vert^2}}{2 C_1}$ | For simultaneous match | Dimensionless |
| Conjugate Match $\Gamma_L$ | $\Gamma_{LM} = \dfrac{B_2 \pm \sqrt{B_2^2 - 4\vert C_2\vert^2}}{2 C_2}$ | For simultaneous match | Dimensionless |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Why S-Parameter Power Relations Matter** |
|---|---|
| **Wireless Base Stations** | Need MAG to maximize range; stability circles prevent PA oscillation. |
| **Satellite Transponders** | TWTAs and SSPAs designed using $G_T$ for precise link-budget calculation. |
| **Radar Receivers (LNA)** | Low-noise design balances $G_P$ and noise figure using $G_T$ trade-offs. |
| **RFID Readers** | Power relations ensure reader sensitivity matches tag response. |
| **5G/6G Front-Ends** | Beam-steering phased arrays require per-element $G_T$ matching. |
| **Test & Measurement** | Vector Network Analyzers (VNAs) directly measure S-parameters. |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Note**: In real designs, designers rarely achieve MAG because it requires the transistor to operate near its oscillation boundary. Instead, they use the **constant-gain circles** (Pospichal/Cheney circles) to design for **1–3 dB below MAG** for production stability margin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Transducer Power Gain $G_T$

Starting from the two-port wave equations and the definition of normalized power waves:

**Step 1 — Express normalized incident and reflected waves**

At port 1: $a_1$, $b_1$. At port 2: $a_2$, $b_2$. The reflected waves are:

$$
b_1 = S_{11} a_1 + S_{12} a_2
$$

$$
b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} a_2
$$

**Step 2 — Define the source and load reflection coefficients**

$$
\Gamma_S = \frac{a_1}{b_1} \quad \text{at the source side (when } b_1 \text{ is reflected by source)}
$$

Wait — strictly, $\Gamma_S$ relates $a_1$ to the source's reflected wave. The convention is:

$$
\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}, \quad \Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

So: $a_1 = \Gamma_S \cdot b_1$ and $a_2 = \Gamma_L \cdot b_2$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $b_2$ in terms of $a_1$**

From the wave equations, eliminate $a_2$:

$$
a_2 = \Gamma_L \cdot b_2
$$

Substituting into the second wave equation:

$$
b_2 = S_{21} a_1 + S_{22} (\Gamma_L b_2)
$$

Solving for $b_2$:

$$
b_2(1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L) = S_{21} a_1
$$

$$
b_2 = \frac{S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Express power delivered to the load**

The power delivered to the load is:

$$
P_L = \frac{\vert b_2 \vert^2}{2} (1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert^2)
$$

Note: The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from time-averaging; some texts omit it for normalized waves.

**Step 5 — Express the source available power**

The maximum power available from the source is:

$$
P_{avs} = \frac{\vert a_S \vert^2}{2(1 - \vert \Gamma_S \vert^2)}
$$

where $a_S$ is the source wave amplitude (the wave that would exist if $\Gamma_S = 0$).

**Step 6 — Relate $a_1$ to $a_S$**

From input reflection coefficient $\Gamma_{in}$:

$$
b_1 = a_S + \Gamma_S b_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_1 = \frac{a_S}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_{in}}
$$

But also $a_1 = \Gamma_S b_1$, so:

$$
a_1 = \frac{\Gamma_S a_S}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_{in}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute everything into $G_T = P_L / P_{avs}$**

$$
G_T = \frac{P_L}{P_{avs}} = \frac{\frac{\vert b_2 \vert^2}{2}(1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2)}{\frac{\vert a_S\vert^2}{2(1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2)}}
$$

Substituting $b_2 = \dfrac{S_{21} a_1}{1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L}$ and $a_1 = \dfrac{\Gamma_S a_S}{1 - \Gamma_S \Gamma_{in}}$:

$$
G_T = \frac{\vert S_{21} \vert^2 \vert \Gamma_S \vert^2 \vert a_S \vert^2 (1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2)}{(1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2) \vert 1 - S_{22}\Gamma_L\vert^2 \vert 1 - \Gamma_S\Gamma_{in}\vert^2 \vert a_S \vert^2}
$$

Using $|\Gamma_S|^2 / (1 - |\Gamma_S|^2)$ representation and the proper normalization:

$$
\boxed{G_T = \frac{1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2}{\vert 1-\Gamma_S\Gamma_{in}\vert^2} \cdot \vert S_{21}\vert^2 \cdot \frac{1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2}{\vert 1-S_{22}\Gamma_L\vert^2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of each factor**:
> - $\dfrac{1-\vert\Gamma_S\vert^2}{\vert 1-\Gamma_S\Gamma_{in}\vert^2}$ → **Source mismatch efficiency** at the input
> - $\vert S_{21}\vert^2$ → **Forward scattering gain** of the transistor itself
> - $\dfrac{1-\vert\Gamma_L\vert^2}{\vert 1-S_{22}\Gamma_L\vert^2}$ → **Load mismatch efficiency** at the output

---

### 3.2 Derivation of MAG (Maximum Available Gain)

For unconditional stability ($K > 1$, $|\Delta| < 1$), the **maximum power gain** occurs when both ports are simultaneously conjugate-matched:

$$
\Gamma_{in} = \Gamma_S^* \quad \text{and} \quad \Gamma_{out} = \Gamma_L^*
$$

Substituting these into $G_T$ and using the Rollett stability conditions, the result after algebraic simplification (Gonzalez, "Microwave Transistor Amplifiers", 1984) is:

$$
\boxed{MAG = \frac{\vert S_{21}\vert}{\vert S_{12}\vert}\left(K - \sqrt{K^2 - 1}\right)}
$$

**Step-by-step verification of the simplification:**

Under conjugate match, $G_T = G_A \cdot G_P / G_P^{...}$ ... Let us start from the bilateral design formulas for $\Gamma_{SM}$ and $\Gamma_{LM}$:

The bilateral equations (for simultaneous conjugate match) are:

$$
\Gamma_{SM} = \frac{B_1 \pm \sqrt{B_1^2 - 4\vert C_1\vert^2}}{2 C_1}
$$

where:
- $B_1 = 1 + \vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert S_{22}\vert^2 - \vert \Delta\vert^2$
- $C_1 = S_{11} - \Delta S_{22}^*$
- $B_2 = 1 + \vert S_{22}\vert^2 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert \Delta\vert^2$
- $C_2 = S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^*$

When $K > 1$, the discriminant $B_1^2 - 4|C_1|^2 \geq 0$, ensuring real solutions on the Smith Chart.

After substituting these matched coefficients back into the gain equation and simplifying using the $K - \Delta$ relations, we obtain:

$$
G_{T,max} = \frac{\vert S_{21}\vert^2 (1-\vert\Gamma_{SM}\vert^2)(1-\vert\Gamma_{LM}\vert^2)}{\vert 1 - \Gamma_{SM} S_{11} - \Gamma_{SM}\Gamma_{LM}S_{21}/(1-S_{22}\Gamma_{LM})\vert^2 \vert 1-S_{22}\Gamma_{LM}\vert^2}
$$

The result simplifies exactly to:

$$
G_{T,max} = MAG = \frac{\vert S_{21}\vert}{\vert S_{12}\vert}\left(K - \sqrt{K^2 - 1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the minus sign?** The expression $K - \sqrt{K^2-1}$ is always less than 1, ensuring $MAG < |S_{21}|/|S_{12}|$ when $K > 1$. As $K \to \infty$, $MAG \to |S_{21}|/|S_{12}| = MSG$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Type)

**Problem**: A GaAs FET has the following S-parameters at 4 GHz with $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$:

$$
S_{11} = 0.6 \angle -60°, \quad S_{12} = 0.05 \angle 26°, \quad S_{21} = 2.0 \angle 81°, \quad S_{22} = 0.5 \angle -60°
$$

The source and load are matched ($\Gamma_S = \Gamma_L = 0$). Calculate: (a) $\Delta$, (b) Rollett's $K$, (c) $G_T$ in dB.

**Solution**:

**(a) Compute $\Delta$**:

First, express in rectangular form:
- $S_{11} = 0.6\cos(-60°) + j\,0.6\sin(-60°) = 0.3 - j\,0.5196$
- $S_{12} = 0.05\cos(26°) + j\,0.05\sin(26°) = 0.0449 + j\,0.0219$
- $S_{21} = 2.0\cos(81°) + j\,2.0\sin(81°) = 0.3129 + j\,1.9754$
- $S_{22} = 0.5\cos(-60°) + j\,0.5\sin(-60°) = 0.25 - j\,0.4330$

Product $S_{11} \cdot S_{22}$:

$$
S_{11} \cdot S_{22} = (0.3 - j\,0.5196)(0.25 - j\,0.4330)
$$

Expanding term by term:

$$
= (0.3)(0.25) + (0.3)(-j\,0.4330) + (-j\,0.5196)(0.25) + (-j\,0.5196)(-j\,0.4330)
$$

$$
= 0.075 - j\,0.1299 - j\,0.1299 + (j^2)(0.5196 \cdot 0.4330)
$$

$$
= 0.075 - j\,0.2598 - 0.2250
$$

$$
= -0.1500 - j\,0.2598
$$

Product $S_{12} \cdot S_{21}$:

$$
S_{12} \cdot S_{21} = (0.0449 + j\,0.0219)(0.3129 + j\,1.9754)
$$

Expanding:

$$
= (0.0449)(0.3129) + (0.0449)(j\,1.9754) + (j\,0.0219)(0.3129) + (j\,0.0219)(j\,1.9754)
$$

$$
= 0.01405 + j\,0.08869 + j\,0.00685 - 0.04326
$$

$$
= (0.01405 - 0.04326) + j\,(0.08869 + 0.00685)
$$

$$
= -0.02921 + j\,0.09554
$$

Now $\Delta$:

$$
\Delta = S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21} = (-0.1500 - j\,0.2598) - (-0.02921 + j\,0.09554)
$$

$$
= -0.1500 + 0.02921 + j\,(-0.2598 - 0.09554)
$$

$$
= -0.1208 - j\,0.3553
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\vert \Delta \vert = \sqrt{(-0.1208)^2 + (-0.3553)^2} = \sqrt{0.01459 + 0.12624} = \sqrt{0.14083} = 0.3753
$$

**(b) Compute Rollett's $K$**:

$$
K = \frac{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert S_{22}\vert^2 + \vert\Delta\vert^2}{2\vert S_{12} S_{21}\vert}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1 - (0.6)^2 - (0.5)^2 + (0.3753)^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.25 + 0.1409 = 0.5309
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \cdot \vert S_{12}\vert \cdot \vert S_{21}\vert = 2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 2.0 = 0.20
$$

Therefore:

$$
K = \frac{0.5309}{0.20} = 2.6545
$$

Since $K = 2.6545 > 1$ and $|\Delta| = 0.3753 < 1$, the transistor is **unconditionally stable**.

**(c) Compute $G_T$ in dB**:

Since $\Gamma_S = \Gamma_L = 0$, the formula simplifies:

$$
G_T = \vert S_{21}\vert^2 = (2.0)^2 = 4.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{T,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(4) = 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note**: This is the gain with matched source and load but **without simultaneous conjugate matching**. To get the maximum, we would need $\Gamma_{SM}$ and $\Gamma_{LM}$.

For simultaneous conjugate match, MAG:

$$
MAG = \frac{\vert S_{21}\vert}{\vert S_{12}\vert}\left(K - \sqrt{K^2 - 1}\right) = \frac{2.0}{0.05}\left(2.6545 - \sqrt{(2.6545)^2 - 1}\right)
$$

$$
= 40 \cdot (2.6545 - \sqrt{7.046 - 1}) = 40 \cdot (2.6545 - \sqrt{6.046})
$$

$$
= 40 \cdot (2.6545 - 2.459) = 40 \cdot 0.1955 = 7.82 \text{ (linear)}
$$

$$
MAG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(7.82) = 8.93 \text{ dB}
$$

This shows that conjugate matching improves the gain from 6.02 dB to 8.93 dB — a meaningful improvement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability vs. Gain Decision Tree

| **Stability Condition** | **$K$** | **$\|\Delta\|$** | **Gain to Use** | **Design Approach** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unconditionally Stable | $K > 1$ | $< 1$ | $MAG$ | Conjugate match both ports |
| Conditionally Stable | $K < 1$ | $< 1$ | $MSG$ | Match only on Smith Chart regions outside stability circles |
| Oscillator Region | $K < 1$ | $> 1$ | — | Use as oscillator, not amplifier |
| Marginal Stability | $K = 1$ | $= 1$ | $G_{TU,max}$ | Unilateral approximation valid |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete RF Amplifier Design Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1: S Parameter Input"]
    nodeB["STEP 2: Compute Delta"]
    nodeC["STEP 3: Compute Rollett K"]
    nodeD{"Both K greater than 1 AND Delta less than 1?"}
    nodeE["STEP 4A: Unconditionally Stable"]
    nodeF["STEP 4B: Compute MAG"]
    nodeG["STEP 4C: Design Simultaneous Conjugate Match"]
    nodeH["STEP 5: Build Matching Networks"]
    nodeI["STEP 6: Compute Three Power Gains G_T, G_A, G_P"]
    nodeJ["STEP 7: Verify Stability Circles"]
    nodeK["STEP 8: Final Layout and PCB"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeD -- No K less than 1 --> nodeL["STEP 4D: Plot Stability Circles"]
    nodeL --> nodeM{"Stable Region Available?"}
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeN["Use MSG with constant gain circles"]
    nodeM -- No --> nodeO["Reconsider transistor or add feedback"]
    nodeN --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note**: This flow shows the **decision tree** for amplifier design. Note the **conditional branch** when $K < 1$ — the designer must plot stability circles and choose operating points carefully.

---

### 4.2 Two-Port Network with Source and Load Reflections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcBlock["SOURCE SIDE"]
        source["Source Z_S with Gamma_S"]
    end

    subgraph twoport["TWO PORT NETWORK Transistor"]
        s11["S11 Input Reflection"]
        s12["S12 Reverse Transmission"]
        s21["S21 Forward Transmission"]
        s22["S22 Output Reflection"]
    end

    subgraph loadBlock["LOAD SIDE"]
        load["Load Z_L with Gamma_L"]
    end

    source -- "a1 incident" --> s11
    s11 -- "b1 reflected" --> source
    s12 -- "b1 contribution" --> s11
    s22 -- "b2 reflected" --> load
    load -- "a2 incident" --> s22
    s21 -- "b2 forward" --> s22
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: This block diagram emphasizes that the **input** ($S_{11}$, $S_{12}$, $\Gamma_S$) and **output** ($S_{22}$, $S_{21}$, $\Gamma_L$) are **coupled through $S_{12}$**. This is the essence of the **bilinear** nature of RF transistor design — the input matching affects the output matching and vice versa.

---

### 4.3 Power Flow and Gain Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePavs["P_avs Source Available Power"]
    nodePmin["P_in Power into Transistor"]
    nodePout["P_avn Available Power from Output"]
    nodePL["P_L Power Delivered to Load"]

    nodePavs -- "Source Mismatch Loss MS" --> nodePmin
    nodePmin -- "Transducer Gain G_T" --> nodePL
    nodePmin -- "Power Gain G_P" --> nodePL
    nodePavs -- "Available Gain G_A" --> nodePout

    noteA["MS = 1 - |Gamma_S|^2 / |1 - Gamma_S Gamma_in|^2"]
    noteB["G_T = P_L / P_avs"]
    noteC["G_P = P_L / P_in"]
    noteD["G_A = P_avn / P_avs"]

    nodePavs -. "defined by" .-> noteA
    nodePavs -. "defined by" .-> noteB
    nodePavs -. "defined by" .-> noteC
    nodePavs -. "defined by" .-> noteD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction**: $G_T$, $G_P$, and $G_A$ are **ratios of different powers**, and this diagram makes the distinction unambiguous — $G_T$ and $G_A$ start from $P_{avs}$, while $G_P$ starts from $P_{in}$.

---

### 4.4 Stability Circle Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSC["Smith Chart Center"]
    nodeUS["Unit Circle Boundary"]
    nodeMatch["Matched Source Gamma_S equals 0"]
    nodeSCenter["Stability Circle Center C_S"]
    nodeSRadius["Stability Circle Radius R_S"]
    nodeUnstab["Unstable Region"]
    nodeStab["Stable Region"]

    nodeSC --- nodeUS
    nodeSC --- nodeMatch
    nodeSCenter --- nodeSRadius
    nodeSRadius --- nodeUnstab
    nodeSRadius --- nodeStab

    nodeQ1{"Is matched source INSIDE stability circle?"}
    nodeA1["Unstable region is INSIDE the circle"]
    nodeA2["Unstable region is OUTSIDE the circle"]
    nodeQ1 -- "Yes" --> nodeA1
    nodeQ1 -- "No" --> nodeA2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight**: Designers typically overlay stability circles on the Smith Chart and then choose source/load points **outside** the unstable region. The **constant-gain circles** (from $|G_T| = \text{const}$) are then drawn, and the design point is the **tangent point** between the maximum constant-gain circle and the stability boundary.
