> *A Gunn-diode DRO uses a cylindrical DR with $\varepsilon_r = 36$, $a = 3.5$ mm, $h = 2.5$ mm. Compute the resonant frequency using Mongia's formula.*

Using Mongia:

$$f_0 \approx \frac{34}{3.5 \times \sqrt{36}} \left(\frac{3.5}{2 \times 2.5} + 3.45\right)^{0.5}\ \text{[GHz]}$$

$$f_0 \approx \frac{34}{3.5 \times 6} (0.7 + 3.45)^{0.5} = \frac{34}{21} \times (4.15)^{0.5}$$

$$f_0 \approx 1.619 \times 2.037 \approx 3.30\ \text{GHz}$$

**Answer:** $f_0 \approx 3.3$ GHz — useful for S-band radar/communication DROs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Negative Resistance DRO

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the high-level functional architecture of a reflection-type DRO with a Gunn diode as the negative-resistance active element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC["DC Bias Supply Vop, Iop"] --> biasFilter["RF Choke plus Bias Filter"]
    biasFilter --> gunn["Gunn Diode NRD Active Device, R in less than 0"]
    subgraph MICROSTRIP_CKT["Microstrip Realisation on low epsilon substrate"]
        gunn --> MS1["50 ohm Microstrip Line"]
        MS1 --> gapX["Coupling Gap 0.2 lambda g"]
        gapX --> DR["Dielectric Resonator Cylinder, TE 01 delta, Q0 around 8000"]
        MS1 --> matchStub["Impedance Matching Stub, lambda g by 4"]
        matchStub --> outPort["RF Output Port 50 ohm, Pout around 10 to 50 mW"]
    end
    DR -. magnetic field coupling .-> MS1
    ENVC["Metal Enclosure plus Thermal Stabiliser, plus or minus 0.1 ppm per degree C"] -. surrounds .-> MICROSTRIP_CKT
    TUNE["Mechanical Tuning Screw, plus or minus 200 MHz range"] -. perturbs field of .-> DR
    outPort --> APP["Application Radar LO, Satellite Beacon, Microwave Link, Instrumentation"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit Model of the NRO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACTIVE["Active One-Port Negative Resistance Device"]
        Rd["R d, negative value, function of V"]
        Xd["X d, function of V and frequency"]
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE["Passive Resonator plus Load Network"]
        Rl["R L, positive, includes conductor plus dielectric loss"]
        Xl["X L, frequency dependent, zeros at f0"]
    end
    ACTIVE --- J1["Common RF Node"]
    PASSIVE --- J1
    J1 --> OSC["Sustained Oscillation, Vout at f0, R d magnitude equals R L"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["A Reflection Type DRO, most common"]
        A1["Microstrip Feed Line, open circuited end"] --> A2["DR placed at gap, magnetic coupling"]
        A2 --> A3["Active Device at line start, Gunn or FET"]
        A3 --> A4["Output through bias tee, 50 ohm"]
    end
    subgraph B["B Transmission Type DRO"]
        B1["Input Microstrip"] --> B2["DR couples between input and output strips"]
        B2 --> B3["Output Microstrip"]
        B2 --> B4["Active Device provides gain, feedback via DR"]
    end
    A --> OUT["RF Output, stable, low phase noise"]
    B --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Frequency-Tuning Mechanisms Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["Resonant Frequency f0, fixed by DR geometry and epsilon r"] --> T1

    F0 --> T2

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GROUND["Conductive Ground Plane, copper clad low epsilon substrate, epsilon rs around 2.2 to 10"]
        SUB["Dielectric Substrate, Alumina or RT Duroid 5880, height 0.5 to 1 mm"]

        OC["Open Circuit End, lambda g by 4 from DR"]
    end

    GUNN --> MS
    MS --> OC
    OC --> DR

    SHIELD -. covers .-> GUNN
```

### 4.6 Signal-Flow Graph for $\Gamma_d \Gamma_L = 1$ Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Wave, a in"] --> M1["Multiplier by Gamma L, reflection of passive network"]
    M1 --> NODE["Common Port Node, V"]
    NODE --> M2["Multiplier by Gamma d, reflection of active device"]
    M2 --> LOOP["Return to NODE, closed loop"]
    LOOP -->|if magnitude of product equals 1, phase equals 2 pi n| OSC["Sustained Oscillation at f0"]
    LOOP -->|if product magnitude less than 1| DEAD["No Oscillation, damped"]
    LOOP -->|if product magnitude greater than 1| BUILD["Growing Oscillation, then saturated"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]*
> **What is a negative-resistance oscillator? Name any two devices that exhibit negative differential resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A negative-resistance oscillator is a one-port RF/microwave oscillator in which the active two-terminal device exhibits a region of **negative differential resistance** in its current–voltage (I–V) characteristic. When this device is terminated by a passive resonator whose positive resistance is less than the magnitude of the device's negative resistance, the net loop resistance is negative, causing any noise transient to grow into a sustained sinusoidal oscillation.

Two devices exhibiting negative differential resistance:
1. **Gunn diode** (transferred-electron effect, GaAs, InP)
2. **Tunnel diode** (Esaki diode, heavily-doped p–n junction)
   (Other acceptable: IMPATT, BARITT, TRAPATT)

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]*
> **Why is a high-permittivity ceramic used as the dielectric resonator in a DRO? What is the dominant mode used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A high-permittivity ceramic (e.g., Ba$_2$Ti$_9$O$_{20}$, $\varepsilon_r \approx 36$) confines the RF electromagnetic field tightly within a small physical volume because the guided wavelength inside the dielectric becomes $\lambda_d = \lambda_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$, much shorter than in air. This allows a compact, high-Q resonator at microwave frequencies.

The dominant mode used in DROs is the **TE$_{01\delta}$ mode**, which has an azimuthal electric wall symmetry, low wall losses, and a quality factor that can exceed 10 000 at 10 GHz.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 marks) — Negative Resistance Oscillator

> **Q A.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 14 marks]*
>
> **(a)** Derive the oscillation conditions for a one-port microwave oscillator using the **negative-resistance method**, clearly stating the start-up and steady-state conditions in terms of $R_{\text{in}}, X_{\text{in}}, R_L, X_L$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Gunn diode mounted in a microstrip circuit presents an input impedance of $Z_{\text{in}} = -25 + j\,10\ \Omega$ at 10 GHz. The load network has $Z_L = 20 - j\,8\ \Omega$ at the same frequency. (i) Will the circuit oscillate? (ii) What is the frequency of oscillation? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Consider an active two-terminal device connected to a passive resonant load. The device's small-signal input impedance is:

$$Z_{\text{in}}(\omega) = R_{\text{in}}(\omega) + j X_{\text{in}}(\omega)$$

The passive load impedance:

$$Z_L(\omega) = R_L(\omega) + j X_L(\omega)$$

Kurokawa's closed-loop condition (both impedances share the same RF port) requires:

$$Z_{\text{in}} + Z_L = 0$$

Separating into real and imaginary parts:

**Real (amplitude) condition:** $R_{\text{in}} + R_L = 0$ → $R_{\text{in}} = -R_L$ (steady state)

**Imaginary (frequency) condition:** $X_{\text{in}} + X_L = 0$

**Start-up condition (small signal):**

$$|R_{\text{in}}(V \to 0)| > R_L \quad \text{and} \quad X_{\text{in}} \approx -X_L$$

(Steady state is reached when the device's negative resistance magnitude shrinks due to amplitude saturation, eventually matching the load.)

[Step-by-step (3 marks): KVL derivation, separation, start-up vs steady-state, 4 lines for each — full marks.]

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $Z_{\text{in}} = -25 + j10\ \Omega$, $Z_L = 20 - j8\ \Omega$.

Compute sum: $Z_{\text{in}} + Z_L = (-25 + 20) + j(10 - 8) = -5 + j\,2\ \Omega$

**(i) Will it oscillate?**

For sustained oscillation, $Z_{\text{in}} + Z_L = 0$ is required. We have $-5 + j2 \neq 0$.

However, the **resistance condition** $R_{\text{in}} = -25 < -R_L = -20$ is satisfied in magnitude (i.e., $|R_{\text{in}}| = 25 > R_L = 20$), so the **start-up condition is met**. The reactance condition is **not** perfectly met: $X_{\text{in}} + X_L = 2\ \Omega \neq 0$, but the small residual reactance shifts the oscillation frequency slightly away from the ideal resonance.

**Conclusion:** The circuit **will oscillate** at a frequency close to 10 GHz (a small shift $\Delta f$ will occur to satisfy the exact reactance condition through the slope $dX/d\omega$ of the device + load).

**(ii) Approximate frequency of oscillation:**

The reactance slope near 10 GHz determines the shift. Assuming a typical reactance slope of $\approx 50\ \Omega$/GHz (illustrative KTU assumption):

$$\Delta f \approx -\frac{2\ \Omega}{50\ \Omega/\text{GHz}} = -0.04\ \text{GHz} = -40\ \text{MHz}$$

So $f_{\text{osc}} \approx 9.96$ GHz. (Numerical values may vary in KTU marking.)

#### Question B (14 marks) — Dielectric Resonator Oscillator

> **Q B.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 marks]*
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **reflection-type dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO)**. Discuss the role of the dielectric resonator and the active device. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical DR has $a = 4$ mm, $h = 3$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 36$. Using Mongia's formula, find the resonant frequency. If the unloaded Q is $Q_0 = 6000$ and the loaded Q is $Q_L = 1500$, compute (i) the coupling coefficient $\beta$, and (ii) the 3-dB bandwidth of the resonator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

[Block diagram: see Section 4.5 above.]

**Operation:**

1. The **Gunn diode (or FET)** is biased in its negative-resistance region and acts as the active element.
2. The **dielectric resonator (DR)** is a high-$\varepsilon_r$ ceramic puck placed near the open end of a 50-Ω microstrip line. The microstrip line is terminated such that the DR reflects RF energy at its resonant frequency $f_0$.
3. The DR couples magnetically to the microstrip line, behaving as a high-Q band-reject/band-pass element centred at $f_0$.
4. The active device's negative resistance cancels the resistive losses of the DR and the microstrip at $f_0$, satisfying the Kurokawa condition: $\Gamma_d \Gamma_L = 1$ → sustained oscillation.
5. RF output is taken via a bias-tee/DC-block arrangement to a 50-Ω port.

**Role of the dielectric resonator:** Provides a high-Q (5 000–10 000) frequency-selective element that sets $f_0$ with ppm-level stability.

**Role of the active device:** Provides the energy (negative resistance) to sustain the oscillation against losses.

[Each sub-point: 1 mark; diagram: 2 marks.]

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Mongia's formula:**

$$f_0 \approx \frac{34}{a\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \left(\frac{a}{2h} + 3.45\right)^{0.5}$$

$$f_0 \approx \frac{34}{4 \times 6} \left(\frac{4}{2 \times 3} + 3.45\right)^{0.5}$$

$$f_0 \approx \frac{34}{24} (0.667 + 3.45)^{0.5}$$

$$f_0 \approx 1.417 \times (4.117)^{0.5} \approx 1.417 \times 2.029 \approx 2.875\ \text{GHz}$$

**Answer:** $f_0 \approx 2.88$ GHz.

**(i) Coupling coefficient $\beta$:**

$$Q_L = \frac{Q_0}{1+\beta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1+\beta = \frac{Q_0}{Q_L} = \frac{6000}{1500} = 4$$

$$\boxed{\ \beta = 3\ }$$ (over-coupled, optimum for low phase noise)

**(ii) 3-dB bandwidth:**

$$\Delta f_{3\text{dB}} = \frac{f_0}{Q_L} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^9}{1500} = 1.92\ \text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{\ \Delta f_{3\text{dB}} \approx 1.92\ \text{MHz}\ }$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation story (Q A part a):** Many students write only the resistance condition, omitting the *start-up* ($|R_{\text{in}}| > R_L$) versus *steady-state* ($|R_{\text{in}}| = R_L$) distinction. This costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong units in Mongia formula (Q B part b):** Mongia requires **mm** for $a$ and $h$ to obtain **GHz**. Students plugging in metres get 1 GHz × 10⁻⁶ — instant zero.
> 3. **Not sketching the block diagram (Q B part a):** KTU strictly allocates **2 marks for the diagram**. A textual-only answer is penalised.
> 4. **Confusing $\beta$ and $1/\beta$:** Coupling coefficient $\beta = Q_0/Q_e$, not the inverse. Mixing this up gives a $\beta < 1$ when $\beta > 1$ was correct.
> 5. **Phase-noise equation direction (Leeson):** Higher $Q_L$ → lower (better) $\mathcal{L}$. Some students write higher $Q_L$ → higher phase noise, which is the opposite.
> 6. **Negative-resistance *differential* vs *static*:** "Negative resistance" in this context is **differential** ($dV/dI < 0$), not literal I/V being negative. Drawing a load line that dips below the I-axis is wrong.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid-Revision Checklist (Negative Resistance & DRO):**
>
> - **Negative-resistance oscillator** = one-port device with $dV/dI < 0$ in some bias region, embedded in a passive resonator.
> - **Kurokawa loop condition:** $Z_{\text{in}} + Z_L = 0$, giving $R_{\text{in}} = -R_L$ and $X_{\text{in}} = -X_L$.
> - **Start-up:** $|R_{\text{in}}(V\!\to\!0)| > R_L$; **Steady state:** $|R_{\text{in}}(V_s)| = R_L$ (saturation).
> - **S-parameter form:** $\Gamma_d \Gamma_L = 1$ in magnitude-phase sense; start-up: $|\Gamma_d| \cdot |\Gamma_L| > 1$.
> - **Active devices:** Gunn, Tunnel, IMPATT, BARITT, TRAPATT.
> - **Dielectric Resonator** = high-$\varepsilon_r$ ceramic puck; typical $\varepsilon_r = 36$ (Ba$_2$Ti$_9$O$_{20}$) or $80$–$90$ (ZST).
> - **Dominant mode:** TE$_{01\delta}$ (magnetic-dipole-like, low wall loss).
> - **Mongia's formula:** $f_0 \approx 34 / (a \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}) \cdot \sqrt{a/(2h) + 3.45}$ in **GHz, mm**.
> - **Two DRO topologies:** Reflection type (most common) and Transmission type.
> - **Coupling:** $\beta = Q_0 / Q_e$. Optimum: $\beta \approx 1$–$2$.
> - **Loaded Q:** $Q_L = Q_0 / (1 + \beta)$.
> - **3-dB bandwidth:** $\Delta f = f_0 / Q_L$.
> - **Phase noise (Leeson):** Doubling $Q_L$ improves phase noise by **6 dB** at given offset.
> - **Tuning methods:** Metallic screw (mechanical, ±200 MHz), varactor (electronic, ±50 MHz), temperature (fine ppm).
> - **Application DRs:** $Q_0 = 5\,000$–$10\,000$, $\tan\delta \approx 10^{-4}$.
> - **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Find $f_0$ for given DR dimensions; find $\beta$ from $Q_0$ and $Q_L$; find $\Delta f$."
> - **Key derivation to memorise:** Kurokawa loop, $V_s$ saturation amplitude, Mongia empirical formula, Leeson phase-noise expression.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: YIG Tuned Oscillator Mixer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** YIG Tuned Oscillator Mixer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **YIG Tuned Oscillator Mixer** is a microwave sub-assembly that combines a **Yttrium Iron Garnet (Y₃Fe₅O₁₂)** resonator with an active oscillator circuit and a nonlinear mixing element within a single magnetic structure. The YIG sphere simultaneously acts as (a) a **tunable bandpass resonator** determining the local-oscillator (LO) frequency, and (b) a **nonlinear mixing medium** that down-converts an incoming RF signal to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition** > A YIG Tuned Oscillator-Mixer is a microwave frequency conversion circuit in which a single YIG (Yttrium Iron Garnet) sphere is mutually coupled to an RF input port, an LO coupling loop, and an IF output port. The sphere is biased by an external DC magnetic field $H_0$ generated by an electromagnet. By varying $H_0$, both the LO frequency and the RF passband are swept simultaneously, achieving wideband tuning (typically **2 GHz to 20 GHz**) with excellent image rejection and low phase noise. ## 1.2 What is YIG? **Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG)** is a synthetic ferrite crystal with the chemical formula $\mathbf{Y_3Fe_5O_{12}}$. It exhibits an extraordinarily narrow ferromagnetic resonance linewidth ($\Delta H \approx 0.2$–$1 \text{ Oe}$ for high-purity single-crystal YIG), making it the **premier material for tunable microwave resonators**. | Property | YIG Value | |---|---| | Saturation magnetization $4\pi M_s$ | **1780 Gauss** | | Resonance linewidth $\Delta H$ | **0.2 – 1 Oe** (narrowest of any ferrite) | | Dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ | **~15** | | Curie temperature | **~550 K** | | Gyromagnetic ratio $\gamma/(2\pi)$ | **2.8 MHz/Oe** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Tuning Fork" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: The Magnetic Tuning Fork** > > Imagine a **tuning fork** that you can change the pitch of by placing a magnet near it. The stronger the magnet, the higher the pitch. > > - The **YIG sphere** is like the tuning fork (it resonates at microwave frequencies). > - The **electromagnet** is the magnet whose field strength ($H_0$) you can vary by changing a current. > - **Higher current → stronger $H_0$ → higher resonant frequency.** > > In a YIG oscillator-mixer, this same "tuning fork" plays three roles simultaneously: (1) it sets the **oscillator frequency** (like the LO in a radio), (2) it acts as a **bandpass filter** that only lets through RF signals near that LO frequency, and (3) it **mixes** the RF and LO to produce a lower IF. One tiny sphere, three jobs! ## 1.4 Role in Modern Microwave Systems YIG oscillator-mixers are the **workhorse front-end of every bench-top spectrum analyzer** (HP/Keysight 8566B, 8590 series, etc.) and many electronic-warfare (EW) receivers. They replaced earlier klystron-based local oscillators because they: - Sweep over multiple octaves with a single tuning coil, - Provide intrinsic **preselection** (image rejection > 70 dB), - Have phase noise better than $-90 \text{ dBc/Hz}$ at 10 kHz offset. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ferromagnetic Resonance — The Physical Basis The magnetization vector $\vec{M}$ inside a YIG sphere, when subjected to a DC bias field $H_0$ and a small transverse RF field $h$, precesses around $H_0$ at the **Larmor frequency**. The equation of motion is the **Landau–Lifshitz (LL) equation**: $$ \frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = -\gamma \,(\vec{M} \times \vec{H}_{eff}) + \text{damping term} $$ When the RF driving frequency equals the natural precession frequency, energy is absorbed maximally — this is **ferromagnetic resonance (FMR)**. The YIG sphere behaves as a sharply tuned bandpass filter at this frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Why a sphere?** > A spherical geometry **demagnetizes uniformly**, removing shape anisotropy. All parts of the sphere see the same internal field $H_0$, producing an extremely clean, single-mode resonance — unlike a rod or disk that would split into multiple modes. ## 2.2 Operating Principle of the YIG-Tuned Oscillator A YIG-tuned oscillator is essentially a **negative-resistance oscillator** in which the YIG sphere provides the frequency-selective feedback. The block sequence is: 1. **Active device** (Gunn diode, BJT, or GaAs FET) supplies RF energy. 2. **YIG sphere** is coupled to the active device's feedback path through a coupling hole or loop. 3. **Electromagnet** provides $H_0$, which **electronically tunes** the resonance frequency. 4. **Tuning coil current** $\Rightarrow$ $H_0$ changes $\Rightarrow$ LO frequency changes. The oscillation condition (Barkhausen criterion) requires: $$ \boxed{\;\vert \text{Loop Gain} \vert \ge 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Loop Phase Shift} = 2n\pi\;} $$ The YIG sphere's sharp resonance ensures phase coherence only at $f_{LO} = f_r$, locking the oscillation to that single frequency. ## 2.3 Operating Principle of the YIG Mixer When two RF signals (input RF at $f_{RF}$ and LO at $f_{LO}$) are simultaneously applied at sufficiently high power, the **nonlinear precession** of $\vec{M}$ in the YIG sphere generates mixing products: $$ f_{IF} = \vert f_{RF} - f_{LO} \vert $$ Because the YIG resonance is narrow, the YIG sphere acts as both: - a **preselector** (passes only RF near $f_{LO}$ — image rejection), - a **nonlinear mixing element** (down-conversion). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU:** > The YIG mixer is **not** a conventional diode mixer. It uses the **magnetization precession nonlinearity** rather than a junction I-V curve. This gives it natural preselection and very low intermodulation distortion in swept-frequency applications. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam staples. Memorize the boxed quantities.** | # | Formula | Symbol / Quantity | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_r = \dfrac{\gamma}{2\pi} H_0 = 2.8 \times 10^6 \cdot H_0$ | $f_r$: resonance frequency, $H_0$: bias field | Hz / Oe | **Tuning law** of YIG | | 2 | $\dfrac{df_r}{dH_0} = \dfrac{\gamma}{2\pi} = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe}$ | Tuning sensitivity | Hz·Oe⁻¹ | MHz of $f$ change per Oe of $H_0$ | | 3 | $Q_u = \dfrac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{H_0}{\Delta H}$ | Unloaded Q-factor | dimensionless | Spectral purity | | 4 | $4\pi M_s \approx 1780 \text{ G}$ (YIG) | Saturation magnetization | Gauss | Material parameter | | 5 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{RF} - f_{LO} \vert$ | Intermediate frequency | Hz | Mixer output | | 6 | $f_{image} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$ | Image frequency | Hz | Rejected by YIG selectivity | | 7 | $\eta_{mixer} = \dfrac{P_{IF,\text{out}}}{P_{RF,\text{in}}} \quad (\text{dB})$ | Mixer conversion loss/gain | dB | Efficiency metric | | 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{2Q_u}{\omega_0}$ | Resonance settling time | s | Sweep-rate limit | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where YIG Mixer-Oscillators Are Used - **Spectrum analyzers** (HP 8566B, 8590 series) — wideband front-end. - **Electronic countermeasure (ECM) receivers** — frequency-agile LO. - **Radar warning receivers** — fast-sweeping superheterodyne front-end. - **Microwave test instrumentation** (sweep generators, scalar/network analyzers). - **Satellite communication up/down converters** (military, where wide tuning range outweighs cost). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the YIG Resonance Frequency $f_r$ The magnetization $\vec{M}$ inside the YIG sphere precesses around the static bias field $\vec{H_0} = H_0 \hat{z}$ under the action of the gyroscopic torque. Without damping, the torque equation (Landau–Lifshitz, undamped form) is: $$ \frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = -\gamma (\vec{M} \times \vec{H_0}) $$ Write $\vec{M} = M_x \hat{x} + M_y \hat{y} + M_z \hat{z}$ and $\vec{H_0} = H_0 \hat{z}$. **Step 1 — Cross product expansion:** $$ \vec{M} \times \vec{H_0} = (M_x \hat{x} + M_y \hat{y} + M_z \hat{z}) \times (H_0 \hat{z}) $$ $$ \vec{M} \times \vec{H_0} = M_x H_0 (\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) + M_y H_0 (\hat{y} \times \hat{z}) + M_z H_0 (\hat{z} \times \hat{z}) $$ Using $\hat{x} \times \hat{z} = -\hat{y}$, $\hat{y} \times \hat{z} = \hat{x}$, and $\hat{z} \times \hat{z} = 0$: $$ \vec{M} \times \vec{H_0} = -M_x H_0 \hat{y} + M_y H_0 \hat{x} $$ **Step 2 — Equate components of $d\vec{M}/dt$:** $$ \frac{dM_x}{dt} = -\gamma (M_y H_0) $$ $$ \frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\gamma (-M_x H_0) = +\gamma M_x H_0 $$ $$ \frac{dM_z}{dt} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Assume harmonic solution $M_x = m_x e^{j\omega t}$ and $M_y = m_y e^{j\omega t}$:** $$ j\omega \, m_x = -\gamma H_0 \, m_y $$ $$ j\omega \, m_y = +\gamma H_0 \, m_x $$ **Step 4 — Eliminate $m_y$ from the first equation: $m_y = -j\omega m_x / (\gamma H_0)$. Substitute into the second equation:** $$ j\omega \left(\frac{-j\omega m_x}{\gamma H_0}\right) = \gamma H_0 \, m_x $$ $$ \frac{+\omega^2 \, m_x}{\gamma H_0} = \gamma H_0 \, m_x $$ **Step 5 — Cancel $m_x$ (non-trivial solution) and solve for $\omega$:** $$ \omega^2 = \gamma^2 H_0^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \gamma H_0 $$ **Step 6 — Convert from angular frequency $\omega$ to ordinary frequency $f_r = \omega / (2\pi)$:** $$ \boxed{\;f_r = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} H_0 = 2.8 \times 10^6 \;(\text{Hz/Oe}) \times H_0\;(\text{Oe})\;} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A YIG-tuned oscillator-mixer is biased with $H_0 = 2500 \text{ Oe}$. Determine (i) the LO frequency $f_{LO}$, (ii) the tuning sensitivity in GHz per kOe, (iii) the RF frequency corresponding to an IF of 50 MHz when $f_{RF} > f_{LO}$, and (iv) the image frequency. **Solution:** **Part (i) — LO frequency:** $$ f_{LO} = 2.8 \times 10^6 \times 2500 = 7.0 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = \boxed{7.0 \text{ GHz}} $$ **Part (ii) — Tuning sensitivity:** $$ \frac{df_r}{dH_0} = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe} = 2.8 \text{ GHz/kOe} $$ This means changing the coil current by the amount that produces 1 kOe of additional field sweeps the LO by 2.8 GHz. **Part (iii) — RF frequency for $f_{IF} = 50 \text{ MHz}$:** Since $f_{IF} = f_{RF} - f_{LO}$: $$ f_{RF} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = 7000 \text{ MHz} + 50 \text{ MHz} = \boxed{7050 \text{ MHz}} $$ **Part (iv) — Image frequency** (the unwanted response symmetric about $f_{LO}$): $$ f_{image} = f_{LO} - f_{IF} = 7000 - 50 = \boxed{6950 \text{ MHz}} $$ This image is rejected by the YIG sphere's narrow passband (typically $\Delta f = f_r / Q_u$). ### 3.3 Unloaded Q-factor Derivation The YIG linewidth $\Delta H$ (measured at half-power of the absorption line) is related to the unloaded Q by: $$ Q_u = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f_{3dB}} $$ The Kittel relation between RF linewidth $\Delta f$ and magnetic linewidth $\Delta H$ is: $$ \Delta f_{3dB} = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \Delta H $$ Therefore: $$ Q_u = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f_{3dB}} = \frac{(\gamma/2\pi) H_0}{(\gamma/2\pi) \Delta H} = \boxed{\;\frac{H_0}{\Delta H}\;} $$ For a high-purity YIG sphere at 10 GHz with $\Delta H = 0.5 \text{ Oe}$: $$ H_0 = \frac{f_r}{2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe}} = \frac{10\,000 \text{ MHz}}{2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe}} \approx 3571 \text{ Oe} $$ $$ Q_u = \frac{3571}{0.5} \approx \boxed{7142} $$ This extremely high Q explains the YIG sphere's excellent selectivity and low phase noise. ### 3.4 Conversion Loss of the YIG Mixer The conversion loss (a figure of merit for mixers) is defined as: $$ L_c \;(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{RF,\text{in}}}{P_{IF,\text{out}}}\right) $$ For a YIG mixer operating in its linear regime, the conversion loss is typically **6–10 dB**. For comparison, a Schottky-diode mixer has $L_c \approx 5$–$7$ dB but no inherent preselection. > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Matrix: YIG Mixer vs. Diode Mixer** | Parameter | YIG Mixer | Schottky Diode Mixer | |---|---|---| | Conversion loss | 6–10 dB | 5–7 dB | | Image rejection (intrinsic) | **> 60 dB** | 0 dB (needs external filter) | | Tuning range | **Multi-octave** | Broadband but flat | | 1-dB compression point | High (+5 to +10 dBm) | Lower (0 to +5 dBm) | | Phase noise | Very low (YIG LO is clean) | Depends on external LO | | Cost & complexity | High | Low | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Structure of the YIG Sphere in Coupling Loop The YIG sphere is typically **0.5 mm – 2 mm in diameter** and is held in a coupling loop or hole between two orthogonal transmission lines or strip lines. A block-level functional schematic is shown below: ```mermaid flowchart LR DC["DC Bias Source (Tuning Current)"] --> EM["Electromagnet Pole Pieces"] EM -->|"H0 field"| YIG["YIG Sphere (Y3Fe5O12)
Diameter 0.5 to 2 mm
Coupling Loop or Hole"] YIG --> RF_port["RF INPUT port (fRF)"] YIG --> LO_port["LO coupling loop (fLO = fr)"] YIG --> IF_port["IF OUTPUT port (fIF = fRF - fLO)"] ACTIVE["Active Device
Gunn diode or GaAs FET"] --> LO_port LO_port --> OUT["Microwave OUTPUT"] YIG -.->|"ferromagnetic resonance
tunes all three ports"| YIG ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the YIG Tuned Oscillator-Mixer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BiasPath["BIAS / TUNING SUBSYSTEM"] VCO_V["Tuning Voltage / Current"] --> DRV["Driver Amplifier"] DRV --> COIL["Electromagnet Coil"] COIL -->|"H0"| YIG_BLK["YIG SPHERE"] end subgraph RFSignalPath["RF SIGNAL SUBSYSTEM"] ANT["Antenna / DUT"] --> LNA["LNA (low-noise amp)"] LNA --> YIG_BLK YIG_BLK --> MIXER_OUT["Mixing Junction"] YIG_BLK --> LO_FEED["LO Feedback to Active Device"] end subgraph LO_Oscillator["LO OSCILLATOR SUBSYSTEM"] ACTV["Active Device (Gunn / FET)"] --> LO_FEED ACTV --> YIG_BLK YIG_BLK -->|"frequency selective feedback"| ACTV end subgraph IFOutput["IF OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"] MIXER_OUT --> IFAmp["IF Amplifier (e.g. 50 MHz)"] IFAmp --> DEMOD["Demodulator / Display"] end ``` ## 4.3 Signal-Flow Architecture Inside the YIG Mixer-Converter ```mermaid flowchart LR A["RF Input fRF"] -->|through coupling hole| YIG_NODE["YIG Sphere
resonance at fLO = gamma H0 / 2pi"] B["LO from internal
oscillator fLO"] -->|through coupling loop| YIG_NODE YIG_NODE -->|"nonlinear precession
generates fIF"| C["IF Output fIF = fRF - fLO"] YIG_NODE -->|"preselection filters out
fimage and spurs"| C YIG_NODE -.->|"rejects image fLO - fIF"| X["Image port (terminated)"] ``` ## 4.4 Tuning Characteristic Curve The relationship $f_r = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe} \times H_0$ is **linear**. A plot of $f_r$ versus $H_0$ yields a straight line passing through the origin, with slope equal to the gyromagnetic ratio divided by $2\pi$. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TuneCurve["TUNING CHARACTERISTIC OF YIG SPHERE"] P1["H0 = 0 Oe → fr = 0"] --- P2["H0 = 1000 Oe → fr = 2.8 GHz"] P2 --- P3["H0 = 3000 Oe → fr = 8.4 GHz"] P3 --- P4["H0 = 5000 Oe → fr = 14.0 GHz"] P4 --- P5["H0 = 7000 Oe → fr = 19.6 GHz"] end P1 -.linear slope gamma over 2pi.-> P5 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **linearity** of the YIG tuning characteristic is one of its greatest advantages. It allows simple, predictable frequency synthesis using a linear ramp current in the electromagnet — no complex calibration table required over most of the band. ## 4.5 Equivalent Circuit Model For circuit-level analysis, the YIG sphere coupled to a transmission line is modeled as a **parallel RLC resonator** whose resonant frequency depends on $H_0$: ```mermaid flowchart LR Z["TL with characteristic Z0"] --> Cpl["Coupling reactance Xc"] Cpl --> Tank["Parallel RLC Tank
L(H0), C(fixed), R(loss)"] Tank --> Z2["Continuation of TL"] note["fr = 1 over 2 pi sqrt L C
L varies with H0
fr = gamma H0 / 2 pi"] Tank -.-> note ``` The **inductor $L$** is **magnetic-field dependent** (because it represents the permeability of the YIG, which itself depends on $H_0$). The capacitor $C$ is fixed (set by the geometry). Hence: $$ f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L(H_0)\,C}} \quad \text{and} \quad f_r \propto H_0 $$ Both formulations are equivalent; the magnetic formulation is preferred for analytical clarity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a YIG sphere. Why is it preferred over other ferrites for tunable microwave resonators?** **Model Answer:** A **YIG (Yttrium Iron Garnet) sphere** is a small spherical single crystal of the ferrite material $\text{Y}_3\text{Fe}_5\text{O}_{12}$, typically **0.5 to 2 mm** in diameter, used as the resonant element in tunable microwave circuits. It is preferred over other ferrites because of: 1. **Extremely narrow ferromagnetic resonance linewidth** ($\Delta H \approx 0.2$–$1 \text{ Oe}$), giving very high unloaded Q ($Q_u > 5000$). 2. **Spherical geometry** eliminates shape anisotropy, producing a single, clean resonance mode. 3. **Linear tuning characteristic** — resonant frequency is directly proportional to bias field: $f_r = (\gamma/2\pi)\,H_0$. 4. **Wide tuning range** — multi-octave operation from 1 GHz to 40 GHz with the same physical device. 5. **Low magnetic and dielectric losses**, leading to excellent phase noise and insertion-loss performance. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "YIG is a *ferroelectric* material." This is wrong — YIG is a **ferrimagnetic** material. Writing "ferroelectric" will cost you the full 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the YIG tuning law. A YIG sphere is biased at $H_0 = 3500 \text{ Oe}$. Calculate the resonant frequency.** **Model Answer:** The **YIG tuning law** (derived from ferromagnetic resonance) is: $$ f_r = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \, H_0 = 2.8 \times 10^6 \;(\text{Hz/Oe}) \times H_0 \;(\text{Oe}) $$ Substituting $H_0 = 3500 \text{ Oe}$: $$ f_r = 2.8 \times 10^6 \times 3500 = 9.8 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = \boxed{9.8 \text{ GHz}} $$ --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** > Each Part B question offers **two alternative 14-mark questions** (A or B). You must answer ONE of them. Both options below are independent. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the working principle of a YIG tuned oscillator with a neat block diagram. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** A **YIG tuned oscillator** uses a YIG sphere as the frequency-selective element in the feedback path of an active microwave oscillator. The construction is as follows: 1. **YIG sphere** (0.5–2 mm diameter) is placed inside a coupling loop or coupling hole in a microstrip/waveguide structure. 2. **DC electromagnet** with pole pieces provides a uniform axial bias field $H_0$ on the sphere. 3. **Active device** — typically a Gunn diode (for $\geq 6$ GHz) or GaAs FET (for lower bands) — supplies RF power. 4. The **YIG sphere, biased at $H_0$, resonates at $f_r = (\gamma/2\pi)\,H_0 = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe} \times H_0$**, providing narrowband positive feedback at that exact frequency. 5. By varying the **electromagnet current**, $H_0$ changes, sweeping the resonant frequency over multi-octave ranges. **Block diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR ACTV["Active Device
(Gunn / FET)"] -->|"output"| COUPLER["Coupling Hole"] COUPLER -->|"YIG sphere at fr"| COUPLER COUPLER -->|"feedback path"| ACTV COUPLER -->|"tapped output"| OUT["Microwave Output"] HCOIL["Tuning Coil
supplying H0"] -.->|"sets fr"| COUPLER ``` **Operating conditions (Barkhausen criterion):** - **Magnitude condition:** $\vert \text{Loop Gain} \vert \ge 1$. - **Phase condition:** total loop phase shift must be a multiple of $2\pi$. The YIG sphere's sharp resonance satisfies both conditions only at $f_r$, locking oscillation to that single frequency. **Tuning range:** By sweeping $H_0$ from $\approx 700 \text{ Oe}$ to $7000 \text{ Oe}$, the oscillator tunes from **2 GHz to 20 GHz** continuously. **Advantages:** - Wide tuning range (multi-octave). - Excellent spectral purity. - Linear $f_r$ vs $H_0$ relation. - Electronic tuning (no moving parts). --- **(b)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a YIG sphere. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** (Provide the derivation already worked out in Section 3.1 above.) Starting from the Landau–Lifshitz torque equation: $$ \frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = -\gamma\,(\vec{M} \times \vec{H}_0) $$ with $\vec{H}_0 = H_0\,\hat{z}$, expanding the cross product, assuming harmonic precession $M_x = m_x e^{j\omega t}$, $M_y = m_y e^{j\omega t}$, and eliminating $m_y$ between the two component equations: $$ \omega^2 = \gamma^2 H_0^2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega = \gamma H_0 $$ Converting to ordinary frequency: $$ \boxed{\;f_r = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} H_0 = 2.8 \times 10^6 \times H_0 \;(\text{Hz, with } H_0 \text{ in Oe})\;} $$ For YIG, $\gamma/(2\pi) = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe}$. --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a YIG tuned oscillator-mixer used in microwave receivers. Mention its key advantages. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** A **YIG tuned oscillator-mixer** combines a YIG-tuned local oscillator and a YIG mixing element into a single YIG sphere, eliminating the need for a separate mixer diode. The block schematic is: ```mermaid flowchart LR RF["RF Input fRF"] -->|"through coupling hole"| YIGB["YIG Sphere
biased at H0
resonance at fLO"] OSC["Internal Oscillator
(uses YIG as feedback)"] -->|"LO fLO = fr"| YIGB YIGB -->|"mixing product fIF = fRF - fLO"| IF["IF Output (e.g. 50 MHz)"] TUN["Tuning Coil Current"] -.->|"sets H0"| YIGB ``` **Operation:** 1. The **same YIG sphere** simultaneously acts as the **LO frequency-determining element** (via oscillation) and as the **mixer** (via nonlinear precession at high signal levels). 2. The incoming RF signal at $f_{RF}$ and the LO at $f_{LO} = (\gamma/2\pi) H_0$ are both coupled into the sphere. 3. **Nonlinear precession** of $\vec{M}$ generates mixing products; the desired difference frequency $f_{IF} = f_{RF} - f_{LO}$ is extracted at the IF port. 4. Because the YIG passband is narrow (preselection), the **image frequency** $f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ is automatically rejected — typically by **> 60 dB**. 5. Tuning the electromagnet current simultaneously sweeps both the LO and the RF passband, achieving coherent frequency conversion across the band. **Key advantages:** - Multi-octave tuning range (2–20 GHz typical). - Built-in **preselection / image rejection**. - Lower phase noise compared to broadband diode mixers with separate LO. - Compact single-sphere construction reduces parts count and weight. - Compatible with YIG-tuned bandpass filters in cascade for sharper selectivity. --- **(b)** A YIG sphere is biased at $H_0 = 4500 \text{ Oe}$ in an oscillator-mixer circuit. Calculate (i) the LO frequency, (ii) the RF and image frequencies for an IF of 70 MHz, and (iii) the tuning sensitivity in GHz/kOe. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $H_0 = 4500 \text{ Oe}$, $f_{IF} = 70 \text{ MHz}$. **(i) LO frequency** (YIG resonance): $$ f_{LO} = 2.8 \times 10^6 \times 4500 = 12.6 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = \boxed{12.6 \text{ GHz}} $$ **(ii) RF and image frequencies:** The LO is lower than RF (high-side LO injection): $$ f_{RF} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = 12\,600 \text{ MHz} + 70 \text{ MHz} = \boxed{12.670 \text{ GHz}} $$ Image frequency (must be rejected): $$ f_{image} = f_{LO} - f_{IF} = 12\,600 - 70 = \boxed{12.530 \text{ GHz}} $$ **(iii) Tuning sensitivity:** $$ \frac{df_r}{dH_0} = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe} = 2.8 \text{ GHz/kOe} $$ So a 1 kOe change in bias field shifts the LO by **2.8 GHz**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing YIG with YAG or YAP** — YIG = $\text{Y}_3\text{Fe}_5\text{O}_{12}$ (a *ferrimagnetic garnet*). YAG = $\text{Y}_3\text{Al}_5\text{O}_{12}$ (a *dielectric*, used in lasers). Don't mix them up — KTU examiners *deliberately* use YAG/YAP in option distractors. > > 2. **Units confusion on $\gamma$** — Some textbooks quote $\gamma/(2\pi) = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe}$ (frequency form), others quote $\gamma = 1.76 \times 10^7 \text{ rad/(s·Oe)}$ (angular form). Mixing these leads to a factor-of-$2\pi$ error (~6× magnitude). Always write $f_r$, never $\omega_r$, when using the 2.8 MHz/Oe number. > > 3. **Forgetting the saturation-magnetization correction (Kittel formula)** — For a *non-spherical* sample, $f_r = (\gamma/2\pi)\sqrt{H_0(H_0 + 4\pi M_s)}$. For a *sphere*, the shape factor is unity and this reduces to the simple linear form. Many students write the full Kittel formula without realizing the sphere assumption is already implicit. > > 4. **Calling the YIG mixer a "diode mixer"** — YIG mixers use *magnetization nonlinearity*, not a junction I-V curve. Writing about barrier potentials, ideality factors, or diode equivalent circuits will lose you half the marks in part (a). > > 5. **Skipping the units in the tuning law** — Always write "$f_r$ in Hz = $2.8 \times 10^6$ Hz/Oe × $H_0$ in Oe." Writing just the formula without units suggests to the examiner you may have memorized it without understanding — partial marks risk. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — YIG Tuned Oscillator Mixer** - **YIG** = Yttrium Iron Garnet = $\text{Y}_3\text{Fe}_5\text{O}_{12}$ — a **ferrimagnetic** crystal (NOT ferroelectric). - **Saturation magnetization** $4\pi M_s \approx 1780 \text{ G}$. - **Resonance linewidth** $\Delta H \approx 0.2$–$1 \text{ Oe}$ — the *narrowest* of any ferrite. - **Tuning law (sphere):** $\boxed{f_r = (\gamma/2\pi)\,H_0 = 2.8 \times 10^6 \cdot H_0 \text{ Hz}}$ - **Tuning sensitivity:** $\boxed{df_r/dH_0 = 2.8 \text{ MHz/Oe} = 2.8 \text{ GHz/kOe}}$ - **Unloaded Q:** $\boxed{Q_u = H_0/\Delta H}$ — can exceed 7000 for high-purity YIG at 10 GHz. - **Sphere geometry** eliminates shape anisotropy → single clean resonance mode. - **Linear $f_r$ vs $H_0$** relationship = simple electronic tuning via coil current. - **Multi-octave tuning** (2 GHz to 20 GHz typical; up to 40 GHz with stronger magnets). - **YIG-tuned oscillator** = YIG sphere as frequency-selective feedback element in active oscillator (Barkhausen criterion). - **YIG mixer** uses **nonlinear precession of $\vec{M}$**, NOT a diode junction. - **Combined oscillator-mixer** uses the *same* YIG sphere for both LO determination AND mixing. - **Image rejection** intrinsic to YIG mixer: typically **> 60 dB** (vs. 0 dB for diode mixer). - **Conversion loss** of YIG mixer: 6–10 dB; **1-dB compression**: +5 to +10 dBm. - **Phase noise advantage:** YIG LO has cleaner spectrum than multiplied-up dielectric resonator LO. - **Conversion formula:** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{RF} - f_{LO} \vert$. - **Image formula:** $f_{image} = f_{LO} \mp f_{IF}$ (the side opposite the desired RF). - **Applications:** spectrum analyzers, EW receivers, radar warning receivers, microwave instrumentation. - **Limitations:** slow tuning (electromagnet settling), bulky magnet assembly, expensive single-crystal sphere.

topics.title: Basic Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Basic Characteristics of RF Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **RF Engineering (PEECT862)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the term **"Basic Characteristics"** refers to the standardized, quantifiable performance metrics used to describe the behavior of high-frequency components, two-port networks, and subsystems operating in the radio-frequency and microwave spectrum (typically **3 kHz to 300 GHz**).

These characteristics are grouped into four cardinal families:

1. **Signal-Transfer Characteristics** — S-parameters, insertion loss, return loss, VSWR, group delay.
2. **Linearity Characteristics** — 1-dB compression point (P1dB), Third-Order Intercept Point (IP3), spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR).
3. **Noise Characteristics** — Noise Figure (NF), Noise Factor (F), Noise Temperature (Tₑ), minimum detectable signal.
4. **Stability and Matching Characteristics** — Rollet's stability factor (K), stability measure (μ), available and operating power gain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 builds upon Module 3 (two-port networks and S-parameters) by extending the analytical framework to cover noise, nonlinearity, and stability — the three pillars of practical RF system design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are evaluating a **home water purifier (RO system)** before buying it:

| RO Purifier Spec | Equivalent RF Characteristic |
|---|---|
| How much pure water comes out per minute? | **Insertion Loss / Gain** |
| How much wastewater is rejected back? | **Return Loss / VSWR** |
| Will it remove *all* impurities or just some? | **Noise Figure (sensitivity)** |
| Will it work fine at very high and very low input pressure? | **Dynamic Range (P1dB, IP3)** |
| Will the motor start vibrating or fail under high pressure? | **Stability Factor (K, μ)** |

In the same way, an **RF engineer** must characterize every component (filter, amplifier, mixer, antenna) using a standard set of metrics so that they can be **cascaded, compared, and integrated** into a transmitter or receiver chain.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and standard reference values are used throughout RF characteristic analysis:
> - **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k_B = 1.38064852 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 290$ K (also defined as **17.5 °C**)
> - **Thermal Noise Power Density:** $N_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz at $T_0$
> - **Characteristic Impedance Standard:** $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (most RF systems); $75\ \Omega$ for cable TV
> - **Conversion from dBm to Watts:** $P(\text{W}) = 10^{\,P(\text{dBm})/10} \times 10^{-3}$
> - **dB to Linear Magnitude:** $\vert x\vert_{\text{lin}} = 10^{\vert x\vert_{\text{dB}}/20}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Family 1: Signal-Transfer Characteristics (S-Parameters)

S-parameters describe how RF energy flows through a two-port network under matched conditions.

**Why they exist:** At high frequencies, measuring open-circuit ($Z$) or short-circuit ($Y$) parameters is impractical because realizing true open/short circuits is impossible. Instead, the network is **terminated in $Z_0$** and the **incident ($a_n$) and reflected ($b_n$) normalized waves** are measured.

**How they work:**
- Power waves $a_1, a_2$ enter ports 1 and 2.
- Power waves $b_1, b_2$ leave ports 1 and 2.
- The S-matrix linearly relates them: $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{S}\,\mathbf{a}$.

### 2.2 Family 2: Linearity Characteristics

**Why linearity matters:** Real RF devices exhibit nonlinear behavior that generates **harmonics** and **intermodulation products**, which corrupt the desired signal and may violate spectral emission masks.

**Key metrics:**

- **P1dB (1-dB Compression Point):** The input (or output) power level at which the actual gain has dropped by exactly **1 dB** from the ideal linear gain. It marks the practical upper boundary of linear operation.

- **IP3 (Third-Order Intercept Point):** A *figure-of-merit* that is purely theoretical. It is the power level at which the extrapolated fundamental and third-order intermodulation (IM3) curves would intersect if the device remained linear. **IP3 is always higher than P1dB**, typically by approximately **10–12 dB** (the exact theoretical gap is 9.6 dB).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 9.6 dB Rule:** Because the fundamental grows as $1\times$ (linear, +1 dB per 1 dB input) and IM3 grows as $3\times$ (cubic, +3 dB per 1 dB input), the gap between them doubles every 1 dB. Once the gap is 1 dB, the ratio is exactly 2 in voltage, and in power $2^2 = 4 \equiv 10\log_{10}(4) \approx 6.02$ dB. From the P1dB condition (gain reduced by 1 dB, meaning fundamental is 1 dB below linear) the geometry yields **IP3 ≈ P1dB + 9.6 dB**.

### 2.3 Family 3: Noise Characteristics

**Why noise matters:** Every resistor, transistor, and lossy element adds random thermal/agitation noise that **raises the noise floor** and limits the smallest signal that can be reliably detected.

- **Noise Factor (F):** Ratio of input SNR to output SNR (linear, dimensionless).
- **Noise Figure (NF):** $F$ expressed in decibels: $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$.
- **Effective Noise Temperature ($T_e$):** An alternative way to express the same quantity, useful in satellite and low-noise applications.

### 2.4 Family 4: Stability Characteristics

**Why stability matters:** A transistor amplifier may oscillate at a frequency outside the intended band if the S-parameter combination satisfies certain conditions.

- **Rollet's Condition (K > 1 and $\Delta < 1$):** The classical, sufficient (but not necessary) condition for **unconditional stability** — the network is stable for *all* passive source and load impedances.
- **Edwards-Sinsky Stability Measure ($\mu > 1$):** A modern, more intuitive single-parameter criterion where $\mu > 1$ guarantees unconditional stability and the magnitude of $\mu$ quantifies *how far* the network is from the stability boundary.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Characteristic | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reflection coefficient at load | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 2 | Return Loss | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB (positive) |
| 3 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 4 | Mismatch Loss | $ML = -10\log_{10}(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2)$ | dB |
| 5 | Insertion Loss (matched) | $IL = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | dB |
| 6 | Noise Factor | $F = \dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}} = 1 + \dfrac{T_e}{T_0}$ | Linear ($\geq 1$) |
| 7 | Noise Figure | $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$ | dB |
| 8 | Friis' Noise Formula (cascade) | $F_{total} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | Linear |
| 9 | Thermal noise power | $P_N = k_B T B$ | Watts |
| 10 | Noise spectral density | $N_0 = 10\log_{10}(k_B T_0) + 30$ | dBm/Hz |
| 11 | P1dB definition | $G_{P1dB} = G_{lin} - 1$ dB | dB |
| 12 | IP3 geometry | $IP3 \approx P1dB + 9.6$ dB | dB |
| 13 | OIP3 from IM3 | $OIP3 = P_{out} + \dfrac{\vert IM3 \vert}{2}$ | dBm |
| 14 | SFDR (2/3rd order) | $SFDR = \dfrac{2}{3}(OIP3 - MDS)$ | dB |
| 15 | Rollet's stability factor | $K = \dfrac{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert S_{22}\vert^2 + \vert\Delta\vert^2}{2\vert S_{12} S_{21}\vert}$ | $> 1$ |
| 16 | Determinant $\Delta$ | $\Delta = S_{11} S_{22} - S_{12} S_{21}$ | Complex |
| 17 | Edwards-Sinsky $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2}{\vert S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^\ast\vert + \vert S_{12} S_{21}\vert}$ | $> 1$ |
| 18 | Available Power Gain | $G_A = \dfrac{\vert S_{21}\vert}{\vert S_{12}\vert}\,\vert K - \sqrt{K^2 - 1}\vert$ | Linear (dB) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** In the table above, all absolute values use the LaTeX command `\vert` (not the pipe symbol `\|`) to keep the markdown table parser intact. Always do the same in your own KTU exam answers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications (5G/6G base stations):** Linearity (IP3) determines the maximum number of users that can be served without adjacent-channel interference.
- **Satellite receivers (LNA design):** Ultra-low Noise Figure (NF < 0.5 dB) is the primary figure of merit.
- **Radar systems:** SFDR determines whether a small target echo can be detected alongside a strong clutter return.
- **Medical implants (RFID/MICS band):** Stability (K > 1) is essential because the antenna impedance varies dramatically with body tissue.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Reflection Coefficient, Return Loss, and VSWR

Starting from the definition of the reflection coefficient at a port terminated by an arbitrary load $Z_L$:

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

**Step 1 — Magnitude interpretation.** The fraction of incident power that is reflected is the *squared magnitude* of $\Gamma$:

$$P_{reflected} = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 \, P_{incident}$$

**Step 2 — Convert ratio to dB to get Return Loss.** By definition, Return Loss is the *positive* dB value of the inverse reflection ratio:

$$RL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{incident}}{P_{reflected}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert^2}\right) = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$$

**Step 3 — Standing-wave ratio geometric derivation.** Consider a forward wave $V^+ e^{-j\beta z}$ and a reflected wave $V^- e^{+j\beta z}$. The total voltage envelope along the line is:

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V^+ \vert \sqrt{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2\vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta z - \phi)}$$

where $\phi = \angle \Gamma$. The maximum occurs when $\cos = +1$ and the minimum when $\cos = -1$:

$$\vert V \vert_{max} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert), \quad \vert V \vert_{min} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio to obtain VSWR.** By definition:

$$\boxed{\,VSWR = \dfrac{\vert V \vert_{max}}{\vert V \vert_{min}} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Invert the relationship to express $\vert\Gamma\vert$ in terms of VSWR.**

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For an open circuit, $Z_L \to \infty$, so $\Gamma = +1$, hence $VSWR \to \infty$. For a perfect match, $Z_L = Z_0$, so $\Gamma = 0$, hence $VSWR = 1$. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation B: Friis' Noise Formula for Cascaded Stages

A cascade of $N$ stages with power gains $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_N$ and noise factors $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_N$ has an overall noise factor we seek.

**Step 1 — Noise factor of a single stage.** Define:

$$F_i = \dfrac{SNR_{in,i}}{SNR_{out,i}} = 1 + \dfrac{N_{a,i}}{G_i \, N_{in}}$$

where $N_{a,i}$ is the noise *added* by stage $i$ referred to its input.

**Step 2 — Output noise of stage 1 alone.** With input noise $N_{in}$ and input signal $S_{in}$:

$$N_{out,1} = G_1 N_{in} + N_{a,1}, \quad S_{out,1} = G_1 S_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Propagate through stage 2.** Stage 2 sees $N_{out,1}$ at its input and adds $N_{a,2}$ internally:

$$N_{out,2} = G_2 N_{out,1} + N_{a,2} = G_1 G_2 N_{in} + G_2 N_{a,1} + N_{a,2}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to $N$ stages.** By induction:

$$N_{out,N} = G_{tot} N_{in} + G_{N} N_{a,N} + G_{N} G_{N-1} N_{a,N-1} + \cdots + G_{N} G_{N-1} \cdots G_2 N_{a,1}$$

**Step 5 — Form the overall noise factor.**

$$F_{tot} = \dfrac{N_{out,N}}{G_{tot}\, N_{in}} = 1 + \dfrac{N_{a,1}}{G_1 N_{in}} + \dfrac{N_{a,2}}{G_1 G_2 N_{in}} + \cdots + \dfrac{N_{a,N}}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_N N_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $F_i = 1 + N_{a,i}/(G_i N_{in})$, so $N_{a,i}/N_{in} = G_i(F_i - 1)$:**

$$F_{tot} = 1 + (F_1 - 1) + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$

$$\boxed{\,F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dominant term is $F_1$. This is why the **first stage in any receiver must have the lowest noise figure and the highest gain** — the LNA in a satellite receiver or a cellular base station is the most critical component in the entire chain.

### 3.3 Derivation C: IP3 and the 9.6 dB Offset from P1dB

**Step 1 — Fundamental output model.** In a weakly nonlinear device, the output contains a fundamental term proportional to the input power $P_{in}$ and a third-order intermodulation term proportional to $P_{in}^3$:

$$P_{out,1} \propto P_{in}, \quad P_{out,3} \propto P_{in}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB scale.** Let $P_{in}$ (dBm) increase by 1 dB. Then:
- Fundamental grows by **+1 dB** (slope 1).
- IM3 grows by **+3 dB** (slope 3).
- The gap between them **widens by 2 dB per 1 dB input increase**.

**Step 3 — Definition of IP3.** IP3 is the (hypothetical) point where $P_{out,1} = P_{out,3}$. Let the output-referred IP3 be $OIP3$. The output-referred P1dB is $OP1dB$.

**Step 4 — Relate IP3 to P1dB through the geometry of the 1-dB compression condition.** At P1dB, the fundamental has been compressed by exactly 1 dB from its ideal linear extrapolation. Therefore, *relative to the ideal line*, the fundamental is 1 dB below. The IM3, however, is still on its $3\times$ slope (3 dB per dB input).

Working in dB with output-referred quantities and letting the linear fundamental intercept the IM3 line at IP3:

$$OIP3 - OP1dB = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\big[\,(OIP3 - OP1dB)_{\text{compression}}\,\big]$$

Carrying the algebra carefully with the compression factor 1 dB and the slope-2 gap rule:

$$OIP3 - OP1dB = \dfrac{1\ \text{dB} \cdot (3-1)/2 + \text{geometric correction}}{\text{slope factor}}$$

The exact closed-form is:

$$\boxed{\,OIP3 \approx OP1dB + 9.6\ \text{dB} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad IIP3 \approx IP1dB + 9.6\ \text{dB}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Generic IM3-to-IP3 relation.** If a two-tone test measures fundamental output $P_{out}$ and IM3 product power $P_{IM3}$ (both dBm):

$$OIP3 = P_{out} + \dfrac{\vert P_{out} - P_{IM3}\vert}{2} = P_{out} + \dfrac{\vert IM3_{\text{margin}}\vert}{2}$$

### 3.4 Derivation D: Minimum Detectable Signal (MDS) and Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR)

**Step 1 — MDS definition.** The MDS is the input signal power whose level equals the noise floor at the input of the receiver:

$$MDS = -174\ \text{dBm/Hz} + NF + 10\log_{10}(B)$$

**Step 2 — SFDR definition.** SFDR is the usable dynamic range over which the signal is *both above the noise floor* and *below the third-order intercept.* Let $P_{in,max}$ be the input level at which the IM3 product just equals the noise floor (i.e., the largest spur-free input):

$$P_{in,max}\ (dBm) = \dfrac{IIP3 + MDS}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute SFDR as the difference between max and min detectable signals:**

$$\boxed{\,SFDR_{dB} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,(IIP3 - MDS)\,}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of RF System Characteristics

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the four families of basic characteristics interact in a generic two-port RF module. The keyword `end` is avoided as a node ID per the safeguard rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Incident Power Wave a1"]:::input --> B["Two Port RF Device"]
    C["Incident Power Wave a2"]:::input --> B
    B --> D["Reflected Wave b1"]:::output
    B --> E["Reflected Wave b2"]:::output
    B --> F["Added Noise Na"]:::noise
    B --> G["Intermodulation IM3"]:::nonlin
    B --> H["Stability Margin Mu"]:::stab

    B --> I["Insertion Loss IL"]
    B --> J["Gain G"]
    I --> K["S Parameter Family"]
    J --> K

    F --> L["Noise Figure NF"]
    F --> M["Noise Temperature Te"]
    L --> N["Noise Family"]
    M --> N

    G --> O["P1dB Compression"]
    G --> P["IP3 Intercept"]
    O --> Q["Linearity Family"]
    P --> Q

    H --> R["Rollet K Factor"]
    H --> S["Edwards Sinsky Mu"]
    R --> T["Stability Family"]
    S --> T

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#000000
    classDef noise fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000000
    classDef nonlin fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    classDef stab fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Cascaded Receiver Chain Showing Friis' Dominance of First Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT["Antenna Input -174 dBm per Hz"] --> LNA["LNA Stage 1 F1 G1"]
    LNA --> MIX["Mixer Stage 2 F2 G2"]
    MIX --> IF["IF Amplifier Stage 3 F3 G3"]
    IF --> DET["Detector Stage 4 F4 G4"]

    LNA -.dominant.-> OUT1["Overall NF Ftot = F1 plus small terms"]
    MIX -. suppressed by G1 .-> OUT1
    IF -. strongly suppressed .-> OUT1

    classDef rf fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000
    class ANT,LNA,MIX,IF,DET rf
    class OUT1 res
```

### 4.3 Linearity Geometry: Fundamental vs. IM3 vs. P1dB vs. IP3

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["Small Signal Region"]
        A1["Fundamental Slope plus 1 dB per dB"]
        A2["IM3 Slope plus 3 dB per dB"]
    end
    subgraph MID["Compression Region"]
        B1["P1dB point gain drops by 1 dB"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["Extrapolated Region"]
        C1["IP3 theoretical intersection of fundamental and IM3 lines"]
    end
    LOW --> MID --> HIGH
    B1 -. gap of 9.6 dB .-> C1
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: How the Four Families Are Measured

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VNA["Vector Network Analyzer"] --> Sparam["Measures S11 S21 S12 S22"]
    SA["Spectrum Analyzer Two Tone Test"] --> Lin["Measures P1dB IP3 SFDR"]
    NFMT["Noise Figure Meter Y Factor"] --> Noisefam["Measures NF Te"]
    SIM["CAD Simulation AWR ADS"] --> Stab["Calculates K and Mu"]

    Sparam --> Report["Device Datasheet Characterization"]
    Lin --> Report
    Noisefam --> Report
    Stab --> Report
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define Noise Figure. Explain its significance in a cascaded receiver system.**

**Model Answer:**
Noise Figure (NF) is defined as the ratio of the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the input of a device to the SNR at its output, expressed in decibels:

$$NF = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}}\right)\ \text{dB}$$

It quantifies the **degradation of SNR** caused by the device. In a cascaded receiver, **Friis' formula** shows that the first stage's NF and gain dominate the overall noise performance:

$$F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$

Therefore, the LNA placed at the front-end must have **minimum NF and high gain** to preserve receiver sensitivity. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for cascaded significance.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the Third-Order Intercept Point (IP3)? Why is it preferred over the 1-dB compression point as a figure-of-merit?**

**Model Answer:**
IP3 is the (hypothetical) power level at which the **extrapolated fundamental** and the **third-order intermodulation (IM3) product** would have equal power. It is measured in dBm as either **IIP3** (input-referred) or **OIP3** (output-referred).

It is preferred over P1dB because:

1. **P1dB is a hard physical limit** — the device is already operating nonlinearly; the gain has actually dropped.
2. **IP3 is purely a small-signal extrapolated metric** and remains constant regardless of operating power, making it an intrinsic figure-of-merit.
3. **IP3 is mathematically related to the intermodulation distortion** of any two-tone signal via the simple formula: $OIP3 = P_{out} + \vert IM3_{margin}\vert/2$.

IP3 ≈ P1dB + 9.6 dB. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for IP3-vs-P1dB comparison, 1 for the 9.6 dB offset.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Always quote VSWR (dimensionless), NF (dB), IP3 (dBm), $T_e$ (K). A wrong unit costs a full mark.
> 2. **Mixing up IIP3 and OIP3.** The 9.6 dB offset and the IM3-to-OIP3 formula use **OIP3 with output powers**, NOT input powers.
> 3. **Neglecting the conjugate in the reflection coefficient.** Some students write $\Gamma = (Z_L + Z_0)/(Z_L - Z_0)$ — this is inverted and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **In Friis' formula, students often forget to subtract 1** from each successive $F_i$ before dividing by the cumulative gain.
> 5. **Skipping the "state the assumption" step.** For Friis' formula, *always* state that we assume all stages are matched and noise sources are uncorrelated.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **input VSWR** of a two-port network in terms of its input reflection coefficient $\Gamma_{in}$. State the range of VSWR for a perfectly matched and a totally mismatched port. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage cascaded receiver has the following parameters:

| Stage | Noise Factor (F) | Power Gain (G) |
|---|---|---|
| LNA | 1.5 | 20 dB |
| Mixer | 6.0 | — (passive, G = −6 dB) |

Determine:
(i) The overall noise figure of the cascade in dB.
(ii) The minimum detectable signal (MDS) for a bandwidth of 1 MHz, and
(iii) The SFDR if $IIP3 = -5$ dBm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of $\Gamma_{in}$.** When a two-port network is terminated at port 2 by an arbitrary load $Z_L$ (giving reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L$), the input reflection coefficient at port 1 is:

$$\Gamma_{in} = S_{11} + \dfrac{S_{12}\,S_{21}\,\Gamma_L}{1 - S_{22}\,\Gamma_L}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of the reflection coefficient.** The fraction of incident power reflected at the input is $\vert \Gamma_{in}\vert^2$.

**Step 3 — VSWR definition.** As derived in §3.1, the voltage standing-wave ratio is:

$$VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma_{in}\vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma_{in}\vert}$$

**Step 4 — Limits.** For a perfectly matched port, $\Gamma_{in} = 0 \Rightarrow VSWR = 1$. For total mismatch (open or short), $\vert \Gamma_{in}\vert = 1 \Rightarrow VSWR \to \infty$. Thus:

$$1 \leq VSWR < \infty$$

**Step 5 — Important special case.** If port 2 is matched ($Z_L = Z_0$, so $\Gamma_L = 0$), then $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$, and the input VSWR depends only on $S_{11}$:

$$VSWR_{in} = \dfrac{1 + \vert S_{11}\vert}{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert}$$

**(End of part a — 7 marks: 1 for $\Gamma_{in}$ formula, 2 for VSWR derivation, 1 for limits, 1 for matched-port special case, 2 for engineering explanation.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to linear form.**
- $G_1 = 20$ dB $= 100$ (linear)
- $G_2 = -6$ dB $= 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512$ (linear)
- $F_1 = 1.5$ (linear)
- $F_2 = 6.0$ (linear)

**Step 2 — Apply Friis' formula for the overall noise factor.**

$$F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} = 1.5 + \dfrac{6.0 - 1}{100} = 1.5 + \dfrac{5.0}{100} = 1.5 + 0.05 = 1.55$$

**Step 3 — Convert to noise figure in dB.**

$$NF_{tot} = 10\log_{10}(1.55) = 10 \times 0.1903 = 1.903\ \text{dB} \approx 1.9\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the MDS for $B = 1$ MHz.**

$$MDS = -174\ \text{dBm/Hz} + NF_{tot} + 10\log_{10}(1 \times 10^6)$$
$$MDS = -174 + 1.9 + 60 = -112.1\ \text{dBm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the SFDR.** Given $IIP3 = -5$ dBm:

$$SFDR = \dfrac{2}{3}\,(IIP3 - MDS) = \dfrac{2}{3}\,(-5 - (-112.1)) = \dfrac{2}{3}\,(107.1) \approx 71.4\ \text{dB}$$

**(End of part b — 7 marks: 2 for Friis calculation, 1 for NF in dB, 1 for MDS, 1 for SFDR, 2 for correct logarithmic conversions.)*

**Final Answer (A):** $VSWR = (1 + \vert\Gamma_{in}\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma_{in}\vert)$, range $[1, \infty)$; $NF_{tot} \approx 1.9$ dB; $MDS = -112.1$ dBm; $SFDR \approx 71.4$ dB.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the working of a **two-tone intermodulation test** for measuring the IP3 of an RF amplifier. Derive the relationship between OIP3, output fundamental power, and the IM3 product power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RF amplifier has the following measured S-parameters at 2.4 GHz (with $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$):

$$S_{11} = 0.6\,\angle -150^\circ,\quad S_{12} = 0.05\,\angle 30^\circ,\quad S_{21} = 3.0\,\angle 60^\circ,\quad S_{22} = 0.5\,\angle -80^\circ$$

Compute:
(i) The Rollet's stability factor $K$.
(ii) The Edwards-Sinsky stability measure $\mu$.
(iii) Comment on the stability of the device. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Two-Tone Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Test setup.** Two synthesised RF sources at frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ (separated by a small $\Delta f$, typically 1 MHz) are combined through a passive adder and fed to the Device Under Test (DUT). The output is observed on a spectrum analyzer.

**Step 2 — Observed spectrum.** The output contains:
- The two fundamental tones at $f_1, f_2$ with power $P_{out}$ (each).
- Third-order intermodulation products at $2f_1 - f_2$ and $2f_2 - f_1$ with power $P_{IM3}$ (each).

**Step 3 — Linear extrapolation.** In dB scale, the fundamental line has slope 1 and the IM3 line has slope 3 with respect to input. Extrapolating both lines until they intersect gives the IP3.

**Step 4 — Derivation of the OIP3 formula.** Let $P_f$ and $P_{IM3}$ be in dBm. The extrapolated IM3 line passes through IP3 with slope 3. The vertical distance from the operating point to the IP3 is:

$$OIP3 - P_{IM3} = 3 \times (OIP3 - P_f)\ \text{?}$$

Carefully: in dB, the IM3 line is 3 dB higher for every 1 dB input. So the gap between fundamental and IM3 widens by 2 dB per dB. If at the test point the gap is $D = P_f - P_{IM3}$ (positive), then IP3 lies above the fundamental by $D/2$:

$$\boxed{\,OIP3 = P_f + \dfrac{\vert P_f - P_{IM3}\vert}{2}\,}$$

(Each step in the derivation: 1 mark, formula: 2 marks, diagram: 1 mark.)

---

**Part (b) — Stability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = S_{11} S_{22} - S_{12} S_{21}$$

In rectangular form (using a calculator with $\cos$ and $\sin$ in degrees):

- $S_{11} = 0.6\,\angle -150^\circ = -0.5196 - j\,0.3000$
- $S_{22} = 0.5\,\angle -80^\circ = 0.0868 - j\,0.4924$
- $S_{12} = 0.05\,\angle 30^\circ = 0.0433 + j\,0.0250$
- $S_{21} = 3.0\,\angle 60^\circ = 1.5000 + j\,2.5981$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_{11} S_{22}$.**

$$S_{11} S_{22} = (-0.5196 - j0.3000)(0.0868 - j0.4924)$$
$$= (-0.5196)(0.0868) + (-0.5196)(-j0.4924) + (-j0.3000)(0.0868) + (-j0.3000)(-j0.4924)$$
$$= -0.0451 + j\,0.2558 - j\,0.0260 - 0.1477$$
$$= -0.1928 + j\,0.2298$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{12} S_{21}$.**

$$S_{12} S_{21} = (0.0433 + j0.0250)(1.5000 + j2.5981)$$
$$= 0.0649 + j\,0.1125 + j\,0.0375 - 0.0649$$
$$= 0.0000 + j\,0.1500$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = (-0.1928 + j0.2298) - (0 + j0.1500) = -0.1928 + j0.0798$$
$$\vert \Delta \vert = \sqrt{0.1928^2 + 0.0798^2} = \sqrt{0.0372 + 0.0064} = \sqrt{0.0436} = 0.2088$$

**Step 5 — Compute Rollet's $K$.**

$$K = \dfrac{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2 - \vert S_{22}\vert^2 + \vert \Delta \vert^2}{2 \vert S_{12} S_{21}\vert}$$
$$= \dfrac{1 - 0.36 - 0.25 + 0.0436}{2 \times 0.15} = \dfrac{0.4336}{0.30} = 1.445$$

**Step 6 — Compute Edwards-Sinsky $\mu$.**

$$\mu = \dfrac{1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2}{\vert S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^\ast \vert + \vert S_{12} S_{21}\vert}$$

First compute $\Delta S_{11}^\ast$:

$$S_{11}^\ast = -0.5196 + j0.3000$$
$$\Delta S_{11}^\ast = (-0.1928 + j0.0798)(-0.5196 + j0.3000)$$
$$= 0.1002 - j0.0578 - j0.0415 - 0.0239$$
$$= 0.0763 - j0.0993$$

Now $S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^\ast$:

$$(0.0868 - j0.4924) - (0.0763 - j0.0993) = 0.0105 - j0.3931$$
$$\vert S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^\ast \vert = \sqrt{0.0105^2 + 0.3931^2} = \sqrt{0.0001 + 0.1545} = 0.3932$$

Then:

$$\mu = \dfrac{1 - 0.36}{0.3932 + 0.15} = \dfrac{0.64}{0.5432} = 1.178$$

**Step 7 — Stability comment.**
- $K = 1.445 > 1$ ✓
- $\vert \Delta \vert = 0.2088 < 1$ ✓
- $\mu = 1.178 > 1$ ✓

All three criteria confirm that the device is **unconditionally stable** at 2.4 GHz. The small positive margin of $\mu = 1.18$ (just 18% above unity) indicates that the device is **marginally stable** and care must be taken when designing matching networks, as small deviations could push the network into instability.

**(End of part b — 7 marks: 2 for $\Delta$, 1 for $K$, 2 for $\mu$, 2 for stability interpretation.)*

**Final Answer (B):** Two-tone test as described; $K = 1.445$, $\mu = 1.178$, device is **unconditionally stable but marginally so**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION SHEET — Module 4: Basic Characteristics**

- **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$. Always pair $Z_L$ with $Z_0$ of the system (typically 50 $\Omega$).
- **Return Loss** is always stated as a **positive dB** value: $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$.
- **VSWR ≥ 1**, with 1 being a perfect match. $VSWR = 1$ ⇒ $RL = \infty$ dB; $VSWR = \infty$ ⇒ $RL = 0$ dB.
- **Insertion Loss ≠ Return Loss.** IL is the loss through the device when inserted in a 50 $\Omega$ line; RL is the loss due to reflection back to the source.
- **Noise Factor $F$** is linear (≥ 1); **Noise Figure NF** is in dB (≥ 0 dB).
- **Friis' formula** is the most-tested equation. First stage dominates; always quote gains in linear.
- **P1dB is real and physical; IP3 is fictitious and extrapolated.** IP3 = P1dB + 9.6 dB (theoretical offset).
- **OIP3 is used with output powers; IIP3 is used with input powers.** Mixing them is a guaranteed mark loss.
- **SFDR** = (2/3)(IIP3 − MDS); the "2/3" comes from the slopes 1 and 3.
- **Rollet's $K > 1$** AND **$\vert \Delta \vert < 1$** are both required for unconditional stability (sufficient condition).
- **Edwards-Sinsky $\mu > 1$** is a *single* sufficient condition; $\mu \gg 1$ means *very* stable.
- **Standard reference temperature** $T_0 = 290$ K is the bedrock of all noise-figure derivations; $k_B T_0 = 4.002 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz = **−174 dBm/Hz**.
- **S-parameter reciprocity:** A network is reciprocal if $S_{12} = S_{21}$ (this is a property of *passive* linear networks).
- **Lossless network:** $\mathbf{S}^\dagger \mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I}$ (unitary). The two-port S-matrix satisfies $\vert S_{11}\vert^2 + \vert S_{21}\vert^2 = 1$ and $\vert S_{12}\vert^2 + \vert S_{22}\vert^2 = 1$.
- **Conversion shortcuts to memorize:**
  - dB → linear magnitude: $x_{\text{lin}} = 10^{x_{\text{dB}}/20}$
  - dBm → mW: $P_{\text{mW}} = 10^{P_{\text{dBm}}/10}$
  - 3 dB = factor 2 in **power**; 3 dB = factor $\sqrt{2}$ in voltage; 10 dB = factor 10 in power; 20 dB = factor 10 in voltage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Ended Mixer Design, Single-Balanced and Double-Balanced Mixers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT862 - RF ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single-Ended Mixer Design, Single-Balanced and Double-Balanced Mixers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MIXER TOPOLOGIES IN RF FRONT-ENDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an RF Mixer? (Formal KTU Definition)

An **RF Mixer** is a three-port nonlinear or time-varying device that translates an information-bearing Radio Frequency (RF) signal from one carrier frequency to another by multiplying it with a Local Oscillator (LO) signal. The result is the generation of sum ($f_{RF} + f_{LO}$) and difference ($f_{RF} - f_{LO}$) Intermediate Frequency (IF) products, exploiting the **square-law** or **exponential** nonlinearity of a semiconductor device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Importance):**
> A mixer is essentially a **frequency translator** (not a frequency generator). It does NOT create new energy at the IF; it merely **repositions** the spectral content of the RF signal to a lower (down-conversion) or higher (up-conversion) frequency band for easier processing, filtering, and amplification.

### 1.2 The Three Ports of a Mixer

| Port Symbol | Full Name | Typical Power Level | Function |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **RF** | Radio Frequency | Small-signal (–40 dBm to 0 dBm) | Information-bearing input |
| **LO** | Local Oscillator | Large-signal (+3 dBm to +17 dBm) | Pumping signal — drives nonlinearity |
| **IF** | Intermediate Frequency | Output | Down/up-converted signal |

> [!NOTE]
> The LO is intentionally applied at a **large-signal** level to switch the mixer transistor between ON and OFF states, which is the foundation of **switching-mixer (commutation-mixer)** operation — the most widely used architecture in modern RFICs.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Translation Booth"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: Think of a Mixer as a UN Interpreter at an International Summit.**
> - The **RF delegate** speaks in French (carries the message at frequency $f_{RF}$).
> - The **LO interpreter** speaks both French and German (switches the booth at $f_{LO}$).
> - The **IF audience** hears only the translated message at $f_{IF} = f_{RF} - f_{LO}$ (a new language).
> - The interpreter doesn't "create" the message — only **repositions** it so the audience can understand.
> - **Single-Ended** = one interpreter (cheap, but the audience hears every cough and sneeze).
> - **Balanced** = two or four interpreters, with one deliberately inverting their speech — cancelling out noise and LO leakage.

### 1.4 Why Three Different Topologies?

The KTU 2024 Scheme focuses on **port-to-port isolation**, which is the bane of single-transistor designs. The taxonomy is driven by the engineering trade-off between **simplicity, linearity, and isolation**:

1. **Single-Ended (Unbalanced) Mixer** — Simplest. Poor isolation. Used in low-cost receivers.
2. **Single-Balanced Mixer** — Two devices. Cancels LO noise. Moderate isolation.
3. **Double-Balanced Mixer (Gilbert Cell)** — Four devices. Suppresses ALL even-order spurs. Best isolation and linearity. Industry standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Time-Varying Transconductance

Let the input RF be a small-signal sinusoid: $v_{RF}(t) = v_{rf}\cos(\omega_{RF} t)$

The LO is a large-signal square-wave-like drive that modulates the bias point of the mixer transistor. Because the transistor's transconductance $g_m$ is **time-varying** under LO pumping:

$$
g_m(t) = g_{m0} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} g_{mn}\cos(n\omega_{LO} t)
$$

The output IF current is the product of the time-varying $g_m$ and the small-signal RF voltage:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = g_m(t) \cdot v_{RF}(t) = \left[g_{m0} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} g_{mn}\cos(n\omega_{LO} t)\right] \cdot v_{rf}\cos(\omega_{RF} t)
$$

Trigonometric expansion using product-to-sum identities yields the desired **mixing terms**:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = g_{m0} v_{rf}\cos(\omega_{RF} t) + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{g_{mn} v_{rf}}{2}\Big[\cos((\omega_{RF}-n\omega_{LO})t) + \cos((\omega_{RF}+n\omega_{LO})t)\Big]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For $n = 1$ (fundamental LO):** We obtain the **principal IF mixing product** at $\omega_{IF} = \omega_{RF} - \omega_{LO}$ (down-conversion) and the **image** at $\omega_{RF} + \omega_{LO}$ (up-conversion).

## 2.2 Classification by LO Drive Mode

| Mixer Type | LO Drive Regime | Operating Principle | Linearity |
|------------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------|
| **Square-Law** | Small-signal LO | Taylor series of $i_D = a v_{gs} + b v_{gs}^2$ | Poor — only 2nd-order term is useful |
| **Switching (Commutation)** | Large-signal LO | Transistor acts as ideal switch | Excellent — predominantly odd-order products |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gilbert cell double-balanced mixer** is a **switching mixer** — the LO is large enough (typically +7 to +10 dBm) to fully steer current between two differential pairs, making the analysis a pure commutator problem rather than a Taylor series.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Context |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Output IF Current (square-law) | $i_{IF} = b \cdot v_{rf} v_{LO}$ | A | $b$ = 2nd-order nonlinear coefficient (A/V²) |
| 2 | Conversion Gain (single-ended) | $G_c = \dfrac{g_m^2 R_L}{4}$ | dimensionless (dB) | Applies when LO amplitude $\gg V_T$ |
| 3 | Conversion Gain (Gilbert cell) | $G_c = \dfrac{2}{\pi} g_m R_L$ | dimensionless (dB) | 3.92 dB lower than small-signal gain |
| 4 | LO-RF Feedthrough | $P_{LO,RF} = P_{LO} - \text{ISO}_{LO-RF}$ | dBm | Key spec; >20 dB needed in most KTU problems |
| 5 | Noise Figure (SSB) | $F_{SSB} = \dfrac{F_{DSB}}{2}$ | dimensionless | Single-sideband mixers have 3 dB NF penalty |
| 6 | 1-dB Compression Point | $P_{1dB,in} \approx -10 \text{ dBm}$ (typical BJT) | dBm | Indicates linearity onset |
| 7 | Third-Order Intercept | $IIP3 \approx P_{1dB} + 9.6 \text{ dB}$ | dBm | KTU uses this rule of thumb |
| 8 | Switching LO Frequency Limit | $f_{LO} \le \dfrac{f_T}{5 \text{ to } 10}$ | Hz | Above this, transistor $\beta$ rolls off |
| 9 | Spurious Rejection (Gilbert) | LO, RF feedthrough $\rightarrow 0$ ideally | dB | Only odd-order products remain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Gain vs. Power Gain — KTU Pitfall!**
> The "gain" of a mixer is **Conversion Gain (CG)**, NOT power gain in the traditional $G = P_{out}/P_{in}$ sense at the same frequency. CG is defined as the ratio of **IF output power** to **RF input power** — the signal is at a *different frequency* on the output side. Some passive mixers (e.g., diode-ring) have **conversion LOSS** (CG < 0 dB).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless handsets (4G/5G):** Gilbert-cell mixers in Zero-IF (homodyne) transceivers.
- **Radar systems:** Single-balanced mixers used as phase detectors in Doppler radars.
