- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Double-balanced mixers suppress LO self-mixing and DC offsets.
- **Cable TV head-ends:** Double-balanced diode-ring mixers for 50–1000 MHz up-conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Gain of a Single-Ended BJT Mixer

### Step 1 — Define the BJT large-signal transconductance under LO pumping

A BJT with LO applied at the base and RF at the emitter (or vice versa) operates in the **forward-active region**. The collector current is:

$$
i_C(t) = I_S \exp\!\left(\frac{v_{BE}(t)}{V_T}\right)
$$

where $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at room temperature (**bold** = physical constant).

The LO voltage at the base is large: $v_{LO}(t) = V_{LO}\cos(\omega_{LO} t)$, with $V_{LO} \gg V_T$.

### Step 2 — Compute the incremental transconductance

The time-varying transconductance is the derivative of the collector current with respect to the base-emitter voltage:

$$
g_m(t) = \frac{1}{V_T}\, i_C(t) = \frac{I_S}{V_T}\exp\!\left(\frac{v_{BE}(t)}{V_T}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Express $g_m(t)$ as a Fourier series

Because $v_{LO}(t)$ is a large sinusoid, $g_m(t)$ is a **periodic pulse train** (not a pure sinusoid). The Fourier series of $g_m(t)$ is:

$$
g_m(t) = g_{m0} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} g_{mn}\cos(n\omega_{LO} t + \phi_n)
$$

The fundamental Fourier coefficient (the most important for mixing) is:

$$
g_{m1} = \dfrac{g_{m0}}{2}, \quad \text{where } g_{m0} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Meaning:** $g_{m1}$ is the **effective mixing transconductance** — it directly sets the conversion gain. The factor of $1/2$ is the price we pay for using a *pulsed* LO instead of a pure sinusoid.

### Step 4 — Multiply by the small RF signal

The output IF current at the difference frequency is the product of $g_{m1}$ and the RF voltage, with a factor of 1/2 from the cosine product identity:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\, g_{m1}\, v_{rf}\cos\!\big((\omega_{RF} - \omega_{LO})t\big)
$$

Substituting $g_{m1} = g_{m0}/2$:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = \dfrac{g_{m0}\, v_{rf}}{4}\cos\!\big((\omega_{IF})t\big)
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the output IF voltage across a load $R_L$

$$
v_{IF}(t) = i_{IF}(t) \cdot R_L = \dfrac{g_{m0}\, R_L\, v_{rf}}{4}\cos(\omega_{IF} t)
$$

### Step 6 — Form the conversion gain

$$
G_c = \dfrac{v_{IF}}{v_{rf}} = \dfrac{g_{m0} R_L}{4}
$$

In decibel form:

$$
\boxed{G_c \,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{g_{m0} R_L}{4}\right)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the factor 4?** Two factors of 2: (i) $g_{m1} = g_{m0}/2$ from Fourier series, (ii) $1/2$ from the cosine product-to-sum identity. KTU examiners LOVE asking "where did the 1/4 come from?"

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Gain of a Gilbert Cell (Double-Balanced) Mixer

### Step 1 — Model the LO switches as ideal commutation

In a Gilbert cell, the LO is large enough that the cross-coupled differential pair (Q5, Q6) acts as a **perfect polarity-reversing switch** at frequency $\omega_{LO}$. The commutated RF current is therefore:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = \text{sgn}\!\big(\cos(\omega_{LO} t)\big) \cdot g_m v_{rf}\cos(\omega_{RF} t)
$$

### Step 2 — Fourier-expand the sign function

The square wave $\text{sgn}(\cos(\omega_{LO} t))$ has a Fourier series:

$$
\text{sgn}\!\big(\cos(\omega_{LO} t)\big) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\cos(n\omega_{LO} t)
$$

The fundamental term ($n = 1$) has amplitude $\dfrac{4}{\pi}$.

### Step 3 — Multiply and extract the IF term

The IF (down-conversion) component comes from the $n=1$ term times the RF cosine:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\cos(\omega_{LO} t) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} g_m v_{rf}\cos(\omega_{RF} t)
$$

Using $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
i_{IF}(t) = \dfrac{2 g_m v_{rf}}{\pi}\cos\!\big((\omega_{RF}-\omega_{LO})t\big)
$$

### Step 4 — Form the conversion gain

With load resistor $R_L$:

$$
\boxed{G_c = \dfrac{2 g_m R_L}{\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_c\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 g_m R_L}{\pi}\right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** $\dfrac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.637$, which in dB is $20\log_{10}(0.637) = \mathbf{-3.92 \text{ dB}}$.
> This is the famous **"Gilbert cell conversion gain penalty"** — the switching action inherently loses 3.92 dB compared to a small-signal amplifier with the same $g_m R_L$.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Single vs. Single-Balanced vs. Double-Balanced)

| Performance Metric | Single-Ended | Single-Balanced | Double-Balanced (Gilbert) |
|--------------------|:------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------------:|
| **Number of transistors** | 1 | 2 (or 1 balun) | 4 (Gilbert) or 4 diodes (ring) |
| **LO drive level** | Low (+0 dBm) | Medium (+3 to +7 dBm) | High (+7 to +13 dBm) |
| **RF–LO isolation** | Poor (0–10 dB) | Good (20–30 dB) | Excellent (>40 dB) |
| **LO–IF isolation** | Poor (0–10 dB) | Excellent (theoretical ∞) | Excellent (theoretical ∞) |
| **RF–IF isolation** | Depends on filter | Good with filter | Excellent (>30 dB) |
| **LO feedthrough to IF** | Present (large) | Suppressed by 180° cancellation | Suppressed by full symmetry |
| **RF feedthrough to IF** | Present | Present (similar to single-ended) | Suppressed by symmetry |
| **Conversion gain** | $g_m R_L / 4$ | $g_m R_L / 2$ | $2 g_m R_L / \pi$ |
| **Noise figure (SSB)** | 8–12 dB | 6–10 dB | 8–14 dB (Gilbert is noisy!) |
| **Linearity (IIP3)** | 0 dBm | +3 dBm | –2 to +5 dBm |
| **Power consumption** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (needs bias) |
| **Spur rejection** | None | LO & even-order spurs | All even-order spurs |
| **Application** | Disposable tags | TV tuners, low-cost Rx | Cell phones, SDR, radar |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Ended Mixer — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    RFIN[RF Input Port] --> ATTN1[Matching Network]
    ATTN1 --> BIAS1[DC Bias Tee]
    BIAS1 --> M1[Mixer Transistor BJT or FET]
    LOIN[LO Input Port] --> BIAS2[DC Bias Tee]
    BIAS2 --> M1
    M1 --> IFPORT[IF Output Port via Lowpass Filter]
    M1 -.->|Undesired Leakage| IFPORT
    M1 -.->|LO to RF Leakage| RFIN

    style RFIN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style LOIN fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300
    style IFPORT fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e5b1e
    style M1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the *dashed* lines — these are **leakage paths** that single-ended mixers CANNOT cancel. They are the dominant source of port-to-port isolation failure.

## 4.2 Single-Balanced Mixer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    LOIN[LO Port] --> SPLIT[180 Degree Hybrid Balun or Active Splitter]
    SPLIT --> IN0[LO Plus 0 degree]
    SPLIT --> IN180[LO Minus 180 degree]
    IN0 --> M1[Mixer 1 BJT]
    IN180 --> M2[Mixer 2 BJT]
    RFIN[RF Port] --> SPLITRF[RF Power Splitter In-Phase]
    SPLITRF --> M1
    SPLITRF --> M2
    M1 --> COMBINE[Summing Combiner]
    M2 --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> LPF[Lowpass IF Filter]
    LPF --> IFPORT[IF Output Port]

    style LOIN fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300
    style RFIN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style IFPORT fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e5b1e
    style M1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style M2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style SPLIT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style COMBINE fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Cancellation Mechanism:** The LO drives M1 with $+V_{LO}\cos(\omega_{LO}t)$ and M2 with $-V_{LO}\cos(\omega_{LO}t)$. The resulting LO-induced IF currents are **equal in magnitude and opposite in sign** at the summing node — they cancel perfectly. This is the origin of *theoretical infinite* LO-to-IF isolation in single-balanced designs.

## 4.3 Double-Balanced Gilbert Cell Mixer — Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Transconductance_Stage
        RFIN[RF Input] --> QM1[Transistor M1]
        RFIN --> QM2[Transistor M2]
        VBIAS1[DC Bias Tail Current] --> QM1
        VBIAS1 --> QM2
    end

    subgraph LO_Switching_Quad
        QM1 --> QS1A[LO Switch SA]
        QM1 --> QS1B[LO Switch SB]
        QM2 --> QS2A[LO Switch SC]
        QM2 --> QS2B[LO Switch SD]
        LOIN_P[LO Plus] --> QS1A
        LOIN_P --> QS2B
        LOIN_M[LO Minus] --> QS1B
        LOIN_M --> QS2A
    end

    subgraph Load_Stage
        QS1A --> RL1[Load Resistor RL]
        QS1B --> RL2[Load Resistor RL]
        QS2A --> RL2
        QS2B --> RL1
        RL1 --> IFP[IF Output Plus]
        RL2 --> IFM[IF Output Minus]
    end

    style RFIN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style LOIN_P fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300
    style LOIN_M fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300
    style IFP fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e5b1e
    style IFM fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e5b1e
    style VBIAS1 fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#3b0d5e
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the double balance is "double":**
> 1. The RF stage (M1, M2) is *differential* → RF feedthrough to IF cancels at the load.
> 2. The LO quad is *cross-coupled* → LO feedthrough to IF cancels at the load.
> 3. Only the product term (RF × LO) survives — all even-order spurs vanish.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RF Mixer Design Requirement] --> B{Isolation Needed?}
    B -->|No Low Cost| C[Single-Ended Mixer]
    B -->|Yes LO Only| D[Single-Balanced Mixer]
    B -->|Yes RF and LO| E[Double-Balanced Gilbert Cell]
    C --> F1[Used in RFID tags sensors]
    D --> F2[Used in TV tuners low cost Rx]
    E --> F3[Used in cell phones SDR radar]
    E --> G{Spurs Critical?}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define conversion gain of a mixer. How is it different from the power gain of an amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Conversion Gain ($G_c$) of a mixer is defined as the ratio of the **IF output power** to the **RF input power**, expressed in dB. Mathematically:

$$
G_c\,\text{[dB]} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{IF}}{P_{RF}}\right)
$$

**Difference from amplifier gain (1 mark):** In an amplifier, the input and output signals are at the **same frequency**; hence the gain is a direct power amplification. In a mixer, the input is at $\omega_{RF}$ and the output is at $\omega_{IF} = \omega_{RF} - \omega_{LO}$ — the signal undergoes a **frequency translation**. Some passive mixers even have **conversion loss** (negative dB) because resistive dividers dissipate power.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional II]`
**Why is a double-balanced mixer preferred over a single-ended mixer in modern transceivers? (Mention any three reasons.)**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Superior port-to-port isolation:** In a Gilbert cell double-balanced mixer, both LO-to-IF and RF-to-IF feedthroughs are suppressed by circuit symmetry (typically >40 dB), whereas a single-ended mixer suffers 0–10 dB leakage.

2. **Spurious rejection:** All even-order intermodulation products (2RF, 2LO, RF±LO with 2nd harmonic) are cancelled, leaving only the desired odd-order mixing terms.

3. **Higher linearity and dynamic range:** The differential structure provides better linearity (higher IIP3) and rejects common-mode noise and power supply variations, making it ideal for high-density wireless standards like LTE and 5G NR.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **single-balanced mixer**. Derive an expression for its conversion gain.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
          LO Input
             |
          180° Splitter (Balun)
           /                \
      LO+ (0°)           LO- (180°)
         |                    |
     Mixer Transistor 1   Mixer Transistor 2
         ^                    ^
         |____ RF Input (in-phase split) ____|
         |                    |
         |                    |
       Summing Node (virtual ground)
             |
        Low-Pass IF Filter
             |
          IF Output
```

**Operation (2 marks):** The LO signal is split by a 180° balun into two anti-phase components driving two identical mixer transistors. The RF signal is split in-phase and fed to both transistors. At the IF summing node, the LO-induced contributions from both transistors are equal and opposite → they cancel. The mixing products (RF × LO) add constructively.

**Conversion Gain Derivation (2 marks):**

Let the transconductance of each transistor be $g_m$, and assume the LO is large enough to switch each transistor. The fundamental Fourier coefficient of the switching function is $\dfrac{2}{\pi}$.

The IF current at the difference frequency from one transistor is:

$$
i_{IF,1}(t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot g_{m1}\cdot v_{rf}\cos((\omega_{RF}-\omega_{LO})t), \quad g_{m1} = \dfrac{2 g_m}{\pi}
$$

The second transistor contributes an equal in-phase current (because its RF is the same, but its LO is inverted — and the cosine product is symmetric in sign of LO):

$$
i_{IF,2}(t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot g_{m1}\cdot v_{rf}\cos((\omega_{RF}-\omega_{LO})t)
$$

Total IF current: $i_{IF} = g_{m1} v_{rf} \cos(\omega_{IF} t)$.

Across load $R_L$:

$$
\boxed{G_c = \dfrac{g_{m1} R_L}{2} = \dfrac{g_m R_L}{\pi}}
$$

**Numerical Example (bonus, not mandatory):** For $g_m = 30$ mA/V and $R_L = 1$ kΩ, $G_c = 0.03 \times 1000 / \pi = 9.55$ → in dB: $20\log_{10}(9.55) = 19.6$ dB.

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:** A single-ended BJT mixer uses a transistor with $g_m = 25$ mA/V biased at $I_C = 1$ mA, $R_L = 800\,\Omega$, and is driven by a large LO. Calculate the (i) conversion gain in dB, (ii) the conversion gain in dB if the same transistor were used in a single-balanced configuration, and (iii) comment on why the single-balanced version provides better LO-to-IF isolation.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Single-ended conversion gain (3 marks):**

Using the single-ended formula $G_c = g_m R_L / 4$:

$$
G_c = \dfrac{(25\times 10^{-3})(800)}{4} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5.0
$$

$$
G_c\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}(5.0) = \mathbf{13.98 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(ii) Single-balanced conversion gain (2 marks):**

Using $G_c = g_m R_L / \pi$ (from derivation in A(a)):

$$
G_c = \dfrac{(25\times 10^{-3})(800)}{\pi} = \dfrac{20}{3.1416} = 6.366
$$

$$
G_c\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}(6.366) = \mathbf{16.08 \text{ dB}}
$$

This is **2.1 dB higher** than the single-ended case.

**(iii) LO-to-IF isolation (2 marks):**

In a single-ended mixer, the LO signal couples directly to the IF output through the transistor's intrinsic parasitic capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) and is un-cancellable. The single-balanced mixer drives two transistors with **anti-phase** LO signals, generating IF currents from LO leakage that are **equal in magnitude and opposite in phase** at the summing node. They cancel, providing theoretically infinite LO-to-IF isolation. Practically, mismatches between the two transistors limit isolation to 20–30 dB — still vastly better than the 0–10 dB of single-ended.

---

### CHOICE B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **double-balanced Gilbert cell mixer**. Why is it called "double-balanced"?

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):**

```
         VDD
          |
        RL        RL
          |        |
   IF+ -- +        + -- IF-
          |        |
    +--[Q1]      [Q3]--+
    |     \      /     |
    |      [Q5]        |
RF--+       /  \       +--RF
    |      [Q6]        |
    |     /      \     |
    +--[Q2]      [Q4]--+
          |        |
         VSS (Tail Current Source)
```

Where:
- Q1, Q2 form the **lower differential pair** (RF input).
- Q3, Q4 form the **upper differential pair** (RF input crossed).
- Q5, Q6 form the **LO switching quad** (cross-coupled).
- RL are the **load resistors**.

**Operation (2 marks):** The RF signal enters the lower differential pair (Q1, Q2), which linearly converts it to a differential current. This current is steered alternately between the two output nodes by the LO-driven cross-coupled quad (Q5, Q6). The cross-coupling causes a **180° polarity reversal** of the RF current at rate $\omega_{LO}$. Multiplication with the LO switching function produces the IF output at the difference frequency.

**Why "Double-Balanced" (2 marks):**

1. **First balance (LO):** The cross-coupling of Q5, Q6 makes the LO drive *differential* — any LO leakage to the IF port cancels at the output summing node.
2. **Second balance (RF):** The differential RF drive through Q1–Q4, combined with the cross-coupling of the LO quad, ensures that the RF feedthrough to the IF port is also cancelled (because the RF-induced IF current has equal positive and negative components on the two load resistors that sum to zero).

Thus, both RF and LO feedthroughs are suppressed — the mixer is balanced in *two* dimensions → **double-balanced**.

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:** A Gilbert cell mixer has $g_m = 20$ mA/V (transconductance stage), load resistors $R_L = 1.5$ kΩ, and is driven by a large LO. Compute the (i) conversion gain in dB, and (ii) the 3-dB improvement in noise figure when the mixer is preceded by an image-reject filter.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Conversion gain (4 marks):**

For a Gilbert cell double-balanced mixer:

$$
G_c = \dfrac{2 g_m R_L}{\pi} = \dfrac{2 \times (20 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1500}{\pi} = \dfrac{60}{3.1416} = 19.10
$$

$$
G_c\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}(19.10) = \mathbf{25.62 \text{ dB}}
$$

(Equivalently, the small-signal gain is $g_m R_L = 30$ → $20\log_{10}(30) = 29.54$ dB, and we subtract the 3.92 dB switching penalty: $29.54 - 3.92 = 25.62$ dB. ✓)

**(ii) Image-reject filter noise figure benefit (3 marks):**

A mixer is inherently an **SSB device by default** — it has *two* RF sidebands (upper and lower) that both down-convert to the same IF. Each sideband adds uncorrelated noise, so the SSB noise figure is **3 dB worse** than the DSB noise figure:

$$
F_{SSB} = 2 F_{DSB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad NF_{SSB} = NF_{DSB} + 3 \text{ dB}
$$

An **image-reject filter** placed before the mixer suppresses the unwanted sideband (the image at $f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ when down-converting $f_{RF} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$). This removes one of the two noise contributions, lowering the noise figure by:

$$
\Delta NF = 10\log_{10}(2) = \mathbf{3 \text{ dB}}
$$

So if the mixer's DSB NF is, say, 8 dB, adding the image-reject filter brings the *effective* NF down to ~5 dB (closer to DSB performance).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing conversion gain with voltage gain.** Many students write $G_c = v_{IF}/v_{RF}$ but call it "power gain." It is **conversion** gain — frequency-translated, not at the same frequency. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the $\pi$ and the factor of 2.** For Gilbert cell, the correct formula is $G_c = 2 g_m R_L / \pi$, not $g_m R_L$ or $g_m R_L/2$. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Not stating the LO must be "large-signal."** A derivation that assumes a small-signal LO and uses Taylor series to *second order* is a square-law mixer, not a switching mixer. State the assumption explicitly. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up SSB and DSB noise figures.** Always clarify which one is being asked. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing or wrong unit in Part B numerical sub-parts.
> 6. **Drawing single-ended as balanced.** Block diagrams without the 180° balun for a single-balanced question will lose 2 full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Memorize List — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ☐ Mixer = **frequency translator**, not generator. Three ports: **RF, LO, IF**.
- ☐ Conversion gain: $G_c = P_{IF}/P_{RF}$ — *not* $P_{out}/P_{in}$ at the same frequency.
- ☐ **Single-Ended** → 1 transistor, factor of **1/4** in $G_c = g_m R_L/4$.
- ☐ **Single-Balanced** → 2 transistors with 180° LO, $G_c = g_m R_L / \pi$, **infinite LO-IF isolation (ideal)**.
- ☐ **Double-Balanced (Gilbert Cell)** → 4 transistors, $G_c = 2 g_m R_L/\pi$, **3.92 dB switching penalty**, suppresses both RF and LO feedthrough.
- ☐ **Active mixer** has conversion **gain** (CG > 0 dB). **Passive mixer (diode ring)** has conversion **loss** (CG < 0 dB).
- ☐ **SSB noise figure = DSB noise figure + 3 dB** (image-reject filter saves the 3 dB).
- ☐ **IIP3 ≈ P1dB + 9.6 dB** (KTU rule of thumb).
- ☐ LO drive must be **large** ($V_{LO} \gg V_T$) for switching-mixer operation.
- ☐ Gilbert cell: $f_{LO} \le f_T/5$ to $f_T/10$ for proper switching action.
- ☐ Single-balanced mixer is **not** immune to RF-to-IF feedthrough — only double-balanced is.
- ☐ Image frequency = $f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ (when $f_{RF} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$).
